title: Trying to understand the errors in ‘Initial Supply’ (on Tue 11th and Thu 6th November) as the source of the gremlins
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trying to understand the errors in ‘Initial Supply’ (on Tue 11th and Thu 6th November) as the source of the gremlins" posted at 13-11-2025 10:32:00 AM is: This article investigates two recent frequency spike events in the Australian National Electricity Market on November 6 and November 11, 2025, caused by errors in the 'Initial Supply' data for Victoria. By cross-checking bottom-up calculations with AEMO's aggregated data, the author confirms that non-zero errors in INITIALSUPPLY align perfectly with the periods of frequency deviations, indicating a transient gremlin in the data gathering (step 1a) or pre-processing (step 1b) steps that inflated market demand; the exact root cause remains unidentified. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the dispatch intervals on November 6 and November 11, 2025. Its root_cause is: Error in the INITIALSUPPLY calculation for Victoria due to a transient gremlin in data gathering or pre-processing steps. Its accident scale is: Unknown (involved 4 dispatch intervals on 6 Nov and 14 on 11 Nov). Its dates is: November 6 and November 11, 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely AEMO, generators, and consumers). Its cause type is: Data/calculation error. Its causes is: Error in INITIALSUPPLY data leading to inflated market demand; potential issues in step 1a (data gathering) or step 1b (pre-processing) of the dispatch interval calculation.

title: How often have we seen these gremlins, injecting ‘phantom’ demand and driving frequency high? … a 1st pass
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How often have we seen these gremlins, injecting ‘phantom’ demand and driving frequency high? … a 1st pass" posted at 12-11-2025 07:37:00 PM is: The article documents repeated occurrences of phantom demand being injected into AEMO's dispatch process, causing NEMDE to over-dispatch supply and drive frequency spikes; two recent events on 6 and 11 November 2025 saw glitch sizes of approximately 1,812 MW and 1,130 MW respectively, with earlier incidents dating back to 2024, all attributed to scheduling errors. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – during dispatch intervals when glitches occur, with frequency deviations lasting minutes. Its root_cause is: Scheduling error or data glitch causing phantom demand injection into NEMDE. Its accident scale is: Largest recent glitch sizes: ~1,812 MW (6 Nov 2025) and ~1,130 MW (11 Nov 2025); historical maximum ~3,100 MW (9 Dec 2024). Its dates are: 6 November 2025, 11 November 2025, 19 February 2025, 31 January 2025, 9 December 2024, and period 24 January to 7 March 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria (primary) and New South Wales. Its affected key users is: AEMO, generators, market participants, and end consumers (due to frequency excursions outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band). Its cause type is: Operational glitch / scheduling error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Phantom demand injection into AEMO's NEMDE dispatch process, leading to over-dispatch of supply and frequency spikes.

title: AEMO (and the BOM) warning about possible ‘Severe Space Weather’ (at G4, possible G5) in next ~24 hours
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO (and the BOM) warning about possible ‘Severe Space Weather’ (at G4, possible G5) in next ~24 hours" posted at 12-11-2025 12:33:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 10th November 2025 warning of potential severe space weather (G4 geomagnetic storm with possible G5) within the next 24 hours, based on BOM forecasts. The storm could cause widespread voltage control problems, false tripping of protective systems, and in extreme cases, grid collapse or transformer damage, as outlined by NOAA scales. The event is driven by a strong shock in the solar wind from recent solar activity.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, within 24 hours from 12-Nov-2025, lasting hours to days.; Its root_cause is: Severe geomagnetic storm caused by solar activity (coronal mass ejection/shock in solar wind).; Its accident scale is: G4–G5 on the NOAA space weather scale (Severe to Extreme).; Its dates are: 12-Nov-2025 (event forecast date).; Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly mid to southern parts of Australia.; Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators, power system assets, transformers, and all connected consumers.; Its cause type is: Natural phenomenon (space weather).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Strong shock in solar wind detected at 11/2338UT; solar activity expected at R2-R3; resulting in G4 geomagnetic storm conditions with chance of G5; solar radiation storm at S2 expected to reach S3.

title: AEMO directs again in South Australia for Minimum System Load – Wednesday 12th November 2025 (second day in a row)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO directs again in South Australia for Minimum System Load – Wednesday 12th November 2025 (second day in a row)" posted at 12-11-2025 is: On 12 November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a second consecutive direction to AGL SA Generation’s Torrens Island Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in South Australia to remain synchronised and follow dispatch targets from 07:00 to 14:30 hrs, in response to a forecast Minimum System Load (MSL) condition that created an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state. This market intervention, mandated under clause 4.8.9(a1)(1) of the National Electricity Rules, declared the trading intervals as intervention events without intervention pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 November 2025 (07:00 to 14:30). Its root_cause is: Forecast minimum system load leading to elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 12-11-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO and AGL SA Generation (Torrens Island BESS). Its cause type is: Grid stability management (minimum system load intervention). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Forecast minimum system load, Insufficient demand to maintain secure operating state].

title: More, about noisy MSL warnings for South Australia … and an Actual MSL2 and AEMO Direction on Tuesday 11th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More, about noisy MSL warnings for South Australia … and an Actual MSL2 and AEMO Direction on Tuesday 11th November 2025" posted at 11-11-2025 12:50:00 PM is: On Tuesday 11th November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator issued directions to the Torrens Island BESS and declared actual Minimum System Load (MSL) conditions (MSL1 and MSL2) in South Australia due to extremely low and even negative demand forecasts, creating an elevated risk of insufficient demand for maintaining a secure operating state. The directions and MSL alerts also warned of forecast MSL2 conditions continuing on 12th November 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing (11-12 November 2025). Its root_cause is: Insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state due to high renewable generation penetration (solar/wind) driving operational demand below MSL thresholds. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (system condition, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 11/11/2025 and 12/11/2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: AEMO, AGL SA Generation (Torrens Island BESS), and all National Electricity Market participants. Its cause type is: Minimum System Load event (operational). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Forecast operational demand dropping to -5 MW and below MSL1/MSL2 thresholds; high renewable generation causing negative demand; AEMO direction to BESS to remain synchronized; actual MSL2 condition declared at 12:36 hrs.

title: Kogan Creek trips (on Tuesday morning 11th November 2025) … and ironically helps frequency
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek trips (on Tuesday morning 11th November 2025) … and ironically helps frequency" posted at 11-11-2025 10:25:00 AM is: On Tuesday 11 November 2025, a unit at the Kogan Creek power station tripped unexpectedly, ironically providing frequency relief to a grid that was experiencing an over-frequency condition, thereby helping to avoid a possible excursion outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band. The unit had just returned from a planned outage the previous day and was expected back online later that evening.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: turbine/generator failures.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (same day, expected return to service that evening).; Its root_cause is: Unknown.; Its accident scale is: Minor (single unit trip).; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-11-2025.; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Kogan Creek) and potentially broader National Electricity Market (NEM).; Its affected key users is: CS Energy (owner), AEMO (grid operator).; Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (trip).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unit tripped unexpectedly during operation; no specific cause identified in the article.

title: Has it happened again … have gremlins invaded NEMDE and triggered a frequency excursion (Tuesday 11th November 2025)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Has it happened again … have gremlins invaded NEMDE and triggered a frequency excursion (Tuesday 11th November 2025)?" posted at 11-11-2025 09:41:00 AM is: On Tuesday 11th November 2025, a repeat incident occurred where erroneous input data for Victoria’s Market Demand caused a large 1,130 MW drop at the 09:15 dispatch interval, leading to a frequency excursion in the NEM, similar to an event on 6th November 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, within the dispatch interval (minutes); Its root_cause is: erroneous input data for Victoria's Market Demand (gremlins in NEMDE); Its accident scale is: 1,130 MW drop in Market Demand; Its dates is: 11-11-2025; Its affected region is: Victoria and the broader National Electricity Market (NEM); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: data/system input error; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Erroneous input data for Victoria's Market Demand, (2) A 1,130 MW drop in Market Demand at the 09:15 dispatch interval, (3) Consequent frequency excursion.

title: Angela’s article in AFR answers some of our "What’s up with Waratah BESS?" question
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Angela’s article in AFR answers some of our "What’s up with Waratah BESS?" question" posted at 11-11-2025 08:53:00 AM is: The article details a catastrophic transformer failure at the Waratah BESS during Hold Point 5 testing, resulting in one transformer damaged beyond repair and a second taken offline for testing. The battery now operates at reduced capacity (350 MW) for SIPS, with full capacity expected in 2026, and the unplanned outage is scheduled until 3 May 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from around 18 October 2025 (when availability dropped to 350 MW) until 3 May 2026 (expected return to full service). Its root_cause is: transformer damage during Hold Point 5 testing, with one transformer beyond repair and potential issues with a second transformer. Its accident scale is: one transformer destroyed, another possibly requiring replacement; battery operating at 350 MW instead of full capacity (unspecified full capacity, but likely ~850 MW based on context). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: event failure occurred around 18 October 2025; outage reported on 7 November 2025; return to service date 3 May 2026. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (NSW grid). Its affected key users is: NSW electricity grid and SIPS service; Eraring Power Station extension relevance noted. Its cause type is: equipment failure (transformer). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Damage to one transformer beyond repair during Hold Point 5 testing; 2) Issues identified with a second transformer requiring testing; 3) Possible design or manufacturing fault common to all transformers.

title: Sunday evening trip at Callide C4, and wobbles coming back online
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunday evening trip at Callide C4, and wobbles coming back online" posted at 10-11-2025 09:21:00 AM is: On Sunday 9 November 2025 at 21:26 NEM time, the Callide C4 generator tripped from near full load, causing frequency dips and subsequent instability as the unit attempted to return to service with multiple false starts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (the trip caused frequency wobbles and grid instability, with ongoing impacts during reconnection attempts). Its root_cause is: Unknown (inferred as a generator trip at Callide C4). Its accident scale is: Single unit trip (Callide C4). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-11-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Callide (Queensland, Australia), NEM (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, grid operators. Its cause type is: Technical failure/generator trip. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ['Callide C4 tripped from close to full load at 21:26 on 9 Nov 2025.', 'Subsequent false starts and wobbles while attempting to return to service.']

title: A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS from 19th October to 8th November 2025 (with PASA Avail 350MW)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS from 19th October to 8th November 2025 (with PASA Avail 350MW)" posted at 08-11-2025 01:24:00 PM is: The article examines rebids and operational data for the Waratah BESS battery from 19 October to 7 November 2025, noting that after Hold Point 5 testing in mid-October, the unit has been consistently showing a PASA Availability of 350MW (matching the SIPS provision enabled by TransGrid). The author questions what further steps are needed to allow higher capacity operation and references a separate article about an unplanned outage until 3 May 2026, indicating ongoing operational uncertainty. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 19 October 2025 to at least 8 November 2025, with the unplanned outage extending to 3 May 2026. Its root_cause is: Unknown; possibly related to incomplete testing or technical issues preventing full capacity operation beyond 350MW. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported, but an unplanned outage of several months is noted). Its dates is: 08-11-2025 (main date) and the event period 19-10-2025 to 08-11-2025. Its affected region is: Waratah BESS, part of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators (TransGrid, AEMO) and possibly consumers relying on system stability services. Its cause type is: Operational/testing issue. Its causes is: Hold Point 5 testing completed in mid-October; subsequent unplanned outage declared; further steps required to enable higher capacity operation; lack of public explanation for the capacity limitation.

title: A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS to 19th October 2025 (during Hold Point 5 testing for full discharge)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS to 19th October 2025 (during Hold Point 5 testing for full discharge)" posted at 08-11-2025 12:54:00 PM is: This article examines the rebidding behavior of the Waratah Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) during a 9-day period ending 18 October 2025, when it briefly operated at full discharge and charge capacity as part of Hold Point 5 testing. The analysis, prompted by the earlier discovery of an unplanned outage at the same facility (extending to May 2026), uses ez2view’s Bids & Offers widget to highlight how the battery’s bids and offers changed during the test window. The piece is one of several follow-up articles providing greater detail on the battery’s operations and market interactions. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 9-day period from 10 October to 18 October 2025 (with rebids submitted and effective during that window). Its root_cause is: Hold Point 5 testing of the Waratah BESS to validate full discharge and charge capability. Its accident scale is: Not an accident; planned operational testing of a single large-scale battery (700 MW / 1,400 MWh). Its dates (if different from main date) is: 10-18 October 2025 (the test and rebid period). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) – the location of the Waratah BESS, which is part of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator), Akaysha Energy (owner/operator), NEM market participants, and grid operators relying on the battery’s services. Its cause type is: Planned commissioning and testing activity. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) The Waratah BESS underwent Hold Point 5 testing to verify its ability to achieve full discharge and charge; 2) As part of this testing, the battery submitted revised bids and offers in the NEM that deviated from its normal pattern; 3) The testing period coincided with the battery briefly reaching its full maximum capacity, prompting the author to examine the corresponding rebid data.

title: Belatedly noting that the AER published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist (back on Friday 29th August 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly noting that the AER published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist (back on Friday 29th August 2025)" posted at 07-11-2025 10:26:00 PM is: This article discusses the belated recognition that the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist on 29 August 2025, which clarifies how battery operators with System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS) contracts should report availability in MT PASA (excluding SIPS reserve) and ST PASA (including SIPS reserve), addressing confusion observed in Waratah BESS outage data. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 29 August 2025 onward (ongoing compliance guidance). Its root_cause is: Need for regulatory clarity on how SIPS reserve capacity should be reflected in PASA availability submissions for batteries, following observed data inconsistencies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 29-08-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery operators (especially those with SIPS contracts), AEMO, market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) AER published PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist on 29 August 2025; 2) Bulletin provides specific guidance on MT PASA (exclude SIPS reserve) vs ST PASA (include SIPS reserve) for batteries; 3) Previous confusion in Waratah BESS outage data prompted this review.

title: An updated longer term trend (and tabulation) of operations at Waratah BESS
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An updated longer term trend (and tabulation) of operations at Waratah BESS" posted at 07-11-2025 09:54:00 PM is: This article provides an updated chronological record and trend analysis of the Waratah BESS (850MW/1,680MWh) in New South Wales, covering operations from August 2025 to 7th November 2025. It highlights a significant unplanned outage flagged on 7th November 2025, reported to last until 3rd May 2026, following earlier peak testing in mid-October 2025 where full discharge (850MW) and charge were achieved. Subsequently, from 19th October 2025, market-based output became more muted with lower availability, and the unit's PASA Availability was reverted to 350MW after Hold Point 5 testing. The article also references related events such as the SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme) going live on 1st August 2025, media coverage of record outputs, and the impact of network upgrades for the battery. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 7th November 2025 to 3rd May 2026 (projected outage duration), with prior muted operations from 19th October 2025. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of the Waratah BESS unit (specific technical cause not disclosed). Its accident scale is: Unknown (significant as it involves a major 850MW battery with a multi-month outage). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-11-2025 (main date), with outage noted from this date. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia (specifically the Central Coast and broader NEM region). Its affected key users is: Grid operators (AEMO, TransGrid), system reliability managers, and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Unplanned generator/asset outage (technical failure or maintenance issue). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Unplanned outage declared for WTAHB1 unit on 7th November 2025, with MT PASA Availability showing outage until 3rd May 2026; 2) Prior reduced output and availability since 19th October 2025 after Hold Point 5 testing ended, with PASA Availability set back to 350MW.

title: Some sizeable cycles in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Thursday 6th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Some sizeable cycles in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Thursday 6th November 2025" posted at 07-11-2025 04:04:00 PM is: On 6 November 2025, South Australia experienced two distinct cycles of Market Demand between 14:00 and 16:00 NEM time, following a suspected data-glitch triggered significant frequency ramp event earlier in the day; the demand cycles were initially attributed to rooftop PV output changes due to a possible disappearance of cloud cover over Adelaide.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the day of 6 November 2025).  
Its root_cause is: A combination of a suspected data glitch causing a frequency ramp and possible rapid changes in rooftop PV generation due to cloud cover disappearance.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (documented as "sizeable cycles" but without quantified magnitude).  
Its dates is: 06-11-2025 (same as the event date).  
Its affected region is: South Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely grid operators and residential/commercial electricity consumers in the region).  
Its cause type is: Operational/technical (data glitch) and weather-related (cloud cover change).  
Its causes is: [1] Suspected data-glitch triggered frequency ramp event earlier in the day; [2] Possible sudden disappearance of cloud cover affecting rooftop PV output around Adelaide.

title: What’s up with Waratah BESS (unplanned outage till 3rd May 2026)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s up with Waratah BESS (unplanned outage till 3rd May 2026)?" posted at 07-11-2025 02:53:00 PM is: On November 7, 2025, AEMO's MT PASA DUID Availability data revealed an unplanned outage for the Waratah BESS (unit WTAHB1) that suddenly extended to May 3, 2026, despite near-term bids and rebids showing the battery currently operational and available. The reason for this lengthy outage is unknown, though the article speculates it could be related to the SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme). The loss of up to 850 MW of battery capacity for about six months could significantly affect grid stability and electricity market prices in the NEM, particularly in New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures (battery storage unit outage). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 07-11-2025 (outage start/extension) to 03-05-2026 (scheduled return). Its root_cause is: Unknown (possibly SIPS-related). Its accident scale is: Loss of up to 850 MW (MaxCap) of battery availability for ~6 months. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-11-2025 (news date) to 03-05-2026 (outage end). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly New South Wales (location of Waratah BESS). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM, especially those relying on battery storage for grid support. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (sudden extension in MT PASA data). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Unknown cause; possible SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme) involvement; no official explanation from Akaysha Energy or AEMO as of the article date].

title: Mainland Frequency wanders south early Friday afternoon 7th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland Frequency wanders south early Friday afternoon 7th November 2025" posted at 07-11-2025 02:03:00 PM is: On Friday 7 November 2025 at 13:56 NEM time, the mainland grid frequency was observed wandering south, indicating a deviation from the nominal 50 Hz. This event signals a brief period of grid instability, likely stemming from an imbalance between supply and demand, though the specific root cause is not detailed in the report.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, short-term frequency deviation event; Its root_cause is: Unknown; Its accident scale is: Minor frequency deviation (no reported blackout or significant disruption); Its dates is: 07-11-2025; Its affected region is: Mainland NEM (National Electricity Market, likely eastern Australia); Its affected key users is: All grid-connected consumers and generators; Its cause type is: Unknown; Its causes is: [Unknown, no specific causal events identified].

title: (First ever?) forecast MSL warnings issued for South Australia for the coming week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "(First ever?) forecast MSL warnings issued for South Australia for the coming week" posted at 07-11-2025 12:37:00 PM is: On 7 November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple Minimum System Load (MSL) warnings for South Australia for 8, 9, 11, and 12 November 2025, forecasting demand to fall below MSL1 and, on 11 November, below MSL2 thresholds. These conditions indicate an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state, highlighting a grid/system instability issue potentially linked to high renewable generation or low consumption. This is believed to be the first time such MSL warnings have been issued for South Australia.

Its relevance level is: Level 1  
Its category is: grid/system instability  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 8-12 November 2025  
Its root_cause is: Forecast low demand threatening system security  
Its accident scale is: Forecast MSL1 and MSL2 conditions  
Its dates (if different) are: 8, 9, 11, 12 November 2025  
Its affected region is: South Australia  
Its affected key users is: AEMO and National Electricity Market participants  
Its cause type is: Forecast low demand  
Its causes is: AEMO published market notices MN130458, MN130461, MN130459, MN130462, and MN130460 warning of MSL1 (and MSL2 on 11 Nov) conditions in SA.

title: A quick look at ‘Dispatch Error’ on Thursday 6th November 2025, in conjunction with the Frequency Spike
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at ‘Dispatch Error’ on Thursday 6th November 2025, in conjunction with the Frequency Spike" posted at 07-11-2025 10:39:00 AM is: On Thursday 6th November 2025, a significant frequency spike peaking at 50.229Hz occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM), caused by a data glitch that added approximately 1,700 MW of 'phantom demand' in Victoria. This erroneous input led the market dispatch engine (NEMDE) to dispatch units to meet the nonexistent demand, causing frequency to exceed the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). The article analyzes 'Dispatch Error' (deviation from target) across fuel types, noting that scheduled units like coal and batteries intentionally reduced output to help rein in frequency, while semi-scheduled units (solar, wind) showed mixed responses. The peak was reached at 12:45:21.2, and frequency returned to normal after the phantom demand was removed in the subsequent dispatch interval. The incident highlights operational risks from data errors and grid instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically on 6th November 2025 during the lunchtime period. Its root_cause is: Data glitch that added phantom demand. Its accident scale is: ~1,700 MW of phantom demand leading to a frequency deviation to 50.229 Hz. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 06-11-2025 (event date), 07-11-2025 (analysis date). Its affected region is: Victoria and the broader National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: AEMO, market participants (generators), and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational/data error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) A data glitch added about 1,700 MW of phantom demand in Victoria; 2) NEMDE dispatched units to meet the erroneous demand; 3) The mismatch caused frequency to spike to 50.229 Hz, exceeding the NOFB.

title: 24 GW in 24 months, a race against time for the NEM
author: David Dixon
classified_content: The summary of the news "24 GW in 24 months, a race against time for the NEM" posted at 06-11-2025 04:15:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenge of building 24 GW of wind capacity within 24 months to meet Australia's 2030 renewable energy target of 82%, given current low construction starts, transmission delays, approval bottlenecks, and high costs. It highlights the rapid growth of utility batteries (short-duration firming) but warns that long-duration dispatchable capacity (coal retirements of 15 GW) and bulk renewable energy (wind) are lagging, leading to a slower transition with the NEM reaching only 60-65% renewables by 2030 and 82% by 2037.; Its relevance level is: 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2025-2037 (ongoing impact from construction delays to 2030 and beyond). Its root_cause is: Inability to start construction of 24 GW of wind in 24 months due to low Capacity Investment Scheme bids, unavailable transmission, and lengthy approval processes. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-11-2025 (article date; presentation given 19-10-2025). Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), with New South Wales most exposed to coal retirements. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers (homes and businesses), investors, and project developers. Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy and market design issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Low CIS bid floors causing bank reluctance; transmission infrastructure not yet available; project approval delays; high capex and transmission cost increases.

title: A quick look at some of the market dynamics around the NEM during this afternoon’s frequency episode
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at some of the market dynamics around the NEM during this afternoon’s frequency episode" posted at 06-11-2025 03:45:00 PM is: Earlier today, a mainland frequency spike occurred in the NEM just after 12:20pm, attributed to an AEMO-reported demand rise in Victoria that may have been a data glitch, leading to an over-frequency event. This prompted additional generation dispatch from NSW and Queensland, with interconnector flows shifting southward and solar farms in QLD, NSW, and VIC briefly spiking output, though no significant spot price changes were observed. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, within the 12:15 to 13:15 dispatch intervals on 06-11-2025. Its root_cause is: A reported demand spike in Victoria (likely an AEMO data glitch) that caused the dispatch engine to allocate more generation than needed, resulting in an over-frequency condition. Its accident scale is: Unknown (brief frequency event, no major outages reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-11-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly Victoria, with impacts on NSW, Queensland, and South Australia. Its affected key users is: Solar farms in QLD, NSW, and VIC; interconnectors VNI and QNI; and AEMO's dispatch system. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (data glitch or erroneous demand forecast). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO reported a steep rise in Victorian demand around 12:20pm; this was likely a data glitch; the dispatch engine responded by increasing generation from NSW and Queensland via interconnectors; resulting in an over-frequency event that altered solar farm outputs and constraint binding.

title: Specifics of the frequency excursion (well outside the NOFB) on Thursday 6th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Specifics of the frequency excursion (well outside the NOFB) on Thursday 6th November 2025" posted at 06-11-2025 02:57:00 PM is: A major frequency excursion occurred in the NEM mainland on 6 November 2025, with the frequency peaking at 50.229 Hz and remaining outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band from 12:34:31.9 to 12:47:04.3. The event is suspected to be caused by a data glitch, and further analysis will be needed to confirm the availability of Contingency Lower FCAS during the period. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, short duration (approximately 20 minutes). Its root_cause is: suspected data glitch (hypothesized). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 06-11-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (mainland, particularly Victoria). Its affected key users is: Unknown (all grid-connected participants). Its cause type is: suspected data glitch / system anomaly. Its causes is: frequency spike above NOFB, potential data error or system failure.

title: ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia drops by 216MW (or 21%) on Thursday afternoon 6th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ in South Australia drops by 216MW (or 21%) on Thursday afternoon 6th November 2025" posted at 06-11-2025 02:29:00 PM is: On November 6, 2025, a 216 MW (21%) drop in market demand was recorded in South Australia at 14:11 NEM time, triggering an alert. The drop was confirmed as real since no frequency aberration was observed, contrasting with phantom demand changes in Victoria. The cause remains under investigation, with no immediate explanation provided. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (afternoon of 6 November 2025). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: 216 MW (21% of South Australia demand). Its dates is: 06-11-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: Was that a data glitch that saw lunchtime ‘Market Demand’ ramp in Victoria by 1,812MW?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Was that a data glitch that saw lunchtime ‘Market Demand’ ramp in Victoria by 1,812MW?" posted at 06-11-2025 01:39:00 PM is: A data glitch caused NEMDE to incorrectly register a steep increase in Victoria's Market Demand, ramping it by 1,812 MW over 20 minutes (from 2,734 MW at 12:25 to 4,546 MW at 12:45) and then dropping back sharply at 12:50. This phantom demand led to higher generation targets, spiking system frequency until corrective action was taken. No market notices were issued by AEMO.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes, on 06-11-2025).  
Its root_cause is: A data glitch in NEMDE that falsely indicated a large demand increase.  
Its accident scale is: Medium (significant 1,812 MW ramp but isolated to data error, no physical damage).  
Its dates is: 06-11-2025.  
Its affected region is: Victoria (NEM region).  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely AEMO and scheduled/semi-scheduled generators).  
Its cause type is: Technical/data glitch.  
Its causes is: A data glitch causing NEMDE to misinterpret grid demand; subsequent over-targeting of generation units; resulting frequency overshoot; corrective removal of phantom demand.

title: Major spike in mainland frequency, on Thursday 6th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Major spike in mainland frequency, on Thursday 6th November 2025" posted at 06-11-2025 01:08:00 PM is: A significant frequency spike occurred on the mainland grid on 6 November 2025, pushing the reading well outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band, as captured by live monitoring over the past hour; further details are expected in subsequent reports. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate grid stability concerns, potential market disruptions during the spike). Its affected key users is: All grid-connected consumers and generators. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Unspecified].

author: Paul McArdle

Its affected key users is: Industry participants (generators, retailers, network operators). Its cause type is: Regulatory review/audit. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO conducted a review of thermal generation fleet flexibility and capability to assist industry participants in understanding its evolving role.

title: The implications of the Solar Sharer Offer (SSO) and Default Market Offer (DMO) review outcome on energy sellers
author: Connor James
classified_content: The summary of the news "The implications of the Solar Sharer Offer (SSO) and Default Market Offer (DMO) review outcome on energy sellers" posted at 05-11-2025 04:15:00 PM is: The Australian government announced the Solar Sharer Offer (SSO) as part of a broader Default Market Offer (DMO) review, introducing a regulated ‘free electricity’ period during solar export hours for residential customers with smart meters in South East Queensland, New South Wales, and South Australia starting July 2026. The changes aim to smooth the duck curve, shift demand, and improve consumer protections, but will pressure retailers to adjust market offers, potentially shifting costs to daily supply charges or higher peak rates. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1 July 2026 onward, with further consultation on national expansion by 2027. Its root_cause is: A policy review by the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water to improve consumer protections for standing-offer customers and address grid stability issues caused by high rooftop solar penetration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-11-2025 04:15:00 PM. Its affected region is: South East Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia (with potential national expansion from 2027). Its affected key users is: Residential customers with smart meters on standing offers, and energy retailers in those jurisdictions. Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) DMO review recommendations published 4 November 2025; 2) Introduction of Solar Sharer Offer requiring retailers to offer free power during solar window; 3) Extension of DMO protections to embedded networks; 4) New DMO objective focused on fair and essential service pricing.

title: Dispatch, trading and bidding: Is that what a control room does?
author: Ellise Janetzki
classified_content: The summary of the news "Dispatch, trading and bidding: Is that what a control room does?" posted at 05-11-2025 11:30:00 AM is: The article discusses the dual roles of a control room in the National Electricity Market (NEM): financial operations (bidding, trading, dispatch) and power system security (voltage, frequency, outage management). It highlights that AEMO and the AER mandate human oversight, not just automated bidding systems, and that participants must have competent operators available 24/7 to respond to AEMO instructions within 15 minutes. The piece warns against relying solely on software and stresses that control rooms serve as an insurance policy for maintaining grid reliability; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (general operational requirement). Its root_cause is: Misconception that auto-bidding systems replace human operators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 05-11-2025. Its affected region is: Australian NEM and WEM. Its affected key users is: Power station owners, operators, and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO registration requirements for human operators, AER compliance bulletins emphasizing human oversight, Need to manage both financial and physical plant operations.

title: Large under-performance across Solar Farms as the main driver of frequency wobbles on Thursday afternoon 30th October 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large under-performance across Solar Farms as the main driver of frequency wobbles on Thursday afternoon 30th October 2025?" posted at 03-11-2025 09:55:00 AM is: On Thursday 30 October 2025 at approximately 17:00, a significant frequency drop to 49.84 Hz occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM), driven primarily by a large collective under-performance of solar farms (aggregate Dispatch Error of +613 MW) compounded by wind farm under-performance (+270 MW), totaling +883 MW from semi-scheduled units. BESS units (-480 MW) and coal units (-242 MW) partially offset the deviation, but the event highlighted issues with renewable forecastability and the scalability of the semi-scheduled category, with particular large deviations at Western Downs solar farm (+153 MW), Wellington North solar farm (+97 MW), and Clark Creek 1 wind farm (+287 MW), possibly storm-related. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the 17:00 dispatch interval on 30 October 2025). Its root_cause is: Large under-performance of solar and wind farms due to variability and forecasting errors, exacerbated by storm season. Its accident scale is: Aggregate dispatch error of +883 MW from semi-scheduled units; frequency drop to 49.84 Hz (just outside normal operating frequency band). Its dates is: 30-10-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators, market participants, and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting issue (variability of renewable generation). Its causes is: (1) Large collective under-performance across solar farms NEM-wide (+613 MW); (2) Notable collective under-performance across wind farms (+270 MW); (3) Specific large deviations at WDGPH1 (+153 MW), WELNSF1 (+97 MW), and CLRKCWF1 (+287 MW); (4) Use of persistence forecasts by solar farms contributing to under-performance; (5) Potential storm-related weather impacts.

title: The return to service of Callide B2 and C4 in October 2025, following storm issues
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The return to service of Callide B2 and C4 in October 2025, following storm issues" posted at 02-11-2025 is: CS Energy announced the return to service of Callide B2 and C4 coal units in late October 2025 after storm-related outages. Callide B2 tripped on 28 October due to a mill trip, causing dispatch errors of +211 MW and +175 MW and frequency drops; it returned early on 29 October but tripped again on 31 October. Callide C4 returned on 29 October following an earlier trip on 26 October that caused a sharp mainland frequency drop. These events occurred during storm season in south-central Queensland, impacting grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: October 26-31, 2025 (immediate frequency drops and dispatch errors). Its root_cause is: Storm season in south-central Queensland causing unit trips and mill issues. Its accident scale is: Two coal unit outages, one with multiple trips. Its dates is: October 26-31, 2025. Its affected region is: South-central Queensland, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the NEM, particularly in Queensland. Its cause type is: Natural (storm) and technical (mill trip). Its causes is: Storm impacts; Mill trip at Callide B2 on 28 Oct; Subsequent trip of Callide B2 on 31 Oct; Trip of Callide C4 on 26 Oct.

title: A quick look at Dispatch Error on Friday afternoon 31st October 2025, with the frequency drop
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at Dispatch Error on Friday afternoon 31st October 2025, with the frequency drop" posted at 01-11-2025 12:35:00 PM is: On 31 October 2025, a frequency drop to 49.841 Hz occurred in the NEM due to a significant dispatch error, primarily from semi-scheduled VRE units underperforming. At the 13:55 dispatch interval, large solar farms had an aggregate positive dispatch error of +755 MW and wind +130 MW, dragging frequency down, while coal and BESS units compensated with negative dispatch errors. This event highlights ongoing issues with VRE under-performance and triggered Contingency Raise FCAS. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the 13:55 dispatch interval on 31 October 2025. Its root_cause is: Under-performance of semi-scheduled VRE units (large solar and wind) causing positive aggregate dispatch error, leading to frequency drop below the Normal Operating Frequency Band. Its accident scale is: Frequency dropped to 49.841 Hz, outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band, triggering Contingency Raise FCAS. Its dates is: 31 October 2025 (event date) while article date is 01-11-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/performance issue (dispatch error due to VRE under-performance). Its causes is: Large solar farms aggregate dispatch error +755 MW; Wind aggregate dispatch error +130 MW; Collective under-performance of semi-scheduled units.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A second look at the frequency drop on Friday afternoon 31st October 2025" posted at 01-11-2025 11:51:00 AM is: This article provides a detailed technical analysis of a frequency drop event that occurred on 31 October 2025 in the Australian National Electricity Market. The frequency declined from around 13:46, reaching a low of 49.841 Hz at 13:55, which is outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band, and then recovered by approximately 14:10, spanning five 5-minute dispatch intervals. The analysis notes that one frequency monitor was not recording due to previous storm aftermath and power outage in Brisbane, while another monitor exhibited power quality related noise issues. The root cause of the frequency drop itself is not explicitly identified in the article, but the event highlights grid instability and monitoring challenges. Its root_cause is: Unknown; the article does not specify a direct cause for the frequency drop, but mentions possible power quality issues and storm-related monitoring problems. Its accident scale is: Frequency drop to 49.841 Hz, lasting 5 dispatch intervals (approximately 15 minutes). Its dates is: 31-10-2025. Its affected region is: Brisbane (due to monitor issues from storms) and broader NEM regions. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely grid operators and consumers). Its cause type is: Grid instability / frequency event. Its causes is: 1) Frequency decline from 13:46 to 13:55 with recovery by 14:10; 2) One frequency monitor not recording due to storm aftermath and power outage in Brisbane; 3) Another monitor experiencing power quality noise issues.

title: Comparing AggROT for (44x) Coal Units with AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units through the history of the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Comparing AggROT for (44x) Coal Units with AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units through the history of the NEM" posted at 29-10-2025 09:06:00 AM is: This article analyzes the Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) metric over more than 20 years for 44 coal units and ~10 years for the growing fleet of semi-scheduled units in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights a stark divergence: while coal units' collective under-performance remains consistent, semi-scheduled units have experienced a rapid escalation in large collective under-performance (AggROT > +500MW) beginning in 2021, accelerating in 2022, pausing in 2023-2024, and resuming sharply in 2025 (through Q3). Specific incidents in 2025 (e.g., 31 Jan, 15 Feb, 31 Mar, 19 Apr, 19 Aug) show semi-scheduled under-performance often exceeding 800MW, causing frequency disturbances that coal units then attempt to correct via FCAS and PFR. The article argues that the semi-scheduled category's looser dispatch control and increasing penetration of variable renewable energy (VRE) create scalability and sustainability concerns, as diversity does not compensate for simultaneous under-performance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing, with trend from 2021 to 2025 Q3, and specific incidents in 2025. Its root_cause is: The semi-scheduled category’s much looser dispatch control compared to fully scheduled units, combined with resource intermittency and the inability of spatial diversity to offset simultaneous under-performance, leading to large collective off-target deviations that strain frequency control. Its accident scale is: Multiple large incidents; the largest observed is AggROT of +1,678MW over-performance (19 Aug 2025) and under-performance up to +944MW; total of 6 dispatch intervals >500MW under-performance on 15 Feb 2025, and numerous others in 2025. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 31 Jan 2025, 9 Feb 2025, 15 Feb 2025, 31 Mar 2025, 19 Apr 2025, 19 Aug 2025, 14 Sep 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users are: AEMO, coal-fired power stations, semi-scheduled wind and solar farms, and market participants relying on system frequency stability. Its cause type is: Operational and regulatory (design of semi-scheduled category). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Looser dispatch obligations for semi-scheduled units versus fully scheduled units; 2) Increasing penetration of variable renewable energy (wind and solar) with intermittent output; 3) Simultaneous under-performance of multiple semi-scheduled units without counterbalancing over-performance from other semi-scheduled units; 4) Inadequate forecasting and self-forecast errors (noted for 19 Aug 2025); 5) Nelson Review panel equating semi-scheduled and fully scheduled categories despite critical differences in predictability.
