title: Talk about two-shifting … such as with BW02 on Saturday 4th and Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Talk about two-shifting … such as with BW02 on Saturday 4th and Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 05-10-2025 02:41:00 PM is: The article discusses AGL's two‑shifting of Bayswater Unit 2 (BW02) on 4–5 October 2025, a strategy where the boiler remains fired but the turbine is briefly offline to avoid low or negative prices and create midday headroom for renewables. It also notes a coincident AEMO direction for system strength in NSW on 5 October, which was cancelled shortly after BW02 went offline, and cautions observers against misidentifying similar events as two‑shifting without verifying rebid reasons.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2.  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short‑term (hours on 4–5 October 2025, with previous occurrences in spring and autumn 2025).  
Its root_cause is: AGL’s operational decision to two‑shift BW02 as a cost‑effective alternative to running through very low or negative wholesale prices, while also supporting renewable integration.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 4–5 October 2025 (and earlier instances on 27 September 2025, 17/22/26 March/April 2025, etc.).  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (Bayswater power station).  
Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO).  
Its cause type is: Operational strategy (intentional two‑shifting).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- AGL successfully trialled two‑shifting on Bayswater Unit 4 (7 September 2024).  
- Repeat two‑shifting on BW02 occurred in autumn 2025 (17, 22 March; 26 April) and spring 2025 (27 September; 4–5 October).  
- On 5 October 2025, an AEMO direction for system strength was issued at 07:25 and cancelled by 08:30, coinciding with BW02 going offline at 08:11.

title: AEMO issues (short-notice, short-lived) direction for System Strength in NSW on Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO issues (short-notice, short-lived) direction for System Strength in NSW on Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 05-10-2025 08:52:00 AM is: On 5 October 2025, AEMO issued a series of market notices for a short-notice intervention in the NSW region due to a system strength deficiency, directing Snowy Hydro Limited’s MURR2-2 MURRAY2 unit to synchronise and operate as a synchronous condenser from 07:30 to 08:30 hrs. The direction was issued at 07:20, declared an intervention event with no intervention pricing, and was cancelled shortly after at 08:30, resolving the system strength issue without escalation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Very short-term, from the 07:25 dispatch interval to 08:30 hrs on 05-10-2025; Its root_cause is: Insufficient system strength in the NSW region due to a lack of adequate market response to maintain secure power system operation; Its accident scale is: Minor (short-lived directional intervention, no damage or extended outage); Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-10-2025; Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW); Its affected key users is: AEMO (operator), Snowy Hydro Limited (directed participant), and NEM market participants; Its cause type is: Operational/Grid management issue; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO identified a foreseeable system strength deficiency in NSW requiring intervention; no sufficient market response was received by the 07:20 deadline; AEMO issued a direction to a synchronous condenser to provide system strength; the system returned to a secure state within one hour.

title: AEMO updated MT PASA guidance, on 3rd October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO updated MT PASA guidance, on 3rd October 2025" posted at 04-10-2025 08:29:00 AM is: AEMO’s Market Notice 129413, issued on 3 October 2025, updates the Medium-Term Projected Assessment of System Adequacy (MT PASA) for the coming two years. It reports that previous Low Reserve Conditions for South Australia (December 2026 – March 2027) have been resolved due to the extension of operations at Torrens B station, but new concerns have emerged for New South Wales: annual unserved energy exceeding the Reliability Standard is projected between October 2026 and October 2027, with Low Reserve Conditions specifically declared for August 2027 to October 2027. The outlook for NSW remains uncertain because a formal decision on further prolonging Eraring’s operations has not been made, and transmission augmentations such as the Waratah Super Battery Project and Project EnergyConnect Stage 2 are not yet fully commissioned. AEMO notes that upcoming transmission rating increases for lines between Yass and Sydney West may reduce risks, and the market is expected to respond to these signals. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: August 2027 to October 2027 (NSW) and previously December 2026 to March 2027 (SA, now resolved). Its root_cause is: Possible closure or delayed extension of Eraring coal-fired plant, combined with transmission constraints that are being addressed by the Waratah BESS and Project EnergyConnect. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no actual accident; projected unserved energy exceeding Reliability Standard). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Notice date 03-10-2025, article date 04-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (primary), with a resolved risk for South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW, especially during August–October 2027. Its cause type is: Generation adequacy and transmission limitations. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Eraring closure decision pending; 2) Transmission augmentations (Waratah BESS, PEC Stage 2) not yet commissioned; 3) Forecast demand exceeding supply under some circumstances in NSW.

title: Belatedly recording the QLD LNP’s comments about ‘coal for decades’ in September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly recording the QLD LNP’s comments about ‘coal for decades’ in September 2025" posted at 03-10-2025 09:26:00 AM is: The article belatedly records comments made by Queensland LNP leaders in September 2025, including Premier Crisafulli and Treasurer David Janetzki, at the Energy Nation 2025 conference and earlier events, reiterating their commitment to coal-fired power generation for "decades", which contrasts with federal Labor's emissions reduction targets and signals a prolonged reliance on coal in Queensland. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (several decades), anchored by policy statements in September 2025. Its root_cause is: Political commitment by the Queensland LNP to maintain coal-fired power generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 24-09-2025 (conference date) and 23-09-2025 (CEDA address), with article posted on 03-10-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Power generators, energy consumers, emissions policy stakeholders. Its cause type is: Policy direction. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) LNP's stated intention to keep coal plants operating for decades; (2) Public remarks at the CEDA State of the State address on 23 Sept 2025; (3) Treasurer Janetzki's speech at Energy Nation 2025 on 24 Sept 2025; (4) Contrast with federal Labor's more aggressive climate targets.

title: VP5 into the Planned part of it’s lengthy Unplanned-Planned-Unplanned outage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "VP5 into the Planned part of it’s lengthy Unplanned-Planned-Unplanned outage" posted at 03-10-2025 08:26:00 AM is: This article updates the ongoing outage at Vales Point Power Station Unit 5 (VP5), which initially experienced an unplanned shutdown on 27 August 2025 due to turbine vibrations. The outage was later extended from an originally planned return on 9 November 2025 to 15 December 2025, and as of 3 October 2025 the planned maintenance phase has begun, though no further details on the turbine issue have been released. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 27 August 2025 to 15 December 2025 (ongoing during the news date). Its root_cause is: turbine vibrations leading to an unplanned shutdown. Its accident scale is: single unit (VP5) at a coal-fired power station. Its dates are: 27 August 2025 (initial unplanned outage) to 15 December 2025 (current extended return date). Its affected region is: Vales Point, New South Wales, Australia (Delta Electricity). Its affected key users are: electricity market operators, grid stability managers, and consumers reliant on the unit's generation capacity. Its cause type is: technical failure (mechanical). Its causes are: turbine vibrations that forced an unplanned offline event, followed by a scheduled planned outage that was subsequently extended.

title: NEM Mainland Frequency wanders south as the sun sets, on Thursday 2nd October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM Mainland Frequency wanders south as the sun sets, on Thursday 2nd October 2025" posted at 02-10-2025 06:29:00 PM is: A brief observation of the NEM Mainland grid frequency drifting downward during sunset on 2 October 2025, captured via live readings in Brisbane, indicating a likely imbalance between declining solar generation and load as evening demand rises. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (within minutes to hours of the sunset event on 2 October 2025). Its root_cause is: decline in solar photovoltaic generation at sunset causing a supply-demand imbalance that pulls frequency below the nominal 50 Hz. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific magnitude or duration reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-10-2025. Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (eastern and southern Australia, excluding Tasmania). Its affected key users is: all electricity consumers, generators, and grid operators on the NEM mainland. Its cause type is: operational/technical. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [solar generation ramp-down at sunset, insufficient fast frequency response or inertia to counter the ramp, possible delayed or inadequate market dispatch adjustments].

title: (3 units at) Torrens B station to remain open until mid-2028 after funding arrangement reached
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(3 units at) Torrens B station to remain open until mid-2028 after funding arrangement reached" posted at 02-10-2025 02:48:00 PM is: AGL has agreed to keep the Torrens B gas station in South Australia operational until mid-2028 following a funding arrangement with the state government, prompted by transmission delays that created a reliability gap. The extension, which adds capacity from October 2025 to June 2028, resolves the previously forecast reliability shortfall for South Australia in 2026-27, as confirmed by AEMO's updated Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO), eliminating the need for a Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) in that period. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: October 2025 to June 2028. Its root_cause is: Transmission delays necessitated the retention of the Torrens B gas plant to ensure grid reliability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-10-2025 (announcement date). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: South Australian electricity consumers, AGL, and AEMO. Its cause type is: Infrastructure delay. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Transmission delays created a reliability gap for South Australia; 2) The South Australian government requested AGL to keep Torrens Island open; 3) A funding arrangement was negotiated between the government and AGL.

title: Eraring Unit 3 comes offline early Thursday 2nd October 2025 – on the (delayed) short-notice maintenance outage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Unit 3 comes offline early Thursday 2nd October 2025 – on the (delayed) short-notice maintenance outage" posted at 02-10-2025 08:38:00 AM is: Eraring Unit 3 (ER03) in NSW experienced a delayed short-notice maintenance outage, coming offline on Thursday 2 October 2025 due to issues with one of its ID fans. The outage was originally scheduled for 25 September but postponed multiple times, with the unit now planned to return to service by 13 October 2025, though ST PASA indicates it may be offline at least until 9 October. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 2 October 2025 to at least 13 October 2025 (planned return to service). Its root_cause is: Issues with one of the unit’s ID fans. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO and wholesale electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes is: ID fan issues leading to short-notice maintenance outage.

title: The Nelson Review panel ‘has identified’ the Schism
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-10 considering the draft Report of the Nelson Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Nelson Review panel ‘has identified’ the Schism" posted at 01-10-2025 07:04:00 PM is: The article discusses the Nelson Review draft report’s identification of 'the schism' in Australia's National Electricity Market – a gap where historic policy interventions (like the Renewable Energy Target) promoted renewable bulk energy but neglected essential services such as shaping, firming, system stability, and hedging. It critiques the Capacity Investment Scheme for focusing primarily on 'shaping' while excluding gas-fired generation and ignoring firming, and examines the proposed Electricity Service Entry Mechanism as a potential but still incomplete solution. The article also emphasizes the need for developing organizational capability alongside physical capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (future policy implementation). Its root_cause is: Incomplete policy design that prioritized bulk renewable energy over essential system services. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 01-10-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants including generators, retailers, and consumers. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory. Its causes is: 1. Historic policy interventions (e.g., Renewable Energy Target) focused only on bulk energy; 2. Exclusion of gas-fired generation from the Capacity Investment Scheme; 3. CIS emphasis on shaping over firming; 4. Neglect of organizational capability development in new entrants.

title: ABC reports ‘Rio Tinto’s Gladstone coal-fired power station could be retired early’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ABC reports ‘Rio Tinto’s Gladstone coal-fired power station could be retired early’" posted at 01-10-2025 12:04:00 PM is: The article reports that Rio Tinto’s coal-fired power station in Gladstone, Queensland, may be retired earlier than planned, contradicting recent statements by the Queensland LNP government about the future of coal power. This potential early retirement could reduce baseload generation capacity and affect electricity market prices in the region.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: coal-fired policies.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Uncertain – if retirement proceeds, impacts would likely occur over a medium to long-term horizon (years).; Its root_cause is: Economic and/or policy pressures for decarbonization, potentially combined with aging plant economics.; Its accident scale is: Unknown.; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-10-2025.; Its affected region is: Gladstone, Queensland, Australia.; Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in Queensland, particularly industrial users and Rio Tinto’s own operations.; Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory or economic decision.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Changing energy market conditions, potential emissions reduction targets, possible plant age and maintenance costs.

title: Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 3: Treatment of curtailment risk
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 3: Treatment of curtailment risk" posted at 01-10-2025 11:00:00 AM is: This article examines whether the Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) covers network curtailment risk, detailing a debate that concluded with the government clarifying that no executed CIS Agreement (CISA) underwrites curtailment, despite an earlier draft including such a provision. The change occurred during Tender #1 after Stage A submissions, raising questions about bidder awareness and impact on revenue floor bids. The piece also discusses the challenges of forecasting curtailment and the overlap between network and economic curtailment, emphasizing that curtailment patterns are uncertain and evolving. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From August 27, 2024 onward, ongoing as it affects project viability and bidding in subsequent CIS tenders. Its root_cause is: The government’s decision to remove the curtailment provision from the final CISA to avoid open-ended risk, as clarified by the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (DCCEEW). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: August 27, 2024 (publication of final CISA pro-forma). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Developers and bidders in CIS tenders, particularly those involved in Tender #1. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Original draft CISA for Tender #1 included a clause that deemed generation during force majeure (including network curtailment) as part of the notional quantity for revenue top-ups; 2) Following consultation, DCCEEW revised the clause in the final CISA, excluding PFME Generation from the notional quantity calculation; 3) The final CISA pro-forma was published on August 27, 2024, after Stage A applications but before Stage B bidding; 4) DCCEEW later explicitly stated that no executed CISA provides revenue underwriting for curtailment.

title: Finally!! … A Major Review that sees (more closely) what’s actually been happening with Demand Response!
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-10 considering the draft Report of the Nelson Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "Finally!! … A Major Review that sees (more closely) what’s actually been happening with Demand Response!" posted at 30-09-2025 07:22:00 PM is: This article discusses the draft Report of the Nelson Review, which, through extensive engagement with commercial and industrial users, retailers, and aggregators, acknowledges that a significant amount of demand response is already occurring in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The author expresses surprise and approval that this long-standing reality—previously obscured by misleading statements from some stakeholders and uncritical media coverage—is now formally recognized. The article categorizes various forms of demand response (scheduled load, spot exposure, negawatts, out-of-market response, and IPRR dispatch mode) and notes the Review’s recommendation to require broader price-responsive resources to be visible or dispatchable, while cautioning against potential unintended consequences; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (the recognition formalizes existing behavior, with no immediate new market shock). Its root_cause is: Misleading narratives about demand response levels were corrected by the Nelson Review Panel’s open-minded engagement with actual market participants. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 30-09-2025. Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Commercial and industrial energy users, retailers, aggregators, and residential customers (via retailers like Amber Electric). Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory recognition. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Stakeholder self-interest led to propagation of misleading statements about demand response. 2) Commentators and journalists uncritically repeated those statements. 3) The Nelson Review Panel conducted extensive engagement, leading to an accurate assessment.

title: 3 high-level questions, for considering the draft report of the Nelson Review
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-10 considering the draft Report of the Nelson Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "3 high-level questions, for considering the draft report of the Nelson Review" posted at 30-09-2025 03:54:00 PM is: The article reflects on the Draft Report of the Nelson Review, released on 6 August 2025, ahead of the NEMdev conference in Brisbane on 8 October 2025. The author poses three high-level questions: why the review was needed (implying a failure in standard governance), which of four quadrants of scope (from limited to broad) the review addresses, and which of three time horizons (past, present, future) it focuses on. The aim is to stimulate discussion at the conference about the draft report's implications for the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 3 – Less Relevant. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term policy development; immediate market impact not expected. Its root_cause is: Perceived inadequacy in existing market governance and operations framework (AEMC, AEMO, AER) that prompted a major review. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (policy review, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 30-09-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Policymakers, market participants, regulators, and conference attendees. Its cause type is: Policy review. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Release of the Nelson Review Draft Report on 6 August 2025; closure of public submissions on 17 September 2025; upcoming NEMdev conference on 8 October 2025.

title: Some of the ‘Major Reviews’ in the history of the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some of the ‘Major Reviews’ in the history of the NEM" posted at 30-09-2025 03:18:00 PM is: The article compiles a chronological list of major reviews that have shaped Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), from the 1997 APEx study identifying six strategic challenges to the Nelson Review commissioned in November 2024, with notable milestones including the Parer Review (2002), ERIG Review (2007), Finkel Review (2017) triggered by the SA System Black Event, and the ESB's NEM 2.0 process (2020-2022). It underscores that many fundamental issues remain unresolved decades later. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (1997 to 2025). Its root_cause is: Recurring structural and operational challenges in electricity market design, including responses to system black events and the need for post-2025 reforms. Its accident scale is: Unknown (though the Finkel Review was prompted by the SA System Black Event in 2016). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Multiple dates: 1997, Dec 2002, Jan 2007, Jun 2017, 2020-2022, Nov 2024 (commissioned). Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and regulatory review. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Identification of enduring strategic issues in electricity markets, the SA System Black Event of September 2016, and the need for market design reforms beyond 2025.

title: AEMO forecasts MSL1 for the coming Saturday 4th October 2025 and Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts MSL1 for the coming Saturday 4th October 2025 and Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 29-09-2025 03:16:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued two market notices on 29 September 2025, forecasting Minimum System Load (MSL1) conditions in the Victorian region for the upcoming weekend of 4–5 October 2025, due to an elevated risk of insufficient demand—with demand expected to fall below the MSL1 threshold of 1675 MW on Saturday (minimum 1547 MW) and 1535 MW on Sunday (minimum 1399 MW)—prompting concerns about maintaining a secure operating state; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 4–5 October 2025 (Saturday and Sunday); Its root_cause is: Insufficient regional demand to maintain a secure operating state; Its accident scale is: MSL1 (forecast demand below advisory thresholds, with specific minimums of 1547 MW on 4 Oct and 1399 MW on 5 Oct); Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 04-10-2025 and 05-10-2025; Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC), Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown (not specified in the news); Its cause type is: Forecast low demand; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Low forecast demand due to likely reduced electricity consumption over the weekend (e.g., spring public holiday or seasonal patterns).

title: A quick review of QLD solar (and curtailment) on Monday 29th September 2025 with price at MPF
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick review of QLD solar (and curtailment) on Monday 29th September 2025 with price at MPF" posted at 29-09-2025 01:08:00 PM is: On Monday 29 September 2025, Queensland's solar generation experienced significant curtailment and a price drop to the Market Price Floor (–$1,000/MWh) during the 10:05 and 10:10 dispatch intervals. This occurred due to an outage on the Larcom Creek to Calliope River 275kV line, which triggered a hard ramping constraint (R033913_002_RAMP_F) that previously constrained down many solar farms. When the constraint unbound at 10:05, solar output surged by 487 MW to 1,567 MW, driving prices to the floor. The article reviews aggregate solar output and curtailment over preceding days (26–28 September) and highlights the role of transmission constraints in causing price volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (10:05 and 10:10 dispatch intervals on 29 September 2025). Its root_cause is: outage on the Larcom Creek to Calliope River 275kV line leading to a hard ramping constraint. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 29-09-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: solar farms (including Clermont, Emerald, Columboola, Aldoga, Collinsville, Daydream, Hamilton, Hayman, and others). Its cause type is: transmission line outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Outage on the Larcom Creek to Calliope River 275kV line; (2) Activation of hard ramping constraint equation #R033913_002_RAMP_F; (3) Unbinding of the constraint at 10:05 dispatch interval; (4) Resultant increase in solar output by 487 MW, driving prices to the Market Price Floor.

title: Start date of short notice outage at ER03 delayed again, now Friday 3rd October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Start date of short notice outage at ER03 delayed again, now Friday 3rd October 2025" posted at 26-09-2025 01:36:00 PM is: Origin Energy has again postponed the start of an unplanned outage for the ER03 unit (likely Eraring Unit 3 in NSW), now forecast to begin on 3 October 2025, due to an issue with one of the two induced draft fans, causing the unit to operate at half load and leading AEMO to cancel a planned system strength direction. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 25 September 2025 (when the half-load operation was noted) through the outage period starting 3 October 2025 and continuing until the unit returns to full service. Its root_cause is: failure of one of the two induced draft fans on the unit. Its accident scale is: one unit (ER03) operating at half load and scheduled for a forced outage. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-09-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) and Origin Energy. Its cause type is: equipment failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) an issue with one of the two induced draft fans; (2) forced half-load operation to maintain boiler negative pressure; (3) subsequent need for a full outage to repair the fan.

title: Yet another data point raising questions about whether Eraring will be able to close in 2027, as per current plan
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yet another data point raising questions about whether Eraring will be able to close in 2027, as per current plan" posted at 26-09-2025 10:29:00 AM is: The article highlights that system strength deficiencies, rather than electricity supply, are the primary barrier to Eraring Power Station's planned 2027 closure, citing AEMO directions on 28 August 2025 to Snowy Hydro for synchronous support, a near-miss on 25 September 2025, and an ongoing debate about whether grid-forming inverters can replace synchronous machines; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from August 2025 through at least 2027, with increasing frequency as coal units close; root_cause is: Insufficient system strength (synchronous inertia and fault current) from retired coal units, not yet fully replaceable by batteries or syncons; accident scale is: Multiple grid-directions and a near-system-strength emergency; dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 28-08-2025 (AEMO directions) and 25-09-2025 (near-miss incident); affected region is: New South Wales, Australia; affected key users is: AEMO, Snowy Hydro, Eraring Power Station, NSW electricity consumers; cause type is: Technical/operational – system strength deficiency from synchronous machine loss; causes (a summarized list of cause events): 1) Coal plant outages in late August 2025 reduced synchronous machines. 2) Synchronous condenser rollout in NSW not yet complete. 3) AEMO’s position that batteries cannot yet provide sufficient fault current for system strength. 4) Eraring typically runs 3-4 units; closure would remove that inertia/strength.

title: Tesla Energy whitepaper ‘The Role of Grid-Forming Inverters in Providing Inertia (in the NEM)’ will be of interest
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tesla Energy whitepaper ‘The Role of Grid-Forming Inverters in Providing Inertia (in the NEM)’ will be of interest" posted at 26-09-2025 08:19:00 AM is: This article highlights a Tesla Energy whitepaper arguing that grid-forming inverters (via batteries) can supply inertia to the National Electricity Market (NEM) at much lower cost than traditional spinning machines, prompted by recent developments that make its insights particularly timely for stakeholders. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (the whitepaper itself has no immediate market impact, but its concepts may influence long-term grid planning). Its root_cause is: The need for cost-effective inertia in the NEM as renewable penetration grows and synchronous generators retire. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-09-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market operators, grid operators, and battery storage developers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Not applicable (no causal event). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Not applicable (the article discusses a whitepaper, not a cause event).

title: Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 2: Understanding why there are delivery challenges
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 2: Understanding why there are delivery challenges" posted at 26-09-2025 07:00:00 AM is: The article examines why projects awarded under Australia’s Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) are struggling to reach financial close, highlighting that the scheme has become the dominant route to market but is sterilizing hedgeable capacity and worsening the PPA/contract market. Oversubscription pressures developers to bid aggressively, often at unviable floor prices, while the introduction of a reserve list for future tenders adds further uncertainty. These dynamics create a paradox where the CIS, intended to solve investment challenges, may suppress conditions for a healthier contract market to re-emerge. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing medium- to long-term impact, from scheme inception through future project development and contract market evolution. Its root_cause is: The design of the CIS as a two-way contract for difference that removes projects from the pool of available hedgeable capacity, combined with high oversubscription in tenders that incentivizes aggressive bidding and delays in project finalization. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 26-09-2025 07:00:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Project developers, retailers, corporate offtakers, financiers, and electricity consumers indirectly through market price effects. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory design and market structure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] CIS contracts sterilize hedgeable capacity, reducing availability of PPAs and forward contracts; [2] High oversubscription (only 5-20% of projects awarded) leads to aggressive bidding on floor prices; [3] Lack of alternative bankable routes to market for developers; [4] Delays in finalizing EPC/BOP contracts after award, risking cost escalations; [5] Introduction of a government reserve list creates additional uncertainty about project progress and renegotiation expectations.

title: Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 1: Tracking the progress of CIS projects post-award
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Are CIS-ters doin’ it for themselves? Part 1: Tracking the progress of CIS projects post-award" posted at 25-09-2025 05:30:00 PM is: This article analyzes the progress of projects awarded under Australia's Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS), finding that many remain in the 'proposed' stage even a year after receiving contracts, based on AEMO's Generation Information data. The author highlights difficulties in tracking project advancement and questions whether the scheme's ambitious capacity targets will translate into actual system reliability, given the slow movement toward financial close and construction. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Delays in project development and financial close. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-09-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (NEM and WEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, market participants, generators. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory and project development. Its causes is: Slow progress from 'proposed' to 'committed' status; lack of financial close; inconsistent data tracking.

title: The start of the unplanned outage at ER03 is delayed till early Saturday morning 27th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "The start of the unplanned outage at ER03 is delayed till early Saturday morning 27th September 2025" posted at 25-09-2025 10:23:00 AM is: The article reports that the planned unplanned outage at ER03, initially slated to begin early Thursday 25th September 2025, has been delayed to early Saturday morning 27th September 2025. This change, made prior to the 20:00 data update from AEMO, led to the cancellation of a foreseeable intervention notice (MN129282) for managing system strength in the NSW region and the cancellation of a Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) notice (MN129283) for NSW on 25/09/2025. The delay alleviated immediate concerns about system strength and reserve shortfalls that had prompted AEMO to consider market intervention.

Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The impact was initially expected from 25th September 2025 but is now deferred to 27th September 2025, reducing immediate market stress until that date.  
Its root_cause is: The operator delayed the start of the unplanned outage at ER03.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (unplanned outage of a single generating unit).  
Its dates is: The event (outage start) is now scheduled for 27-09-2025; the news date is 25-09-2025.  
Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region.  
Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, and NSW electricity consumers.  
Its cause type is: Unplanned generator outage (delay of start).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: The operator decided to push back the start of the unplanned outage at ER03 from 25th September to 27th September 2025, which prompted AEMO to cancel its earlier intervention and reserve notices.

title: Unplanned outage at YWPS2 extended – return to service slated for 15th December 2025 (and may well extend further)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-08-26 YWPS2 unplanned outage due to 'LP turbine issues'
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage at YWPS2 extended – return to service slated for 15th December 2025 (and may well extend further)" posted at 24-09-2025 07:19:00 PM is: The unplanned outage at Yallourn Power Station Unit 2 (YWPS2), which began on 26 August 2025 due to low-pressure (LP) turbine issues, has been extended to a return-to-service date of 15 December 2025, approximately 8 weeks later than the previous expectation of 22 October 2025. The extent of the turbine damage is not fully disclosed, but EnergyAustralia is reportedly considering sourcing a replacement turbine from China. The article highlights similarities with other prolonged coal unit outages and raises concerns about further delays and potential impacts on plant maintenance and closure plans; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 26 August 2025 to at least 15 December 2025, with possible further extension. Its root_cause is: Low-pressure (LP) turbine issues. Its accident scale is: Unplanned outage of a major coal-fired unit (estimated capacity ~370 MW) at Yallourn Power Station. Its dates are: Outage start: 26 August 2025; Extension announcement: 23 September 2025; News publication: 24 September 2025; Scheduled return: 15 December 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Latrobe Valley, part of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: AEMO, EnergyAustralia (operator), wholesale market participants, and electricity consumers in Victoria and the NEM. Its cause type is: Technical failure / equipment damage (mechanical). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: ['LP turbine failure', 'Potential need for replacement turbine from China'].

title: Some leading indicators of possible volatility in NSW on Thursday evening 24th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some leading indicators of possible volatility in NSW on Thursday evening 24th September 2025" posted at 24-09-2025 04:53:00 PM is: AEMO has flagged potential early-morning intervention and a forecast LOR1 condition for NSW due to unplanned outages at three coal units (MP2, ER03, VP5 from turbine vibrations) combined with transmission constraints from outages on the Avon–Macarthur (17) and Wagga–Walla Walla (6Y) 330kV lines, which may create congestion and raise wholesale electricity prices above typical bid-stacking levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures and grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 24 September 2025 (post date) with the main impact expected on 25 September 2025 evening. Its root_cause is: A combination of unplanned coal unit outages (MP2, ER03, VP5) and planned transmission line outages (Avon–Macarthur, Wagga–Walla Walla) limiting generation deliverability. Its accident scale is: Multiple units (three coal units) and two 330kV transmission lines. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 24-09-2025 (post date), with event date 25-09-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in NSW, including residential, commercial, and industrial). Its cause type is: Technical failures and maintenance outages. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Unplanned outage at MP2 coal unit (already commenced). 2. Looming unplanned outage at ER03 coal unit (coinciding with direction timing). 3. Extended unplanned outage at VP5 due to turbine vibrations. 4. Outage on Avon–Macarthur (17) 330kV line (constraint set N-AVMA_17). 5. Outage on Wagga–Walla Walla (6Y) 330kV line (constraint set N-WGWA_X5).

title: A quick review of AEMO direction for System Strength in NSW back on Thu 28th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick review of AEMO direction for System Strength in NSW back on Thu 28th August 2025" posted at 24-09-2025 04:31:00 PM is: On 28 August 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a direction to Snowy Hydro Limited's TUMUT3-1 generating unit in NSW to synchronise and operate as a synchronous condenser to provide system strength, as intervention was required to maintain a secure operating state. This direction was issued under clause 4.8.9(a1)(1) of the National Electricity Rules and was expected to remain in effect until further notice. The article also notes that similar directions for system strength in NSW have been rare but may become more frequent, referencing an upcoming possibility on 25 September 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 28 August 2025 until further notice. Its root_cause is: System strength deficiencies in NSW requiring AEMO intervention. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 28-08-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales). Its affected key users is: Snowy Hydro Limited (TUMUT3-1). Its cause type is: Operational intervention. Its causes is: System strength circumstances necessitating direction to maintain power system security.

title: AEMO notes possible early-morning direction in NSW on Thursday 25th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes possible early-morning direction in NSW on Thursday 25th September 2025" posted at 24-09-2025 03:45:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 24 September 2025 warning of a foreseeable intervention in the NSW region from 0130 hrs on 25 September due to system strength concerns, with insufficient market response possibly leading to a direction; changing outage plans for ER03 are identified as a likely cause. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, early morning hours of 25 September 2025 (from 0130 hrs). Its root_cause is: System strength shortfall caused by changing outage plans for ER03. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 25-09-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in NSW). Its cause type is: Operational/outage-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Changing outage plans for ER03 leading to system strength deficiency].

title: ER03 now (again) slated to come offline early Thursday morning 25th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ER03 now (again) slated to come offline early Thursday morning 25th September 2025" posted at 24-09-2025 03:34:00 PM is: This article reports that the ER03 generating unit is scheduled to come offline early on Thursday 25th September 2025, following previous reliability warnings from AEMO about a system strength shortfall in New South Wales. The unit has been experiencing operational challenges over the past two weeks, as indicated by rebid and output profile data, and is expected to return by Tuesday 30th September 2025. AEMO's forecasts have oscillated regarding intervention timing; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures and grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 25th September 2025 (early morning) until 30th September 2025 (expected return), with potential immediate market impacts during the outage. Its root_cause is: Unknown (not explicitly stated, but implied mechanical or operational challenges causing the unit to need to come offline). Its accident scale is: Single generating unit (ER03) offline. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Event date 25-09-2025; news date 24-09-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO (grid operator), market participants, and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Unknown (not specified, but likely mechanical failure or maintenance). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Unit ER03 experienced operational challenges over the past two weeks; rebid reasons were not definitive but output profile showed reduced availability; AEMO oscillated system strength warnings leading to this offline schedule.

title: Yes, MP2 did come offline in the early hours of Wednesday morning 24th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yes, MP2 did come offline in the early hours of Wednesday morning 24th September 2025" posted at 24-09-2025 03:16:00 PM is: Mt Piper Unit 2 (MP2) in New South Wales came offline early on 24th September 2025 after consistently signaling the need for outage since 17th September. The unit underwent forced cooling, indicating a technical issue, and is expected to return on 1st October 2025. AEMO had previously oscillated warnings about system strength shortfalls in NSW due to this outage. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 24th September 2025 to 1st October 2025 (expected return). Its root_cause is: forced outage due to unknown technical issue (force cooling indicated). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 24-09-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: forced outage. title: AEMO oscillates warnings – possible need to intervene in NSW on Thu 25th Sept 2025 for System Strength reasons
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "AEMO oscillates warnings – possible need to intervene in NSW on Thu 25th Sept 2025 for System Strength reasons" posted at 23-09-2025 04:07:00 PM is: On 23 September 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple warnings about a possible intervention in the New South Wales (NSW) region on 25 September 2025 due to forecast low system strength, driven by several coal-fired unit outages (planned and unplanned) that reduced available generation and stability. The warning was initially cancelled later the same day but reinstated on 24 September, with AEMO also declaring a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) for the evening of 25 September. The root cause is a combination of planned outages at Bayswater (BW04) and Eraring (ER04), an unplanned outage at Vales Point (VP5) due to turbine vibration, and additional unplanned outages at Mount Piper (MP2) and Eraring (ER03). The volatile situation underscores grid fragility from multiple generator failures.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability (primary) and turbine/generator failures (contributing).  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Thursday 25 September 2025 (forecast period from 0000 hrs to 2300 hrs, with specific LOR1 from 1730 to 2300 hrs).  
Its root_cause is: Multiple coal-fired unit outages reducing system strength and reserve margins in NSW.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (multiple unit outages totaling significant capacity; at least 4-5 units affected).  
Its dates are: 23-09-2025 (main notice), with updates on 24-09-2025 (reinstatement).  
Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers and market participants in NSW).  
Its cause type is: Combination of planned outages and unplanned generator failures.  
Its causes are: Planned outages of BW04 and ER04; unplanned outage of VP5 due to turbine vibration; unplanned outage of MP2; unplanned outage of ER03 (beginning early 25 September).

title: MT PASA shows forecast Low Reserve Conditions in both NSW (Aug-Sept 2027) and SA (Dec 2026 to March 2027)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "MT PASA shows forecast Low Reserve Conditions in both NSW (Aug-Sept 2027) and SA (Dec 2026 to March 2027)" posted at 23-09-2025 01:54:00 PM is: AEMO's September 2025 MT PASA update identifies Low Reserve Conditions in New South Wales for August-September 2027 and in South Australia for December 2026-March 2027, where forecast demand exceeds supply under some circumstances and annual unserved energy is expected to exceed the reliability standard, putting pressure on the NSW Government and Origin Energy to delay the closure of Eraring Power Station; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 2026 – March 2027 (SA) and August 2027 – September 2027 (NSW), with annual unserved energy exceeding standard from September 2026 to September 2027. Its root_cause is: Forecast demand exceeding supply under some circumstances, likely exacerbated by the planned closure of Eraring Power Station and insufficient new generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown (forecast, not an actual event). Its dates are: 23-09-2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW and SA. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance / Capacity inadequacy. Its causes are: 1) Forecast demand growth exceeding supply under certain conditions; 2) Potential closure of Eraring coal-fired power station; 3) Rolling forward of the MT PASA window revealing tighter supply-demand balance.

title: Mainstream media reports about blades lost at Flyers Creek Wind Farm
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainstream media reports about blades lost at Flyers Creek Wind Farm" posted at 22-09-2025 08:00:00 PM is: A turbine blade at the Flyers Creek Wind Farm in NSW Central West was initially damaged during a storm in June 2025, and on 10 September 2025, a combination of loss of electrical power and strong winds caused further damage, resulting in over a dozen turbines being switched off for safety. This represents a turbine/generator failure event with potential short-term generation loss.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (turbines offline until repair, uncertainty on duration). Its root_cause is: Storm damage (strong winds) and subsequent loss of electrical power leading to uncontrolled blade movement. Its accident scale is: One turbine blade severely damaged (bent in half), leading to over a dozen turbines being switched off as a precaution. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Initial damage: June 2025; Further damage: 10 September 2025; Reported: 22 September 2025. Its affected region is: Flyers Creek Wind Farm, New South Wales Central West, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural (storm) and technical (loss of electrical power). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Storm in late June 2025 caused initial blade damage; 2) On 10 September 2025, a loss of electrical power combined with strong winds caused further, more severe damage to the already weakened blade.

title: Prices for Lower Regulation FCAS elevated across the mainland Sunday afternoon 21st September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prices for Lower Regulation FCAS elevated across the mainland Sunday afternoon 21st September 2025" posted at 21-09-2025 04:32:00 PM is: On Sunday 21 September 2025, elevated prices for Lower Regulation FCAS were observed across the mainland NEM during the 16:10 dispatch interval, driven by a high share of variable renewable energy (VRE) that pushed scheduled units to near minimum load, reducing the pool of available suppliers for the regulation lower service, as highlighted by NEMwatch and ez2view snapshots showing low scheduled generation and limited bid offers from fossil-fueled plant, hydro, and BESS. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (prices elevated at the 16:10 dispatch interval on 21 September 2025). Its root_cause is: Lack of suppliers for Regulation Lower FCAS due to high VRE penetration and scheduled units operating near minimum load, constraining the ability to adjust supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-09-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) – excluding Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO) and consumers exposed to FCAS prices. Its cause type is: Operational/market design (insufficient flexible capacity to provide regulation lower service under high VRE conditions). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] High share of semi-scheduled, non-scheduled, and invisible VRE during daylight hours, [2] Scheduled units (fossil-fueled) running down to minimum load or offline, [3] Reduced supply offers across fossil, hydro, and BESS categories for regulation lower FCAS, [4] Consequent scarcity of providers for the service, driving spot prices up.

title: A brief recap of a large afternoon frequency wobble on the NEM mainland on Saturday 20th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A brief recap of a large afternoon frequency wobble on the NEM mainland on Saturday 20th September 2025" posted at 21-09-2025 12:40:00 PM is: The article details a significant frequency dip on the NEM mainland on the afternoon of Saturday 20th September 2025, with the largest deviations below 50Hz occurring between 16:45 and 16:56. The event was caused by systemic weakness in frequency support from large solar and wind generation (under-performance relative to target), dragging frequency down despite counteracting efforts from coal and battery units. The author notes this pattern is becoming increasingly common and raises concerns about the sustainability and scalability of the Semi-Scheduled generation category. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: afternoon of 20 September 2025 (immediate, within the dispatch intervals 16:50 and 16:55). Its root_cause is: systemic weakness in frequency support provided by late-afternoon large solar and coincident weakness in wind generation, causing collective under-performance relative to target. Its accident scale is: frequency deviation near but not outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) limits; no mention of load shedding or outages. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-09-2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all grid-connected users, but not specified). Its cause type is: operational/technical (generation under-performance). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Large solar generation under-performance (positive dispatch error) in late afternoon; 2) Wind generation under-performance (positive dispatch error) coincident with solar; 3) Combined effect of both technologies dragging system frequency below 50Hz.

title: A clean break: Wivenhoe and Swanbank operating profiles pre and post CleanCo takeover
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A clean break: Wivenhoe and Swanbank operating profiles pre and post CleanCo takeover" posted at 19-09-2025 08:00:00 AM is: This article examines the shifts in operating and bidding profiles of Wivenhoe Pumped Hydro and Swanbank E gas power station across three periods (2016-2018, 2019-2021, 2022-2024) following CleanCo’s takeover in late 2019. Wivenhoe has increased its evening generation and daytime pumping, moving bids to lower price bands, while Swanbank E has transitioned from baseload to a peaking/firming role with more variable output and redistributed bids. These changes reflect CleanCo’s gentailer strategy and renewable energy mandate, altering the assets’ contribution to the Queensland electricity market.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2019-2024 and ongoing. Its root_cause is: CleanCo’s formation and its gentailer strategy with a renewable contracting mandate. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 19-09-2025 08:00:00 AM. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Organizational/strategic change. Its causes is: CleanCo takeover from CS Energy and Stanwell, shift to gentailer model, renewable energy mandate.

title: Oliver Nunn (from Endgame Analytics) illustrates how wind droughts are ‘a thing’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Oliver Nunn (from Endgame Analytics) illustrates how wind droughts are ‘a thing’" posted at 16-09-2025 07:16:00 PM is: Oliver Nunn of Endgame Analytics presented modelled results at the 2025 QCES focusing on wind droughts—prolonged periods of very low aggregate wind farm output in Queensland based on historical wind speed patterns. He used these findings to argue for investing in long-duration energy storage and maintaining gas-fired generation, warning that such sustained low wind conditions could severely challenge grid stability if a large share of coal capacity were also absent. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Sustained periods of weeks (based on modelled historical wind droughts). Its root_cause is: Lack of wind (wind drought). Its accident scale is: Unknown (modelled analysis, not an actual event). Its dates is: 16-09-2025 (same as news date, as the analysis was presented on that date). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, grid operators, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Natural (weather). Its causes is: [Prolonged low wind speeds, wind drought conditions].

title: Some snippets, from the ‘Long Duration Energy Storage’ session on day 2 of CEC QCES 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some snippets, from the ‘Long Duration Energy Storage’ session on day 2 of CEC QCES 2025" posted at 16-09-2025 06:29:00 PM is: The article summarizes highlights from the Long Duration Energy Storage (LDES) session at the CEC QCES 2025 conference, including a quip comparing short-duration batteries to an espresso fix and LDES to an intravenous drip, updates on the Kidston Pumped Hydro project (energisation expected later in 2025), discussions on why LDES is seen as the 'unsexy' part of the energy transition, operational profile changes for Swanbank E and Wivenhoe over a decade, CleanCo's modelled results for a future year, and Oliver Nunn's illustration of wind droughts as a recurring challenge. The session underscores the role of LDES in firming renewable generation and mitigating grid instability from intermittent wind and solar. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing and projected (long-term, with Kidston energisation later in 2025 and scenario modeling for late next decade). Its root_cause is: the inherent variability of renewable energy sources (wind and solar) leading to supply gaps that long-duration storage is designed to address. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-09-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (specifically Queensland, given Kidston, Swanbank, Wivenhoe, and CleanCo operations). Its affected key users is: electricity grid operators, renewable energy developers, and energy consumers reliant on stable supply. Its cause type is: structural/systemic (energy transition challenges). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Insufficient long-duration storage capacity to firm intermittent renewables; (2) Historical underinvestment and perception of LDES as 'unsexy'; (3) Wind droughts reducing wind generation output; (4) Operational shifts of existing hydro and gas plants over the past decade.

title: Update to Constraint Violation Penalty (CVP) Factors – effective 19th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Update to Constraint Violation Penalty (CVP) Factors – effective 19th September 2025" posted at 16-09-2025 05:16:00 PM is: AEMO issued Market Notice MN129117 on 16 September 2025 announcing changes to Constraint Violation Penalty (CVP) factors, effective from 19 September 2025. The update adds a new Minimum System Load (MSL) constraint with a CVP of 3 and revises Profiled Max/Min Energy Limit constraint CVPs to resolve potential conflicts between the bid MaxAvail constraint and the profiled energy limit constraint. This is a procedural system change implemented under the Enhancing Reserve Information (ERI) project, with no immediate operational disruptions reported. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 19 September 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Need to manage potential conflict between bid MaxAvail constraint and profiled energy limit constraint, and to formally incorporate the Minimum System Load constraint with a penalty factor. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19 September 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, aggregators, and other registered entities). Its cause type is: Administrative system update. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Addition of Minimum System Load constraint with a new CVP of 3; 2. Update to Profiled Max/Min Energy Limit constraint CVPs to resolve conflict between bid MaxAvail constraint and profiled energy limit constraint.

title: Six different lenses to view the ESOO 2025 … giving wildly different perspectives
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Six different lenses to view the ESOO 2025 … giving wildly different perspectives" posted at 16-09-2025 10:59:00 AM is: This article analyzes the 2025 Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) by presenting six scenarios that produce vastly different outlooks for firming capacity in the National Electricity Market (NEM) through 2035, depending on assumptions about project commitment levels (optimistic, base, pessimistic) and whether battery storage capacity contracted for System Integrity Protection Schemes (SIPS) is de-rated. The analysis highlights that only the optimistic scenarios show growth in scheduled capacity, while pessimistic cases indicate a decline of up to 9,758 MW by 2035, emphasizing the critical uncertainty in the energy transition and the need for stakeholders to understand the underlying assumptions behind the ESOO's reliability projections.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2025 to 2035 (10-year outlook). Its root_cause is: Differing assumptions about project commitment status and SIPS de-rating lead to widely divergent capacity forecasts, reflecting fundamental uncertainty in the pace of the energy transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 21 August 2025 (ESOO release) and 16 September 2025 (article publication). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, investors, policymakers, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Forecasting methodology and assumption uncertainties. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Optimistic scenario includes all 'Actionable' projects; 2) Base case includes 'Anticipated' projects with government support but not fully committed; 3) Pessimistic scenario only includes 'Committed' projects; 4) SIPS de-rating reduces available battery capacity for reliability purposes.
