title: Trended physical/technical availability of Semi-Scheduled units – aggregated
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trended physical/technical availability of Semi-Scheduled units – aggregated" posted at 11-09-2025 04:25:00 PM is: This article provides a long-term aggregated analysis of physical and technical availability for semi-scheduled wind and solar units across the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), examining metrics such as Max Capacity, Max Availability, elements available, local limit, and dispatch availability up to September 2025. It identifies that wind availability remains highly variable, with upper increases but lower troughs dropping below 4,000 MW in 2025 despite capacity additions, while solar availability shows strong seasonality and is significantly affected by specific transmission line outages and weather events, such as network constraints on the Jindera-Wagga, Darlington Point-Wagga, and Wagga-Walla Walla lines, and widespread cloud cover in Queensland. The trend reflects commercial decisions, commissioning activities, and unplanned outages, with notable anomalous periods in September 2024, May–June 2025, and August 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from August 2023 to September 2025, with specific anomalous periods in September 2024, May–June 2025, and August 2025; Its root_cause is: Combination of network outages (transmission constraints), weather patterns (low wind, cloud cover), planned and unplanned turbine outages, and commercial decisions (MaxAvail usage) affecting availability; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 21-27 September 2024, 26 May 2024, 31 May-7 June 2025, 17 June 2025, 28 May-4 June 2025, 13 August 2025 (main date 11-09-2025); Its affected region is: NEM, particularly New South Wales (due to transmission line outages) and Queensland (due to weather), and overall NEM wind and solar farms; Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled wind and solar generators (e.g., Bulgana Wind Farm, Clark Creek, Ararat Wind Farm), market operators (AEMO), and electricity market participants;

title: Will I see you at the inaugural ‘NEMdev’ conference in Brisbane on Tue 7th and Wed 8th October 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Will I see you at the inaugural ‘NEMdev’ conference in Brisbane on Tue 7th and Wed 8th October 2025?" posted at 11-09-2025 03:13:00 PM is: The article announces the inaugural NEMdev conference in Brisbane on October 7-8, 2025, blending academic and real-world perspectives to address tricky questions about the energy transition and operating the NEM with high renewable penetration, with the author inviting readers to attend; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 2025-10-07 to 2025-10-08. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: 3.5 months after the turbine fire at Bulgana Wind Farm, we take an updated look
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-05-28 turbine fire at Bulgana Wind Farm
classified_content: The summary of the news "3.5 months after the turbine fire at Bulgana Wind Farm, we take an updated look" posted at 11-09-2025 11:35:00 AM is: This article provides an updated analysis of the Bulgana Wind Farm three and a half months after a turbine fire on 28 May 2025 destroyed a blade, causing the entire farm to go offline for about two weeks. Subsequently, units were progressively brought back, reaching ~75% availability by late June and a peak of 90% (183 MW) by early July, but then declining to ~50% by end of August due to unknown reasons. The analysis also covers chronological availability data from 2021, including prior outages, and notes that the sale of the farm to HMC Capital closed in August 2025 with 51 of 56 turbines operational at that time. The article underscores that physical plant availability for wind farms is a critical factor, similar to coal units.; Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: turbine/generator failures; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 28 May 2025 fire onwards, with reduced availability persisting through at least August 2025; Its root_cause is: Turbine fire at Bulgana Wind Farm on 28 May 2025; Its accident scale is: One turbine destroyed, farm output reduced to about 50-75% capacity over subsequent months; 49 of 56 turbines operational by late June, 51 by August; Its dates are: Event date: 28-05-2025; Post date: 11-09-2025; Its affected region is: Bulgana Wind Farm, Victoria, Australia; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, AEMO, and HMC Capital (new owner); Its cause type is: Accident (fire); Its causes is: Turbine fire of unknown origin on 28 May 2025 leading to blade destruction and subsequent operational restrictions.

title: The new ‘V_NWVIC_GFT1_750’ constraint equation binds, on Wednesday evening 10th September 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The new ‘V_NWVIC_GFT1_750’ constraint equation binds, on Wednesday evening 10th September 2025" posted at 11-09-2025 08:09:00 AM is: The new ‘V_NWVIC_GFT1_750’ constraint equation, introduced to manage contingency size in north-west Victoria, first bound during the 19:20 dispatch interval on 10th September 2025, curtailing 6 MW of output from wind farm ARWF1 due to its marginal bid of -$148/MWh, with four other wind farms equally constrained. The constraint is a system normal measure to limit generation from five wind farms in the region to avoid overloading the network under contingency conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The constraint bound for a brief period on the evening of 10th September 2025, with potential for recurrence. Its root_cause is: AEMO introduced the constraint equation to manage contingency size on north-west Victoria's transmission system. Its accident scale is: Minor (6 MW curtailment on a single wind farm). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-09-2025. Its affected region is: north-west Victoria. Its affected key users is: Wind farm operators in north-west Victoria (specifically ARWF1 and four other wind farms on the LHS of the constraint). Its cause type is: Grid constraint. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] AEMO identified a need for a new system normal constraint to manage contingency size; [2] The constraint equation 'V_NWVIC_GFT1_750' was created with uniform LHS factors for five wind farms; [3] On 10th September 2025, the constraint became binding during a high wind output period, leading to curtailment of wind farms.

title: Newport Power Station running, with Yallourn’s yips yielding 1 unit operations
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-08-26 YWPS2 unplanned outage due to 'LP turbine issues'
classified_content: The summary of the news "Newport Power Station running, with Yallourn’s yips yielding 1 unit operations" posted at 10-09-2025 12:31:00 PM is: The article discusses the operation of Newport Power Station coinciding with reduced output from Yallourn Power Station, specifically noting that Yallourn’s Unit 2 (YWPS2) experienced an unplanned outage on 2025-08-26 due to low-pressure (LP) turbine issues, leaving only one Yallourn unit operational; this situation prompted Newport to run more frequently, though not exclusively during Yallourn's low output periods. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2025-08-26 until the unit is repaired (exact resolution date unknown). Its root_cause is: LP turbine issues at Yallourn Unit 2. Its accident scale is: Unplanned outage of one generating unit (YWPS2, 350 MW nominal capacity). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 2025-08-26. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (specifically the Latrobe Valley and the National Electricity Market (NEM) region). Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants, Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: LP turbine issues.

title: Noting the NERC Alert ‘Large Load Interconnection, Study, Commissioning, and Operations’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Noting the NERC Alert ‘Large Load Interconnection, Study, Commissioning, and Operations’" posted at 2025-09-10 is: This news highlights a recent alert from the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) concerning the interconnection, study, commissioning, and operations of large loads, referencing a July 2025 white paper on the characteristics and risks of emerging large loads; the alert is noted as relevant to the evolution of the Australian National Electricity Market, emphasizing the need for careful grid planning and stability assessments as large loads like data centers and AI facilities increase. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing/long-term (as large loads continue to interconnect). Its root_cause is: Increasing interconnection of large loads without adequate study and coordination, posing risks to grid stability. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported). Its dates is: 2025-09-10 (same as main date). Its affected region is: North America (NERC jurisdiction) and possibly Australia (by analogy). Its affected key users is: Grid operators, large load customers, system planners. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Operational (planning and interconnection procedures). Its causes is: Rapid growth of large loads (e.g., data centers, AI, crypto), insufficient upfront system studies, and need for updated commissioning and operational protocols.

title: Wobbles at Yallourn unit 4
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wobbles at Yallourn unit 4" posted at 09-09-2025 08:34:00 AM is: This article details a series of unplanned outages at Yallourn Unit 4 between 26 August and 7 September 2025, caused by recurring equipment failures including an economiser tube leak, turbine valve issues, and a boiler tube leak, as well as separate LP turbine problems at Yallourn Unit 2 and turbine vibrations at Vales Point 5. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 26 August 2025 to 7 September 2025 and ongoing as of the publication date. Its root_cause is: Recurring equipment problems at Yallourn Unit 4 (economiser tube leak, turbine valve issues, boiler tube leak) plus separate issues at Yallourn Unit 2 and Vales Point 5. Its accident scale is: Multiple unplanned outages at a single generating unit over 20 days, with additional outages at two other units. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 26 August 2025 to 7 September 2025. Its affected region is: Yallourn power station, Victoria, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: AEMO, electricity market participants, and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Technical failures (turbine, boiler tubes, valves). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: LP turbine issues at Yallourn Unit 2; turbine vibrations at Vales Point 5; economiser tube leak at Yallourn Unit 4; turbine valve issues at Yallourn Unit 4; boiler tube leak at Yallourn Unit 4.

title: Highlighting particular (frequency and/or AggROT) events through 2024 and 2025
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Highlighting particular (frequency and/or AggROT) events through 2024 and 2025" posted at 08-09-2025 12:42:00 PM is: This article compiles and analyzes frequency excursions and Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) events for semi-scheduled units in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) during 2024 and 2025. It systematically lists and correlates instances where system frequency deviated outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) with large AggROT values, identifying that VRE collective under/over-performance was a significant contributing factor in many events, while other events were caused by coal unit trips, transmission outages, or forecast glitches. The analysis raises questions about the sustainability and scalability of the semi-scheduled category given its impact on grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2024 to 2025 with multiple specific events. Its root_cause is: Imbalance between generation and demand, primarily from semi-scheduled VRE units' aggregate off-target and conventional generator trips. Its accident scale is: Multiple events with frequency excursions outside NOFB and AggROT up to 849 MW. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Various specific dates in 2024 and 2025 as listed in the article (e.g., 29 Aug 2025, 19 Aug 2025, 23 Jun 2025, etc.). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, semi-scheduled generators, and all electricity consumers in NEM. Its cause type is: Operational/technical (system imbalance, forecast errors, generator trips). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Collective under/over-performance of semi-scheduled VRE units (AggROT); 2) Glitch in self-forecasts for solar farms; 3) Coal unit trips (Bayswater, Loy Yang, Tarong); 4) Transmission line trips; 5) High demand conditions.

title: Outage at Vales Point unit 5 extended – return to service now not expected until 15th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Outage at Vales Point unit 5 extended – return to service now not expected until 15th December 2025" posted at 05-09-2025 04:29:00 PM is: Vales Point unit 5, a coal-fired generator in New South Wales, experienced an extended unplanned outage due to turbine vibrations; its return to service was initially expected on 9th November 2025 but has been pushed out to 15th December 2025 as of the article's publication, with the unit's availability dropping to 0 MW for a longer duration in the market forecasts.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 26th August 2025 (original outage start) to 15th December 2025 (extended return date), with ongoing impact as of 5th September 2025.  
Its root_cause is: turbine vibrations.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates is: 05-09-2025 (article date), outage began 26-08-2025.  
Its affected region is: Vales Point power station, New South Wales, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market).  
Its affected key users is: wholesale electricity market participants and consumers in the NEM.  
Its cause type is: mechanical failure.  
Its causes is: turbine vibrations leading to unit unavailability and extended outage duration.

title: AEMO notes ‘New Victorian system normal constraint to manage contingency size on north-west Victoria’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes ‘New Victorian system normal constraint to manage contingency size on north-west Victoria’" posted at 05-09-2025 01:21:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 5 September 2025 stating that studies found the 220kV Generator Fast Trip scheme (GFT1) and inherent tripping of Waubra Wind Farm in north-west Victoria could create a single contingency size of up to 1000MW, exceeding the 600MW threshold. To maintain power system security, a new constraint equation (V_NWVIC_GFT1_750) will be added to constraint set V-NIL from 1000 hrs on 9 September 2025, limiting total generation output from affected generators to 750MW. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1000 hrs on 9 September 2025 onward (when the constraint is implemented). Its root_cause is: Potential large contingency size (up to 1000MW) from GFT1 scheme and Waubra Wind Farm tripping, which could compromise power system security. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no actual accident, only preventive constraint). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: Implementation date 09-09-2025; main notice date 05-09-2025. Its affected region is: North-west Victoria. Its affected key users is: Generators included in the GFT1 scheme and Waubra Wind Farm. Its cause type is: Technical/operational constraint. Its causes is: (1) 220kV Generator Fast Trip scheme (GFT1) has potential to create large contingency; (2) Inherent tripping of Waubra Wind Farm adds to contingency size; (3) Combined contingency could reach up to 1000MW, exceeding safe limits.

title: Vales Point unit 5 (partly unplanned) outage – return to service currently expected 9th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Vales Point unit 5 (partly unplanned) outage – return to service currently expected 9th November 2025" posted at 05-09-2025 12:57:00 PM is: Vales Point unit 5 in NSW experienced an unplanned outage starting 26 August 2025 due to turbine vibrations, following a prior tube leak outage from 18-24 August. Initially scheduled to return on 10 September, the outage was merged with a planned major outage, pushing the expected return to 9 November 2025, likely to minimize costs and align with low seasonal prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 26 August 2025 to 9 November 2025 (approximately 2.5 months). Its root_cause is: Turbine vibrations causing unplanned outage, merged with a planned major outage. Its accident scale is: Single unit outage (Vales Point unit 5, capacity not specified). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: Unplanned outage start 26 August 2025, earlier tube leak outage 18-24 August 2025, planned outage originally September-October 2025, return to service expected 9 November 2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: NSW electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Tube leak on 18 August 2025 (fixed); turbine vibrations on 26 August 2025; decision to merge unplanned outage with planned major outage.

title: Daily NOFB exceedances since 2021
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Daily NOFB exceedances since 2021" posted at 04-09-2025 12:28:00 PM is: The article reviews instances of system frequency falling outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) in the Australian NEM from 2021 onward, finding that below-NOFB events occur more frequently than above-NOFB events and no clear monthly trend is apparent, with data presented from January 2021 through August 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Since 2021, ongoing. Its root_cause is: Unknown (trend observation without specific attribution). Its accident scale is: Multiple days with at least one NOFB exceedance (dozens of dates listed). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Various dates from January 2021 to August 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators and market participants. Its cause type is: Grid frequency instability / system frequency excursions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Not specified in the article; general imbalance between generation and load is implied.

title: AEMC releases July 2024 working paper ‘Addressing the risk of algorithmic collusion’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC releases July 2024 working paper ‘Addressing the risk of algorithmic collusion’" posted at 04-09-2025 12:26:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) published a belated staff working paper (July 2024, released to the public on 4 September 2025) that examines the risk of algorithmic collusion in the National Electricity Market, driven by the rapid increase in auto-bidders and algorithm-based bidding strategies, which have raised concerns about potential tacit coordination and market manipulation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (potential future regulatory changes). Its root_cause is: Increasing use of automated bidding algorithms and self-forecasting systems in the NEM, creating opportunities for tacit collusion without explicit communication. Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident). Its dates is: July 2024 (working paper date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, traders) and regulators (AEMC, AER). Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy issue. Its causes is: 1) Proliferation of auto-bidders and high-frequency rebids; 2) Potential for algorithmic tacit collusion; 3) Lack of existing market rules addressing collusion via algorithms.

title: AEMO initiates new operational requirements in SA (1 synchronous unit, in certain circumstances) and NSW (system strength)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO initiates new operational requirements in SA (1 synchronous unit, in certain circumstances) and NSW (system strength)" posted at 03-09-2025 05:05:00 PM is: AEMO issued Market Notice 128912 on 3 September 2025, updating generator combinations in South Australia to allow single synchronous generator operation under specific conditions, and publishing revised system strength requirements for New South Wales, following earlier notices (93862, 114893) and collaboration with ElectraNet and Transgrid to maintain grid stability and system strength. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing from 03/09/2025, as the new operational requirements take effect. Its root_cause is: Need to maintain grid stability and system strength amidst changing generation mix (high penetration of inverter-based renewables) in South Australia and New South Wales. Its accident scale is: N/A (not an accident; regulatory operational change). Its dates is: 03/09/2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: South Australia and New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Generators, market participants, AEMO, ElectraNet, Transgrid. Its cause type is: Operational/regulatory. Its causes is: (1) Evolving grid conditions requiring reduced synchronous generation in SA; (2) Updated system strength requirements in NSW from Transgrid; (3) Earlier steps (e.g., four synchronous condenser operation) paving the way for this change.

title: Instances of system frequency outside the normal operating band
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Instances of system frequency outside the normal operating band" posted at 03-09-2025 05:02:00 PM is: The article reviews historical frequency performance in mainland Australia's electricity grid, highlighting that excursions outside the normal operating frequency band (NOFB) were frequent between 2017 and 2020. A March 2020 determination mandated Primary Frequency Response (PFR), with a staggered rollout among generators starting in September 2020. This initiative rapidly improved frequency control, making excursions rare by January 2021. The article lists power stations and their PFR implementation dates, illustrating a multi-year process. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The PFR mandate and rollout began in September 2020, with frequency stability markedly improved by January 2021 and sustained thereafter, reducing market risks from frequency deviations. Its root_cause is: Insufficient primary frequency response from generators prior to the mandate. Its accident scale is: Not an accident; prior frequency deviations were significant, but the event describes corrective measures and improved performance. Its dates is: 03-09-2025 (post date); key implementation dates span from September 2020 to July 2022. Its affected region is: Mainland Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Generators listed (e.g., Vales Point, Eraring, Bayswater, etc.) and all NEM participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Operational. Its causes is: 1) Historical lack of adequate primary frequency response from generators; 2) AEMO's March 2020 determination mandating PFR; 3) Staggered rollout of PFR from September 2020 across multiple power stations.

title: (Two not one!) more media articles (and 5, not 4, analysts) questioning Eraring’s mooted closure in 2027 … and same for other coal units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(Two not one!) more media articles (and 5, not 4, analysts) questioning Eraring’s mooted closure in 2027 … and same for other coal units" posted at 02-09-2025 08:23:00 AM is: Multiple analysts and media articles highlight a growing disconnect between the ideal energy transition and real-world delays, questioning whether Origin's Eraring coal plant can close by 2027 (or even 2029) and noting that other coal units like Loy Yang A and Yallourn may also stay open longer due to renewable energy shortfalls; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with closure deadlines of 2027/2029 under debate, affecting medium-term supply reliability. Its root_cause is: Disconnect between ambitious energy transition targets and slow renewable deployment, leading to political and operational pressure to keep coal plants running. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 02-09-2025 08:23:00 AM. Its affected region is: New South Wales (Eraring) and Victoria (Loy Yang A, Yallourn). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Policy/planning issue. Its causes is: Renewable energy delays, political wrangling, unrealistic closure deadlines, and analyst concerns over grid reliability.

title: Yes, Tamar Valley CCGT did switch off last week (on Wednesday 27th August 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yes, Tamar Valley CCGT did switch off last week (on Wednesday 27th August 2025)" posted at 01-09-2025 09:32:00 AM is: Tamar Valley CCGT unit switched off on 27 August 2025, a day earlier than anticipated, as indicated by market monitoring tools. The outage is classified as a basic planned outage with a 20-day recall period, and no further generation is scheduled in the three-year forward outlook. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 27 August 2025, with a recall period of 20 days, and no further usage scheduled in the 3-year forward horizon. Its root_cause is: Planned outage (basic planned outage). Its accident scale is: Not applicable (planned outage). Its dates is: 27 August 2025. Its affected region is: Tasmania, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AEMO, electricity market participants, and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Planned outage. Its causes is: ["Planned maintenance outage", "Earlier than expected cessation of operation"].

title: Looking more at (commissioning) operations at Waratah BESS through August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking more at (commissioning) operations at Waratah BESS through August 2025" posted at 31-08-2025 12:59:00 PM is: This article reviews Waratah BESS commissioning in August 2025, highlighting multiple non-conformance notices (e.g., MN128772 on 29 Aug for a 638 MW under-performance), large dispatch errors (e.g., -638 MW at 10:25 and +708 MW at 10:45 on 29 Aug), and the battery's role in two frequency incidents: a frequency decay on 29 Aug (lowest 49.847 Hz) partly due to its under-performance during Hold Point 4 testing, and a frequency spike on 19 Aug (to 50.2 Hz) where a +80 MW dispatch error from the battery helped restrain the rise. The article notes that while large batteries are valuable, their rapid responses during commissioning can challenge grid stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours), specifically the 29 Aug incident (10-minute non-conformance window) and the 19 Aug incident (multiple dispatch intervals from 11:45 to 12:20). Its root_cause is: Commissioning testing (Hold Point 4) at Waratah BESS led to large dispatch errors (under-performance and over-performance), which contributed to frequency deviations; additionally, on 19 Aug, a separate issue with solar farm self-forecasts was a primary cause, but the battery’s response was a mitigating factor. Its accident scale is: Minor frequency excursions—29 Aug (49.847 Hz, just outside normal band) and 19 Aug (50.2 Hz, outside normal band); no load shedding or equipment damage reported. Its dates is: 19 August 2025 and 29 August 2025. Its affected region is: NSW1 (New South Wales region of Australia’s National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown (general NEM market participants and system operators). Its cause type is: Operational/testing activity during commissioning. Its causes is: 1) Waratah BESS Hold Point 4 testing caused a 638 MW under-performance relative to target at 11:50 on 29 Aug, dragging frequency down; 2) On 19 Aug, solar farm self-forecast errors caused a large unexpected injection, while Waratah BESS exhibited a +80 MW dispatch error (under-performance) that assisted in frequency control; 3) Earlier non-conformances likely related to hold point tests or primary frequency response delivery.

title: More about that decay of mainland frequency at lunchtime on Friday 29th August 2025 (i.e. Part 2)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More about that decay of mainland frequency at lunchtime on Friday 29th August 2025 (i.e. Part 2)" posted at 31-08-2025 11:28:00 AM is: On Friday 29th August 2025, a gradual decay of mainland frequency occurred around midday, with the lowest point reaching 49.847 Hz (slightly outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band) at 11:50:30.8, contrasting with the faster drop from a later Loy Yang A1 trip. The event is attributed to increased frequency management challenges due to high penetration of invisible rooftop solar PV during strong daylight hours. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term disruption (within hours) on 29-08-2025 from 10:00 to 13:00 NEM time. Its root_cause is: frequency imbalance driven by rapid changes in rooftop solar PV output and insufficient frequency control reserves. Its accident scale is: minor frequency deviation (0.153 Hz below 50 Hz) with no reported load shedding or blackout. Its dates is: 29-08-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Australian mainland (National Electricity Market, NEM). Its affected key users is: all grid-connected electricity consumers. Its cause type is: operational/grid management issue. Its causes is: gradual supply-demand imbalance due to variability of rooftop solar generation; insufficient fast frequency response during high solar penetration hours.

title: Mainland frequency drop with Loy Yang A1 trip on Saturday evening 30th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland frequency drop with Loy Yang A1 trip on Saturday evening 30th August 2025" posted at 31-08-2025 11:13:00 AM is: On Saturday evening 30th August 2025, the Loy Yang A1 unit in Victoria tripped from 505 MW, causing a rapid frequency drop on the mainland grid to 49.873 Hz (within the Normal Operating Frequency Band). The frequency recovered subsequently, and the event was recorded via system notifications. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours, as frequency deviation remained within normal band and recovery followed). Its root_cause is: Trip of Loy Yang A1 unit (unplanned generator outage). Its accident scale is: Single unit trip of 505 MW capacity. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Event date 30-08-2025, news date 31-08-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, particularly Victoria (Loy Yang location) and interconnected regions. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and grid operators on the mainland NEM (frequency deviation affects entire interconnected system). title: Tasmanian demand drop on 16 August 2025 – part 2
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tasmanian demand drop on 16 August 2025 – part 2" posted at 28-08-2025 12:59:00 PM is: On 16 August 2025 at around 14:09, Basslink experienced an unplanned outage while importing ~310 MW into Tasmania, causing a 292 MW drop in market demand and a frequency disturbance. The System Protection Scheme (SPS) and Frequency Control System Protection Scheme (FCSPS) likely activated load shedding within milliseconds to stabilize frequency, as evidenced by the demand reduction in the next dispatch interval. Analysis of 4-second data confirms the sequence: Basslink trip first, then frequency dip in Tasmania, with supply-side deviations insufficient to compensate. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, event on 16-08-2025. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of Basslink interconnector. Its accident scale is: 292 MW demand drop (loss of 310 MW Basslink flow). Its dates is: 16-08-2025. Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS). Its affected key users is: Tasmanian electricity consumers (load shed via SPS). Its cause type is: Transmission interconnector trip / system protection scheme activation. Its causes is: Basslink unplanned outage at 14:09 on 16/08/2025; frequency disturbance; activation of SPS/FCSPS resulting in load shedding.

title: Frequency change with Yallourn unit 2 trip
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency change with Yallourn unit 2 trip" posted at 27-08-2025 03:49:00 PM is: On 26 August 2025, the Yallourn Unit 2 coal-fired generator tripped due to an LP turbine issue, losing 368 MW of output within the 13:35 interval. This caused a system frequency drop of approximately 0.1 Hz (from 49.951 Hz to 49.851 Hz), with a nadir of 49.838 Hz, as measured by Global-Roam loggers and corroborated by AEMO 4-second data. The deviation was most pronounced in Victoria, and corrective actions were observed from scheduled units across all mainland regions except Tasmania, where Basslink was unavailable for FCAS transfer. The incident highlights the immediate impact of a large generator trip on grid frequency stability. Its **relevance level** is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its **category** is: turbine/generator failures. Its **Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market** is: Immediate minutes (frequency deviation lasting seconds, with potential short-term market rebalancing). Its **root_cause** is: LP turbine issue. Its **accident scale** is: Forced outage of a 368 MW unit. Its **dates** (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-08-2025 (event date). Its **affected region** is: Victoria (primary), with frequency impact across all mainland NEM regions (NSW, QLD, SA, VIC). Its **affected key users** is: Grid operators (AEMO) and market participants. Its **cause type** is: Forced outage / mechanical failure. Its **causes** (a summarized list of cause events) is: LP turbine issue leading to unit trip.

title: Peeling the onion a little more – Solar Farm performance on Tuesday 19th August 2025, with the frequency spike to 50.2Hz
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Peeling the onion a little more – Solar Farm performance on Tuesday 19th August 2025, with the frequency spike to 50.2Hz" posted at 27-08-2025 01:00:00 PM is: This article provides a detailed analysis of the performance of 106 semi-scheduled solar farms during a frequency spike to 50.2 Hz on 19 August 2025. It presents charts showing the aggregate deviation between target and final MW over a 30-minute period, calculates dispatch error (target minus final MW) revealing widespread negative errors (over-performance), and trims a list of 55 solar farms of interest with highly variable dispatch errors, some exceeding 100 MW in either direction. The root cause is the over-performance of solar farms relative to their targets causing the frequency spike, with a possible data glitch in AEMO's UIGF value noted. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 August 2025. Its root_cause is: Over-performance of semi-scheduled solar farms relative to their dispatch targets, leading to a significant negative aggregate dispatch error that drove the frequency spike to 50.2 Hz. Its accident scale is: Fleet-wide, involving 106 solar farms with 55 units of particular interest; individual deviations up to >100 MW. Its dates (if different) is: 19 August 2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) and solar farm operators. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (dispatch deviation). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Large aggregate deviation between target and actual output from solar farms during high solar irradiance mid-day; 2) Some solar farms over-performing by >100 MW; 3) Possible data glitch in AEMO's aggregate UIGF value at the 13:35 dispatch interval.

title: An updated tabular list of instances of large AggROT across Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "An updated tabular list of instances of large AggROT across Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 24-08-2025 06:56:00 PM is: This article provides a comprehensive tabular update of large Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) events across all Semi-Scheduled units in the National Electricity Market (NEM), covering 42 instances exceeding ±600 MW from October 2024 to June 2025 (with extended records back to 2020). It details how these collective deviations—predominantly under-performance but also occasional over-performance—have repeatedly caused mainland frequency disturbances, including a sustained 50.2 Hz event on 19th August 2025 and numerous frequency drops linked to weather-driven output variability and unit trips, raising questions about the sustainability and scalability of the Semi-Scheduled category.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Multiple discrete events from October 2024 to August 2025, with ongoing trend implications.; Its root_cause is: Large deviations in aggregate output of semi-scheduled renewable units due to variable wind/solar conditions, sudden unit trips, and operational challenges in matching dispatch targets.; Its accident scale is: 42 instances of collective deviations exceeding 600 MW, with maximum recorded under-performance of 866 MW (27th Oct 2022) and 851 MW (31st Mar 2025).; Its dates are: Article date 24-08-2025; events span from 2020 to 25-08-2025 (data up to 30th June 2025, plus recent 19th Aug 2025 mentioned).; Its affected region is: NEM mainland (Australia).; Its affected key users are: System operators, frequency control providers, semi-scheduled wind and solar generators, and market participants.; Its cause type is: Operational/technical.; Its causes are: A list of 42 specific dispatch intervals with large AggROT, including 19th Aug 2025 (over-performance), 23rd Jun 2025 (+657 MW), 16th Jun 2025 (+600 MW), 9th Jun 2025 (+688 MW), 26th May 2025 (+695 MW), 24th May 2025 (+616 MW), 21st Apr 2025 (+618 MW), 19th Apr 2025 (+685 and +834 MW), 31st Mar 2025 (four instances up to +851 MW), 19th Mar 2025 (+647 MW), 22nd Feb 2025 (+606 and +620 MW), 21st Feb 2025 (+602 MW), 15th Feb 2025 (four instances up to +849 MW), 14th Feb 2025 (+708 MW), 13th Feb 2025 (+686 MW), 17th Jan 2025 (+661 MW), 27th Dec 2024 (+692 MW), 16th Dec 2024 (four instances up to +644 MW), 14th Dec 2024 (+662 MW), 8th Dec 2024 (-610 and -661 MW over-performance), 7th Dec 2024 (+633 MW), 16th Nov 2024 (+642 MW), 6th Nov 2024 (+629 MW), 24th Oct 2024 (+612 MW), 5th Oct 2024 (+609 MW), 28th Feb 2024 (+605 MW), 22nd Feb 2024 (+613 and +654 MW), 13th Feb 2024 (+701 and +815 MW), 4th Feb 2024 (+697 MW), and earlier events in 2023 and 2022.

title: An updated long-range trend of large instances of Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) across all Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "An updated long-range trend of large instances of Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) across all Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 24-08-2025 04:30:00 PM is: The article updates the trend of large Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) instances across all Semi-Scheduled units in the NEM up to 30 June 2025, showing that while growth in incidence of 100-200MW exceedances moderated after 2023, larger exceedances (300-600MW) continue to rise, with over 100 instances of >500MW under-performance per year, raising concerns about frequency control stability despite recent enhancements like Primary Frequency Response and Frequency Performance Payments.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing; trend data from 2024 to 2025 projects continued escalation.  
Its root_cause is: The operational rules for Semi-Scheduled units that do not require them to meet their dispatch target (only a cap), leading to large aggregate under-performance or over-performance.  
Its accident scale is: Multiple large exceedances (300-600MW+) occurring frequently, with >100 instances of >500MW under-performance in 2025.  
Its dates is: 24-08-2025 (news post date); data analyzed up to 30-06-2025.  
Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia.  
Its affected key users are: System operators (AEMO), frequency control operators, Semi-Scheduled unit operators.  
Its cause type is: Regulatory/operational design flaw.  
Its causes are: 1) Semi-Scheduled units not required to meet dispatch target (only cap), 2) Growing installed capacity of Semi-Scheduled assets, 3) Insufficient conformance requirements despite recent frequency performance payments and primary frequency response.

title: Three broader questions (re)triggered by the Frequency Spike on Tuesday 19th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three broader questions (re)triggered by the Frequency Spike on Tuesday 19th August 2025" posted at 23-08-2025 08:16:00 PM is: On Tuesday, 19 August 2025, a significant frequency spike (reaching 50.2 Hz) occurred in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland regions, driven by a suspected glitch in a self-forecasting vendor's UIGF system. This caused a large mismatch between expected and actual output of semi-scheduled renewable units (solar and wind), resulting in an aggregate dispatch error of approximately -1,500 MW across six dispatch intervals. The article explores three broader implications: sustainability of the semi-scheduled category, concerns about increasing automation ('rise of the machines'), and potential gamesmanship in self-forecasting practices, highlighting ongoing grid frequency control vulnerabilities. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the 19 August 2025 frequency excursion, with ongoing regulatory and market design implications. Its root_cause is: Suspected glitch in one self-forecasting vendor's UIGF system causing inaccurate forecasts and unhelpful deviations from target by semi-scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Frequency spike to 50.2 Hz; aggregate dispatch error of ~1,500 MW; affecting multiple dispatch intervals (11:55 to 12:15). Its dates is: 19-08-2025 (event date; news posted 23-08-2025). Its affected region is: Mainland NEM regions (excluding Tasmania) – New South Wales, Queensland, Victoria, South Australia. Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled renewable generators (solar and wind farms), self-forecasting vendor(s), AEMO (market operator), and market participants relying on frequency stability. Its cause type is: Technical failure / software glitch. Its causes is: 1) Glitch in a self-forecasting vendor's UIGF system; 2) Resulting inaccurate self-forecasts for semi-scheduled units; 3) Large aggregate unhelpful deviations (dispatch error) from semi-scheduled units; 4) Coincidence with frequency disturbance, exacerbating the spike.

title: AEMO releases 2025 ESOO on Thursday 21st August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases 2025 ESOO on Thursday 21st August 2025" posted at 21-08-2025 10:14:00 AM is: The article details the release of the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) 2025 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) on 21 August 2025, which assesses the reliability outlook for the National Electricity Market (NEM) under two scenarios—one factoring in government schemes and actionable transmission developments, and another considering only committed and anticipated projects. The report indicates an improved reliability outlook if all planned investments in renewables, storage, and transmission are delivered on time, but highlights risks from potential project delays and cost escalations, drawing commentary from media and industry organizations. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (2025–2030). Its root_cause is: Need to assess reliability of electricity supply given retiring coal plants and growing demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 21-08-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, generators, investors, policymakers. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: - Release of 2025 ESOO report assessing reliability under two scenarios; - Planned retirement of coal-fired power stations; - Requirement for timely delivery of new generation, storage, and transmission investments.

title: AGL considering State Government request to keep Torrens Island open for two more years

classified_content: The summary of the news "AGL considering State Government request to keep Torrens Island open for two more years" posted at 21-08-2025 09:57:00 AM is: AGL has agreed in-principle to the South Australian Government’s request to extend the operation of its Torrens Island ‘B’ gas-fired power station by two years beyond its planned closure on 1 July 2026, following AEMO’s 2025 Electricity Statement of Opportunities which warned of a 390 MW power shortfall in South Australia by 2026 if the plant closes as scheduled; the extension, if finalized, is intended to ensure sufficient long-duration firming capacity for grid reliability during the energy transition.
