Its root_cause is: Potential power shortfall in South Australia due to planned closure of Torrens Island B, as identified by AEMO’s 2025 ESOO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Planned closure 1 July 2026; extension consideration 21 August 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Policy/Regulatory. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO forecast of 390 MW shortfall; South Australian Government request; AGL in-principle agreement to extend.

title: Unit deviations from target on Tuesday 19th August 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unit deviations from target on Tuesday 19th August 2025" posted at 20-08-2025 03:45:00 PM is: On 19 August 2025, a frequency excursion occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to generating units, particularly solar farms, deviating above their dispatch targets. A glitch in solar unit information gathering functions (UIGFs) caused availability to be set to zero, yet units continued generating, leading to over-production and system frequency spikes. Battery storage, coal, and hydro units countered with negative deviations, but the event highlighted vulnerabilities in the semi-scheduled category and reliance on technology. The incident affected regions VIC, NSW, QLD, and SA, with multiple units receiving non-conformance constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (during the frequency excursion on 19 August 2025, approximately 11:45 to 12:20). Its root_cause is: Glitch in solar unit information gathering functions (UIGFs) causing low availability targets and over-production. Its accident scale is: Moderate to large scale with deviations up to 150MW and multiple units affected across VIC, NSW, QLD, SA. Its dates is: 19-08-2025 (event date), article posted 20-08-2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland regions: Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia. Its affected key users is: Solar generators, battery storage, coal and hydro generators, system operator (AEMO). Its cause type is: Technical/software glitch. Its causes is: (1) Glitch in solar UIGFs leading to incorrect availability data; (2) Semi-scheduled units not constrained by semi-dispatch cap; (3) Lack of robust system response to prevent over-production.

title: The increasingly stochastic and volatile NEM
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "The increasingly stochastic and volatile NEM" posted at 20-08-2025 09:55:00 AM is: The article explains that weather variability—temperature driving demand and wind/solar irradiance driving supply—is becoming a dominant factor in electricity prices in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM) as renewable penetration increases. It uses modelling to show that after projected coal-fired power station closures (from FY2033 onward), the system becomes highly weather-dependent, with annual average prices varying by almost a factor of two between favourable and unfavourable weather years. The analysis stresses that using single weather reference years underestimates future volatility and that stakeholders must consider a distribution of weather outcomes for hedging, investment in storage and gas generation, and climate adaptation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term future impact (post-coal closure, from 2030s onward). Its root_cause is: High penetration of variable renewable energy (wind and solar) combined with the planned retirement of coal-fired power plants, making grid operation and prices heavily dependent on stochastic weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 20-08-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia (with specific examples for Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales). Its affected key users is: Market participants, investors, grid operators, energy advisors, policymakers, and analysts. Its cause type is: Stochastic weather variability and coal-fired power plant closures. Its causes is: (1) Temperature variations driving heating and cooling demand; (2) Wind speed and solar irradiance variations affecting renewable energy supply; (3) Projected closure of coal-fired power stations removing baseload capacity and increasing weather dependence.

title: A ~50-day view of rising spurts of volatility in the LOWER1SEC FCAS market in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A ~50-day view of rising spurts of volatility in the LOWER1SEC FCAS market in South Australia" posted at 20-08-2025 08:33:00 AM is: This article highlights a prolonged period of rising volatility in the LOWER1SEC FCAS (FFR) market in South Australia over the past approximately 50 days, driven by network outages on the Tailem Bend–Tungkillo 275kV transmission line. The cumulative price for this FCAS commodity has shown a clear upward trend, and administered pricing has continued even after the outage ended on 19 August 2025. The article notes that similar pressure may recur when the next related constraint set ('S-TBTU') returns, indicating ongoing grid instability risks.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from approximately early July 2025 to present (20 August 2025) and expected to continue with future outages; Its root_cause is: Network outage on the Tailem Bend–Tungkillo 275kV transmission line; Its accident scale is: Unknown (single transmission line outage); Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main date: 20 August 2025; event period starts approximately 1 July 2025; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, particularly those involved in FCAS (frequency control ancillary services) trading; Its cause type is: Network outage (transmission line); Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Network outage on the Tailem Bend–Tungkillo 275kV line; 2. Resulting constraint set 'S-TBTU' leading to market pressure.

title: Network outage on Tailem Bend – Tungkillo 275kV line ended Tue 19th Aug, but Administered Pricing Continues into Wed 20th Aug (part 3)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Network outage on Tailem Bend – Tungkillo 275kV line ended Tue 19th Aug, but Administered Pricing Continues into Wed 20th Aug (part 3)" posted at 20-08-2025 08:09:00 AM is: A planned outage on one Tailem Bend–Tungkillo 275 kV transmission line ended on Tuesday 19 August 2025, removing the associated “S-TBTU” constraint set that had been driving exceptionally high Lower 1 Second (FFR) FCAS prices in South Australia. The prolonged high prices pushed the cumulative price threshold, triggering administered pricing across all ten FCAS commodities in the SA1 region. Despite the outage ending, the cumulative price sum had not yet fallen below the threshold, prompting AEMO to continue administered pricing into Wednesday 20 August 2025 with a price cap of $600/MWh. The event underscores how a single transmission line outage can cascade into market-wide price controls. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 19 August 2025 (outage end) into 20 August 2025 and until the rolling sum of uncapped prices falls below the cumulative price threshold. Its root_cause is: Outage on one Tailem Bend – Tungkillo 275kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Outage ended 19 August 2025; Administered pricing declared 20 August 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA1 region). Its affected key users are: All market ancillary services participants (FCAS providers and consumers) in SA. Its cause type is: Network outage (transmission line). Its causes are: Outage on Tailem Bend–Tungkillo 275kV line leading to invocation of constraint set S-TBTU, sustained high FFR FCAS prices, cumulative price threshold exceeded, and declaration of administered pricing.

title: High level view of some contributing factors to 19 August frequency spike
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "High level view of some contributing factors to 19 August frequency spike" posted at 19-08-2025 06:21:00 PM is: On 19 August 2025, the mainland Australian NEM experienced a significant frequency spike reaching 50.209 Hz (peak) at 12:05, exceeding the normal operating frequency band of 50.15 Hz. Key contributing factors included a sharp drop in solar UIGF from 5,167 MW to 3,406 MW between 11:45 and 11:55 (possibly a data glitch), a smaller wind UIGF dip, and non-conformance notices for some generating units that deviated above their dispatch targets, exacerbating the frequency deviation. The mainland regulation lower requirement was increased to 400 MW (from typical 210 MW), with 50 MW enabled from Tasmania; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 August 2025, intraday (approximately 11:44 to 12:35). Its root_cause is: A sudden drop in solar generation UIGF combined with unit non-conformance (deviation above targets) led to the frequency spike. Its accident scale is: Major frequency deviation (peak 50.209 Hz, outside NOFB for several minutes). Its dates is: 19-08-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland NEM regions (New South Wales, etc.). Its affected key users is: Generators, system operators, and market participants. Its cause type is: Operational/technical issue (data glitch and generation deviation). Its causes is: 1. Solar UIGF drop from 5,167 MW to 3,406 MW between 11:45 and 11:55. 2. Wind UIGF drop of approximately 100 MW. 3. Non-conformance by some units deviating above dispatch targets. 4. Increased regulation lower requirement to 400 MW on the mainland.

title: Mainland Frequency Spikes to 50.2 on Tue 19th August 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Mainland Frequency Spikes to 50.2 on Tue 19th August 2025" posted at 19-08-2025 01:48:00 PM is: On Tuesday 19 August 2025, a significant frequency deviation occurred on the mainland NEM, peaking above 50.2 Hz between 11:45 and 12:20. AEMO issued Market Notice 128594 at 12:35, invoking constraint set F-MAIN_LREG_0400 at 12:10 to increase FCAS lower regulation requirements and manage frequency. This variation was later cancelled via Notice 128602 at 12:58, with the constraint revoked at 12:45. No root cause was specified in the notice.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1.  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Same day (19 August 2025).  
Its root_cause is: Unknown.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates (if different) is: 19-08-2025.  
Its affected region is: Mainland NEM (Australia, excluding Tasmania).  
Its affected key users is: All grid-connected consumers and generators.  
Its cause type is: Unknown.  
Its causes is: Unknown.

title: Part 2 with respect to Administered Pricing for Lower 1 second FCAS (and 9 other services) in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Part 2 with respect to Administered Pricing for Lower 1 second FCAS (and 9 other services) in South Australia" posted at 18-08-2025 09:59:00 AM is: The article details the continuation of administered pricing for all FCAS services in South Australia after Lower 1 second FCAS prices drove the cumulative price above the threshold on 17 August 2025. A network outage on the Tailem Bend-Tungkillo 275kV line from 16 August increased local FCAS enablement requirements, while bidding patterns—with loads offering low volumes and batteries offering high prices—further stressed the market. AEMO declared an administered price period starting 0405 hrs on 18 August, capping ancillary service prices at $600/MWh. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of event impact on the electricity_market is: 17 August 2025 to 18 August 2025 (with network outage extending to 19 August). Its root_cause is: Network outage on Tailem Bend-Tungkillo 275kV line increasing local Lower 1 second FCAS requirement, combined with limited and high-priced supply from batteries and loads. Its accident scale is: Single 275kV transmission line outage. Its dates are: 16-19 August 2025 (network outage) and 17-18 August 2025 (administered pricing). Its affected region is: South Australia (SA1 region). Its affected key users are: AEMO, market participants including loads (VPPs) and batteries (Lake Bonney, Tailem Bend), and end consumers. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (network outage) and market behavior (bidding strategies). Its causes are: 1) Outage on Tailem Bend-Tungkillo 275kV line. 2) Increased local FCAS enablement requirements due to risk of islanding. 3) Loads offered only ~16 MW at zero price, batteries offered all capacity at high prices near or at market price cap. 4) Cumulative price threshold of $1,823,600 exceeded for Lower 1 second FCAS.

title: The ‘2025 Victorian Transmission Plan’ released on Sunday 17th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "The ‘2025 Victorian Transmission Plan’ released on Sunday 17th August 2025" posted at 18-08-2025 08:40:00 AM is: VicGrid released the 2025 Victorian Transmission Plan on Sunday 17th August 2025, a 134-page document outlining renewable energy zone expansion and transmission routes in Victoria. The plan includes a six-week consultation period with webinars for community and industry feedback. Multiple news outlets reported that the projected cost of the transmission build has nearly doubled compared to earlier estimates, sparking debate over the true cost of the energy transition.  
Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant).  
Its category is: Energy policy.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (years), as transmission infrastructure planning and cost increases will influence future electricity prices.  
Its root_cause is: Release of the 2025 Victorian Transmission Plan with revised cost estimates showing a near-doubling of projected expenses.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates is: 17-08-2025 (plan release) and 18-08-2025 (media coverage and article date).  
Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, landholders, communities, Traditional Owners, and industry stakeholders.  
Its cause type is: Planning/policy.  
Its causes is: VicGrid finalized transmission routes for renewable energy zones; cost projections increased significantly from earlier drafts; public consultation and media scrutiny followed.

title: New ‘lowest ever’ point for ‘Market Demand’ in Queensland, on Sunday 17th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New ‘lowest ever’ point for ‘Market Demand’ in Queensland, on Sunday 17th August 2025" posted at 17-08-2025 02:22:00 PM is: The article reports a new record low for Market Demand in Queensland, reaching 2,962 MW on 17 August 2025, 13 MW lower than the previous record from August 2024, reflecting ongoing demand reduction trends likely driven by high rooftop solar penetration and mild weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term. Its root_cause is: increased rooftop solar generation and mild weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17-08-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: high solar generation, low consumption.

title: Administered pricing for FCAS in South Australia from 05:20 on Sunday 17th August 2025 (part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Administered pricing for FCAS in South Australia from 05:20 on Sunday 17th August 2025 (part 1)" posted at 17-08-2025 12:53:00 PM is: On 17 August 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an administered price period for all 10 Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) in the South Australia (SA1) region, effective from 05:20. This was triggered because the rolling sum of uncapped market ancillary Lower 1 Second services prices over the previous 2016 trading intervals exceeded the cumulative price threshold of $1,823,600. An administered price cap of $600/MWh was applied to all FCAS prices in SA1 for the remainder of the trading day. The notice did not specify the underlying cause of the high FCAS prices; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 17 August 2025 from 05:20 to end of trading day. Its root_cause is: Lower 1 Second FCAS service prices in SA1 region exceeded the cumulative price threshold of $1,823,600 over the preceding 2016 trading intervals. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17-08-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA1 region). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants in SA1 (FCAS providers and consumers). Its cause type is: Market mechanism (administered price cap triggered by cumulative price threshold). Its causes is: High spot prices for Lower 1 Sec FCAS commodity; cumulative sum of uncapped prices reached the cumulative price threshold.

title: A 292MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Tasmania, on Saturday 16th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "A 292MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Tasmania, on Saturday 16th August 2025" posted at 16-08-2025 02:36:00 PM is: On 16 August 2025, an unplanned outage of the Basslink interconnector caused a 292 MW drop in Tasmanian Market Demand (approximately 30% of the region's demand) at the 14:15 dispatch interval, leading to frequency spikes to about 50.10 Hz and significant gyrations in FCAS prices. AEMO issued market notice MN128546 at 14:33 confirming the unplanned Basslink outage and invoking constraint set I-BL_ZERO.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (dispatch intervals from 14:10 to 14:20 on 16 August 2025, with ongoing constraint set invoked).  
Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of the Basslink interconnector.  
Its accident scale is: 292 MW drop (approximately 30% of Tasmanian Market Demand).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-08-2025.  
Its affected region is: Tasmania (and by interconnection, Victoria).  
Its affected key users is: Wholesale electricity market participants (including generators, retailers, and AEMO).  
Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (equipment/transmission failure).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Basslink interconnector unplanned outage at 14:09 hrs on 16 August 2025.

title: Eraring Power Station in the news, again … might stay open (even) longer
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Eraring Power Station in the news, again … might stay open (even) longer" posted at 15-08-2025 06:31:00 PM is: Origin Energy’s annual results and investor presentation rekindle discussion about extending the operation of the Eraring coal-fired power station beyond its previously slated closure on 19 August 2025, highlighting the deep uncertainties in Australia’s energy transition, a slowdown in clean energy investment, and the reliance on government-backed capacity schemes amid concerns over supply reliability and market stability.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: coal-fired policies.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing and future (from August 2025 onward, pending final decision).  
Its root_cause is: uncertainties in coal plant retirement timelines, lack of sufficient replacement generation, and policy/regulatory gaps leading to an investment drought.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-08-2025 (post date), 14-08-2025 (Origin annual results release), 19-08-2025 (original closure date).  
Its affected region is: Australia, primarily New South Wales (Eraring Power Station).  
Its affected key users is: electricity consumers, energy retailers, grid operators (AEMO), and large industrial users.  
Its cause type is: policy/regulatory/economic.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Origin Energy’s annual report signals potential extension of Eraring’s operation; (2) previous closure date of 19 August 2025 approaches; (3) slow large‑scale renewable and storage investment (Clean Energy Council report); (4) government Capacity Investment Scheme influencing decisions; (5) Nelson Review recommendations on energy market reform; (6) historical operational incidents (e.g., 2016 planned outage, 2023 low system strength, 2024 simultaneous unit trips).

title: Submissions due today (Thu 14th Aug 2025) on the AEMC draft report ‘Review of WDRM’
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022-04-14 expand eligibility WDRM rule change request
classified_content: The summary of the news "Submissions due today (Thu 14th Aug 2025) on the AEMC draft report ‘Review of WDRM’" posted at 14-08-2025 08:55:00 AM is: The article reports that the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) released a draft report on 10 July 2025 reviewing the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism (WDRM), with two draft recommendations: to continue operating the WDRM and to initiate a rule change to expand eligibility. Submissions on the draft report were due on 14 August 2025. The author expresses skepticism about the scheme's effectiveness, noting that despite a recent 33% increase in registered capacity to 92MW, the WDRM has delivered minimal results over four years while incurring costs and introducing a permanent Tripwire #3 in AEMO’s Market Management System. The article questions whether the expansion is a genuine improvement or a superficial boost to make the program appear successful.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant).; Its category is: Energy policy.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future policy implementation (no immediate market impact).; Its root_cause is: AEMC draft report and rule change request to expand WDRM eligibility.; Its accident scale is: Unknown.; Its dates is: 14-08-2025.; Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market).; Its affected key users is: Wholesale demand response participants and potentially electricity consumers.; Its cause type is: Policy review.; Its causes is: AEMC draft report recommending WDRM continuation and eligibility expansion, with submissions closing on 14 August 2025.

title: Turbine availability at Bulgana wind farm
author: Linton Corbet
topic: 2025-05-28 turbine fire at Bulgana Wind Farm
classified_content: The summary of the news "Turbine availability at Bulgana wind farm" posted at 13-08-2025 09:39:00 AM is: A fire in a turbine nacelle at Victoria's Bulgana Wind Farm on 28 May 2025 caused a full site shutdown, with elements available dropping to zero. Gradual recovery began on 8 June, and typical availability above 45 elements was not regained until 27 June. By August 2025, availability fluctuated around 40-45 of 56 turbines, and after the acquisition by HMC Capital on 4 August, 51 turbines were operational with the rest expected to return within two months. The event highlighted generator outage transparency via AEMO's ELAV dataset.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: turbine/generator failures.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 28 May 2025 onward, with full shutdown lasting about two weeks and reduced availability persisting for months, still not fully resolved as of August 2025.; Its root_cause is: Fire in the nacelle of one turbine (exact cause not specified).; Its accident scale is: One turbine fire and blade loss, entire 204 MW wind farm temporarily inoperable, with partial recovery ongoing.; Its dates is: 28-05-2025 (event date), news posted 13-08-2025.; Its affected region is: Bulgana Wind Farm, Victoria, Australia.; Its affected key users is: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), electricity market participants, and consumers relying on renewable generation; also HMC Capital as new owner.; Its cause type is: Technical failure (fire).; Its causes is: Fire in nacelle; blade fell; exact cause unknown.

title: Submissions closing tomorrow (Wed 13th Aug 2025) to AEMO consultation on ‘Primary Frequency Response Test Guidelines for Semi-Scheduled Plant’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Submissions closing tomorrow (Wed 13th Aug 2025) to AEMO consultation on ‘Primary Frequency Response Test Guidelines for Semi-Scheduled Plant’" posted at 12-08-2025 03:55:00 PM is: The article reminds that submissions to AEMO's consultation on Primary Frequency Response Test Guidelines for Semi-Scheduled Plant close on 13 August 2025, highlighting that draft report and guidelines were released in early July 2025 and many may have missed the deadline. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (post-consultation implementation). Its root_cause is: AEMO's initiative to define standardised test procedures for primary frequency response from semi-scheduled generators to ensure grid reliability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 12-08-2025 (post date), submission deadline 13-08-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled power plant operators, AEMO, and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory process. Its causes is: AEMO released draft Primary Frequency Response Test Guidelines; consultation period ending with deadline for submissions on 13 August 2025.

title: Basslink flows over the past 18 days
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Basslink flows over the past 18 days" posted at 12-08-2025 03:35:00 PM is: The article reports that Basslink interconnector flows have shifted following the end of an agreement on 30 June 2025, leading to the rare dispatch of the Tamar Valley CCGT gas plant for the first time in a year. A chart from ez2view’s Trends Engine illustrates the recent trend of Basslink flow and price differential over the past 18 days, reflecting the market impact of the contractual change.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).; Its category is: market reactions.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 30 June 2025 onwards, with notable changes observed in the 18-day period prior to 12 August 2025.; Its root_cause is: End of the Basslink agreement on 30 June 2025.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident).; Its dates are: 30 June 2025 (agreement end) and 25 July – 11 August 2025 (observed flows).; Its affected region is: Tasmania and Victoria (Basslink interconnection region of the National Electricity Market).; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants including generators, retailers, and consumers in Tasmania and Victoria.; Its cause type is: Contractual/regulatory.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Expiration of the Basslink flow agreement on 30 June 2025. 2. Subsequent shift in interconnector flows and price differentials. 3. Resulting dispatch of Tamar Valley CCGT for the first time in a year.

title: Tamar Valley CCGT gets a run (late Monday 11th August 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tamar Valley CCGT gets a run (late Monday 11th August 2025)" posted at 12-08-2025 09:03:00 AM is: The Tamar Valley CCGT unit (TVCC201) in Tasmania started up on Monday 11 August 2025 at 21:21 NEM time for the first time since a period of operation in June–August 2024, a rare event driven partly by the extended outage of Reece Unit 2 (a hydro generator) and other market factors; the unit is expected to be available until at least 18 August 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 11 August 2025 to 18 August 2025. Its root_cause is: Extended outage of Reece Unit 2 (hydro generator) combined with other market factors. Its accident scale is: Single unit outage (Reece Unit 2). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 11-08-2025. Its affected region is: Tasmania, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generator failure/outage. Its causes is: Extended outage of Reece Unit 2; Hydro Tasmania's earlier planned run of Tamar Valley CCGT in July 2025 did not materialize, but current startup is partly attributed to the hydro outage.

title: Commencement of (testing) operations at Hunter (Kurri Kurri) GT in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Commencement of (testing) operations at Hunter (Kurri Kurri) GT in NSW" posted at 11-08-2025 08:44:00 AM is: The article reports that the Hunter Power Project (Kurri Kurri) in NSW, a 660 MW open-cycle gas turbine plant built by Snowy Hydro, began testing operations with its first unit (HUNTER1) observed generating power on 27 July 2025, bidding down to –$1,000/MWh during overnight test runs. The second unit (HUNTER2) had not yet started as of the report. The testing phase follows a first fire milestone on 7 July 2025 and gas testing from 21 July 2025, with full operations expected within months after commissioning. The project, initially announced in 2021, is designed to provide fast-start on-demand capacity to the National Electricity Market (NEM), with an extended timeline due to gas pipeline delays. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from July 2025 (short to medium term as testing progresses to full commercial operation). Its root_cause is: Commissioning of a new gas-fired power station to increase generation capacity in NSW. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 27 July 2025 (first observed output). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (part of the NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in NSW and the broader NEM. Its cause type is: Planned commissioning. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Completion of construction, first fire on 7 July 2025, gas testing recommencement on 21 July 2025, and first observed output on 27 July 2025.

title: Reece Unit 1 returns to service after outage (coincident with fire onsite) … but Unit 2 outage lengthens
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reece Unit 1 returns to service after outage (coincident with fire onsite) … but Unit 2 outage lengthens" posted at 09-08-2025 12:33:00 PM is: Reece Power Station’s Unit 1, which experienced a fire and subsequent outage, returned to full service on the evening of 8 August 2025, while Unit 2’s forced outage was extended to a partial return of 80 MW on 8 November 2025, with full restoration not expected until 2027, causing a short-lived forecast LOR1 in Tasmania that was later rescinded. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from early August 2025 to at least November 2025 (and possibly beyond for full availability). Its root_cause is: fire onsite at the hydro station and subsequent equipment damage or failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: August 2025 (Unit 1 outage ended 8 August; Unit 2 outage extended from earlier dates to 8 November 2025). Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS region) of the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Hydro Tasmania (operator), AEMO (market operator), and Tasmanian electricity consumers. Its cause type is: forced outage. Its causes is: fire at Reece Power Station coincident with Unit 1 trip; equipment issues leading to extended unavailability of Unit 2.

title: Recapping operations at Waratah BESS (including enablement for SIPS) … to August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping operations at Waratah BESS (including enablement for SIPS) … to August 2025" posted at 08-08-2025 12:31:00 PM is: The article recaps the operational history of the Waratah Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) up to August 2025, focusing on the enablement of the System Integrity Protection System (SIPS) by TransGrid on 1 August 2025, which allows the battery to provide grid support under System Normal conditions to facilitate more variable renewable energy generation. It also notes that the battery’s peak discharge to date was 369 MW (under half its 850 MW capacity), and discusses project delays including a March 2025 adjustment proposal due to delayed commercial operation, the June 2025 Chapter 11 bankruptcy of battery supplier Powin, and the context of Eraring Power Station’s delayed closure. Its relevance level is: 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 1 August 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: need to enhance grid stability and accommodate higher variable renewable energy (VRE) penetration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 01-08-2025 (SIPS enablement), 08-08-2025 (article post date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: NEM operators, TransGrid, Akaysha Energy, and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: operational commissioning and grid support enhancement. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: project commissioning delays, Powin bankruptcy, transformer issues (alluded to in article), and the need to manage grid stability risks from increasing VRE generation.

title: Would it even be possible – to operate the NEM to achieve 82% renewable energy in 2030 (if the Semi-Scheduled category continues in its current form)?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Would it even be possible – to operate the NEM to achieve 82% renewable energy in 2030 (if the Semi-Scheduled category continues in its current form)?" posted at 07-08-2025 10:52:00 AM is: The article questions the sustainability and scalability of the Semi-Scheduled category in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM) as a pathway to 82% renewable energy by 2030, highlighting growing concerns about the category’s design—where units are loosely bound to their dispatch targets—leading to increasing aggregate raw off-target (overs and unders) that must be compensated by scheduled resources, particularly for frequency control. The author cites previous analyses (GenInsights21, GRC2018) showing a trend of frequency disruptions from collective VRE underperformance, and notes that while the Nelson Review draft report assumes semi-scheduled units pose no problem, the author warns of potential grid stability challenges at certain times, especially as the share of semi-scheduled energy far exceeds scheduled resources by 2050. The piece calls for deeper analysis, possibly suggesting restricting new entries to the semi-scheduled category or requiring full hybrid registration. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (2030 and beyond), with ongoing gradual degradation of grid stability and frequency performance. Its root_cause is: The design of the Semi-Scheduled category that allows units to deviate from their dispatch targets except during Semi-Dispatch Cap periods, incentivizing inaccurate self-forecasting and collective underperformance, thereby increasing reliance on scheduled resources for frequency regulation. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported; systematic operational risk). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-08-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), semi-scheduled VRE generators (wind and solar), scheduled generators providing frequency control, market participants, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Regulatory/operational design issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 
1. Semi-Scheduled units are not required to strictly follow their UIGF target except during Semi-Dispatch Cap periods, leading to loose compliance.
2. Self-forecasters can game the system, further increasing deviations.
3. Growing penetration of variable renewable energy (VRE) under the semi-scheduled category without corresponding firming or strict dispatch obligations.
4. Collective underperformance of semi-scheduled units causes frequency disruptions that must be counteracted by scheduled resources via Regulation FCAS.

title: Possible Fire at Reece Power Station – units out of service
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Possible Fire at Reece Power Station – units out of service" posted at 05-08-2025 01:23:00 PM is: A suspected fire at Tasmania’s Reece Power Station has forced two hydro units offline—Unit 1 is expected to return by 8 August 2025, but Unit 2 is out until at least 16 September 2025—prompting AEMO to declare a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) for the Tasmanian region on 8 August 2025, with Hydro Tasmania yet to provide official updates. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures & extreme weather (fire). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 3 August 2025 (initial alerts) through at least 16 September 2025 (full return of Unit 2). Its root_cause is: suspected fire at the power station. Its accident scale is: two generating units forced out of service, one for ~5 days (Unit 1) and one for ~42 days (Unit 2). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 3–4 August 2025 (event occurred). Its affected region is: Tasmania, Australia (TAS region). Its affected key users is: AEMO (market operator), Hydro Tasmania (plant owner/operator), and Tasmanian electricity consumers. Its cause type is: fire (incident). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Possible fire at or near Reece Power Station on 3 August 2025; Unit 2 tripped at 19:38:33 on that day; Unit 1 tripped about an hour later at 20:43:45; both units subsequently declared forced outages.

title: Catching up, after ~5 weeks away … what (else) did I miss?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Catching up, after ~5 weeks away … what (else) did I miss?" posted at 05-08-2025 12:19:00 PM is: The article is a personal recap from a WattClarity analyst returning from leave, covering key National Electricity Market (NEM) developments during June–August 2025, including the Clean Energy Council's Australian Clean Energy Summit (ACES), massive transmission cost blowouts (VNI West, Marinus Link), record gas demand for power generation, coal closure pressures (Yallourn, Eraring), a boom in battery storage investments, market volatility, and ongoing challenges in renewable integration and grid stability; the author poses several questions to readers on technical and policy aspects. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from June 2025 to present (August 2025). Its root_cause is: Insufficient transmission and firming capacity to support rapid renewable rollout and coal retirements, exacerbated by policy uncertainty and rising costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accidents reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-08-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) – primarily New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and industrial electricity consumers, especially large energy users (smelters: Tomago, Nyrstar, Whyalla, Liberty Bell Bay). Its cause type is: Infrastructure and Policy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: VNI West cost doubling and delay, Marinus Link FID, Basslink regulatory transition, Yallourn closure pressure, Eraring re-commitment to 2027, record gas demand for power generation, NEM-wide wind output record, battery FID surge (e.g., Tomago), Waratah BESS activation, and rising transmission costs.

title: Four answers we received during the 2025 Clean Energy Summit in Sydney this week
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four answers we received during the 2025 Clean Energy Summit in Sydney this week" posted at 01-08-2025 03:00:00 PM is: The article reviews key takeaways from the 2025 Clean Energy Summit in Sydney, addressing four pre-summit questions. It notes a gradual shift in the clean energy compass, with AEMO CEO Daniel Westerman acknowledging gas's ongoing role for reliability, while Ross Garnaut warned that the energy transition is "sick" and called for a carbon price. A proposed contract warehousing mechanism to address the 'tenor gap' for new investments is being developed by a panel. Data centre load in the NEM is projected at 3–4 GW new capacity and 12–15 TWh annual consumption by 2030, driven mainly by AI inferencing, though demand response remains marginal. System stability, particularly system strength and oscillatory risks, emerged as a recurring concern, with ACEREZ's Kate Summers highlighting that stability must be treated as a public good and noting the Iberian Peninsula blackout as a cautionary example. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Not applicable (conference discussion, no direct market event). Its root_cause is: Not applicable. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Unknown.

title: Frequency performance payments 44 days in, part 2
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency performance payments 44 days in, part 2" posted at 01-08-2025 01:11:00 PM is: This article provides a detailed breakdown of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) and regulation costs for individual fuel types and units across the National Electricity Market (NEM) regions over a 44-day period (June–July 2025). Solar units in Queensland and elsewhere incur the highest relative costs as a percentage of spot energy market revenue, while battery storage, hydro, and coal units generally see net positive PFR payments. Specific unit-level analysis highlights that batteries dominate FPP payments, though some underperform during commissioning; coal units cluster near zero net cost; and wind units in Tasmania face proportionally high costs due to local FCAS requirements. The analysis normalises costs by estimated spot revenue and excludes units in commissioning or with minimal generation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June–July 2025 (ongoing as the FPP mechanism operates). Its root_cause is: Introduction and operation of the Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) mechanism by AEMO, combined with unit-level operational characteristics (e.g., intermittent generation, commissioning tests). Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-08-2025. Its affected region is: Australian NEM regions (NSW, QLD, SA, TAS, VIC). Its affected key users is: Generators of various fuel types (solar, wind, battery, coal, gas, hydro, diesel) in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market mechanism design and operational factors. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Implementation of FPP cost and payment allocations; unit testing and commissioning activities (e.g., Waratah battery, Dalrymple North battery); intermittent operation of solar, wind, and fast-start units; high local FCAS requirements in Tasmania; and data processing exclusions for units with negative estimated revenue or low generation.

title: CSIRO releases the 2024-25 GenCost Final Report on Tuesday 29th July 2025
author: Christian Vandersee
classified_content: The summary of the news "CSIRO releases the 2024-25 GenCost Final Report on Tuesday 29th July 2025" posted at 30-07-2025 02:32:00 PM is: The CSIRO, in collaboration with AEMO, released the 2024-25 GenCost Final Report on July 29, 2025, providing long-run cost comparisons for various electricity generation technologies up to 2050. The report features Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) metrics while acknowledging its limitations, and includes capital cost trends over three years. Media coverage highlights divergent interpretations, with some outlets emphasizing that firmed renewables remain lowest-cost, while others claim coal is competitive or that nuclear/offshore wind costs are higher than expected.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant); Its category is: Energy policy; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (cost projections to 2050, no immediate market disruption); Its root_cause is: Not applicable (a scheduled report, not a causal event); Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Report release: 29-07-2025, News posted: 30-07-2025; Its affected region is: Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Unknown; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Lower 1-second FCAS volatility continues in SA on Sunday 27th July 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Lower 1-second FCAS volatility continues in SA on Sunday 27th July 2025" posted at 28-07-2025 11:11:00 AM is: The article reports that the Tailem Bend to Tungkillo transmission line outage has caused continued volatility in the 1-second FCAS market in South Australia, with prices reaching the market cap on Sunday 27th July 2025. The constraint set S-TBTU managing the outage remains in effect until August 10th (with a brief intermission on the night of the 3rd), indicating that the drivers of FCAS volatility will persist. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity market participants (generators and consumers exposed to FCAS costs). Its cause type is: transmission line outage. Its causes is: outage of the Tailem Bend to Tungkillo line requiring local procurement of contingency FCAS.

title: NEM-wide wind generation exceeds 10GW for the first time, on Friday evening 25th July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide wind generation exceeds 10GW for the first time, on Friday evening 25th July 2025" posted at 28-07-2025 11:09:00 AM is: On Friday 25 July 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia achieved a new all-time high for wind generation, reaching 10,159 MW during the 21:10 dispatch interval, surpassing the previous record of 9,491 MW set on 23 June 2025, reflecting favorable wind conditions across the region. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate/short-term (evening of 25 July 2025, hourly dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: sustained strong wind speeds across multiple NEM regions. Its accident scale is: not applicable (positive milestone, no accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: event date 25-07-2025, news date 28-07-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (eastern and southern Australia: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania, South Australia). Its affected key users is: NEM electricity consumers, market operators, and generators. Its cause type is: meteorological/natural. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: favorable wind conditions across the NEM leading to record high wind turbine output.

title: Basslink flows shift since end of agreement on 30th June 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Basslink flows shift since end of agreement on 30th June 2025" posted at 25-07-2025 02:11:00 PM is: The expiration of the network services agreement between APA Group and Hydro Tasmania on 30 June 2025 led APA to regain control of Basslink’s bidding strategy, shifting from a zero‑cost bid to higher price bands (initially $10/MWh). This change has reduced northward flows, increased zero‑flow periods, and raised concerns about higher frequency control costs and grid instability in Tasmania and Victoria. Impacts are expected until potential regulation in July 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1 July 2025 onward, with potential regulation in July 2026. Its root_cause is: Expiration of the network services agreement between APA Group and Hydro Tasmania on 30 June 2025. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-07-2025 (post date), 01-07-2025 (event effective start). Its affected region is: Tasmania and Victoria (Basslink interconnector). Its affected key users are: APA Group, Hydro Tasmania, Tasmanian and Victorian electricity consumers, FCAS market participants. Its cause type is: Contractual/regulatory change. Its causes are: (1) End of network services agreement between APA and Hydro Tasmania; (2) APA’s adoption of higher bid prices for Basslink capacity.

title: Four questions we’re pondering ahead of the 2025 Clean Energy Summit in Sydney next week
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four questions we’re pondering ahead of the 2025 Clean Energy Summit in Sydney next week" posted at 25-07-2025 06:51:00 AM is: The article previews four key topics for the upcoming Clean Energy Council (CEC) Australian Clean Energy Summit, including the CEC's recent shift to a more pragmatic stance supporting gas in the short to mid-term, the progress of the Nelson Review addressing the 'tenor gap' in market contracting, the potential impact of surging data centre demand on the National Electricity Market (NEM) illustrated by PJM's record-high capacity auction, and evolving market risks and uncertainties such as investment in gas infrastructure and battery storage. The piece serves as a forward-looking discussion on policy and market design rather than reporting a discrete event affecting electricity prices directly. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future (ongoing through 2025 and beyond; recommendations expected end of 2025). Its root_cause is: Evolving energy transition policies and growing demand from data centres. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-07-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) and referenced PJM (US). Its affected key users are: Market participants, policy makers, data centre operators, generators, retailers. Its cause type is: Policy and market trend analysis. Its causes are: Departures of CEC executives leading to a pro-gas advocacy shift; Nelson Review identifying the tenor gap as a key problem; PJM capacity auction clearing at record levels due to data centre demand; growing speculation about data centre demand wave in the NEM.

title: Volatility within the contingency FCAS market in SA today, Thursday 24th July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Volatility within the contingency FCAS market in SA today, Thursday 24th July 2025" posted at 24-07-2025 is: On Thursday, 24 July 2025, the contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) market in South Australia experienced extreme volatility, with the 'Lower 1 Second' commodity price flickering between $0/MWh and the market price cap of $20,300/MWh from around 11:00am. This instability was triggered by an outage on the 275kV Tailem Bend-Tungkillo transmission line, which forms part of the SA-VIC interconnector, leading to the invocation of the 'S-TBTU' constraint set. The constraint set limits FCAS procurement to within South Australia (west of the outage) to manage system security risks, and is scheduled to alternate with another constraint set over the next four days, with an extended period from 28 July to 10 August. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (hours to days, ongoing for several days). Its root_cause is: Outage on the 275kV Tailem Bend-Tungkillo line. Its accident scale is: Transmission line outage (single circuit, no generation loss). Its dates is: 24-07-2025 (news date) and additional scheduled periods until 10 August 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users is: FCAS market participants, electricity market operators, and potentially end consumers via price impacts. Its cause type is: Infrastructure outage (transmission line). Its causes is: [Outage on the 275kV Tailem Bend-Tungkillo line, Invocation of the 'S-TBTU' constraint set limiting FCAS procurement].

title: Frequency Performance Payments 44 days in
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency Performance Payments 44 days in" posted at 23-07-2025 03:19:00 PM is: The article reviews the first 44 days of financial operation of the Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) arrangements, which started on 8 June 2025 and cover mechanisms for recovering regulation FCAS costs and rewarding primary frequency response (PFR). It highlights that frequency-raise needs dominate, with the highest cost event on 12 June 2025, and notes that default contribution factors and residual costs (largely from retail customers) play material roles, while mainland PFR costs exceed regulation recovery costs and Tasmanian regulation costs show higher volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since 8 June 2025, with initial 44-day period ending around 22 July 2025. Its root_cause is: Implementation of FPP financial operation to improve frequency response and allocate costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; market mechanism change). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 8 June 2025 to 22 July 2025 (main date is 23-07-2025). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – mainland regions and Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Generators (including wind, solar, batteries), retail customers (via residual), and other participants. Its cause type is: Market mechanism implementation. Its causes is: Start of financial operation of Frequency Performance Payments on 8 June 2025, leading to cost allocations for regulation FCAS and PFR payments based on unit contributions and default factors.

title: Suspect SCADA drove data discrepancy on 9th December 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Suspect SCADA drove data discrepancy on 9th December 2024" posted at 23-07-2025 12:22:00 PM is: A SCADA data glitch on 9 December 2024 caused a suspect flag for the Guthega unit, leading to incorrect NEM demand calculation in Victoria, which in turn produced erroneous interconnector flows and dispatch outcomes, with a total demand discrepancy of 6,125 MWh and a peak interconnector flow deviation of 2,850 MW.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: During the event from 1325 hrs to 1450 hrs on 9 December 2024, with immediate operational impacts.  
Its root_cause is: SCADA data for the Guthega unit was flagged as suspect, causing the system to incorrectly calculate NEM demand.  
Its accident scale is: Total demand difference of approximately 6,125 MWh; interconnector flow target deviation of 2,850 MW.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 09-12-2024.  
Its affected region is: Victoria (Market Demand) and the VNI interconnector (Victoria to New South Wales).  
Its affected key users are: All market participants relying on accurate dispatch and interconnector flows, including generators, retailers, and system operators.  
Its cause type is: Technical/IT (SCADA data issue).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Suspect SCADA data for the Guthega unit; incorrect NEM demand calculation; resulting erroneous interconnector flows and dispatch outcomes.

title: A quick view into constraints on the Heywood interconnector, on Tuesday 22nd July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick view into constraints on the Heywood interconnector, on Tuesday 22nd July 2025" posted at 22-07-2025 09:39:00 AM is: This article analyzes binding constraints on the Heywood interconnector caused by a planned outage on the 275kV Heywood-South East transmission line, leading to regional price adjustments. The 'SA_ISLE_STRENGTH' equation limits non-synchronous generation in South Australia to 1,900MW to maintain system strength, negatively pricing semi-scheduled wind and solar units, while the 'V::N_HYSE_V1' equation prevents transient instability in Victoria, giving positive price adjustments to many units. Additional generator outages include Torrens Island B4 (210MW) on planned outage and Pelican Point Gas Turbine (522MW) on planned derating. The 'I-HYSE' constraint set is scheduled until tomorrow afternoon, posing risks of elevated energy and FCAS prices.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 21-23 July 2025 (short-term, planned outage period); Its root_cause is: Planned outage on the Heywood-South East 275kV transmission line; Its accident scale is: Planned outage (not an accident); Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-23 July 2025; Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria; Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled wind and solar units in SA, units in Victoria receiving price adjustments, and generators Torrens Island B4 and Pelican Point; Its cause type is: Planned transmission outage and scheduled generator outages/deratings; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Planned outage on Heywood-South East 275kV line. 2. Planned outage of Torrens Island B4 unit (210MW). 3. Planned derating of Pelican Point Gas Turbine (522MW).

title: Final incident report on the power system incident of 13th February 2024 released
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Final incident report on the power system incident of 13th February 2024 released" posted at 21-07-2025 03:35:00 PM is: The final incident report on the 13 February 2024 Victorian power system event details how extreme winds up to 216 km/h collapsed 500 kV transmission towers, leading to non‑credible three‑phase faults during auto‑reclosure attempts. These faults caused voltage disturbances that triggered coal feeder conveyor system restarts at Loy Yang A, resulting in a master fuel trip and loss of 2,210 MW from four units (total 2,507 MW generation lost). Additionally, about 424 MW of distributed PV was disconnected, leading to an 870 MW net drop in operational demand. The report recommends that all coal‑fired stations review conveyor settings and that synchronous generators examine auxiliary plant ride‑through capabilities. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13 February 2024, with immediate generation loss and demand reduction lasting several hours, and subsequent market adjustments. Its root_cause is: Extreme winds caused transmission tower collapse, which led to system faults and voltage disturbances that disrupted coal conveyor systems at Loy Yang A. Its accident scale is: Loss of 2,507 MW of generation (2,210 MW from Loy Yang A), 870 MW net drop in operational demand. Its dates is: 13 February 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (within the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AGL (Loy Yang A operator), AusNet (transmission owner), Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), distributed PV owners, and electricity consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (extreme weather) leading to technical cascade failure. Its causes is: 1. Extreme wind gusts (150–216 km/h) collapsed transmission towers; 2. Auto‑reclosure attempts caused non‑credible three‑phase faults; 3. Voltage dips propagated to Loy Yang A; 4. Coal feeder conveyor systems restarted due to voltage dips, reducing fuel supply; 5. Loss of furnace pressure triggered master fuel trip (MFT); 6. Generating units ran back and tripped on reverse power; 7. Distributed PV inverters disconnected.

title: AEMO issue market notice regarding a ‘High Impact Outage’ in NSW this Sunday 20th July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO issue market notice regarding a ‘High Impact Outage’ in NSW this Sunday 20th July 2025" posted at 18-07-2025 08:46:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 18 July 2025 for a planned high impact outage of the Dapto–Kangaroo Valley 330 kV transmission line in NSW on 20 July 2025 from 06:15 to 16:30, warning of potential energy and FCAS prices near the Market Price Cap and restricted power transfer between VIC/Snowy and NSW; Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 20 July 2025, 06:15 to 16:30 NEM time; Its root_cause is: Planned outage of Dapto - Kangaroo Valley 330 kV transmission line; Its accident scale is: High Impact Outage (HIO) as designated by AEMO; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-07-2025; Its affected region is: New South Wales, specifically the transmission corridor between VIC/Snowy and NSW; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in NSW and potentially VIC due to transfer restrictions; Its cause type is: Planned transmission outage; Its causes is: Planned maintenance outage of a 330 kV line between Dapto and Kangaroo Valley.

title: To what extent does the Capacity Investment Scheme cover curtailment risk, and how to interpret its ‘force majeure’ terms?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "To what extent does the Capacity Investment Scheme cover curtailment risk, and how to interpret its ‘force majeure’ terms?" posted at 17-07-2025 is: The article examines the Australian Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS), focusing on whether its revenue floor mechanism protects projects from curtailment risk, comparing Tender #1 and Tender #4 documents, and highlighting ambiguity in force majeure wording that may shift risk to taxpayers; it poses interpretive questions about the coverage of curtailment and congestion. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (ongoing policy design). Its root_cause is: Ambiguity and changes in force majeure and curtailment definition between CIS tenders, and difficulty in forecasting curtailment. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17-07-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) and Western Australia (WEM). Its affected key users is: CIS project operators, taxpayers, government (DCCEEW). Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory ambiguity. Its causes is: Change in force majeure generation definition between Tender #1 and Tender #4; ambiguous wording regarding curtailment as force majeure; difficulty in measuring curtailment attribution; potential for shifting volume risk to government.

title: Transgrid’s Project Assessment Conclusions Report for System Strength released on Monday 14th July 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transgrid’s Project Assessment Conclusions Report for System Strength released on Monday 14th July 2025" posted at 16-07-2025 11:54:00 AM is: Transgrid released a Project Assessment Conclusions Report evaluating over 100 solutions to meet system strength obligations under the National Electricity Rules, focusing on portfolio options that include new synchronous condensers, grid-forming battery energy storage systems (BESS), and increased re-dispatch of gas, hydro, and some coal generators until synchronous condensers become available. Portfolio option 2, which accelerates one synchronous condenser to 2029/30, was identified as optimal for resilience and net market benefits, while additional options (e.g., assuming 2027/28 availability) were deemed not credible due to procurement lead times. The report follows a similar Queensland release and will be briefed on 18 July 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long term, with obligations starting 2 December 2025 and key infrastructure additions through 2031/32. Its root_cause is: Transgrid must comply with AEMO’s system strength standards (clause S5.1.14) to maintain grid stability in the NSW power system. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (planning and regulatory assessment). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 14-07-2025 (report release date, different from post date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (with implications for the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers (via market benefits) and generators (especially gas, hydro, and coal units subject to increased re-dispatch). Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance driven by grid stability requirements. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Need to meet AEMO’s system strength standards from 2 December 2025; procurement lead times for synchronous condensers prevent earlier availability; reliance on re-dispatch of synchronous generators (gas, hydro, coal) as interim solution.
