title: Review of the System Restart Standard 2025 – Technical advice provided
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Review of the System Restart Standard 2025 – Technical advice provided" posted at 14-07-2025 12:02:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) is reviewing the System Restart Standard (the Standard) following its commencement on December 12, 2024, with technical advice from the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) published in June 2025. The advice highlights growing uncertainties for system restoration due to the energy transition, including a limited pool of System Restart Ancillary Services (SRAS) providers, lack of system strength for inverter-based resources (IBR), increasing impacts from distributed photovoltaic (PV) systems, and evolving generation mixes. These challenges, informed by AEMO’s 2025 draft General Power System Risk Review (GPSRR), aim to support rapid recovery after major supply disruptions, with the review expected to conclude by end of 2025. Stakeholder submissions from network service providers, the Clean Energy Council, generators, Tomago aluminium smelter, and energy user groups have been considered.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1  
Its category is: grid/system instability  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from December 2024 to end of 2025, with potential long-term implications for system restoration reliability and procurement of SRAS  
Its root_cause is: Energy transition leading to limited SRAS providers, lack of system strength for inverter-based resources, growing distributed PV impacts, and changing generation mix  
Its accident scale is: Unknown  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Review started 12-12-2024, technical advice published June 2025, news posted 14-07-2025  
Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia  
Its affected key users is: AEMO, network service providers (NSPs), generators, large industrial users (e.g., Tomago aluminium smelter), Energy Users Association of Australia, Australian Aluminum Council, Clean Energy Council  
Its cause type is: Systemic and technical challenges from energy transition  
Its causes is: [Limited pool of SRAS providers in some NEM regions; Lack of system strength preventing most inverter-based resources from participating in early stages of restoration; Risk of distributed PV impacts on system restart; Changing generation mix altering system restart paths across NEM regions]

title: AEMO releases the 2025 Enhanced Locational Information (ELI) report on Wednesday 9th July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases the 2025 Enhanced Locational Information (ELI) report on Wednesday 9th July 2025" posted at 10-07-2025 06:16:00 PM is: AEMO published the 2025 ELI report to help investors identify optimal locations for renewable and firming projects, highlighting that network congestion, curtailment, and energy losses vary by region, with media coverage focusing on rising solar curtailment due to grid logjams and transmission delays. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and future – the report forecasts increased curtailment and informs long-term investment decisions, with existing congestion already affecting market operations. Its root_cause is: Inadequate transmission infrastructure leading to network congestion and forced solar farm curtailment. Its accident scale is: Significant – solar PV curtailment exceeded 25% in 2024, affecting multiple regions in the NEM. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-07-2025 (report release date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Solar farms, renewable energy investors, transmission network owners, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and planning – transmission constraints and delays in network expansion. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Network congestion from insufficient poles and wires. 2. Delays in transmission infrastructure upgrades. 3. High solar penetration exceeding local grid capacity. 4. Lack of adequate firming and storage to absorb excess generation.

title: A look at forecasts for SA this Wednesday evening 9th July 2025, where forecast LOR2 conditions are appearing
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A look at forecasts for SA this Wednesday evening 9th July 2025, where forecast LOR2 conditions are appearing" posted at 07-07-2025 01:56:00 PM is: This article analyzes the emergence and expansion of a forecast LOR2 (Load-Over-Resource) condition in South Australia for the evening of 9th July 2025, driven by high uncertainty in demand and variable renewable energy (VRE) generation forecasts, as well as scheduled constraint sets due to transmission line outages. The post notes similarities to LOR conditions seen earlier in the week and highlights the impact of weather-related forecast spreads and specific constraint invocations (S-HVMG and S-WE_MWP4) linked to outages on the Happy Valley-Magill 275 kV line and the Morgan Whyalla PS 4-Waterloo East 132 kV line.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 9th July 2025 evening (forecast event), with the news posted on 7th July 2025; Its root_cause is: Weather uncertainty leading to wide forecast ranges for demand and wind/solar generation, combined with transmission line outages triggering constraint sets; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 07-07-2025 (post date) and 09-07-2025 (event date); Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in SA); Its cause type is: Weather and infrastructure (line outages); Its causes is: (1) High forecast uncertainty in market demand, (2) High forecast uncertainty in semi-scheduled wind and solar UIGF, (3) Scheduled constraint sets S-HVMG (due to Happy Valley-Magill 275 kV line outage) and S-WE_MWP4 (due to Morgan Whyalla PS 4-Waterloo East 132 kV line outage).

title: Not all renewable projects are created equal: Why approval times vary dramatically across Australia
author: Alice Matthews
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Not all renewable projects are created equal: Why approval times vary dramatically across Australia" posted at 07-07-2025 11:57:00 AM is: This article analyzes how environmental and planning approval timelines for renewable energy projects in Australia vary significantly by technology and location. Using data from 875 development applications and 641 EPBC applications, it finds that solar farms have the shortest approvals (mean 8 months EPBC, 16 months planning), while onshore wind takes longest (mean 17 months EPBC, 34 months planning), with batteries in between. State-level differences are stark: New South Wales has the slowest approvals (wind planning averaging 47 months), while Queensland and Western Australia offer faster pathways. The analysis highlights that these regulatory variations, combined with evolving state policies, critically affect project development strategies for investors and policymakers in Australia's clean energy transition.

Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant).  
Its category is: Energy policy.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term – the approval timelines affect the pace of renewable capacity additions, which in turn influence future electricity supply and prices over several years.  
Its root_cause is: Differences in environmental complexity, regulatory frameworks, community engagement requirements, and grid connection processes across states and technologies.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-07-2025 11:57:00 AM.  
Its affected region is: Australia (with specific analysis for New South Wales, Queensland, Victoria, South Australia, Western Australia).  
Its affected key users is: Renewable energy developers, investors, and policymakers.  
Its cause type is: Regulatory and administrative processes.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- Environmental approval complexity under the EPBC Act  
- State-specific planning approval regulations and resource allocation  
- Technology-specific challenges (footprint, wildlife studies, community consultation for wind; simpler impacts for solar)  
- Grid connection requirements for battery storage  
- Evolving state policies (e.g., Queensland's new social impact assessments, Victoria's ambitious renewable targets)  
- Historical timing and sample size variations across states.

title: Volatile price period runs over 5 hours on 2 July 2025 in SA
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Volatile price period runs over 5 hours on 2 July 2025 in SA" posted at 03-07-2025 10:01:00 AM is: On the evening of 2 July 2025, the South Australian region experienced a volatile price period lasting 5.5 hours (from 17:30 to 23:05 NEM time), with prices spiking above 14,500 $/MWh and reaching a cumulative price threshold of $1,576,239.12, narrowly avoiding administered pricing. The volatility was primarily driven by a transmission line outage (South East to Tailem Bend 275kV line) that restricted interconnector exports from Victoria, limiting flow to 100-150 MW and forcing reliance on high-cost local generation; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 5.5 hours on the evening of 2 July 2025. Its root_cause is: Outage of one South East to Tailem Bend 275kV line, which constrained interconnector flow and triggered high prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown (single transmission line outage, no major accident reported). Its dates is: 2 July 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in SA. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure (transmission line outage). Its causes is: Outage of a 275kV transmission line, leading to constrained interconnector exports from Victoria to SA, which limited supply and caused extreme price spikes.

title: Evening spot prices less volatile than anticipated in SA on July 2, 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Evening spot prices less volatile than anticipated in SA on July 2, 2025" posted at 02-07-2025 06:30:00 PM is: On the evening of July 2, 2025, South Australian spot electricity prices exhibited less volatility than initially feared, despite low wind availability and cold, clear-sky conditions driving brief spikes above $1,000/MWh for several intervals. The predispatch outlook suggested subdued volatility could persist until 21:30, but the cumulative price threshold breach became less likely. The event underscores the impact of light wind and cold weather on near-term power pricing, with market expectations of high prices not fully materializing.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: Extreme weather and fuel supply issues (light wind conditions).  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of July 2, 2025, with effects forecast until 21:30).  
Its root_cause is: Light wind conditions (low wind availability) combined with cold clear-sky temperatures driving higher demand and reduced renewable supply.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident, but a market event).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-07-2025.  
Its affected region is: South Australia (SA).  
Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, market participants, and grid operators in South Australia.  
Its cause type is: Weather and fuel supply issue.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Light wind conditions causing low wind generation, Cold clear-sky temperatures increasing electricity demand].

title: More than 40,000 customers without power in NSW on Wednesday 2nd July 2025 after wild weather overnight
author: Dan Lee
classified_content:
The summary of the news "More than 40,000 customers without power in NSW on Wednesday 2nd July 2025 after wild weather overnight" posted at 02-07-2025 11:57:00 AM is: A powerful coastal low-pressure system (bomb cyclone) brought damaging winds and torrential rain, causing over 40,000 customers across Ausgrid, Endeavour, and Essential Energy networks in New South Wales to lose power as of 11:30am NEM time on 2 July 2025, with conditions expected to ease from Thursday.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: Extreme weather.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing (outages persisting until winds ease from Thursday, i.e., approximately 1-2 days).  
Its root_cause is: A coastal low-pressure system (bomb cyclone) bringing extreme winds and rain.  
Its accident scale is: More than 40,000 customers without power across three distribution networks.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-07-2025 to 02-07-2025 (overnight storm into 2 July).  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, specifically the Ausgrid, Endeavour Energy, and Essential Energy distribution areas (including South Coast, Mid North Coast, Northern Tablelands).  
Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial customers (general public).  
Its cause type is: Natural disaster (extreme weather event).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Damaging winds from the bomb cyclone; torrential rain; localized flooding and wind damage to power lines and infrastructure.

title: Keep an eye on the SA Cumulative Price this evening on Wednesday 2nd July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keep an eye on the SA Cumulative Price this evening on Wednesday 2nd July 2025" posted at 02-07-2025 is: An analysis of the South Australian electricity market highlights that the cumulative price is approaching the Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) of $1,823,600 after extended bursts of high prices overnight and this morning, with forecasts indicating the threshold will be exceeded this evening. The report notes a downgrade in lack of reserve forecasts from LOR3 to LOR1, and the cumulative price may retreat from Thursday as previous volatility drops off. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 02-07-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity consumers, market participants (AEMO, generators, retailers). Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: [Unknown].

title: NSW records highest winter demand in fifteen years, whilst high prices eventuate in SA on Tuesday evening 1st July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW records highest winter demand in fifteen years, whilst high prices eventuate in SA on Tuesday evening 1st July 2025" posted at 01-07-2025 06:34:00 PM is: Cold conditions drove NSW's electricity demand to a fifteen-year winter high of 13,159 MW on the evening of 1 July 2025, while South Australia experienced elevated spot prices reaching $3,411.15/MWh. The Market Price Cap also rose to $20,300/MWh at the start of the new financial year, though forecasted high-end prices in SA did not fully materialise. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate/ongoing (evening of 1 July 2025). Its root_cause is: Cold weather conditions leading to increased heating demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 01-07-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Weather-related (cold). Its causes is: Cold weather; increased heating demand; possible supply constraints in South Australia.

title: Directions Paper adjusts approach to loop IRSR
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Directions Paper adjusts approach to loop IRSR" posted at 01-07-2025 05:14:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) published a Directions Paper on 19 June 2025 proposing a 'netting off' approach for allocating Inter-regional Settlements Residue (IRSR) in transmission loops, specifically for the upcoming Project Energy Connect (PEC) loop connecting New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria. This new approach supersedes a previous draft rule that was deemed insufficient to mitigate the risk of high negative IRSR, which could expose Coordinating Network Service Providers (CNSPs) to cash flow risks and ultimately increase transmission prices for consumers. The paper seeks feedback by 10 July 2025, with a final determination expected on 25 September 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future, from the implementation of the final determination (expected 25 September 2025) and when PEC Phase 2 completes. Its root_cause is: Introduction of Project Energy Connect transmission loop causing complex IRSR and risk of high negative IRSR, prompting AEMC to revise allocation rules. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19 June 2025 (Directions Paper publication), with other key dates: 12 December 2024 (draft determination), 10 July 2025 (submission deadline), 25 September 2025 (final determination). Its affected region is: New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria. Its affected key users is: Coordinating Network Service Providers (CNSPs), Settlement Residue Distribution (SRD) holders, and consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Policy change. Its causes are: 1) Construction of Project Energy Connect loop; 2) AEMC's draft rule deemed insufficient; 3) Proposal of netting off approach to mitigate negative IRSR.

title: VNI West given an official two year delay, with expected completion date pushed back to late 2030
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "VNI West given an official two year delay, with expected completion date pushed back to late 2030" posted at 01-07-2025 is: The VNI West transmission project in Victoria has been delayed by two years, with completion now expected by late 2030 instead of 2028, due to extended environmental, geotechnical, and cultural assessments, increased landholder engagement, and complex access/easement arrangements, as announced by Transmission Company Victoria. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, from now until at least late 2030, affecting future grid reliability and price expectations. Its root_cause is: Extended environmental, geotechnical, and cultural assessments, along with landholder engagement and access/easement arrangements. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-07-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Landholders, electricity consumers in Victoria, and the broader electricity market relying on the VNI West interconnection. Its cause type is: Regulatory and engagement delays. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Extended environmental assessments, extended geotechnical assessments, extended cultural assessments, increased landholder engagement, complex access/easement arrangements.

title: Market Price Cap increases to $20,300/MWh from today, 1st July 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Price Cap increases to $20,300/MWh from today, 1st July 2025" posted at 01-07-2025 11:45:00 AM is: The National Electricity Market's Market Price Cap (MPC) increased to $20,300/MWh from $17,500/MWh effective July 1, 2025, as the first of three staged increases through the 2027-28 financial year. The Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) also rose to $1,823,600 (equivalent to 7.5 hours of MPC prices), with further increases planned. This adjustment follows the Australian Energy Market Commission's final rule determination from December 2023.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing from 1 July 2025 through to 2027-28 financial year. Its root_cause is: Regulatory rule change by the AEMC to progressively increase price caps. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-07-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: All market participants including generators, retailers, and consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC final rule determination (December 2023) to increase MPC and CPT progressively.

title: Triple C conditions (cold, calm and cloudy) across the NEM last Thursday evening 26th June 2025
author: Carl Daley
classified_content: The summary of the news "Triple C conditions (cold, calm and cloudy) across the NEM last Thursday evening 26th June 2025" posted at 30-06-2025 01:58:00 PM is: On Thursday 26 June 2025, the National Electricity Market experienced a severe crisis driven by Triple C conditions (cold, calm, cloudy), which eliminated wind generation and drove peak heating demand, compounded by five major coal unit outages and interconnector constraints. This led to extreme spot prices exceeding $10,000/MWh in 61 intervals across mainland regions (NSW, VIC, SA, TAS) with daily averages of $1,951-$2,049/MWh, while QLD was partially isolated by southbound constraints. Thermal generation market share rose to 61-93% during extreme pricing, while battery storage output declined due to exhaustion, exacerbating price escalation. The event validated predispatch forecasts, caused significant quarterly price increases (e.g., VIC +22.27 $/MWh), and highlighted system vulnerabilities to adverse weather, ageing coal units, and limited storage duration. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 26 June 2025, with sustained quarterly price effects visible in the days following. Its root_cause is: Triple C conditions (cold, calm, cloudy) combined with five major coal unit outages and southbound interconnector constraints from QLD to NSW. Its accident scale is: Major market crisis with 61 intervals exceeding $10,000/MWh across mainland regions, daily spot price averages of $1,951-$2,049/MWh, and widespread generation disruptions. Its dates is: 26 June 2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland regions (NSW, VIC, SA, TAS) and QLD partially. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, residential and industrial, thermal and renewable generators, battery storage operators, and financial market participants. Its cause type is: Weather-induced supply-demand imbalance. Its causes is: Cold weather driving high heating demand, calm conditions eliminating wind generation, cloudy conditions limiting solar output, five major coal units offline (Bayswater BW02, Eraring ER04, Gladstone GSTONE1/GSTONE3, YWPS3), southbound interconnector constraints limiting power transfer from QLD to NSW at 1,142MW, and battery storage exhaustion during extreme price periods reducing flexible capacity.

title: East coast low brings challenging conditions for start of July in 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "East coast low brings challenging conditions for start of July in 2025" posted at 30-06-2025 12:48:00 PM is: An East Coast Low is forecast to form off the coast of New South Wales, bringing flooding rain, gales, and huge waves, and is expected to drive uncertainty in electricity generation and demand, particularly on 3rd July, with declining wind availability and variable solar output. Additionally, a high pressure system over South Australia will create light winds, further reducing wind generation, and the S-TBSE constraint set is invoked, potentially affecting reserve conditions.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: Extreme weather.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 30 June 2025 to 5 July 2025.  
Its root_cause is: East Coast Low weather system and associated high pressure system over South Australia.  
Its accident scale is: Regional severe weather event (no accident).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 30-06-2025 (main date), with impact extending to 05-07-2025.  
Its affected region is: Eastern New South Wales (coastal) and South Australia.  
Its affected key users are: Electricity generators (solar and wind), grid operators, and market participants.  
Its cause type is: Meteorological (weather).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Formation of an East Coast Low off NSW; stable high pressure system over SA leading to light winds.

title: Evening volatility on Friday 27th June 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility on Friday 27th June 2025" posted at 27-06-2025 07:03:00 PM is: On Friday 27 June 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced evening spot price volatility, with multiple intervals above $1,000/MWh, most frequently in Victoria, although the spikes were less persistent than the previous day (Thursday 26 June 2025). After 18:00, prices fell below $600/MWh. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions.
