title: How low did it go?  NEM-wide wind production in recent days
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How low did it go? NEM-wide wind production in recent days" posted at 27-06-2025 02:08:00 PM is: On Thursday 26 June 2025, NEM-wide wind production dropped to a recent low of 392 MW at the 16:55 dispatch interval, contributing to evening price volatility. This decline followed a record high just days earlier, and the article notes even lower wind outputs earlier in 2025 (132 MW on 17 February and 211 MW on 4 March) due to a combination of low wind conditions, economic curtailment, and network curtailment. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (day-ahead and real-time price volatility on 26 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Low wind conditions combined with potential economic and network curtailment. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (no accident, only low wind generation). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-06-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all market participants and consumers exposed to wholesale prices). Its cause type is: Natural (low wind) and operational (curtailment). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Low wind speed; economic curtailment due to negative prices; network curtailment constraints.

title: Next steps in two big market changes (in terms of transparency)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Next steps in two big market changes (in terms of transparency)" posted at 27-06-2025 09:54:00 AM is: AEMO published Market Notice MN127877 on 27 June 2025 detailing two market changes (Enhancing Reserve Information and STPASA Recall Period) to improve transparency. The changes involve upgrading the NEMDE solver and related applications from 30 June to 4 July 2025, with a brief planned outage for the Bidding application on 30 June. The upgrades become effective from 1 July 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1 July 2025 onwards (ongoing). Its root_cause is: AEMO implementing rule changes to enhance market transparency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 30/06/2025 to 04/07/2025 (outage on 30/06/2025). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, traders). Its cause type is: Planned system upgrade/market rule change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events): AEMO upgrades NEMDE solver and other applications for Dispatch, 5-Minute Predispatch, Predispatch, PD7, Bidding, SOMMS and Registration; a planned outage for Bidding application on 30 June 2025 for 5-7 minutes; effective from 1 July 2025.

title: Past 3 hours of continuous volatility on Thursday evening 26th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Past 3 hours of continuous volatility on Thursday evening 26th June 2025" posted at 26-06-2025 07:57:00 PM is: The NEM experienced sustained volatility starting at 16:45 NEM time on 26 June 2025, with prices remaining elevated through to at least 19:55, and BESS discharge data indicates batteries were actively responding during this period. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 16:45 to at least 19:55 on 26 June 2025 (3+ hours). Its root_cause is: Unknown (not specified in the article). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), presumably Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Evening volatility ongoing (Thursday 26th June 2025), with NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ above 31,000MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility ongoing (Thursday 26th June 2025), with NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ above 31,000MW" posted at 26-06-2025 06:22:00 PM is: On the evening of 26 June 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced ongoing volatility as market demand surged above 31,000 MW, though it fell short of earlier forecasts of up to ~32,500 MW. Prices remained elevated during the 18:20 dispatch interval, reflecting system stress. The event started earlier than typical evening peaks, indicating potential grid/system instability; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 26 June 2025). Its root_cause is: high market demand exceeding typical levels, possibly driven by weather or consumption patterns. Its accident scale is: Not an accident (market event). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 26-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: wholesale electricity market participants and end consumers. Its cause type is: demand-driven. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Higher-than-expected market demand leading to price spikes and volatility; mismatch between actual demand and earlier forecasts.

title: Run of evening volatility starts early, on Thursday 26th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Run of evening volatility starts early, on Thursday 26th June 2025" posted at 26-06-2025 05:00:00 PM is: Cold weather drove market demand higher and wind generation dropped to approximately 400 MW (only ~4% of a recent record), triggering an early run of evening volatility in the southern regions of the NEM, with market demand still climbing at 28,683 MW. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 26 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Cold weather driving high demand and very low wind generation output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2025. Its affected region is: Southern regions of the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: NEM market participants and consumers. Its cause type is: Weather-related generation shortfall. Its causes is: Cold weather increased demand, wind generation dropped to approximately 400 MW (4% capacity factor).

title: AER approves application for Basslink to be converted into a regulated transmission service
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER approves application for Basslink to be converted into a regulated transmission service" posted at 26-06-2025 03:39:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has reversed its earlier draft determination and approved APA Group’s application to convert the Basslink interconnector from a merchant network service provider to a regulated transmission network service. The decision, announced on 26 June 2025, was based on additional stakeholder submissions, expert analysis, and information from APA Group and Hydro Tasmania. The AER will now consult on Basslink’s allowed revenue under the new regulated status, with submissions open until 11 July 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown, pending revenue determination and implementation timeline. Its root_cause is: APA Group’s application and subsequent stakeholder input persuading the AER to approve conversion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-06-2025. Its affected region is: Tasmania and Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in Tasmania and Victoria, as well as generators and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory decision. Its causes is: APA Group’s application to convert Basslink; submission of additional information and expert analysis; stakeholder submissions; AER’s reversal of its draft determination.

title: Cold weather drives electricity ‘Market Demand’ higher (and higher than AEMO’s earlier forecasts)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Cold weather drives electricity ‘Market Demand’ higher (and higher than AEMO’s earlier forecasts)" posted at 26-06-2025 03:19:00 PM is: A polar blast brought extreme cold weather to southeast Australia, driving electricity market demand significantly higher than AEMO's earlier forecasts. Actual demand in the National Electricity Market (NEM) exceeded projections, with Victoria setting a new winter maximum operational demand of 8,818 MW on 25 June 2025. The cold temperatures, wet and windy conditions led to increased heating demand, particularly during evening peaks. AEMO's operational demand data and market demand figures both reflected the surge, with forecasts ratcheting upward as the cold spell progressed.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: Extreme weather.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing over several days (22-26 June 2025).  
Its root_cause is: Polar blast / cold weather.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported; high demand event).  
Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 22-26 June 2025.  
Its affected region is: Southeast Australia (Victoria, eastern NSW, National Electricity Market).  
Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers, grid operators (AEMO).  
Its cause type is: Weather-related.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Cold temperatures from polar blast, wet and windy weather conditions across southeast Australia.

title: Morning volatility in the southern regions – Thursday 26th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Morning volatility in the southern regions – Thursday 26th June 2025" posted at 26-06-2025 08:21:00 AM is: A cooler morning in southern regions drove NEM-wide market demand above 29,500 MW, while gusty winds abated, leading to a bout of volatility on Thursday 26th June 2025.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – morning of 26 June 2025.  
Its root_cause is: Combination of cooler temperatures boosting electricity demand and declining wind generation reducing supply flexibility.  
Its accident scale is: Minor/moderate – localized morning volatility without reported failures.  
Its dates is: 26-06-2025.  
Its affected region is: Southern regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM), likely including South Australia and Victoria.  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all market participants and consumers in the affected region).  
Its cause type is: Weather and operational conditions.  
Its causes is: Cooler morning temperatures increasing market demand; abating gusty wind reducing renewable output; resulting supply-demand imbalance causing price volatility.

title: Opaque Congestion and Hidden Curtailment … such as on Tue 24th June 2024 with the ‘N-DPWG_63_X5’ constraint set invoked
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Opaque Congestion and Hidden Curtailment … such as on Tue 24th June 2024 with the ‘N-DPWG_63_X5’ constraint set invoked" posted at 25-06-2025 06:26:00 PM is: The article examines hidden curtailment and self-forecast issues in the NEM following an unplanned outage of the Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line, which triggered the N-DPWG_63_X5 constraint set and forced several solar farms to zero inverters, causing invisible curtailment that is often misinterpreted as cloud cover, while some self-forecast models ignore the ELAV=0 signal and produce non-zero UIGF, leading to misrepresentation of congestion and local price adjustments; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Several days, from 23 June 2025 (constraint set invoked at 17:25) through 25 June 2025 and ongoing. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown (transmission line outage, but scale not specified). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 23-06-2025 to 25-06-2025 (main news date 25-06-2025). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Solar farm operators (e.g., Sunraysia, Limondale), AEMO, self-forecast modelers, and market analysts. Its cause type is: Transmission line outage (unplanned). Its causes is: Unplanned outage of Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line leading to invocation of constraint set N-DPWG_63_X5, which forced zero inverters on multiple semi-scheduled solar farms, causing hidden curtailment and self-forecast discrepancies.

title: Wholesale Demand Response Capacity in June 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wholesale Demand Response Capacity in June 2025" posted at 25-06-2025 05:16:00 PM is: The news reports a significant increase in registered Wholesale Demand Response (WDR) capacity in the National Electricity Market (NEM) as of June 2025, rising to 92 MW from 69 MW in October 2023, comprising 22 dispatchable units aggregating 140 individual loads; new units added in New South Wales (18 MW) on or after 13 June 2025 are highlighted, but they did not contribute to high-dispatch events on 11 and 12 June. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 13 June 2025 onwards. Its root_cause is: Unknown (not explicitly stated). Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: 25-06-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (Australia), particularly New South Wales. Its affected key users is: 22 dispatchable units representing 140 individual loads. Its cause type is: Market development. Its causes is: Increased registration of WDR capacity; addition of 18 MW in NSW on or after 13 June 2025; overall increase from 69 MW in October 2023 to 92 MW in June 2025.

title: Another look at conditions in South Australia next week as a forecast LOR3 now appears for Wednesday
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another look at conditions in South Australia next week as a forecast LOR3 now appears for Wednesday" posted at 25-06-2025 03:27:00 PM is: The article updates earlier LOR forecasts for South Australia, noting that LOR3 (lack of reserve level 3) is now forecast for two periods on Wednesday July 2nd, while LOR1 remains for Tuesday June 30th. These conditions arise from scheduled constraint sets invoked due to an outage on the Tailem Bend-South East 275kV line and one of the black range series capacitors being out of service. Four constraint sets (S-BC_CP, S-TBSE, S-X_BDU+BC_CP, S-X_BDBU+TBSE) will limit Heywood and Murraylink interconnector flows and require local FCAS services, increasing the risk of islanding and potential price spikes in the region. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: next week (June 30 to July 2, 2025). Its root_cause is: outages on the Tailem Bend-South East 275kV line and a black range series capacitor, triggering stability constraint sets. Its accident scale is: forecast LOR3 (likely load shedding), four constraint sets invoked, multiple transmission limitations. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: June 30 and July 2, 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: wholesale electricity market participants and end-use consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: grid infrastructure failure (transmission line and capacitor outages). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: outage on Tailem Bend-South East 275kV line, outage of one black range series capacitor.

title: Frequency change associated with coal generation events
author: Linton Corbet
topic: Long term trend of coal unit performance
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency change associated with coal generation events" posted at 25-06-2025 01:14:00 PM is: The article analyzes how sudden MW drops from coal unit trips (using Δ300 events) affect system frequency in Australia's NEM, highlighting a significant improvement after mandatory Primary Frequency Response (PFR) was introduced in 2020. It references a recent event on 25 June 2025 where Loy Yang A2 tripped from 443 MW, causing a frequency deviation, but notes that post-2020 frequency swings are smaller and less variable compared to earlier years (2017-2020), which saw larger deviations up to 0.4 Hz. The improvement is attributed to PFR acting instantaneously, unlike the slower FCAS market services, with the AEMC rule change in March 2020 requiring large generators to provide PFR. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate frequency deviation with long-term trend analysis from 2017 to 2025. Its root_cause is: Sudden loss of generation from coal unit trips (e.g., Loy Yang A2 trip) causing frequency drop; improved frequency resilience due to mandatory Primary Frequency Response (PFR) introduced in 2020. Its accident scale is: Single unit trip of 443 MW (Loy Yang A2) and historical multiple unit trips up to over 600 MW. Its dates is: 25-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly Victoria (Loy Yang A power station). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and grid operators in the NEM. Its cause type is: Technical failure (generator trip). Its causes is: ["Loy Yang A2 unit trip on 25 June 2025", "Historical coal unit trips (e.g., 4 Loy Yang A units in February 2024)", "Inadequate primary frequency response before 2020 due to market design (FCAS markets)", "Implementation of mandatory PFR from 2020 onward"].

title: Loy Yang A2 apparent trip from 443MW on Wednesday 25th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Loy Yang A2 apparent trip from 443MW on Wednesday 25th June 2025" posted at 25-06-2025 11:42:00 AM is: This article reports an apparent trip of the Loy Yang A2 unit from 443MW just prior to 11:31 NEM time on 25 June 2025, with a corresponding small and slow frequency dip observed in the NEM Mainland Frequency trace, suggesting a generation loss event. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (real-time generation loss affecting frequency and supply). Its root_cause is: Apparent trip of Loy Yang A2 unit. Its accident scale is: Unit trip of 443MW capacity. Its dates (if different) is: 25-06-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Loy Yang power station location, impacting the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: National Electricity Market (NEM) participants, including AEMO and market operators. Its cause type is: Generation outage (unplanned unit trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Loy Yang A2 unit apparently tripped from 443MW.

title: ENTSO-E is also investigating the 28th April 2025 blackout on the Iberian Peninsula
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "ENTSO-E is also investigating the 28th April 2025 blackout on the Iberian Peninsula" posted at 25-06-2025 08:35:00 AM is: The article reports that ENTSO-E is conducting an investigation into the major blackout that occurred on 28 April 2025 across the Iberian Peninsula. It outlines the two-phase investigation process, references reports published by the Spanish government and the Spanish TSO Red Eléctrica, and announces a stakeholder workshop planned for 18 July 2025 to discuss findings and next steps. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 28 April 2025; ongoing investigation with no market disruption reported after that date. Its root_cause is: Under investigation; not yet determined. Its accident scale is: Major blackout affecting the entire Iberian Peninsula. Its dates is: 28 April 2025. Its affected region is: Iberian Peninsula (Spain and Portugal). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in the region. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: No specific cause events confirmed; investigation in progress.

title: Unplanned outage of Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Unplanned outage of Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line" posted at 24-06-2025 07:33:00 PM is: On 23 June 2025, an unplanned outage of the Darlington Point–Wagga 330 kV line in New South Wales triggered a cascade trip of the Darlington Point–Balranald 220 kV line via a control scheme, invoking constraint set N-DPWG_63_X5 that limited inter-regional transfer capability on interconnectors VIC1-NSW1, N-Q-MNSP1, NSW1-QLD1, V-S-MNSP1, and V-SA. The outage was still active as of 24 June 2025, with constraint equations requiring zero inverters at certain semi-scheduled plants in the affected network area.; Its relevance level is: 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 23-06-2025 17:19 to 24-06-2025 (ongoing at article time). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of a 330 kV transmission line (equipment or operational failure, not further specified). Its accident scale is: Single transmission line outage leading to a cascade trip of a second line and activation of a constraint set. Its dates (if different from main date) is: 23-06-2025 to 24-06-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), specifically the Darlington Point, Wagga, and Balranald areas. Its affected key users are: Interconnectors VIC1-NSW1, N-Q-MNSP1, NSW1-QLD1, V-S-MNSP1, V-SA; and semi-scheduled power plants in the region (zero inverter requirements). Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (technical failure). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 
1. Unplanned outage of Darlington Point – Wagga No.63 330 kV line at 17:19 on 23-06-2025.
2. Consequent trip of Darlington Point – Balranald No. X5 220 kV line due to control scheme.
3. Invocation of constraint set N-DPWG_63_X5 at 17:25.

title: A closer look at LOR projections in South Australia next Monday 30th June 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A closer look at LOR projections in South Australia next Monday 30th June 2025" posted at 24-06-2025 01:22:00 PM is: AEMO initially issued an LOR3 notice for South Australia on Monday June 30, 2025, due to forecast high demand and significantly reduced wind availability, but later downgraded it to LOR2, with the condition extending into Tuesday and Wednesday. The situation is driven by a combination of moderately high projected peak demand, a forecast drop in wind generation from recent highs, an ongoing planned outage of the 210 MW Torrens Island A Unit 4, and a planned derating of the 150 MW Hornsdale Power Reserve to 80 MW, along with commissioning delays for several new batteries. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June 30 to July 2, 2025. Its root_cause is: forecast insufficient reserve margin due to high demand coinciding with low wind availability and planned generator outages. Its accident scale is: Forecast LOR2 condition (lack of reserve, not an actual outage). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: June 30, July 1, July 2, 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Combination of supply-side constraints and demand conditions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Forecast high market demand, [2] Significant drop in wind generation availability from Thursday June 26 onward, [3] Planned long-term outage of Torrens Island A Unit 4 (210 MW), [4] Planned derating of Hornsdale Power Reserve (from 150 MW to 80 MW), [5] Commissioning delays for recently energised batteries limiting their output.

title: A 409MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria on Tuesday 24th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A 409MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria on Tuesday 24th June 2025" posted at 24-06-2025 12:16:00 PM is: A 409MW drop in Market Demand occurred in Victoria at the 12:10 dispatch interval on Tuesday 24th June 2025, attributed to persistent windy conditions that likely boosted wind generation and reduced the demand for scheduled generation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (single dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: high wind generation due to windy conditions. Its accident scale is: 409MW drop. Its dates is: 24-06-2025 12:16:00 PM (same as news date). Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: weather-related (windy conditions). Its causes is: persistent windy conditions leading to increased wind generation.

title: Forecast LOR3 for South Australia for Monday 30th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast LOR3 for South Australia for Monday 30th June 2025" posted at 24-06-2025 08:27:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued forecast Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) and Level 2 (LOR2) notices for South Australia on 30 June 2025, indicating potential load shedding of up to 112 MW due to a tight supply-demand balance and low wind generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 30 June 2025 (specific periods: 07:30-09:30 and 17:00-23:00 for LOR3; broader periods for LOR2). Its root_cause is: low available generation, particularly low aggregate wind output, leading to insufficient reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: up to 112 MW of load (non-interruptible) could be interrupted during peak LOR3 periods. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Notice dates: 23 June 2025; Event date: 30 June 2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA region). Its affected key users are: electricity end-users (residential, commercial, industrial) potentially subject to load shedding. Its cause type is: forecast supply shortage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: low wind generation forecast, tight supply-demand balance, and insufficient reserve capacity in the SA region.

title: New all-time record for NEM-wide wind, on Monday 23rd June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record for NEM-wide wind, on Monday 23rd June 2025" posted at 24-06-2025 08:24:00 AM is: On 23 June 2025, a new all-time record for aggregate wind generation in the National Electricity Market (NEM) was set at 9,491 MW at 22:35, surpassing the previous record from 26 May 2025. Additionally, market notices indicate a forecast tight supply-demand balance, with a possible Lack of Reserve (LOR3) condition in South Australia for 30 June 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The wind generation record impacted the market on 23 June 2025 (immediate effect on prices); the forecast LOR3 may impact the market on 30 June 2025. Its root_cause is: High wind speeds caused the record; the LOR3 forecast stems from a projected supply-demand imbalance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23 June 2025 (record) and 30 June 2025 (forecast LOR3). Its affected region is: NEM-wide (record) and South Australia (LOR3). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural (wind) and operational (supply-demand imbalance). Its causes are: 1) High wind speeds across the NEM; 2) Forecast tight supply-demand balance leading to potential LOR3 in South Australia.

title: Red Electrica report on the “Blackout in Spanish Peninsula Electrical System on 28th of April 2025”
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "Red Electrica report on the “Blackout in Spanish Peninsula Electrical System on 28th of April 2025”" posted at 22-06-2025 is: This article reports on the release of a non-confidential report by Red Eléctrica (REE), the Spanish TSO, detailing the blackout that occurred on 28 April 2025 on the Spanish Peninsula electrical system. The report identifies overvoltage as the likely cause of the widespread grid instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate disruption on 28 April 2025, with ongoing market adjustments and regulatory investigations following the report's release. Its root_cause is: Overvoltage that triggered cascading failures. Its accident scale is: Unknown (large-scale blackout affecting the entire Spanish peninsula, but specific MW lost or customers affected not given). Its dates is: Event: 28-04-2025; Report published: 18-06-2025; News article: 22-06-2025. Its affected region is: Spanish Peninsula (Iberian Peninsula). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers, but not specified). Its cause type is: Technical failure (overvoltage). Its causes is: Overvoltage event leading to widespread blackout due to cascading system instability.

title: The frequency of coal generation events in 2025
author: Linton Corbet
topic: Long term trend of coal unit performance
classified_content: The summary of the news "The frequency of coal generation events in 2025" posted at 20-06-2025 10:09:00 AM is: This article reviews a long-term trend (2002–2025) of sudden coal generation output drops exceeding 300 MW in the Australian NEM, using a "Δ300" metric. The monthly count of such events has declined significantly over the 23-year period, even when accounting for units that have closed, with a linear trend of about one fewer event per month every five years. Notable recent events include Bayswater (2025) and several others listed from previous years. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term trend from 2002 to 2025, with specific events recurring throughout. Its root_cause is: Unknown; the article does not specify individual causes, but the events represent sudden generator trips or unplanned reductions. Its accident scale is: Each event involves a drop of more than 300 MW in coal unit output. Its dates is: 20-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australia – National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity grid operators (AEMO), frequency regulation services, and market participants. Its cause type is: Operational (unplanned outages). Its causes is: Not explicitly listed; the article focuses on the decreasing frequency rather than specific root causes.

title: ‘June 12 was one of the most challenging days of the last 4 years’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘June 12 was one of the most challenging days of the last 4 years’" posted at 18-06-2025 06:01:00 PM is: The news reports that June 12, 2025 was identified as one of the most challenging days for a mostly renewable simulation of the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) in the last four years, due to very poor wind generation coinciding with extreme demand, highlighting the vulnerability of renewable-dependent grids under adverse weather conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 June 2025 (single day). Its root_cause is: Combination of very poor wind conditions and extreme electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 12-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, generators, and consumers. Its cause type is: Weather/operational. Its causes is: Very poor wind generation; extreme demand.

title: A dose of evening volatility across the mainland, on Wednesday 18th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A dose of evening volatility across the mainland, on Wednesday 18th June 2025" posted at 18-06-2025 05:40:00 PM is: The article reports a snapshot from NEMwatch at 17:35 on Wednesday 18th June 2025, highlighting evening volatility across the mainland Australian electricity market, with the author noting the absence from Energy Week but suggesting the volatility would be a relevant discussion topic. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Evening of 18 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-06-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: The Non-Confidential Report (182 pages) into the Iberian blackout
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Non-Confidential Report (182 pages) into the Iberian blackout" posted at 18-06-2025 04:15:00 PM is: The Non-Confidential Report (182 pages) into the April 28, 2025 Iberian blackout, released on June 18, 2025, indicates that overvoltage was the likely cause of the grid instability that led to a widespread electricity crisis across the Iberian Peninsula, affecting both Spain and Portugal with a complete blackout. ; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The blackout occurred on April 28, 2025, and the report was published on June 18, 2025; immediate market disruption and subsequent recovery analysis. Its root_cause is: Overvoltage event. Its accident scale is: Widespread blackout across the entire Iberian Peninsula. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 28-04-2025 (event date) and 18-06-2025 (report date). Its affected region is: Iberian Peninsula (Spain and Portugal). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in Spain and Portugal. Its cause type is: Technical (grid overvoltage). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Overvoltage condition on the transmission network triggered protective relays, leading to cascading failures and a system-wide blackout.

title: News Release on the blackout on the Iberian Peninsula (on 28th April 2025) suggests overvoltage was the cause
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "News Release on the blackout on the Iberian Peninsula (on 28th April 2025) suggests overvoltage was the cause" posted at 18-06-2025 08:01:00 AM is: A report released on 18 June 2025 into the 28 April 2025 blackout on the Iberian Peninsula identifies the root cause as a cascade of overvoltage events, insufficient voltage control capacity, and improper generator disconnections. The chronology shows voltage instability days prior, followed by system oscillations that forced mitigation measures which raised voltages further, leading to progressive generation losses and eventual grid collapse. The committee concluded the failure was multifactorial, including insufficient scheduling of synchronous voltage-regulating plants, inadequate response from those plants, and premature disconnections before voltage thresholds were exceeded. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 28 April 2025 (immediate blackout) and subsequent weeks (grid restoration, regulatory review). Its root_cause is: Overvoltage caused by inadequate voltage control resources, system oscillations, and chain reaction of generator disconnections. Its accident scale is: Widespread blackout across the entire Iberian Peninsula. Its dates is: Event: 28 April 2025; Report: 18 June 2025. Its affected region is: Iberian Peninsula (Spain and Portugal). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and industrial users in the region. Its cause type is: Technical (grid instability, protective relay miscoordination). Its causes is: 1. Voltage instability in preceding days; 2. System oscillations (0.6 Hz and 0.2 Hz) on 28 April; 3. Mitigation measures (increased grid mesh, reduced interconnection flow) that raised voltages; 4. Insufficient synchronous voltage-regulating plants connected; 5. Some plants failed to reduce voltage as instructed; 6. Progressive disconnections of generating facilities (Granada, Badajoz, etc.) before voltage thresholds were exceeded; 7. Chain reaction of overvoltage tripping leading to loss of synchronization with France and grid collapse.

title: Frequency wobbles on Tuesday 17th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency wobbles on Tuesday 17th June 2025" posted at 17-06-2025 01:12:00 PM is: The news reports frequency wobbles on Tuesday 17th June 2025 in the NEM, with two instances where frequency dropped to 49.868Hz, approaching the lower bound of the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). These events are linked to sloppy dispatch control of semi-scheduled Variable Renewable Energy (VRE), which was also a factor in a similar drop the previous day. Despite the reintroduction of mandatory Primary Frequency Response, such frequency deviations have become more frequent recently. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day, short-term frequency deviations). Its root_cause is: Sloppy dispatch control of semi-scheduled Variable Renewable Energy (VRE). Its accident scale is: Minor (frequency near but not exceeding lower bound, no blackout or major disruption reported). Its dates is: 17-06-2025 (and reference to 16-06-2025). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia (observed from Brisbane office). Its affected key users is: Grid operators and all NEM participants (frequency instability affects system security). Its cause type is: Operational/control issue. Its causes is: Sloppy dispatch control of semi-scheduled VRE leading to frequency drops.

title: Which units show large positive Dispatch Error for 15:40 on Monday 16th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Which units show large positive Dispatch Error for 15:40 on Monday 16th June 2025" posted at 17-06-2025 10:53:00 AM is: The article examines a frequency drop on the mainland NEM at 15:40 on 16 June 2025, where frequency fell to the lower NOFB bound and below. Using dispatch data, it identifies units with large positive dispatch error (>25 MW), predominantly semi-scheduled VRE units, which contributed to the frequency instability. The author questions the scalability and sustainability of the semi-scheduled category. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 June 2025 at 15:40 dispatch interval, short-term impact (minutes). Its root_cause is: Large positive dispatch error from semi-scheduled variable renewable energy units, leading to insufficient frequency support and causing frequency to drop below NOFB. Its accident scale is: Frequency dropped to lower bound of NOFB and below for approximately one minute (15:40–15:41). Its dates is: 16-06-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators and market participants, especially semi-scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Operational/technical issue (dispatch error). Its causes is: 1) Semi-scheduled VRE units with large positive dispatch error; 2) Inadequate frequency support from these units during frequency decline.

title: Drop in mainland frequency on 16 June 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Drop in mainland frequency on 16 June 2025" posted at 17-06-2025 09:56:00 AM is: On 16 June 2025, mainland Australia experienced a frequency drop below the Normally Operating Frequency Band (NOFB), reaching a low of 49.832 Hz at 15:40:19. The excursion featured a pattern of slowing-then-recovering cycles over approximately 19 minutes, with no large generator trip identified. The cause is speculated to be a variable renewable energy (VRE)-driven event, similar to a previous dip on 13 June. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: Speculated VRE-driven event (no confirmed generator trip). Its accident scale is: Minor frequency excursion below NOFB (to 49.832 Hz). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-06-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland Australia (likely National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: All grid-connected users (frequency deviation). Its cause type is: Speculative, possibly variable renewable energy (VRE) variability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: No identified generator trip; pattern of slowing-recovering cycles; speculation of VRE-driven event; previous similar dip on 13 June.

title: We need more research on storage trading
author: Greg Williams
classified_content: The summary of the news "We need more research on storage trading" posted at 16-06-2025 is: The article argues that as thermal generation retires and battery storage becomes prevalent, wholesale electricity market dynamics change, making price formation less reliant on solid fuel costs and more dependent on storage state-of-charge. The author calls for more research to understand bidding behavior, price discovery, and potential issues with auto-bidding algorithms, similar to algorithmic trading regulations. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (ongoing transition). Its root_cause is: Retirement of thermal generation and increased storage deployment. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from main date) is: 16-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Market participants, regulators, AEMO. Its cause type is: Market evolution / Energy transition. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Retirement of thermal units, increased battery storage deployment, auto-bidding algorithms, need for research and monitoring.

title: AEMO launches consultation to update Negative Residue Management automation procedures
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO launches consultation to update Negative Residue Management automation procedures" posted at 16-06-2025 02:51:00 PM is: AEMO has launched a consultation to update automated Negative Residue Management (NRM) procedures, addressing complications from the looped transmission topology created by Project Energy Connect (PEC) linking NSW, SA, and VIC. The proposed changes aim to reduce "cycling" (frequent auto-clamping/unclamping) by introducing non-zero minimum flow limits (e.g., 20 MW) and a graduated release stage before exit from NRM management. These modifications seek to prevent premature unclamping when negative residues persist. Stakeholders have until 11 July 2025 to provide submissions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2025-2026 (consultation until mid-2025, implementation ahead of PEC completion in late 2026). Its root_cause is: The looped transmission topology from Project Energy Connect complicates individual interconnector residue management, leading to cycling where automated NRM constraints prematurely unclamp and re-clamp multiple times per day. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 16-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (NSW, SA, VIC). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, interconnector operators. Its cause type is: Market design / operational procedure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Looped transmission topology from Project Energy Connect creates complex counter-price flows. 2. Existing NRM process reduces minimum flow to zero, halting negative residue accumulation and triggering premature exit despite persistent market conditions. 3. This leads to repeated auto-clamping and unclamping cycles.

title: Three units at Yallourn Power Station offline on Monday morning 16th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three units at Yallourn Power Station offline on Monday morning 16th June 2025" posted at 16-06-2025 11:29:00 AM is: On Monday 16 June 2025, three units at Yallourn Power Station were offline: YWPS3 (tube leak and air duct collapse, expected return 28 June), YWPS4 (extended outage, expected return 18 June), and YWPS1 (new tube leak). These generator failures reduce available capacity in Victoria's electricity market.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: turbine/generator failures.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (days to weeks).; Its root_cause is: equipment failures – tube leaks and air duct collapse.; Its accident scale is: three units offline at one power station.; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-06-2025.; Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Yallourn).; Its affected key users is: Unknown.; Its cause type is: unplanned outage.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: tube leak at YWPS1; tube leak and air duct collapse at YWPS3; extended outage at YWPS4.

title: Trading Places … BW01 offline, with BW04 online
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trading Places … BW01 offline, with BW04 online" posted at 16-06-2025 11:07:00 AM is: On Saturday 14th June 2025, Bayswater Unit 4 came back online at 685 MW, but its sibling unit BW01 went offline on Sunday 15th June 2025 at 15:31 NEM time, with rebids made from 14:23 reflecting unit issues. The unit is expected to return by Sunday 22nd June 2025, while the MT PASA data set is stale before that date. Additionally, three units at Yallourn are offline as of Monday 16th June 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing from 15 June 2025 to at least 22 June 2025 for BW01, with Yallourn outages also affecting the market from 16 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Underlying unit issues not explicitly specified, likely mechanical or operational failures. Its accident scale is: 1 unit at Bayswater (685 MW capacity) and 3 units at Yallourn (exact capacity not stated, but Yallourn is a coal-fired station). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: BW01 offline from 15 June 2025, expected back 22 June 2025; Yallourn units offline as of 16 June 2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (Bayswater) and Victoria (Yallourn). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, grid operators, and consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Forced outages of generation units. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: BW01 went offline due to unit issues on 15 June; three units at Yallourn are offline (cause not specified).

title: What’s all this, then, about Gas-Powered Generation in Victoria in the past week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s all this, then, about Gas-Powered Generation in Victoria in the past week" posted at 15-06-2025 06:17:00 PM is: Over the past week (especially on 11–12 June 2025) gas-powered generation in Victoria surged due to cold weather driving higher demand, a seasonal low in solar output, a weather-pattern-induced low for wind, and outages of 3 out of 10 coal units in Victoria plus additional coal unit outages in NSW and QLD. The article provides historical context showing similar spikes in 2017, 2019, 2022, and 2024, and notes that while gas capacity is needed, it is intended to be used sparingly; the current event, though notable, is not unprecedented. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, immediate impact during the past week (June 8–15, 2025), with peak on 11–12 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Cold weather increasing electricity demand combined with low renewable generation (solar and wind) and forced outages of coal-fired units. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported; normal operational event). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 11–12 June 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (with broader NEM implications). Its affected key users are: Victorian electricity consumers (and potentially the wider National Electricity Market). Its cause type is: Combination of meteorological factors (cold, low wind/solar) and generation outages. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: (1) Cold weather driving higher demand; (2) Seasonal low solar output; (3) Low wind generation due to weather pattern; (4) Outages of 3 of 10 coal units in Victoria, plus outages in NSW and QLD.

title: AEMO ‘Industry Go Live Plan’ for ERI and ST PASA Recall Period
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO ‘Industry Go Live Plan’ for ERI and ST PASA Recall Period" posted at 14-06-2025 02:32:00 PM is: The article outlines the AEMO's Industry Go-Live Plan for two market changes effective from 1 July 2025: Enhanced Reserve Information (ERI) changes to publish previous day's 5-min battery data, daily energy limits for scheduled generators, and near-real-time aggregated battery state of charge; and ST PASA Recall Period process changes allowing participants to optionally provide bidding data from July 2025, with mandatory use and table population from 31 July 2025, and further ERI battery data publication in real-time by region planned for 1 July 2027. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 1 July 2025 onwards, with phased implementation through 31 July 2025 and 1 July 2027. Its root_cause is: AEMC rulings on 21 March 2024 (for ERI) and May 2022 (for ST PASA processes). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 1 July 2025 (primary go-live), with additional milestones on 31 July 2025 and 1 July 2027. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Market participants (scheduled generators, battery operators, pumped hydro), AEMO, and data users via ez2view. Its cause type is: Regulatory. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC rule changes mandating enhanced reserve information and ST PASA recall period adjustments to improve market transparency and operational planning.

title: AEMO publishes Draft Report (Security Enablement Procedure – Improving Security Frameworks)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes Draft Report (Security Enablement Procedure – Improving Security Frameworks)" posted at 14-06-2025 01:26:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) published a Draft Report and proposed Security Enablement Procedures on 13 June 2025, as part of the Improving Security Frameworks (ISF) rule change. This procedure, effective from 2 December 2025, allows AEMO to schedule security services across the NEM to enhance system stability, with a methodology for determining security requirements and processes for enabling services; consultation runs until 15 July 2025, with final publication expected by 11 August 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future (from 2 December 2025 onward). Its root_cause is: Recent market events during early winter cold snap highlighting need for improved system security frameworks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 13-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, TNSPs, and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy change. Its causes is: 1. Recent market events during early winter cold snap; 2. AEMC's Improving Security Frameworks rule change.

title: With some wobbles, Bayswater Unit 4 coming back online – Saturday 14th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With some wobbles, Bayswater Unit 4 coming back online – Saturday 14th June 2025" posted at 14-06-2025 09:35:00 AM is: Bayswater Unit 4 (BW04) made an initial return to service at 01:35 NEM time on 14 June 2025, but tripped offline at 03:10 from 36 MW, as indicated by a rebid noting 'unit trip'. A second reconnection attempt was observed at 10:21 the same morning, with the generator still expecting to bring the unit back online in the coming hours. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 14 June 2025; unit outage lasting several hours, with ongoing restoration attempts. Its root_cause is: Unknown (described only as a 'unit trip'). Its accident scale is: Single unit trip at Bayswater Unit 4 (BW04). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 14-06-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (NEM region). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the NEM, particularly in NSW. Its cause type is: Forced outage (unit trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: BW04 unit tripped offline from 36 MW at 03:10 on 14 June 2025, as per a rebid noting 'unit trip'.

title: AEMO (also) forecasts LOR2 for QLD on Monday 16th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO (also) forecasts LOR2 for QLD on Monday 16th June 2025" posted at 13-06-2025 01:14:00 PM is: AEMO issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) for Queensland on 16 June 2025 from 0900 to 1000 hrs due to planned network outages, but canceled the notice later on 13 June after a market response. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 June 2025 09:00-10:00. Its root_cause is: Planned network outages. Its accident scale is: Forecast LOR2 with a shortfall of 36 MW (845 MW requirement vs 809 MW available), later canceled. Its dates are: 13 June 2025 (notice issuance and cancellation) and 16 June 2025 (forecast period). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Planned network outages (infrastructure). Its causes is: Planned network outages reducing available capacity.

title: AEMO updated forecasts for Tuesday 17th June 2025 show possible LOR2 for Queensland (in the middle of the day!)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO updated forecasts for Tuesday 17th June 2025 show possible LOR2 for Queensland (in the middle of the day!)" posted at 13-06-2025 09:42:00 AM is: AEMO issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for Queensland on 17 June 2025 for two midday periods (11:00–11:30 and 14:00–14:30) due to forecast poor aggregate solar and wind generation from cloud cover and low wind, compounded by network limitations, indicating potential supply shortfalls of up to 270 MW; the notice was later cancelled at 14:00 on 13 June 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 17 June 2025 (forecast periods), but cancelled before actual impact. Its root_cause is: Forecast poor solar and wind generation due to cloud cover and low wind, combined with network limitations. Its accident scale is: Forecast lack of reserve with capacity shortfalls of 42 MW (first period) and 34 MW (second period). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 17 June 2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-related and network constraints. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1. Poor aggregate solar generation due to widespread cloud cover; 2. Poor aggregate wind generation due to low wind speeds; 3. Network limitations (scheduled outages).

title: Sharp drop in frequency on Friday morning 13th June 2025, with trip at Newport Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sharp drop in frequency on Friday morning 13th June 2025, with trip at Newport Power Station" posted at 13-06-2025 08:47:00 AM is: On Friday 13 June 2025, a sharp drop in frequency occurred shortly after 07:30 NEM time due to a trip at Newport Power Station, where output fell from 500 MW to 0 MW at 07:35:27, causing the frequency to drop to a low of 49.867 Hz. This generator failure led to price spikes in Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania around 07:50. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (prices spiked within minutes of the trip; event occurred at 07:35 and price effects visible by 07:50). Its root_cause is: Trip of Newport Power Station (a forced outage resulting in loss of 500 MW generation). Its accident scale is: Loss of 500 MW generation; frequency disturbance within normal operating frequency band (NOFB). Its dates is: 13-06-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC), South Australia (SA), Tasmania (TAS) – markets where prices spiked; also broader NEM. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, consumers in VIC, SA, TAS. Its cause type is: Forced outage / generator trip. Its causes is: A sharp drop in output from Newport Power Station from 500 MW to 0 MW at approximately 07:35:27 NEM time on 13 June 2025.

title: Brief summary of outcomes (Price, Demand, IRPM and more) for Thursday evening 12th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief summary of outcomes (Price, Demand, IRPM and more) for Thursday evening 12th June 2025" posted at 13-06-2025 08:30:00 AM is: On Thursday evening 12th June 2025, the NEM saw low wind yields at 16:20 that contributed to high prices, followed by a partial wind recovery; the market experienced a low IRPM of 10.34% at 20:00, coinciding with reduced BESS availability, while Loy Yang A1 returned online, partially alleviating supply constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures and fuel supply issues (low wind yield). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 12 June 2025). Its root_cause is: combination of low wind generation and the earlier outage of Loy Yang A1 (which returned online during the evening). Its accident scale is: unknown (multiple events, no single large-scale accident). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 12-06-2025 (same as event date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity market participants (generators, retailers, consumers). Its cause type is: operational issues (fuel supply variability and generator unavailability). Its causes is: (1) low wind yield reducing renewable supply, (2) unplanned outage of Loy Yang A1 unit (implied by its return) reducing coal-fired capacity.

title: Elevated prices on Friday morning 13th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Elevated prices on Friday morning 13th June 2025" posted at 13-06-2025 08:01:00 AM is: The article reports elevated electricity prices on Friday morning 13 June 2025, based on a NEMwatch snapshot at 07:50. Cumulative prices in Queensland reached just over $700,000/MWh and in Victoria over $850,000/MWh, both approaching the Cumulative Price Threshold. It also notes a temporary 319 MW dip in Victorian market demand between 07:05 and 07:10, and a NEM-wide market demand peak of 30,784 MW at 07:30, the highest of the morning. The event reflects market reactions to high demand conditions. Its relevance level is: 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (intraday morning of 13 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 13-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – specifically Queensland, Victoria, and NEM-wide. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: Callide B2 commences return to service, Friday morning 13th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide B2 commences return to service, Friday morning 13th June 2025" posted at 13-06-2025 07:41:00 AM is: The Callide B2 unit, which had been on a long planned outage, began its return to service around 07:06 NEM time on Friday 13th June 2025, following a similar process at Loy Yang A1 the previous night. Market data shows its output progression and bids, providing a welcome supply boost amid ongoing cold weather and low wind conditions, and helping to address AEMO's forecasted tightness for the following week.; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: Market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (supply increase begins as unit ramps up); Its root_cause is: Planned outage completion; Its accident scale is: None (planned maintenance, no accident); Its dates is: 13-06-2025; Its affected region is: Queensland (Callide power station location); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Planned maintenance; Its causes is: Long planned outage of Callide B2 unit; outage completed and unit returned to service.

title: Loy Yang A1 back online, Thursday evening 12th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Loy Yang A1 back online, Thursday evening 12th June 2025" posted at 13-06-2025 07:32:00 AM is: The alert at 22:36 NEM time on Thursday 12th June 2025 confirmed that Loy Yang A1, previously on an unplanned outage, has begun its return to service, providing a welcome supply boost during ongoing cold weather and low wind conditions, which helped end earlier market volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing from the evening of 12 June 2025, with benefits continuing during the cold weather period. Its root_cause is: Unknown (only described as an unplanned outage). Its accident scale is: Single generator unit (Loy Yang A1, typically ~500 MW capacity). Its dates is: 12 June 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (National Electricity Market region). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in Victoria, market operators, and other generators impacted by supply conditions. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (technical failure). Its causes is: Unplanned outage due to an unspecified technical issue.

title: NEM-wide IRPM drops to 10.34% on Thursday evening 12th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide IRPM drops to 10.34% on Thursday evening 12th June 2025" posted at 12-06-2025 07:35:00 PM is: The NEM-wide Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropped to a low of 10.34% during the evening of 12 June 2025, indicating a tight supply-demand balance and potential system stability risks, lower than the previous evening and historically low. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (evening of 12 June 2025, with possible after-effects for a few hours or days). Its root_cause is: Unknown from article; likely a combination of high evening peak demand and reduced available generation (e.g., thermal or renewable constraints). Its accident scale is: Not an accident; system-wide low reserve margin event. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 12-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) covering Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania, South Australia. Its affected key users are: All NEM electricity consumers, particularly large industrial users and residential customers sensitive to reliability. Its cause type is: Market demand and supply imbalance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High evening demand; potential generation outages or intermittent renewable shortfalls; no explicit cause given in article.

title: Low wind yield drops lower, on Thursday evening 12th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low wind yield drops lower, on Thursday evening 12th June 2025" posted at 12-06-2025 07:34:00 PM is: On Thursday evening 12 June 2025, NEM-wide wind yield dropped below 1,000 MW, reaching a low of 721 MW (5% capacity factor) from ~14,000 MW installed capacity, with forecasts indicating low wind persisting into Friday 13 June before recovery, impacting electricity supply and prices due to reduced renewable generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues (wind). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, from evening of 12 June through afternoon/evening of 13 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Low wind speeds across the National Electricity Market (NEM) leading to very low wind generation. Its accident scale is: Not an accident; event is low wind yield affecting entire NEM with capacity factor of ~5%. Its dates (if different) is: 12-06-2025 (main event), with forecast low wind continuing into 13-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Wholesale electricity market participants, including generators, retailers, and consumers. Its cause type is: Weather (low wind). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Low wind speeds, reduced wind generation across NEM.

title: A short-lived return to service for Yallourn unit 4
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "A short-lived return to service for Yallourn unit 4" posted at 12-06-2025 07:01:00 PM is: This article reports that the Yallourn unit 4 (YWPS4) in the Australian NEM had a brief return to service: it came online on the evening of Wednesday 11th June 2025 but then tripped offline the following afternoon (Thursday 12th June 2025), thereby missing both evening revenue opportunities and the subsequent higher price volatility event.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (short-lived outage affecting dispatch and prices on 11-12 June 2025).  
Its root_cause is: Unknown (news does not specify the reason for the trip or the initial return).  
Its accident scale is: Single unit (Yallourn unit 4, ~350 MW).  
Its dates (if different) is: 11-06-2025 and 12-06-2025.  
Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market, NEM).  
Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO) and consumers exposed to spot prices.  
Its cause type is: Equipment/operational failure (unplanned outage or trip).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unit returned to service on 11 June 2025 around 22:00, then tripped offline on 12 June 2025 around 15:30; exact root cause not disclosed.

Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/Grid stability. title: North-south switcheroo as solar conditions swing opposite directions over Autumn 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "North-south switcheroo as solar conditions swing opposite directions over Autumn 2025" posted at 12-06-2025 05:18:00 PM is: The article describes how unusual autumn weather in 2025, driven by a strongly positive Southern Annular Mode (SAM) and a persistent high-pressure system over southern Australia, created starkly contrasting solar irradiance conditions across the National Electricity Market (NEM). Northern and eastern regions (Queensland, northern NSW) experienced above-average rainfall and cloud cover, reducing solar farm availability by an average of 5.6%, while southern states (Victoria) enjoyed clearer skies, increasing availability by 7.7%. This seasonal variability impacted utility-scale solar farm performance and highlighted the growing influence of weather patterns on renewable energy output in the NEM.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: March to May 2025 (Autumn 2025). Its root_cause is: Strongly positive Southern Annular Mode (SAM) causing a north-south weather split, reinforced by a high-pressure system over southern Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: March to May 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – particularly Queensland, northern New South Wales, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Utility-scale solar farms (semi-scheduled units registered before 2020). Its cause type is: Weather/climate driver. Its causes are: Strongly positive Southern Annular Mode (SAM), persistent high-pressure system over southern Australia, above-average rainfall and cloud cover in north-east, below-average rainfall in south-west.

title: An initial review of demand response on Wednesday 11 June 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "An initial review of demand response on Wednesday 11 June 2025" posted at 12-06-2025 04:58:00 PM is: On the evening of 11 June 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a tight supply-demand balance with IRPM dropping to 11.48%, leading to high price volatility. Demand-side responses included 13 MW of Wholesale Demand Response (WDR) dispatched across QLD, SA, VIC, and NSW, and a 250 MW reduction at Tomago in NSW lasting about 2 hours, plus a smaller drop at Portland in VIC. No RERT intervention was observed. The event was primarily driven by system instability from low spare capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: evening of Wednesday 11 June 2025, lasting about 2 hours. Its root_cause is: tight supply-demand balance due to low IRPM (spare capacity). Its accident scale is: Not an accident; controlled demand-side response. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-06-2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland regions – NSW, QLD, VIC, SA. Its affected key users is: Large industrial loads (Tomago aluminium smelter, Portland aluminium smelter) and WDR participants. Its cause type is: market-driven response to high prices and low reserve margin. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Tight supply-demand balance on Wednesday evening; low NEM-wide IRPM (11.48%); high energy prices triggered demand-side actions including WDR dispatch and voluntary load reductions from large consumers.

title: Brief summary of outcomes (Price, Demand and IRPM) for Wednesday evening 11th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief summary of outcomes (Price, Demand and IRPM) for Wednesday evening 11th June 2025" posted at 12-06-2025 12:25:00 PM is: On the evening of 11th June 2025, the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced significant price volatility and extremely low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM), which dropped to 11.48% at 19:15, indicating tight spare supply. Prices surged across all regions during the 16:00–20:00 period, reflecting system instability due to insufficient generation capacity relative to demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 11 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Unknown (not explicitly identified in the article, but implied tight supply-demand balance). Its accident scale is: N/A (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: event date 11-06-2025, article date 12-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: all electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: operational/system stress. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: high demand, low reserve margin, potential generation unavailability.

title: Battery bidding is reshaping FCAS — But Demand Response remains a strategic asset
author: Paul Moore
classified_content: The summary of the news "Battery bidding is reshaping FCAS — But Demand Response remains a strategic asset" posted at 12-06-2025 is: The article examines how the rapid growth of utility-scale battery storage (BESS) has driven Contingency FCAS prices to extremely low levels due to price-taking bids and spare capacity, squeezing demand response (DR) providers out of the bid stack despite DR's critical sub-150 ms contingency response capability that enhances grid stability. It argues for maintaining technology diversity and considers interim mechanisms to preserve DR participation until robust two-sided market structures are established. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (trend observable since 2024, with specific data from 23 May 2025). Its root_cause is: Rise of battery storage with price-taking bids in Contingency FCAS markets. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 12-06-2025 (main date), 23-05-2025 (specific event reference). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, particularly South Australia, New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Demand response providers, battery storage operators, grid operators. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes is: 1) Rapid growth of utility-scale BESS providing low-cost FCAS bids, 2) BESS traders using price-taking strategies to maximize energy arbitrage while offering spare capacity at low prices, 3) Saturation of Contingency FCAS demand relative to supply, 4) Decline in Contingency FCAS prices to below cost of service provision for some participants.

title: High prices and tight spare supply, as NEM-wide IRPM drops down to 11.48% on Wednesday 11th June 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "High prices and tight spare supply, as NEM-wide IRPM drops down to 11.48% on Wednesday 11th June 2025" posted at 11-06-2025 07:28:00 PM is: On the evening of 11 June 2025, cold weather led to elevated electricity prices exceeding $7,000/MWh across all five NEM regions, with the NEM-wide Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropping as low as 11.48%, indicating critically tight spare supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 11 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Cold weather driving high demand and reducing reserve margins. Its accident scale is: NEM-wide (all five regions). Its dates is: 11-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – all five regions. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes is: Cold weather, high electricity demand, low spare capacity.
