title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 5 – afternoon underestimations
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 5 – afternoon underestimations" posted at 11-06-2025 09:02:00 AM is: This article is part of a series illustrating self-forecasting games played by semi-scheduled generators, focusing on a solar farm that deliberately biased its self-forecasts low during late afternoon intervals (e.g., 17:40–18:05) to reduce its share of Regulation FCAS costs under the Causer Pays mechanism, while also avoiding AEMO suppression. The behavior exploits the pre-June 8, 2025 frequency cost allocation rules, and the article anticipates possible changes after the introduction of Frequency Performance Payments on that date. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Historical (pre-June 8, 2025), with ongoing implications for future market design. Its root_cause is: Self-forecast biasing by a solar farm to minimize regulation FCAS cost allocation under the Causer Pays mechanism. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; market manipulation behavior). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-06-2025 09:02:00 AM (article date; specific event date obscured). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled solar farms, AEMO, market participants (especially those involved in frequency control ancillary services). Its cause type is: Behavioral/manipulative (self-forecast biasing). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Solar farm intentionally lowers its self-forecast during late afternoon. 2. Actual output remains consistent with solar availability and AWEFS/ASEFS forecasts. 3. Biasing reduces the unit’s contribution to Regulation FCAS causer-pays factors in some intervals. 4. The unit sometimes uses a follow-up strategy of matching output to a lowered target to make the AWEFS/ASEFS forecast appear worse, gaining a performance comparison advantage when constrained.

title: AEMO notes increased forecast for Unserved Energy (USE) in QLD due to network outages scheduled between Tue 17th to Fri 20th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle

Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 17–20 June 2025 (short-term), with potential residual effects on the annual USE forecast. Its root_cause is: Scheduled network outages (planned maintenance) in Queensland. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17–20 June 2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in Queensland (residential, commercial, and industrial). Its cause type is: Planned network maintenance (scheduled outages). Its causes is: [Scheduled network outages between 17–20 June 2025 in Queensland].

title: Cold winter evening driving NEM-wide demand higher, such as on Tuesday 10th June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cold winter evening driving NEM-wide demand higher, such as on Tuesday 10th June 2025" posted at 10-06-2025 06:14:00 PM is: On a cold winter evening, elevated demand across the National Electricity Market (NEM) led to tight supply-demand conditions, with NEM-wide market demand reaching 31,402 MW, spare capacity below 6,000 MW, and an IRPM of 19% (below the 20% tightness threshold). Prices rose across all regions but remained non-extreme, and no interconnector constraints were observed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (the evening of 10 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Cold weather driving higher heating demand. Its accident scale is: None (no accident reported). Its dates is: 10-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: All regions within the NEM. Its cause type is: Weather. Its causes is: Cold winter evening temperatures increasing electricity consumption for heating.

title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 4 – avoiding SDC periods
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 4 – avoiding SDC periods" posted at 10-06-2025 03:26:00 PM is: This article examines how some self-forecasting systems in the Australian electricity market deliberately suppress forecasts during Semi-Dispatch Cap (SDC) periods, potentially to avoid difficult forecasting and to bypass performance assessments by staying below the 60% assessment threshold, while also noting that new Frequency Performance Payments from 8 June 2025 may alter these behaviors; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing behavior, with a specific example from a week in early June 2025. Its root_cause is: Self-forecasting systems suppress forecasts during SDC periods due to difficulty in predicting availability under caps and the possibility of skipping performance assessments if the share of self-forecasted intervals falls below 60%, leveraging the self-suppression option. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Unknown (anonymized in the article, example week around 8 June 2025). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Self-forecasting participants, AEMO, generators. Its cause type is: Market participant behavior. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Difficulty in forecasting unconstrained availability during SDC periods; 2. Potential to avoid performance assessment if self-forecast intervals <60% of the week; 3. Use of the self-suppression option as a default behavior rather than case-by-case accuracy.

title: Unplanned outage at YWPS3 (tube leak) extended (due to air duct collapse)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage at YWPS3 (tube leak) extended (due to air duct collapse)" posted at 10-06-2025 10:35:00 AM is: An unplanned outage at Yallourn Power Station unit 3 (YWPS3) initially detected with a suspect tube leak on June 3, 2025, was extended after an air duct collapsed during maintenance, causing the unit to go offline around midday on June 7 and be expected to return by June 28, 2025. The incident, covered by ABC News and analyzed through market data, reduces generation capacity in Victoria's National Electricity Market for approximately three weeks.; Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: turbine/generator failures; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June 7 to June 28, 2025 (approx 3 weeks); Its root_cause is: Initial tube leak followed by air duct collapse during maintenance; Its accident scale is: Unplanned outage of a 375 MW unit for about three weeks; Its dates are: June 3 (tube leak detected), June 7 (outage started), June 28 (expected return); Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (National Electricity Market region); Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants in the NEM, particularly generators, retailers, and consumers in Victoria; Its cause type is: Equipment failure; Its causes are: Suspected tube leak on June 3 confirmed; unit taken offline on June 7; during maintenance, an air duct collapsed, extending the outage.

title: Beyond the fence line: Why risk needs to be part of your renewable project narrative
author: Cameron Sheild
classified_content: The summary of the news "Beyond the fence line: Why risk needs to be part of your renewable project narrative" posted at 10-06-2025 09:51:00 AM is: This article, drawn from a presentation at the Clean Energy Council’s Investor Forum, argues that renewable project developers and investors must proactively treat risk as a narrative and strategically engage insurers to manage emerging variables such as technology volatility, extreme weather, and global supply chain lag, thereby improving project bankability, asset valuation, and stakeholder trust in Australia’s decarbonised grid; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (the article discusses ongoing risk management rather than a specific event). Its root_cause is: Increasing complexity of renewable project risks and evolving insurer interpretation that affect premium pricing, terms, and asset valuation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-06-2025. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Renewable project developers, investors, insurers, grid operators, and financiers. Its cause type is: Risk management and communication issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Technology volatility, extreme weather, global supply chain lag, poor project documentation and risk communication, and insurer scrutiny of design assumptions (e.g., thermal runaway spacing, panel fragility, turbine scale, spares planning, NatCat assumptions).

title: Release of Report into Broken Hill blackout in October 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Release of Report into Broken Hill blackout in October 2024" posted at 09-06-2025 09:36:00 AM is: The article reports the release of a 158-page report by the NSW Government’s Committee on Environment and Planning on June 5, 2025, investigating a severe blackout in Broken Hill and far western NSW. The blackout began overnight on October 16, 2024, when severe storms toppled transmission towers on the X2 line, cutting power for several weeks. The report notes the network operator knew backup was offline but did not alert authorities, and recommends compensation for affected residents. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: October 16 to November 2, 2024 (approximately). Its root_cause is: Severe storms causing transmission tower collapse on the X2 line. Its accident scale is: Unknown (extended regional blackout). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-10-2024. Its affected region is: Far West NSW (Broken Hill and surrounding areas). Its affected key users is: Residents and businesses in Broken Hill and western NSW. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (severe storms). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Severe storms, transmission tower failure on X2 line, grid disruption.

title: The commencement of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The commencement of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP)" posted at 09-06-2025 07:09:00 AM is: The news reports the start of the Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) mechanism in the Australian National Electricity Market on 8 June 2025, replacing the 'Causer Pays' approach for Regulation FCAS costs and introducing payments for Primary Frequency Response. However, early teething issues emerged with a service interruption in the FPP system from 20:25 on 8 June, resolved by 00:25 on 9 June, leading to the use of default contribution factors for affected intervals. The article also notes potential behavioral changes among self-forecasters under the new incentive structure.; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: Grid frequency issues; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 8 June 2025 onwards; Its root_cause is: Introduction of the new Frequency Performance Payments system and a temporary system service interruption; Its accident scale is: Minor service interruption affecting FPP factor calculations for approximately 4 hours; Its dates is: 08-06-2025 to 09-06-2025; Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM); Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, etc.) receiving FPP factors; Its cause type is: System/technical failure; Its causes is: Implementation of new Frequency Performance Payments mechanism, temporary system service interruption starting at 20:25 on 08/06/2025, and resolution by AEMO at 00:25 on 09/06/2025.

title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – under the hood of part 1
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – under the hood of part 1" posted at 06-06-2025 is: The article examines how self-forecast biasing by generators aims to earn helpful Causer Pays factors for regulation FCAS costs by aligning unit deviations with the Frequency Indicator (FI), but demonstrates through a detailed example of seven dispatch intervals that the strategy is highly uncertain. Despite all five biased intervals having deviations that matched the FI direction, only one earned a positive factor; two intervals were excluded due to bad data or manual review, and two more were excluded because a high percentage of FI datapoints were misaligned with system frequency (exceeding the 66% threshold). The analysis underscores that the FI is often misaligned, and manual reviews can nullify the biasing effort, with the entire Causer Pays procedure for regulation FCAS set to be replaced by Frequency Performance Payments on June 8 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, until June 8, 2025. Its root_cause is: Self-forecast biasing to exploit the Frequency Indicator for regulation FCAS cost reduction, but with uncertain outcomes due to FI misalignment, data exclusions, and manual review processes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 06-06-2025 (post date) and 08-06-2025 (change date). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Generators and market participants using self-forecasting strategies for regulation FCAS. Its cause type is: Market design and operational strategy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Self-forecast biasing to create positive unit MW deviations; Frequency Indicator alignment requirement for helpful factors; FI misalignment with system frequency leading to exclusion of dispatch intervals; Manual review exclusions; Upcoming replacement of Causer Pays with Frequency Performance Payments.

title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 3 – lunar megawatts
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 3 – lunar megawatts" posted at 05-06-2025 04:26:00 PM is: The article examines a self-forecasting tactic called "lunar megawatts," where solar farms forecast generation during night hours when actual output should be zero, in an attempt to reduce their share of Regulation FCAS costs by influencing the Frequency Indicator. The example shows that the strategy often backfired because the Frequency Indicator did not align with the desired bias, and the cumulative effect from multiple units can reach up to 100 MW. This behavior is expected to change after the introduction of Frequency Performance Payments on June 8, 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing until the commencement of Frequency Performance Payments on June 8, 2025. Its root_cause is: Strategic self-forecast biasing by solar farms to reduce Regulation FCAS costs by forecasting generation during night hours (lunar megawatts) in an attempt to influence the Frequency Indicator. Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident); however, cumulative impacts up to 100 MW have been observed. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: Same as article date; event impact period: up to June 8, 2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled solar farms, AEMO, and other market participants. Its cause type is: Self-forecast biasing (strategic manipulation). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Solar farms submitting forecasts of generation at night when output is zero; 2) Attempt to have positive forecast differences during negative Frequency Indicator periods to appear helpful; 3) Coordination among multiple units to amplify system-level impact.

title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 2 – relative accuracy
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 2 – relative accuracy" posted at 04-06-2025 03:42:00 PM is: This article, part of a series on self-forecasting strategies in the Australian National Electricity Market, analyzes a generator’s behavior where its self-forecast was persistently biased below the AWEFS/ASEFS forecast and closer to actual output, thereby boosting its forecast accuracy scores in weekly performance assessments. The author argues this bias was intentional, serving dual purposes: achieving lower dispatch targets to generate positive deviations that support frequency raise (impacting Causer-Pays factors) and improving relative accuracy metrics like RMSE and MAE. The article notes that the upcoming implementation of Frequency Performance Payments on 8 June 2025, which changes the apportionment of regulation FCAS costs and retires the use of the Frequency Indicator, is expected to alter such behaviors. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Imminent (from June 2025 onwards, coinciding with the regulatory change on 8 June 2025). Its root_cause is: Exploitation of market design elements—specifically the Frequency Indicator for apportioning regulation FCAS costs and the self-forecast accuracy assessment—by generators to improve performance metrics and reduce costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 04-06-2025. Its affected region is: Unknown (likely Australian National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown (generators, AEMO, market operators). Its cause type is: Market behavior/exploitation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Persistent low biasing of self-forecast relative to AWEFS/ASEFS forecast; (2) Exploitation of the Frequency Indicator to produce consistent "helped raise" outcomes for frequency support; (3) Intentional manipulation of forecast accuracy scores to pass weekly performance assessments and avoid AEMO suppression.

title: A map showing the geographic spread of semi-scheduled dispatch errors on Monday 26th May 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A map showing the geographic spread of semi-scheduled dispatch errors on Monday 26th May 2025" posted at 04-06-2025 02:52:00 PM is: On Monday, 26 May 2025, a strong cold front and associated dust storm across southern Australia caused high wind generation and reduced solar irradiance, leading to record NEM-wide wind output of 9,287 MW and multiple grid disturbances. Mainland frequency dropped to 49.843 Hz at 16:15, coinciding with an aggregate semi-scheduled dispatch error of 686 MW. Three wind farms in western Victoria (Golden Plains Units 1 & 2, Murra Warra Unit 2) showed significant underperformance, while material errors occurred across wind and solar units in NSW and QLD, partially offset by overperformance elsewhere. Causes may include forecast inaccuracy, high-speed wind cut-outs, and network constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather (cold front, dust storm) and grid/system instability (frequency drop, dispatch errors). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Single day (26 May 2025), with effects observed during the afternoon and evening dispatch intervals. Its root_cause is: A strong cold front bringing powerful westerly winds and a heavy dust storm that reduced solar irradiance, combined with possible operational factors leading to wind and solar under-/overperformance. Its accident scale is: Aggregate semi-scheduled dispatch error of 686 MW for the 16:15 dispatch interval; three wind units with significant underperformance (each >20 MW); mainland frequency deviation to 49.843 Hz. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 26 May 2025. Its affected region is: Southern Australia (South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled units (wind farms and solar farms) in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Weather-related and operational factors. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Strong cold front swept across southern Australia driving high wind generation; (2) Heavy dust storm moved through SA and VIC reducing solar irradiance; (3) High wind speeds possibly causing cut-outs in wind turbines; (4) Forecast inaccuracy for wind and solar generation; (5) Network constraints not fully reflected in dispatch targets; (6) Other operational factors leading to under- and overperformance.

title: Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 1 – biasing
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examples of self-forecasting behaviours – part 1 – biasing" posted at 03-06-2025 04:15:00 PM is: This article explains how a solar farm in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) intentionally biases its self-forecast lower than actual output to exploit the current 'Causer Pays' mechanism for Regulation FCAS costs. By creating positive deviations during periods when the Frequency Indicator (FI) is positive, the unit earns favorable cost allocation factors, reducing its share of regulation costs. The practice is part of a series on self-forecasting games and is expected to be disrupted by the introduction of Frequency Performance Payments on 8 June 2025, which will replace the FI-based cost allocation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing until June 2025 (pre-reform period). Its root_cause is: exploitation of the Frequency Indicator alignment in the Causer Pays mechanism to minimize Regulation FCAS cost allocation through biased self-forecast. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: Unknown (anonymized example data, article date 03-06-2025). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: semi-scheduled solar generators, AEMO, market participants. Its cause type is: strategic market behavior (biasing). Its causes is: (1) desire to reduce Regulation FCAS costs by earning positive causer pays factors; (2) intentional low biasing of self-forecast relative to actual output; (3) timing deviations to align with positive Frequency Indicator periods.

Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing structural change from September 2001 to present, with the latest policy effective from the weekend of 03-06-2025. Its root_cause is: Historical market evolution and regulatory updates to frequency control ancillary services. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-06-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region of the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy-driven market design changes. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Introduction of FCAS markets in 2001; addition of Fast Frequency Response in October 2023; implementation of Frequency Performance Payments in early 2025.

title: Stacking up: Unlocking multiple revenue streams in the NEM
author: Ben Nethersole
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stacking up: Unlocking multiple revenue streams in the NEM" posted at 02-06-2025 04:15:00 PM is: The article, adapted from a presentation at the Australian Wind Industry Forum, explains how renewable and storage projects in the National Electricity Market (NEM) are increasingly stacking multiple revenue streams and contract types to improve business cases, driven by factors such as high balance-of-plant costs, softening corporate offtake appetite, pricing volatility, and the role of government underwriting schemes like the Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) and NSW Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap's Long-Term Energy Service Agreements (LTESAs). It highlights the shift from long-term single-buyer contracts to diversified strategies, and the need for new capabilities in risk governance and commercial strategy to manage short-term positions and portfolio risk. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing/long-term (market structure evolution, not a discrete event). Its root_cause is: Combination of high construction costs, reduced corporate offtake appetite, increased price volatility, and government underwriting schemes enabling more flexible contracting. Its accident scale is: Unknown (not applicable). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Renewable developers, storage operators, large energy buyers (gentailers, data centers), and offtakers. Its cause type is: Market/policy-driven structural change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High balance of plant costs sustaining elevated PPA prices; softening corporate ambition reducing long-term offtake appetite; large swings in PPA pricing highlighting value of optionality; increased near-term volatility affecting revenue risk perception; developers building in-house trading capability for shorter-term positions; CIS and LTESA underwriting enabling shorter contracts and more flexible bidding.

title: Across 188 Semi-Scheduled DUIDs – Dispatch Error at 16:15 on Monday 26th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Across 188 Semi-Scheduled DUIDs – Dispatch Error at 16:15 on Monday 26th May 2025" posted at 02-06-2025 03:18:00 PM is: On 26 May 2025 at 16:15, a dispatch error across 188 semi-scheduled units (105 solar farms and 83 wind farms) in the NEM caused an aggregate underperformance of 191 MW from solar and 495 MW from wind, leading to a mainland NEM frequency drop below the Normal Operating Frequency Band. Three wind farms alone contributed 553 MW of the error. The incident underscores systemic dispatch performance issues with variable renewable energy (VRE) under high wind conditions, though no individual unit blame is assigned.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (dispatch interval at 16:15 on 26-05-2025). Its root_cause is: Poor collective dispatch performance of semi-scheduled wind and solar farms, with large aggregate dispatch error driving frequency outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band. Its accident scale is: 188 DUIDs affected; aggregate dispatch error of 686 MW (191 MW solar + 495 MW wind); three wind farms accounted for 553 MW of underperformance. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-05-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators, market participants, and all NEM electricity consumers (via frequency instability). Its cause type is: Operational/technical failure (dispatch error of semi-scheduled VRE units). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Heavy wind production across all wind farms in the NEM; (2) Semi-scheduled solar farms showed a skew towards underperformance (55% of units underperforming); (3) Semi-scheduled wind farms had three large underperforming DUIDs contributing 553 MW; (4) Aggregate dispatch error across all 188 units was sufficient to drive frequency below the NOFB.

title: Noting the dust storm on Monday 26th May 2025 – and impact on electricity supply-demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Noting the dust storm on Monday 26th May 2025 – and impact on electricity supply-demand" posted at 02-06-2025 11:38:00 AM is: On 26 May 2025, a severe dust storm swept across southeastern Australia, coinciding with record-high wind generation (9,287 MW) and significant aggregate dispatch errors from large solar and wind farms. The storm caused heavy cloud and dust to roll over areas like Adelaide, leading to abrupt drops in rooftop PV output and consequently two sharp declines in 'Market Demand' in South Australia (235 MW and 213 MW). Additionally, poor collective dispatch performance of variable renewable energy (VRE) drove the mainland NEM frequency below the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) after the 16:15 dispatch interval. These events combined to create notable imbalances between electricity supply and demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 26 May 2025 (single-day event with immediate effects). Its root_cause is: A dust storm reducing rooftop solar generation and exacerbating variability in wind and solar output, combined with existing poor dispatch performance of VRE. Its accident scale is: Regional (affecting South Australia and the broader National Electricity Market mainland). Its dates is: 26 May 2025 (event date) – news posted 02-06-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia and the National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, grid operators, and renewable energy generators (wind and solar). Its cause type is: Natural/Weather-related (dust storm and strong winds). Its causes is: (1) Dust storm reducing rooftop PV output and causing cloud cover; (2) Record-high wind generation leading to oversupply and dispatch errors; (3) Aggregate underperformance of large wind and solar relative to targets; (4) Frequency drop due to poor VRE dispatch performance.

title: Evening price volatility in NSW on Sunday 1st June 2025

classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening price volatility in NSW on Sunday 1st June 2025" posted at 01-06-2025 05:18:00 PM is: On Sunday 1 June 2025, New South Wales experienced evening price volatility after AEMO issued a Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) notice from 17:00 to 20:00, with a required capacity reserve of 1638 MW but only 1128 MW available, indicating a significant shortfall that drove market price action. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 01-06-2025 17:00 to 20:00. Its root_cause is: insufficient capacity reserve due to forecast lack of generation or high demand. Its accident scale is: LOR1 (Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1). Its dates is: 01-06-2025. Its affected region is: NSW region. Its affected key users is: electricity market participants and consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: forecast lack of reserve. Its causes is: [high demand or generation shortfall leading to capacity reserve below requirement].

title: Callide C back online, Sunday 1st June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C back online, Sunday 1st June 2025" posted at 01-06-2025 11:43:00 AM is: The article reports that Callide C3, a coal-fired unit at Callide Power Station in Queensland, returned to service early on 1st June 2025 after a forced outage caused by a clinker-related explosion. The unit ramped up for the morning peak but later reduced output to minimum load as solar generation pushed prices below zero, marking the end of major repairs following the earlier incident; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Unit returned to service on 1st June 2025.

title: A trend of aggregate ‘Dispatch Error’ for each Semi-Scheduled fuel type on Monday 26th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A trend of aggregate ‘Dispatch Error’ for each Semi-Scheduled fuel type on Monday 26th May 2025" posted at 31-05-2025 02:28:00 PM is: This article analyzes aggregate dispatch errors for semi-scheduled solar and wind units on 26 May 2025, which contributed to a mainland system frequency drop below the Normal Operating Frequency Band. It identifies persistent negative dispatch errors during morning solar ramps, and significant underperformance from wind (72% of intervals with underperformance) due to forecasting challenges and a persistence forecasting method, leading to sustained frequency drag and a notable frequency drop just after 16:15. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 26 May 2025, immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Poor dispatch performance and forecasting of variable renewable energy (solar and wind) due to persistence forecasting methods and inherent forecast inaccuracies. Its accident scale is: Unknown (but the frequency deviation briefly fell outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-05-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland (Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown (presumably all grid-connected users). Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Persistent negative dispatch error for solar during morning ramp due to output consistently above target; [2] Consistent underperformance for wind in 72% of dispatch intervals, with deviations up to 598 MW; [3] Use of persistence forecasting method exacerbates errors during ramps; [4] Large deviations (up to 250 MW for solar, up to 10% of aggregate target) causing frequency drag; [5] Specific frequency drop at 16:15 linked to evening ramp wind underperformance.

title: A quick look at system frequency, with 2 x coal units offline overnight into Saturday 31st May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at system frequency, with 2 x coal units offline overnight into Saturday 31st May 2025" posted at 31-05-2025 12:09:00 PM is: The article reports on two unplanned coal unit outages in NSW overnight that caused system frequency disturbances: Vales Point 6 (short-notice shutdown from 114 MW at 23:55 Friday) and Mt Piper 1 (trip from 350 MW at 01:40 Saturday). While the frequency impact of VP6 was minimal, the MP1 trip was clearly visible in frequency traces. Inertia levels were moderate (around 83,000–86,000 MW.s), and the frequency remained within the normal operating band (NOFB), contrasting with a larger frequency drop on 26 May 2025 attributed to poor VRE dispatch. The outages highlight 'old style' frequency disruption from conventional generator failures; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Overnight of 30–31 May 2025 (23:55 to 01:40). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outages of two coal-fired units (Vales Point 6 and Mt Piper 1) in NSW. Its accident scale is: Two units offline; one planned short-notice shutdown (VP6, 114 MW) and one trip (MP1, 350 MW). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 31-05-2025 (main post date); event occurred early 31-05-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and the mainland National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in the NEM (system frequency stability affects grid reliability). Its cause type is: Unplanned generator outages (short-notice shutdown and trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Vales Point 6 taken offline at short notice (shutdown complete) just before 23:55 on 30 May 2025; [2] Mt Piper 1 tripped from minimum load (350 MW) at 01:40 on 31 May 2025.

title: Poor dispatch performance of VRE (collectively) on Monday 26th May 2025 sufficiently large to drive frequency outside of NOFB
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Poor dispatch performance of VRE (collectively) on Monday 26th May 2025 sufficiently large to drive frequency outside of NOFB" posted at 28-05-2025 08:17:00 PM is: On 26 May 2025, the mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a significant frequency drop during the 16:15 dispatch interval, falling outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB), which triggered contingency FCAS responses. This incident was attributed to poor aggregate dispatch performance of variable renewable energy (VRE) units, particularly due to a large positive dispatch error that dragged frequency lower. The root cause lies in ongoing challenges with VRE forecasting, the limitations of the Semi-Scheduled category, and perverse incentives in self-forecasting, highlighting systemic instability risks from VRE integration.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact during the 16:15 dispatch interval on 26 May 2025, with ongoing implications for market design; Its root_cause is: Poor VRE dispatch performance due to forecasting inaccuracies, semi-scheduled operational constraints, and self-forecasting incentives; Its accident scale is: Frequency deviation exceeding NOFB, triggering contingency FCAS activation; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Event date: 26-05-2025, News date: 28-05-2025; Its affected region is: Mainland NEM (Australia, likely all regions except Tasmania); Its affected key users is: System operator, all scheduled and semi-scheduled generators, and electricity consumers; Its cause type is: Technical/operational (forecasting and dispatch errors); Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Inaccurate VRE forecasting; 2) Limitations of Semi-Scheduled category for VRE; 3) Perverse incentives in self-forecasting.

title: New all-time record for NEMwide production from Wind on Monday 26th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record for NEMwide production from Wind on Monday 26th May 2025" posted at 27-05-2025 08:33:00 AM is: On 26 May 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia set a new all-time record for wind generation, reaching 9,287 MW at 21:35 NEM time, surpassing the previous record of 8,431 MW by 10%. The record was driven by high overnight wind speeds and actual output exceeded earlier forecasts. There was 128 MW of curtailment of semi-scheduled wind farms, likely due to network constraints rather than economic reasons. This event highlights the increasing contribution of renewable energy and could influence electricity market prices by potentially lowering spot prices due to high supply. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (hours to days). title: Two large drops in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Monday afternoon 26th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two large drops in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Monday afternoon 26th May 2025" posted at 26-05-2025 01:45:00 PM is: On Monday 26th May 2025, South Australia experienced two large drops in Market Demand: a 235MW drop at 13:31 and a 213MW drop at 14:46. Its causes are: Unknown (no cause events listed).

title: *Might* be a new all-time record for NEMwide wind production on Monday night 26th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "*Might* be a new all-time record for NEMwide wind production on Monday night 26th May 2025" posted at 26-05-2025 08:36:00 AM is: The article reports that on Monday 26th May 2025, NEM-wide wind production reached slightly above 7,000 MW, still 1,000 MW below the all-time record from 30 May 2024, but forecasts suggest a potential new record within the next 12-24 hours, though curtailment due to network or economic factors may affect actual output.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: Market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (hours to days) – potential record wind generation could lower wholesale electricity prices during the period.  
Its root_cause is: High wind speeds across the National Electricity Market (NEM) region.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident).  
Its dates is: 26-05-2025.  
Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – covering eastern and southern Australia (Queensland, New South Wales, Australian Capital Territory, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania).  
Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM (not further specified).  
Its cause type is: Natural (meteorological).  
Its causes is: Favorable wind conditions leading to high wind farm output, forecasted wind capability exceeding 7,000 MW.

title: Forecast for high wind capability across the NEM on Monday 26th May 2025

Its root_cause is: Favourable wind conditions forecasted across the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 26-05-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather / renewable generation forecast. Its causes is: High wind speeds forecasted for 26 May 2025.

title: Buronga No.5 330kV synchronous condenser has been commissioned in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Buronga No.5 330kV synchronous condenser has been commissioned in NSW" posted at 20-05-2025 06:28:00 PM is: The article reports that on 20 May 2025, AEMO issued a market notice announcing the commissioning of the Buronga No.5 330kV synchronous condenser at 17:00 hrs, part of Project Energy Connect Stage 2. This network augmentation occurred alongside noted price increases in the southern half of the NEM, though these were not extreme, attributed to intra-regional congestion in VIC and NSW. The commissioning is expected to improve grid stability and support future power transfers; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: Market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (from 17:00 hrs on 20/05/2025); Its root_cause is: Commissioning of the synchronous condenser to enhance network stability; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-05-2025; Its affected region is: NSW (Buronga area); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Network augmentation; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Commissioning of Buronga No.5 330kV synchronous condenser.

title: Prices up in the southern half of the NEM – on Tuesday 20th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Prices up in the southern half of the NEM – on Tuesday 20th May 2025" posted at 20-05-2025 06:20:00 PM is: On the evening of 20 May 2025, electricity prices rose in the southern half of the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to the trip of Loy Yang A unit 3 (LYA3), leaving two of ten coal units offline (along with YWPS3), combined with very low wind generation in the three southern regions and declining solar output at sunset.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 20 May 2025).  
Its root_cause is: loss of coal-fired generation (two units offline) coupled with low renewable output (wind and solar).  
Its accident scale is: 2 out of 10 coal units offline (specific MW loss not stated).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-05-2025.  
Its affected region is: southern half of the NEM (likely Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania).  
Its affected key users is: wholesale electricity market participants and end consumers in southern NEM.  
Its cause type is: forced generator outages and renewable intermittency.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: LYA3 tripped, YWPS3 remained offline after planned outage, low wind generation, and sunset solar decline.

title: Loy Yang A3 offline on Tuesday afternoon 20th May 2025 – small frequency drop

classified_content: The summary of the news "Loy Yang A3 offline on Tuesday afternoon 20th May 2025 – small frequency drop" posted at 20-05-2025 02:32:00 PM is: A 295MW drop in output from Loy Yang A3 occurred just before 14:06 NEM time on 20 May 2025, taking the unit offline and causing a small frequency drop in the mainland NEM; no further details were provided. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: generator trip (Loy Yang A3 offline). Its accident scale is: 295MW generation loss. Its dates (if different) is: 20-05-2025. Its affected region is: mainland NEM (Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Loy Yang A3 unit trip leading to output drop of 295MW.

title: My unofficial report on an unofficial ‘Energy Week’ in Melbourne 5th to 9th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "My unofficial report on an unofficial ‘Energy Week’ in Melbourne 5th to 9th May 2025" posted at 19-05-2025 05:20:00 PM is: This article provides a detailed, informal recap of two parallel energy conferences held in Melbourne during 5-9 May 2025: the Clean Energy Council's Onshore Wind Industry Forum and the EUAA National Conference. It covers key discussions on the energy transition following the ALP's federal election win, the contentious role of coal (with a notable disagreement on its retirement timeline), rising electricity bills, the need for fully scheduled hybrid generation, demand response experiences from large users, and updates on the Nelson Review. The report highlights a persistent disconnect between renewable developers and energy users, and notes personnel changes and the Callide C power station exiting administration. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (the article is a retrospective conference summary with no specified market impact timeframe). Its root_cause is: Unknown (no single root cause; discussions stem from ongoing transition challenges). Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: 19-05-2025 (post date; event dates 5-9 May 2025). Its affected region is: Melbourne, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Large energy users, renewable developers, policy makers. Its cause type is: Unknown (no direct causal event identified). Its causes is: ALP federal election win; coal retirement debate; electricity price concerns; need for hybrids; demand response discussions; Nelson Review forums; leadership changes at AEMO and CEC; Callide C administration exit.

title: Unplanned outage at Callide C3 extended by 3 days – to Monday 2nd June 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage at Callide C3 extended by 3 days – to Monday 2nd June 2025" posted at 19-05-2025 03:41:00 PM is: CS Energy extended the unplanned outage of the Callide C3 coal-fired unit (CPP_3) by three days to Monday 2nd June 2025, after the unit originally came offline on 4th April 2025 due to clinker issues; the extension was noted in AEMO’s MT PASA data and announced via CS Energy’s news release and LinkedIn. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 4th April 2025 to 2nd June 2025 (extended on 19th May 2025). Its root_cause is: Clinker buildup in the boiler (solidified coal ash) causing the unit to trip and requiring extended maintenance. Its accident scale is: Single-unit outage (Callide C3, 750 MW capacity). Its dates is: 4th April 2025 (initial outage) and 19th May 2025 (extension announcement). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: NEM participants, including generators, retailers, and wholesale electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Unplanned generator outage. Its causes is: Clinker issues (ash fusion and deposition) at the Callide C3 coal-fired unit.

title: Adoption and use of dispatch self-forecasting for solar units, Q1 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Adoption and use of dispatch self-forecasting for solar units, Q1 2025" posted at 19-05-2025 is: The article analyzes the adoption and usage patterns of dispatch self-forecasting for semi-scheduled solar units in Australia's National Electricity Market up to Q1 2025. As of March 2025, 47% of dispatch intervals used a self-forecast—a level lower than any month since December 2023—with 67 out of 120 units (56%) actively self-forecasting. A notable glitch on 30-31 March 2025 caused 14 units to stop using self-forecasts simultaneously, likely a system issue rather than a performance assessment. The analysis comes ahead of the Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) reform starting 8 June 2025, which may incentivize greater adoption to manage costs. Self-forecasting, which provides a more accurate availability forecast than AEMO's default, can help maximize generation and minimize exposure to FPP and regulation FCAS costs, particularly during semi-dispatch cap periods. The article also notes historical trends, including a decline in usage from late 2022 before recovery in mid-2023, and highlights that some units rely on participant suppression or experience AEMO suppression due to performance failures. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2019, with potential increased impact from June 2025 (FPP start). Its root_cause is: Upcoming Frequency Performance Payments reform requiring accurate self-forecasting for cost management, driving analysis of current adoption. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 19-05-2025 (article date); data covers Q1 2025 and up to 15 May 2025; notable incident on 30-31 March 2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled solar unit operators, AEMO, and market participants (including forecasters and traders). Its cause type is: Market reform and operational practice (technical/regulatory). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) reform scheduled for June 2025; 2) Need for improved dispatch accuracy to manage revenues and costs under new market rules; 3) Periodic system glitches (e.g., on 30-31 March 2025) and suppression due to performance assessments or participant choices; 4) Historical adoption patterns influenced by AEMO's initial assessment process and commissioning phases for new units.
