title: Directions up to almost five a day
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Directions up to almost five a day" posted at 16-05-2025 02:00:00 PM is: AEMO CEO Daniel Westerman's speech at the CEDA Climate and Energy Summit on 1 May 2025 highlighted that the shift in Australia’s generation mix has increased power system complexity, evidenced by 1,800 directions to generators in 2024—almost five per day. The article analyzes market intervention notices, unit-specific constraints, and intervention pricing intervals, showing a sharp rise in interventions since 2017, particularly in South Australia, as AEMO issues directions to maintain system security and reliability amid growing distributed PV and minimum system load challenges.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from at least 2017 through 2024 and continuing as generation mix evolves.  
Its root_cause is: The ongoing shift in Australia’s generation mix (increasing renewables and distributed PV) has made power systems more complex, requiring frequent operator interventions to maintain security and reliability.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident; involves 1,800 directions in 2024).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-05-2025 (date of the speech).  
Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market), with notable impacts in South Australia and historically in Queensland.  
Its affected key users is: Registered participants (generators) and market participants subject to AEMO directions.  
Its cause type is: Operational/systemic due to generation mix transition.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Increased complexity from shifting generation mix; growing distributed PV and minimum system load issues; need to maintain or restore power system security and reliability.

title: Five (re)bids for URANQ12 on on Tuesday 13th May 2025 – Part 3 of a Case Study
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Five (re)bids for URANQ12 on on Tuesday 13th May 2025 – Part 3 of a Case Study" posted at 15-05-2025 07:50:00 PM is: This article, part of a case study on evening volatility in the NSW electricity market on Tuesday 13th May 2025, details five rebids submitted by the Uranquinty power station (URANQ12) at various dispatch intervals. The rebids included shifting volume to the market price floor (-$1,000/MWh) ahead of an expected price spike, resetting the unit's fast start profile (reducing time to synchronise and increasing time at minimum load), and adjusting availability based on AEMO's demand forecast, plant conditions, and ambient conditions. These actions reflect strategic fine-tuning by the generator to optimise revenue during anticipated high-price periods, contributing to the observed volatility in the NSW region.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (evening of 13th May 2025, with rebids occurring from 15:30 to 16:55 dispatch intervals).  
Its root_cause is: Generator rebidding to anticipate and exploit expected price spikes during system volatility, driven by operational adjustments and market price forecasts.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident occurred).  
Its dates is: Event date: 13-05-2025; Article posted on 15-05-2025.  
Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales), with some spillover volatility noted in QLD (Queensland).  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely electricity market participants, traders, and AEMO).  
Its cause type is: Operational/Market strategic rebidding.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Expectation of price spike (>$14,000/MWh) led to shifts to -$1,000/MWh; 2) Fast start profile reset (T1 reduced by 17 min, T3 increased by 17 min); 3) 150MW shifted to market price floor and ROCDOWN rate reduced to 3 MW/min due to P5 predispatch demand forecast; 4) Increase in MaxAvail by 19MW due to plant conditions, adding 23MW at floor price; 5) Small 3MW drop in MaxAvail due to ambient conditions.

title: Afternoon volatility begins in NSW on Wednesday 14th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon volatility begins in NSW on Wednesday 14th May 2025" posted at 14-05-2025 04:35:00 PM is: On 14 May 2025, the New South Wales electricity market experienced afternoon price volatility, with a price spike observed at the 16:20 dispatch interval. The volatility was driven by a ramp-up in hydro generation, a constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector forcing heavy southward flow, and low wind production due to both low wind resource and transmission congestion limiting output from wind farms in the south. This pattern mirrored similar behavior noted the previous day, indicating recurring system stress. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (day of event). Its root_cause is: Combination of low wind resource, transmission congestion on the VIC1-NSW1 constraint, and market-driven hydro ramp-up. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-05-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational constraints and renewable variability. Its causes is: 1) Low wind resource; 2) Transmission congestion from VIC1-NSW1 constraint limiting northward flow; 3) Market response with increased hydro generation.

title: Part 2 of a Case Study – looking into evening volatility in NSW on Tuesday 13th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Part 2 of a Case Study – looking into evening volatility in NSW on Tuesday 13th May 2025" posted at 14-05-2025 04:24:00 PM is: This case study examines evening volatility in the NSW electricity market on 13 May 2025, focusing on the TUMUT3 unit’s rebid behavior that moved 1,754 MW of capacity to the –$1,000/MWh price floor while simultaneously reducing its ROCDOWN rate from 100 MW/min to 18 MW/min, coinciding with the invocation of the ‘N::N_CTYS_2’ constraint equation from the ‘N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE’ constraint set, which contributed to a price spike above $10,000/MWh at the 16:35 dispatch interval; the analysis aims to inform rather than imply misconduct. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13–14 May 2025 (evening of 13 May and potential spillover to 14 May). Its root_cause is: Rebidding of TUMUT3 unit (large volume moved to price floor and reduced ramp-down rate) combined with persistent constraint set. Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident, but a significant price volatility event). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Event date: 13-05-2025; Post date: 14-05-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales), with mention of QLD (Queensland). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Rebidding and constraint interaction. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) TUMUT3 unit rebid moved 1,754 MW to –$1,000/MWh price floor; 2) TUMUT3 unit reduced ROCDOWN rate from 100 MW/min to 18 MW/min; 3) ‘N::N_CTYS_2’ constraint equation from ‘N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE’ set remained invoked.

title: CS Energy publishes about ‘Upgrading Reporting Processes …’ on Fri 9th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "CS Energy publishes about ‘Upgrading Reporting Processes …’ on Fri 9th May 2025" posted at 14-05-2025 10:01:00 AM is: CS Energy announced on Friday 9th May 2025 that it will upgrade its reporting systems and processes to support the Queensland Government's Electricity Maintenance Guarantee (EMG), following the first quarterly report under the new framework. The upgrade focuses on improving data governance and reporting of key performance indicators for statutory maintenance, personal safety, process safety, and plant performance. An independent review and audit of governance and assurance processes has commenced to ensure robust performance measurement and avoid misinterpretations. This initiative is part of CS Energy's commitment to delivering reliable baseload power for Queenslanders. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (no immediate market impact; medium-to-long-term reliability improvement). Its root_cause is: Implementation of the Electricity Maintenance Guarantee (EMG) policy by the Queensland State Government. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-05-2025 (media release date). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (indirectly, all Queensland electricity consumers relying on baseload supply). Its cause type is: Policy/administrative. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Government announcement of the Electricity Maintenance Guarantee in October 2024, CS Energy's internal review and audit of reporting systems, completion of first quarterly EMG report].

title: Callide C out of Voluntary Administration
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C out of Voluntary Administration" posted at 14-05-2025 09:59:00 AM is: Callide C power station has exited voluntary administration after Sev.en Global Investments (7GI) acquired a 50% stake and paid off creditors, becoming the sole half-owner of the main operating companies. Concurrently, the Callide C3 unit remains in a clinker outage (generator failure) with the return to service still expected on 30 May 2025. A related network outage constraint set ('N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE') is scheduled to end on 15 May 2025 and contributed to recent electricity market volatility. The ownership of Millmerran power station appears to no longer overlap with Callide C. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures (clinker outage) and grid/system instability (network constraint causing volatility). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The clinker outage has been ongoing since April 2025 (as per topic) with expected end on 30 May 2025; the network constraint contributed to volatility on 13-14 May 2025. Its root_cause is: Clinker formation (likely due to boiler issues) causing the Callide C3 generator to trip and remain offline. Its accident scale is: One unit (Callide C3, ~420 MW) offline; network constraint affecting a transmission line. Its dates is: 14-05-2025 (news date) and the outage period from April 2025 to expected 30-05-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide C power station and NEM region). Its affected key users are: Wholesale electricity market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO) and end consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Operational/mechanical failure and network constraint. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Clinker buildup in Callide C3 boiler leading to unit trip; 2) Planned network outage on a transmission line (constraint set N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE) that restricted transfer capacity, exacerbating price volatility.

title: Late afternoon volatility in NSW (and QLD!) on Tuesday 13th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Late afternoon volatility in NSW (and QLD!) on Tuesday 13th May 2025" posted at 13-05-2025 04:55:00 PM is: At 16:35 NEM time on 13 May 2025, spot electricity prices spiked in NSW due to a combination of constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector flow (southward), a large generation increase from NSW sources, and constraint sets triggered by an outage on the Armidale-Dumaresq (8C) line. This resulted in Contingency Lower FCAS prices spiking in Queensland and Local Price Adjustments driving down prices for many units in southern and western NSW via the N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE constraint set. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: immediate dispatch interval and ongoing until the Armidale-Dumaresq line outage is resolved. Its root_cause: Outage on the Armidale to Dumaresq (8C) transmission line. Its accident scale: single transmission line outage. Its dates: 13-05-2025. Its affected region: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users: electricity generators in NSW and QLD, interconnector operators, and consumers exposed to volatile spot prices. Its cause type: equipment failure / planned outage (not specified). Its causes: Outage on the Armidale-Dumaresq 8C line; activation of constraint set N-ARDM_8C causing FCAS price spikes in QLD; binding of constraint equation N::N_CTYS_2 within set N-CTYS_3L_WG_CLOSE affecting southern NSW generation and VNI interconnector flows, leading to local price adjustments and elevated NSW spot prices.

title: AEMO and AER quarterly digests for 2025 Q1 released on the same day (Wednesday 7th May 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO and AER quarterly digests for 2025 Q1 released on the same day (Wednesday 7th May 2025)" posted at 13-05-2025 04:03:00 PM is: The article reports the joint release of AEMO's and AER's quarterly digests for Q1 2025, highlighting growing renewable capacity setting new records, declining coal availability, and a record NEM underlying demand of 25,162 MW. Operational demand dipped 0.8% due to mild weather in NSW and Queensland, while Queensland set a new peak demand record of 11,144 MW on 22 January. Wholesale prices were lower in northern regions and higher in southern regions, driven by high demand periods and operational issues including planned and unplanned network outages. Media coverage noted potential household price pressures and the contrasting effects of renewables and costly coal/hydro.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal); Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q1 2025 (January to March 2025); Its root_cause is: Reduced coal availability combined with operational issues (network outages) and regionally varying weather conditions affecting demand; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: Q1 2025 (Jan-Mar 2025), with a notable peak demand event on 22 January 2025; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, particularly New South Wales, Queensland, and southern mainland regions; Its affected key users is: Households and wholesale electricity consumers; Its cause type is: Operational issues (planned and unplanned network outages) and fuel supply constraints (reduced coal availability); Its causes are: Decline in coal-fired plant availability, planned and unplanned network outages, mild weather in NSW and Queensland reducing demand, warmer weather in southern regions increasing demand, and a peak demand event in Queensland on 22 January.

title: A forecast LOR2 pops up for South Australia on Thursday 15th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A forecast LOR2 pops up for South Australia on Thursday 15th May 2025" posted at 12-05-2025 03:03:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 12 May 2025 forecasting a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) for South Australia on 15 May 2025 between 10:30 and 12:30, with a capacity reserve requirement of 438 MW but only 401 MW available, a 37 MW shortfall. The drop in available generation is due to a firming capacity outage, combined with forecasts of very low wind and cloud cover reducing solar output. AEMO is seeking a market response and may intervene.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 15 May 2025 10:30-12:30. Its root_cause is: insufficient generation capacity due to a scheduled outage of firming capacity and adverse weather (low wind and cloud cover) reducing renewable output. Its accident scale is: 37 MW reserve shortfall. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-05-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: combination of planned outage and weather. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Firming capacity re-scheduled for outage; Low wind conditions; Cloud cover reducing solar availability.

title: Aligning business cases with the current, and future, reality
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aligning business cases with the current, and future, reality" posted at 08-05-2025 11:21:00 AM is: This article, adapted from a presentation at the Australian Wind Industry Forum, discusses the growing curtailment challenges for wind projects in the National Electricity Market (NEM) driven by increased rooftop and utility-scale solar penetration, which depresses daytime prices and forces wind generation to be constrained. It notes that network outages now occur 6-7 days a week, compounding economic and network curtailment that has reached 5-10% annually and is expected to rise to 15-25%. The author, Jonathon Dyson, advises developers and operators to solidify pre-FID revenue cases by choosing grid-strong locations, ensuring proper equipment for future operating regimes, and accounting for real-world network conditions. For operating projects, he emphasizes continuous monitoring of 288 dispatch intervals, self-forecasting, automated bidding with supervision, and a robust Generator Compliance Management Plan to adapt to the changing network. The piece concludes that planning for high curtailment and exploring hybridisation or FCAS are essential to align business cases with the current and future reality of the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and future (current curtailment trends expected to increase to 15-25%). Its root_cause is: High penetration of rooftop and utility-scale solar displacing wind during peak solar hours, combined with frequent network outages and semi-dispatch caps. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different) is: 08-05-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Wind farm developers, owner-operators, and asset managers. Its cause type is: Market and grid operational issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Growth of rooftop and utility-scale solar suppressing midday prices and curtail wind; Network outages occurring 6-7 days a week; Increased economic and network curtailment for wind farms; Semi-dispatch caps limiting generation during many hours; Lack of adequate forecasting for system normal conditions that include frequent outages].

title: Is the price right? A historical exploration into the NEM’s price cap
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Is the price right? A historical exploration into the NEM’s price cap" posted at 07-05-2025 03:25:00 PM is: This article explores the upcoming increase in the National Electricity Market’s Market Price Cap (MPC) to $20,300/MWh, the first of three scheduled rises, and its implications for investment signals, consumer costs, and market dynamics. It reviews the history of the MPC, economic theory behind the "missing money problem," and analyzes how high prices (>$10,000/MWh) have contributed to wholesale averages, hedging costs, and FCAS revenues across NEM regions since 2010. The analysis highlights regional variability and questions whether a uniform cap increase is effective given existing market power, government interventions, and a patchwork of out-of-market mechanisms (CIS, RRO, RERT) that layer on additional incentives, ultimately shifting risk onto consumers.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term; the MPC increase takes effect from 1 July 2025 (first step) with subsequent steps in following years, influencing investment decisions and consumer costs over the next several years. Its root_cause is: The need to address the "missing money problem" – insufficient revenue from energy-only market to cover long-run capital costs for new generation, particularly as renewable penetration rises and coal plants retire – driving the AEMC’s rule determination to raise the cap to signal investment. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main date: 07-05-2025; event confirmation: late February 2025; effective date: 1 July 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia – all regions (NSW, QLD, VIC, SA, TAS). Its affected key users is: Retailers, consumers (residential and commercial), and generators (especially those investing in peaking or renewable capacity). Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy change (AEMC rule determination). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC rule determination from December 2023; increasing MPC to incentivize investment in reliability; recognition that the energy-only market under-recovers capital costs; rising renewable penetration requiring flexible firming resources; and the need to balance investment signals against consumer protection.

title: ‘Market Demand’ jumps by 404MW on Wednesday morning 7th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ jumps by 404MW on Wednesday morning 7th May 2025" posted at 07-05-2025 08:22:00 AM is: On Wednesday 7 May 2025, Victorian region Market Demand surged by 404 MW between the 07:50 and 07:55 dispatch intervals (NEM time), as recorded by an ez2view notification widget and confirmed by NEMwatch time-series data; an earlier smaller reduction in demand did not meet the 400 MW alert threshold; the event is noted as a short-term demand fluctuation without identified cause or impact. Its relevance level is: Level 2 – Relevant. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate dispatch intervals). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-05-2025. Its affected region is: Victorian region (Victoria, Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: A review of the traditional form of Demand Response (part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A review of the traditional form of Demand Response (part 1)" posted at 06-05-2025 04:30:00 PM is: The article examines a long-standing form of Demand Response (Method #2) in the NEM, where large energy users manually curtail or shift load when spot prices exceed internal triggers. Through 25 years of price data analysis across QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, and TAS, it demonstrates the hypothetical benefit of perfect curtailment (avoiding the highest-price hours) and how that benefit varies significantly by year and region. The article highlights increasing market risk and changing price patterns driven by factors such as a growing schism between energy and reliability services and reduced 'green' bidding volumes, suggesting that this traditional DR method may become more difficult to utilize. It also notes practical caveats like price forecast lags and onsite flexibility constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing / historical trend over 25 years (1999-2023). Its root_cause is: Changing price patterns due to increasing market risk, an emerging schism between 'Anytime/Anywhere Energy' and 'Keeping the Lights on Services', and reduced volume of energy bid in green. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-05-2025 04:30:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions: QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS. Its affected key users is: Large energy users utilizing traditional Demand Response (Method #2). Its cause type is: Market structural / behavioral. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Escalating level of risk in the NEM; 2. Emerging schism between energy supply and reliability services; 3. Reduced volumes of energy bid in 'green' (low-price) categories; 4. Increased price volatility and changing price patterns over time.

title: Submissions from the initial consultation of the Nelson Review made public
author: Dan Lee
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Submissions from the initial consultation of the Nelson Review made public" posted at 06-05-2025 12:24:00 PM is: The Nelson Review, an independent examination of wholesale electricity market settings in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), has made public the submissions received during its initial consultation period (11 December 2024 – 14 February 2025). The review, announced in late November 2024 and led by Tim Nelson, aims to produce actionable recommendations. Upcoming public forums are scheduled across NEM capital cities and online in May–June 2025.  
Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant).  
Its category is: energy policy.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (policy review outcomes may affect market design, but direct price impact is indirect and uncertain).  
Its root_cause is: Government-commissioned review to assess and potentially reform wholesale market settings.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main news date: 06-05-2025; Related dates: late November 2024 (announcement), 11 December 2024 – 14 February 2025 (consultation period), and forum dates from 13 May to 3 June 2025.  
Its affected region is: NEM regions (New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, Victoria, plus online).  
Its affected key users is: Market participants, policymakers, electricity consumers, and stakeholders in the NEM.  
Its cause type is: Policy review.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Announcement of the Nelson Review in late November 2024; [2] Initial consultation period opened 11 December 2024, closed 14 February 2025; [3] Submissions published on 5 May 2025; [4] Public forums scheduled for May–June 2025.

title: Green shoots of pragmatism at the CEC’s Clean Energy Investor Forum in Sydney on 3rd April 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Green shoots of pragmatism at the CEC’s Clean Energy Investor Forum in Sydney on 3rd April 2025?" posted at 05-05-2025 is: The article recounts observations from the CEC's Clean Energy Investor Forum on 3 April 2025, noting a pragmatic shift in discourse around gas-fired generation's necessary role in the NEM, calls for a separate support mechanism for gas, occasional references to coal's insurance value, and debates on risk allocation, government intervention, and the lack of focus on energy users. Its relevance level is: 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Shift in policy discourse towards pragmatism regarding gas and coal in the energy transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 03-04-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Energy users, investors, generators, policymakers. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory discourse. Its causes is: 1) Pragmatic acceptance of gas as a necessary complement to renewables; 2) Discussions on separate support mechanisms for gas; 3) References to coal's role in energy security; 4) Concerns about risk allocation and government intervention; 5) Lack of focus on energy users.

title: There are (at least) 4 Lenses, through which to view Coal Closure
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "There are (at least) 4 Lenses, through which to view Coal Closure" posted at 05-05-2025 10:17:00 AM is: The article outlines four distinct lenses—climate change, technical end-of-life, economic end-of-life, and the diminishing need for coal's bundled services—that must be considered together to fully understand the complexities surrounding coal-fired power station closures in the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting how different stakeholders prioritize different aspects, such as decarbonization targets, asset aging, economic pressures, and evolving grid service requirements. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 05-05-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: 464MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Sunday 4th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "464MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Sunday 4th May 2025" posted at 04-05-2025 10:09:00 AM is: On Sunday 4th May 2025, a 464MW drop in Market Demand occurred in New South Wales, decreasing from 5,869MW at the 09:25 dispatch interval to 5,405MW at 09:30, as flagged by a Notification widget alert in ez2view and NEMwatch. Its causes is: Not available.

title: AEMC underway with review of the Centralised Negawatt Dispatch Mechanism (a.k.a. the WDRM), and consultation closed
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-03-13 AEMC commenced Review of the WDRM
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC underway with review of the Centralised Negawatt Dispatch Mechanism (a.k.a. the WDRM), and consultation closed" posted at 03-05-2025 08:23:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has commenced a review of the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism (WDRM) with consultations closed on 24 April 2025. The WDRM, operational since October 2021, has seen limited participation—only two DRSPs registered 74 MW and dispatched 1,258 MWh, far below initial expectations. The review, triggered by the 'Integrating price-responsive resources into the NEM' determination, will evaluate the mechanism's effectiveness and consider a transition to a two-sided market. The article highlights historical critiques and notes the review's deadline is 24 October 2025, running parallel to the Nelson Review.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, ongoing review expected to influence market design post-October 2025. Its root_cause is: Overly optimistic expectations and design flaws leading to limited participation in the WDRM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Review commenced 13-03-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Demand response service providers (DRSPs), large industrial energy users, market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy review. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Limited participation compared to expectations; 2) Low registered capacity (74 MW) and actual dispatch (1,258 MWh); 3) Lack of scalability and sustainability; 4) Shift towards two-sided market as alternative.

title: Playing ‘Blackout Buzzword Bingo’ with recent events
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "Playing ‘Blackout Buzzword Bingo’ with recent events" posted at 03-05-2025 11:20:00 AM is: The article critiques the hasty, ideologically driven speculation surrounding two recent blackouts—one on the Iberian Peninsula in April 2025 and another in Bali on 2 May 2025—calling for patience until expert investigations are completed, while noting that common unsubstantiated narratives include blaming variable renewable energy, lack of inertia, aging infrastructure, or climate change. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: April 2025 (Iberian Peninsula) and 2 May 2025 (Bali) – immediate disruption. Its root_cause is: Unknown (not determined in the article; only speculative claims presented). Its accident scale is: Large-scale regional blackouts affecting Iberian Peninsula and Bali. Its dates is: April 2025 (Iberian Peninsula) and 2 May 2025 (Bali). Its affected region is: Iberian Peninsula (Spain, Portugal) and Bali (Indonesia). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in the affected regions (households, businesses, critical infrastructure). Its cause type is: Unknown (subject to official investigation). Its causes is: No official cause; speculated potential causes include variable renewable energy penetration, insufficient inertia, aging grid infrastructure, climate change effects, but these are presented as unfounded hobby-horse narratives.

title: Blackout in Bali on 2nd May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Blackout in Bali on 2nd May 2025" posted at 03-05-2025 10:57:00 AM is: A major power outage struck the island of Bali on Friday 2nd May 2025, lasting approximately two hours and severely disrupting tourism, commerce, and public services. The blackout was triggered by a failure in the undersea power cable connecting Java and Bali, which caused all power plants on the island to shut down simultaneously. State utility PLN reported ongoing system stabilization and recovery, with some areas still experiencing partial outages and equipment damage. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (hours to days). Its root_cause is: Failure of undersea power cable connecting Java and Bali. Its accident scale is: Island-wide blackout affecting entire Bali. Its dates is: 02-05-2025. Its affected region is: Bali, Indonesia. Its affected key users is: Tourism industry, commercial establishments, public services, residents. Its cause type is: Technical failure (undersea cable fault). Its causes is: Failure of undersea power cable linking Java and Bali; subsequent shutdown of all power plants on Bali.

title: Adoption and use of dispatch self-forecasting for wind units, Q1 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Adoption and use of dispatch self-forecasting for wind units, Q1 2025" posted at 02-05-2025 03:05:00 PM is: The article examines the uptake of dispatch self-forecasting for semi-scheduled wind units in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) through Q1 2025. As of March 2025, 40% of dispatch intervals use a self-forecast, coming from 39 out of 82 wind units (48% of units), with an additional three units in commissioning. Historical data shows adoption surged in 2021 and remained steady around 35% until a slight rise in 2024. In Q1 2025, most units showed regular usage, but an unexpected glitch on 30-31 March caused 19 units to simultaneously lose self-forecasts. The upcoming Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) reform in June 2025 is expected to drive further adoption as wind farms seek to minimize costs through improved availability forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since 2019, with significant developments in Q1 2025 and anticipated impact from June 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Regulatory changes (FPP reform) and market incentives for wind farms to improve forecasting accuracy to reduce exposure to frequency-related costs and regulation FCAS costs. Its accident scale is: None (no accident; a system glitch affected 19 units temporarily). Its dates is: 02-05-2025 (article date), with data covering up to 29 April 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled wind units (wind farms) in the NEM. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational. Its causes is: Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) reform; mandatory initial assessment process for self-forecasting; participant decisions to adopt self-forecasting; system glitch on 30-31 March 2025 causing simultaneous suppression for 19 units.

title: Four days in the NEM in four minutes
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four days in the NEM in four minutes" posted at 30-04-2025 03:17:00 PM is: The article presents a time-lapse video of market activity in Queensland and New South Wales over four days (April 7–10, 2025), highlighting elevated volatility, price spikes above $15,000/MWh, significant variable renewable energy curtailment, and persistent network congestion. A key driver was a sudden clinker incident that caused a long forced outage at the 466 MW Callide C3 unit in Queensland several days earlier, reducing dispatchable supply. The video demonstrates how congestion, interconnector limits, and binding constraints dynamically affect pricing and generator dispatch. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: April 7–10, 2025. Its root_cause is: Clinker incident at Callide C3 unit causing forced outage. Its accident scale is: One unit of 466 MW forced offline. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: April 7–10, 2025. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in Queensland and New South Wales. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Clinker incident at Callide C3, forced outage, simultaneous other coal outages, network congestion, binding constraints.

title: Gremlins in the EMMS upgrade to Data Model v5.5, making ‘the Resurrection of Old Coal’ appear on the cards
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Gremlins in the EMMS upgrade to Data Model v5.5, making ‘the Resurrection of Old Coal’ appear on the cards" posted at 30-04-2025 08:18:00 AM is: A data glitch in AEMO's rollout of EMMS Data Model v5.5 has caused old, retired coal units to incorrectly appear as coming back online in the Generator Outages widget of ez2view software, but this is a false representation due to a bug in the MMS table DUDETAIL (Case CS0022196), not an actual physical return of the units. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Minimal to none; the glitch may cause temporary confusion in data queries but does not alter physical market operations. Its root_cause is: A software bug introduced during the EMMS Data Model v5.5 upgrade, specifically in the DUDETAIL table. Its accident scale is: No physical accident; a data representation error. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 30-04-2025 08:18:00 AM (same as posted date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia (AEMO jurisdiction). Its affected key users is: AEMO, electricity market participants (generators, retailers, traders) using EMMS data feeds and ez2view software. Its cause type is: Software/IT system glitch. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) AEMO implementing EMMS Data Model v5.5 upgrade; 2) A bug in the MMS table DUDETAIL causing incorrect display of retired coal unit status.

title: Major power outage on the Iberian Peninsula on Monday 28th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
topic: April 2025 power outage on Iberian Peninsula in Europe
classified_content: The summary of the news "Major power outage on the Iberian Peninsula on Monday 28th April 2025" posted at 29-04-2025 03:54:00 PM is: A major power outage struck the Iberian Peninsula on Monday 28 April 2025, affecting Spain, Portugal, and parts of southern France. The outage began around 12:30 local time when a rare atmospheric phenomenon caused extreme temperature variations, leading to induced atmospheric vibrations on 400 kV transmission lines. These vibrations caused synchronization failures and cascading disconnections of power plants, including a sudden loss of 15 GW of generation in Spain (60% of national demand) within seconds. Grid frequency dropped below 50 Hz. Restoration was slow, with power gradually returning by the following day. Portuguese and Spanish authorities dismissed cyberattack speculation, attributing the event to natural causes. The incident highlighted vulnerabilities in interconnected European grids.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate from 28 April 2025, with restoration ongoing; short-term impacts on supply and prices likely lasting days to weeks.; Its root_cause is: Rare atmospheric phenomenon causing induced atmospheric vibrations on high-voltage lines, leading to synchronization failures and cascading disconnections.; Its accident scale is: Major; loss of 15 GW in Spain alone, affecting entire Iberian Peninsula and parts of France, millions without power.; Its dates is: 28 April 2025 (event date); news date 29 April 2025.; Its affected region is: Iberian Peninsula (Spain, Portugal, southern France); Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in affected regions, including residential, commercial, industrial, and critical infrastructure.; Its cause type is: Natural/atmospheric (induced atmospheric vibration) with technical consequences.; Its causes is: 1) Extreme temperature variations in interior Spain; 2) Induced atmospheric vibration on 400 kV lines; 3) Synchronization failures between electrical systems; 4) Cascading disconnections of power plants; 5) Possible break-up of interconnection with France.

title: Do we have enough gas to wait until nuclear comes along?
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "Do we have enough gas to wait until nuclear comes along?" posted at 28-04-2025 11:10:00 AM is: The article argues that Australia's Coalition policy to replace retiring coal-fired power plants with nuclear (starting around 2037) and use gas as an interim fuel is fundamentally flawed because domestic gas supplies are insufficient to cover the gap—even with a proposed gas reserve—and because aging coal plants become less reliable, forcing more gas-fired generation and driving up electricity and gas prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 2025 through the 2030s and 2040s as coal units retire and nuclear is delayed. Its root_cause is: aging coal fleet necessitating replacement combined with a policy choice that relies on insufficient gas supply and delayed nuclear build. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 28-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly the east coast gas market. Its affected key users are: electricity consumers, gas consumers, and industrial/commercial users. Its cause type is: policy-driven and technical (reliability). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: (1) Two-thirds of NEM coal plants will exceed average retirement age by 2030; (2) Coalition plans to replace coal with nuclear after 2037 and use gas as a bridge; (3) East coast gas market already projected to face shortages; (4) Deferred coal retirement leads to more frequent outages, increasing gas demand; (5) Proposed domestic gas reserve (50-100 petajoules) is overwhelmed by the extra gas needed to replace coal generation or cover coal outages.

title: Reliability Panel comments on AEMO’s Transition Plan for System Security on 23rd April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
topic: AEMO's annual 'Transition Plan for System Security' (TPSS)
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reliability Panel comments on AEMO’s Transition Plan for System Security on 23rd April 2025" posted at 24-04-2025 12:40:00 PM is: The Reliability Panel issued a formal 4-page response on 23 April 2025 to AEMO's first annual Transition Plan for System Security (TPSS), originally released in December 2024, offering comments and recommendations to enhance grid security during the energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing—the panel's feedback will influence future system security planning and market operation as the transition progresses. Its root_cause is: The need for a secure and reliable transition of the power system, as outlined in AEMO's TPSS. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 23-04-2025 (date of the panel's response). Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all NEM participants and system operators). Its cause type is: Regulatory review and comment. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO released its first Transition Plan for System Security in December 2024; the Reliability Panel reviewed the plan and provided formal comments on 23 April 2025.

title: Games that self-forecasters can* play (Part 1 – An Overview)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Games that self-forecasters can* play (Part 1 – An Overview)" posted at 22-04-2025 08:00:00 AM is: This article examines the strategic "games" that semi-scheduled generators in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) can play using self-forecasting to minimize their Regulation FCAS costs under the flawed "Causer Pays" method (leg 1) and to avoid AEMO suppression by voluntarily self-suppressing forecasts during assessment windows (leg 2). It details AER warnings, the predictable and gameable Frequency Indicator, and the upcoming transition to Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) on 8 June 2025, which will use a less predictable Frequency Measure. The article also lists clues to detect such behaviors, such as low submission rates, patterns during Semi-Dispatch Cap periods, and anomalous "lunar megawatts". Its relevance level is: Level 2 – Relevant. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing – the practices have been possible since September 2019, with the regulatory change to FPP on 8 June 2025 altering the incentive structure. Its root_cause is: The flawed "Causer Pays" method for allocating Regulation FCAS costs, combined with a predictable Frequency Indicator and optional self-forecast submission rules that enable gaming. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 22-04-2025 (news date; the article references historical events from December 2024 to March 2025 and a future date of 8 June 2025). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators (wind and solar farms), self-forecast vendors, AEMO, AER, and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market design flaws. Its causes is: (1) The "Causer Pays" method uses a flawed Frequency Indicator that is predictable and often opposite to actual frequency; (2) Self-forecast submission is optional, allowing generators to choose when to submit; (3) AEMO's sequential suppression windows (1-week, 4-week, 8-week) can be avoided by voluntary self-suppression to prevent AEMO assessment; (4) The presence of semi-dispatch cap periods creates opportunities to suppress forecasts when constrained down; (5) The ability to use FCAS portfolios to net out costs, which will be removed under FPP.

title: A quick walk through a few volatile evening periods
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick walk through a few volatile evening periods" posted at 21-04-2025 04:59:00 PM is: The article analyzes price volatility during evening periods on 7–10 April 2025 in the Queensland and New South Wales electricity regions, driven by a combination of unplanned generator outages (Callide C3 and Bayswater units), low availability of firming capacity, aggressive bidding patterns, and variability from solar and wind resources, leading to tight supply-demand balances and price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 7–10 April 2025 (the evening volatile periods). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outages at Callide C3 and temporary unavailability of Bayswater coal units, combined with low availability of gas, hydro, battery, and negawatt capacity, and the natural decline of solar at sunset plus wind lulls with network curtailment. Its accident scale is: Multiple unplanned generator outages (at least two coal units) affecting capacity availability. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 7–10 April 2025. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales (Australia). Its affected key users is: Wholesale electricity market participants and end consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Technical failure / unplanned outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Unplanned outage at Callide C3 coal unit, (2) Temporary unavailability of Bayswater coal units, (3) Low availability of gas and liquid fuelled generators, (4) Low availability of hydro units, (5) Low available capacity from BESS (batteries) relative to maximum capacity, (6) Very low registered and available negawatt capacity not timed for peak periods, (7) Solar generation decline at sunset, (8) Cyclic wind lull exacerbated by network curtailment on 8 April.

title: AEMO publishes draft ‘Congestion Information Resource Guidelines’ – submissions due 19th May 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes draft ‘Congestion Information Resource Guidelines’ – submissions due 19th May 2025" posted at 19-04-2025 07:01:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) published a draft report on the Congestion Information Resource Guidelines on 15 April 2025, seeking stakeholder consultation. The draft follows earlier consultation stages and includes key dates: submissions due 19 May 2025 and final report expected 24 July 2025. The guidelines aim to improve information about congestion in the electricity network, discussed in an EWCF meeting on 8 April 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with final report expected July 2025; no immediate price impact. Its root_cause is: Unknown (regulatory process to improve congestion data). Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Market participants, generators, network operators. Its cause type is: Regulatory. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [AEMO consultation on Congestion Information Resource Guidelines; previous consultation submissions; extension of time for consultation due to complexity].

title: AEMO published its HLIA for the ISF Implementation … and seeking input on draft ‘Security Enablement Procedures’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO published its HLIA for the ISF Implementation … and seeking input on draft ‘Security Enablement Procedures’" posted at 19-04-2025 06:19:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released a High-Level Implementation Assessment (HLIA) for the implementation of the AEMC's Improving Security Frameworks (ISF) Rule Determination from March 2024, alongside a draft Security Enablement Procedure (SO_OP_3720) for public consultation. Key milestones include publication of the final procedure by 31 August 2025 and commencement of full enablement obligations on 2 December 2025, with submissions due by 8 May 2025; the document outlines the complexity of operationalising security contracts and system impacts. Its relevance level is: Level 3 - Less Relevant. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From December 2025 onwards (operational). Its root_cause is: AEMC rule determination and AEMO's implementation process. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 17-04-2025 (HLIA publication), 07-04-2025 (consultation paper publication), 31-08-2025 (milestone), 02-12-2025 (milestone), and main date 19-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Market participants, generators, and system operators. Its cause type is: Regulatory update and consultation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO published HLIA for ISF rule implementation; AEMO published draft Security Enablement Procedures for consultation; key milestones set for August and December 2025.

title: AEMO commenced a consultation on the Constraint Formulation Guidelines
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO commenced a consultation on the Constraint Formulation Guidelines" posted at 19-04-2025 05:40:00 PM is: On 7 April 2025, AEMO launched a consultation on the Constraint Formulation Guidelines, driven by the AEMC's 28 March 2024 Improving Security Frameworks (ISF) Final Determination and the ongoing Congestion Information Resource consultation. The 11-page paper addresses three aspects: (1) new security service and inertia constraint equations scheduled for implementation in a system security services scheduler by December 2025, (2) a review of the minimum left-hand side factor threshold (0.07) set in 2010, and (3) updates for voluntarily scheduled resources per the IPRR rule change. Submissions are due by 17:00 Melbourne time on 8 May 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term; key changes (security services scheduler) take effect December 2025, with guidelines to be published by August 2025. Its root_cause is: AEMC's ISF Final Determination and the need to update constraint formulation to accommodate new security services, address stakeholder feedback on LHS factors, and implement IPRR rules. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-04-2025 (consultation commencement). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Market participants, transmission network service providers (TNSPs), generators, and other stakeholders. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Policy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC's ISF Final Determination on 28 March 2024, stakeholder feedback on minimum LHS factor thresholds, and the need to implement the IPRR Rule Change for voluntarily scheduled resources.

title: Drop in NEM Mainland Frequency appearing to coincide with trips at New England Solar Farm, on Saturday 19th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Drop in NEM Mainland Frequency appearing to coincide with trips at New England Solar Farm, on Saturday 19th April 2025" posted at 19-04-2025 03:58:00 PM is: On 19 April 2025, a drop in NEM Mainland frequency coincided with the simultaneous trip of both units at New England Solar Farm in NSW, resulting in a loss of 349MW supply. AEMO issued market notices declaring a non-credible contingency event, with the cause initially unknown. The units were returned to service on 20 April, but after further information, AEMO reclassified the event as a credible contingency until August 2025 when the reclassification was cancelled. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 19 April 2025 (trip) to 20 August 2025 (cancellation of reclassification), with return to service on 20 April 2025. Its root_cause is: Initially unknown; later identified but retracted; ultimately resolved by participant. Its accident scale is: Loss of 349MW (comparable to a coal

title: AEMC draft determination, about ‘Allowing AEMO to accept cash as credit support’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC draft determination, about ‘Allowing AEMO to accept cash as credit support’" posted at 19-04-2025 11:51:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) issued a draft determination on 3 April 2025, proposing to allow cash as credit support up to $5 million, along with surety bonds and a broader pool of credit support providers, to help market participants meet prudential requirements without weakening the system; this rule change, seeking stakeholder feedback by 15 May 2025, stems from earlier concerns about Delta Electricity's Vales Point coal plant facing a December 2024 credit support deadline, though the plant has continued operating into 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term, pending final rule implementation (likely later in 2025) which could prevent the closure of a coal plant and thus affect supply and prices. Its root_cause is: The need for Vales Point (and potentially other participants) to secure credit support to continue operating under existing prudential rules, prompting a rule change to increase flexibility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 3 April 2025 (draft determination), 15 May 2025 (feedback deadline), and earlier referenced dates of 8 October 2024 and 21 November 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, with particular impact on New South Wales where Vales Point is located. Its affected key users are: Market participants such as generators (especially Delta Electricity/Vales Point) and retailers required to provide credit support. Its cause type is: Regulatory/financial. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: (1) Delta Electricity submitted a 'relatively urgent' rule change proposal in October 2024 regarding the December 2024 credit support deadline; (2) The rule change reverted to the slower standard consultation process in November 2024; (3) AEMC made a draft determination on 3 April 2025 supporting cash as credit support.

title: AER suspends the MIC component of STIPS following review
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-04-17 AER review of STIPS for TNSPs
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER suspends the MIC component of STIPS following review" posted at 17-04-2025 08:00:00 PM is: On 17 April 2025, the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) published its Final Decision on the Service Target Performance Incentive Scheme (STIPS) for transmission network service providers (TNSPs), suspending the Market Impact Component (MIC) after finding it is no longer fit for purpose due to the energy transition. The rapid growth of renewables, increased congestion on radial lines, and more outages from high transmission investment have caused a significant rise in MIC events, often resulting in maximum penalties regardless of TNSP actions. The AER will form a working group with stakeholders to develop an alternative by mid-to-late 2026, while improving transparency on outage management. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From April 2025 until a replacement mechanism is implemented (expected mid-to-late 2026). Its root_cause is: The energy transition – a shift from centrally located thermal generation to geographically dispersed, weather-dependent renewables – has changed transmission network demands, rendering the MIC ineffective and causing excessive penalties. Its accident scale is: Unknown (regulatory decision, not an accident). Its dates is: 17-04-2025 08:00:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: TNSPs, AEMO, generators, consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy change. Its causes is: (1) High investment in renewables leading to increased congestion and outages; (2) The MIC no longer providing correct incentives; (3) Stakeholder consensus that the MIC is not fit for purpose.

title: Three units at Bayswater (temporarily) offline, on Tuesday 15th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three units at Bayswater (temporarily) offline, on Tuesday 15th April 2025" posted at 15-04-2025 10:44:00 AM is: On 15 April 2025, a plant issue at Bayswater unit 4 forced the unit offline, leaving only one of four Bayswater units online and reducing total operational coal units in NSW to seven (as both Mt Piper units were on planned outage). The outage is expected to last until 23 April 2025, temporarily tightening supply in the region; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 15 April 2025 to 23 April 2025. Its root_cause is: Plant issue at Bayswater unit 4. Its accident scale is: Three Bayswater units offline (one forced, two combined planned/forced) and only seven coal units online in NSW. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-04-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Forced outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Plant issue at Bayswater unit 4.

title: Mainland system frequency in the afternoon of April 10, 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland system frequency in the afternoon of April 10, 2025" posted at 11-04-2025 12:19:00 PM is: On April 10, 2025, at 16:01, a system frequency dip was observed in the mainland grid, with the frequency dropping to 49.824 Hz and remaining near the lower normal operating frequency band (NOFB) for a short period, as recorded by a high-resolution frequency logger; the decline occurred over several seconds with only moderate rates of change, indicating a grid instability event without an explicitly stated root cause. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours) – the frequency dip itself is brief, but market impacts (e.g., contingency reserves, frequency control) may have been triggered. Its root_cause is: Unknown (not specified in the article). Its accident scale is: Minor (a frequency dip within operational limits, no blackout or large-scale disruption reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-04-2025 (event date, distinct from the news post date). Its affected region is: Mainland (likely the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown (no specific users mentioned). Its cause type is: Unknown (possible causes: sudden load change, generator trip, or transmission fault, but not stated). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: A sudden imbalance between generation and load leading to a frequency drop, with no further details provided.

title: Prices in QLD and NSW rise into the four digit mark on Thursday 10th April 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prices in QLD and NSW rise into the four digit mark on Thursday 10th April 2025" posted at 10-04-2025 08:30:00 PM is: On April 10, 2025, spot prices in Queensland and New South Wales spiked above $1,000/MWh during the 17:30-18:00 dispatch intervals, marking the fourth consecutive afternoon of elevated pricing. This price surge is attributed to the extended unplanned outage of Callide C3, a major coal-fired unit, combined with the inherently volatile Q2 season in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day) and ongoing for at least four afternoons. Its root_cause is: Extended unplanned outage of Callide C3. Its accident scale is: Single unit outage (Callide C3). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-04-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown (electricity consumers in the NEM region). Its cause type is: Unplanned generator outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Callide C3 extended unplanned outage, seasonal volatility in Q2].

title: Mainland system frequency on April 10, 2025, associated with VIC transmission outage
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland system frequency on April 10, 2025, associated with VIC transmission outage" posted at 10-04-2025 12:32:00 PM is: On April 10, 2025, an unplanned outage of the APD–Heywood–Mortlake 500 kV transmission line in Victoria caused a 582 MW drop in market demand and a frequency spike peaking at 50.164 Hz, briefly exceeding the normal operating frequency band. Frequency recovered within seconds and the line was restored by 09:48; the disturbance was well within system operating standards. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of the APD–Heywood–Mortlake 500 kV transmission line. Its accident scale is: Single transmission line outage (specific scale unknown). Its dates is: 10-04-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, mainland Australia (interconnected grid). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in the region). Its cause type is: Unplanned transmission line outage. Its causes is: ["Unplanned outage of APD – Heywood – Mortlake 500 kV line"].

title: A boost in transfer capacity with PEC Stage 1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A boost in transfer capacity with PEC Stage 1" posted at 10-04-2025 11:22:00 AM is: AEMO has released PEC Stage 1 limits effective 11 April 2025, increasing transfer capacity between Victoria and South Australia: PEC-1 at 150 MW both directions and combined Heywood+PEC1 limits of 750 MW (Vic to SA) and 700 MW (SA to Vic), while existing Heywood limits remain unchanged. This follows successful testing on 31 January 2025 and release of hold points 4 and 5, with further testing opportunities planned. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 11 April 2025 onwards. Its root_cause is: Successful completion of PEC Stage 1 testing and approval of hold points 4 and 5. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: 11-04-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, Transgrid, Electranet, and NEM participants. Its cause type is: Infrastructure upgrade / capacity release. Its causes is: 1. Testing of PEC Stage 1 completed on 31 Jan 2025; 2. Hold points 4 and 5 released; 3. AEMO approval.

title: AEMO Market Notice 126465 notes transmission link back in service
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "AEMO Market Notice 126465 notes transmission link back in service" posted at 10-04-2025 10:52:00 AM is: AEMO issued Market Notice 126465 confirming that the APD–Heywood–Mortlake 500 kV transmission line in Victoria, which had suffered an unplanned outage earlier that morning, was successfully returned to service at 0948 hrs on 10 April 2025. The outage had previously caused a large system frequency spike and a 582 MW instantaneous drop in market demand, linked to the Portland smelter. The cause was identified and resolved, and the associated inter-regional transfer constraint sets have been revoked, restoring normal grid operation.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact (outage and restoration within the same day, during the morning of 10 April 2025).  
Its root_cause is: The cause of the unplanned outage was identified and resolved, but the specific technical reason is not disclosed in the notice.  
Its accident scale is: Unplanned outage of a major 500 kV transmission line in Victoria.  
Its dates is: 10-04-2025.  
Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (VIC region of the National Electricity Market).  
Its affected key users is: Portland aluminium smelter (large industrial load) and any market participants relying on inter-regional transfers via the Heywood–Mortlake corridor.  
Its cause type is: Transmission line outage (unplanned).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Unplanned outage of the APD–Heywood–Mortlake 500 kV line; cause subsequently identified and resolved].

title: Another long forced outage at Callide C3 … April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another long forced outage at Callide C3 … April 2025" posted at 10-04-2025 10:31:00 AM is: On 4 April 2025, a significant clinker (hardened ash) detached from the internal boiler wall of Callide C3, a supercritical coal-fired unit at Callide Power Station in central Queensland, causing a pressure spike that tripped the unit. CS Energy reported that unit protection systems worked as designed, no injuries occurred, and Workplace Health and Safety Queensland was notified. The forced outage is expected to last until 30 May 2025 (56 days), with repairs needed on cladding and lagging. Investigations are ongoing, and the outage has contributed to evening price volatility in the Queensland electricity market. This event follows a history of long outages at Callide C, including the 1193-day outage of sibling unit C4 after a 2021 catastrophe. The unit is 50% owned by CS Energy and operated by them; the other three units at Callide remain online. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: generator failure. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 4 April 2025 to 30 May 2025 (56-day forced outage). Its root_cause is: Clinker detachment from boiler wall caused a pressure spike, triggering unit trip and requiring repairs to cladding and lagging. Its accident scale is: Single generating unit (Callide C3, capacity ~424 MW) forced offline for 56 days. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: Event start 04-04-2025, expected return 30-05-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide Power Station near Biloela). Its affected key users are: Queensland electricity market participants; wholesale electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Technical/operational failure (clinker incident in boiler). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Clinker formation in boiler (common in coal-fired stations); 2) Detachment of clinker caused a pressure spike; 3) Protection systems shut down unit; 4) Damage to cladding and lagging requires repairs.

title: AEMO Market Notice 126464 confirms trip transmission (linked to the APD smelter)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO Market Notice 126464 confirms trip transmission (linked to the APD smelter)" posted at 10-04-2025 08:58:00 AM is: AEMO Market Notice 126464, issued at 08:49 on 10 April 2025, reports an unplanned outage of the APD – Heywood – Mortlake 500 kV line in Victoria at 08:25, which triggered inter-regional transfer limit variations and invoked constraint set V-HYMO affecting multiple interconnectors. This transmission trip is linked to a 582 MW instantaneous drop in Victorian market demand and a system frequency spike, with the Portland aluminium smelter (APD) suspected as the cause of the demand reduction. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (from 08:25 on 10 April 2025 onward, with constraints applied from 08:35). Its root_cause is: unplanned outage of the APD – Heywood – Mortlake 500 kV line, which likely led to the trip of the Portland aluminium smelter. Its accident scale is: transmission line outage (500 kV) and loss of a large industrial load (~582 MW). Its dates is: 10-04-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, specifically the Heywood–Mortlake corridor and the Portland area. Its affected key users is: Portland aluminium smelter (APD) and downstream electricity consumers on affected interconnectors. Its cause type is: unplanned outage (equipment failure or fault). Its causes is: unplanned outage of APD – Heywood – Mortlake 500 kV line, resulting in constraint set V-HYMO and a sudden drop in demand likely due to smelter trip.

title: 582MW (instantaneous?) drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria delivers large System Frequency spike – Thursday 10th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "582MW (instantaneous?) drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria delivers large System Frequency spike – Thursday 10th April 2025" posted at 10-04-2025 08:45:00 AM is: A large 582MW drop in market demand in Victoria caused a rapid system frequency spike, indicating a real event rather than a data glitch, with further analysis pending. Its relevance level is: 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Unknown (sudden drop in market demand). Its accident scale is: 582MW. Its dates is: 10-04-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, NEM, Australia. Its affected key users is: All grid-connected users (due to frequency deviation). Its cause type is: Demand drop (instantaneous). Its causes is: A 582MW drop in ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria.

author: Dan Lee
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Short burst of high prices in QLD and NSW on Wednesday 9th April 2025" posted at 09-04-2025 06:22:00 PM is: On 9 April 2025, a short burst of high spot prices occurred in Queensland and New South Wales, starting from the 17:10 dispatch interval, continuing a pattern observed earlier that week. Its cause type is: Unknown.  
Its causes are: Unknown.

title: First address by QLD’s new Energy Minister (David Janetzki) at Queensland Energy Club on Tuesday 8th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First address by QLD’s new Energy Minister (David Janetzki) at Queensland Energy Club on Tuesday 8th April 2025" posted at 09-04-2025 02:57:00 PM is: In his inaugural public address, Minister David Janetzki outlined Queensland's Energy Roadmap, which formally scraps the previous government's renewable energy targets, extends the life of coal-fired generation, and orders a review of the 75% by 2035 emissions reduction target, citing massive cost blowouts in the CopperString transmission line and hydro projects. The policy shift, widely covered by media as a doubling down on coal and gas, aims to deliver affordable, reliable, and sustainable electricity, but has drawn criticism from clean energy groups and industry observers. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term policy shift affecting generation mix and price trajectory. Its root_cause is: Political decision to prioritize coal and gas over renewables due to cost blowouts and reliability concerns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-04-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, energy companies, and investors. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes is: Scrapping renewables targets, extension of coal-fired generation, review of emissions reduction target, and cost blowouts of transmission and hydro projects.

title: A few stories in (coal) generator outages (at Wednesday 9th April 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A few stories in (coal) generator outages (at Wednesday 9th April 2025)" posted at 2025-04-09 08:03:00 AM is: This brief article references a snapshot of the 'Generator Outages' widget from the ez2view platform at the 07:55 dispatch interval on Wednesday 9th April 2025, highlighting several coal-fired generator outages but providing no further explanation or annotations. title: Price blip in QLD and NSW on Tuesday 8th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price blip in QLD and NSW on Tuesday 8th April 2025" posted at 08-04-2025 06:38:00 PM is: On 8 April 2025, a brief price spike occurred in Queensland and New South Wales electricity markets, with prices reaching at or above $1,000/MWh during the 17:30 dispatch interval, driven by low wind conditions coinciding with sunset that reduced wind generation output. The aggregate wind yield had ramped back to ~1,000 MW NEM-wide and further ramping prevented additional price escalation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues (wind generation). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term, occurring during a single dispatch interval on the evening of 8 April 2025. Its root_cause is: Lower wind conditions at sunset caused a drop in wind generation, leading to insufficient supply and price spikes. Its accident scale is: Minor price blip; no physical accident. Its dates is: 08-04-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales (QLD and NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural/weather-driven (low wind). Its causes is: Lower wind conditions at sunset, reduced wind generation output, and coincidence with evening demand peak.

title: Evening volatility in NSW and QLD on Monday 7th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
