classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in NSW and QLD on Monday 7th April 2025" posted at 07-04-2025 05:55:00 PM is: On the evening of Monday 7th April 2025, the NEM experienced volatility in New South Wales and Queensland, highlighted by a ~350MW drop in Market Demand in NSW at the 17:35 dispatch interval, likely caused by spot-exposed demand response from large energy users reacting to market conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Evening of 7th April 2025, short-term (hours). Its root_cause is: High spot prices triggering demand response from large energy users. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 07-04-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users is: Large energy users (spot-exposed). Its cause type is: Demand response. Its causes is: Large energy users reducing demand due to spot price exposure.

title: The challenge of imperfect foresight: Storage operation in a highly renewable NEM
author: Josh Boegheim
classified_content: The summary of the news "The challenge of imperfect foresight: Storage operation in a highly renewable NEM" posted at 07-04-2025 11:06:00 AM is: This article by Josh Boegheim from Queensland University of Technology and Powerlink explores the challenge of imperfect foresight for storage operation in a highly renewable National Electricity Market (NEM), using Queensland's projected 2035 grid as a case study. By comparing perfect foresight, deterministic, and stochastic dispatch models based on historic weather and forecast data, the study finds that imperfect foresight leads to 7-17% higher dispatch costs, with stochastic models reducing unserved energy and better valuing firming assets. The research highlights the need for risk-aware modelling and improved forecasting to support a cost-effective energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (future grid operation up to 2035). Its root_cause is: Imperfect weather forecasting and limited foresight in storage dispatch decisions. Its accident scale is: None (modelling study). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 07-04-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, Powerlink, storage operators, grid planners. Its cause type is: Operational and modelling uncertainty. Its causes is: Limited forecast accuracy beyond 7 days; deterministic models ignoring uncertainty; lack of risk-aware dispatch; weather-driven renewable variability.

title: Questions and concerns, about proposals to shift the ~23 second dead zone within the Dispatch Interval (ICF002)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Questions and concerns, about proposals to shift the ~23 second dead zone within the Dispatch Interval (ICF002)" posted at 07-04-2025 09:36:00 AM is: This article raises concerns about AEMO's proposal to shift the approximately 23-second 'dead zone' in the dispatch process earlier in time rather than shortening it, questioning whether this merely relocates the problem and highlighting potential negative interactions with Gate Closure #2 (bid submission) and Gate Closure #3 (VRE forecast deadlines), which could reduce market efficiency and accuracy. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (proposed change with no specific impact timeframe). Its root_cause is: Proposal to shift the 'dead zone' without addressing the underlying issue of its length, and potential misalignment with existing gate closure processes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Market participants (including generators and semi-scheduled units) and AEMO. Its cause type is: Market design/operational change. Its causes are: 1) Shifting the dead zone ~20 seconds earlier without solving its root cause; 2) Creating a mismatch between the new dispatch interval (e.g., 10:04:40 to 10:09:40) and the pricing interval (10:05 to 10:10); 3) Adverse interaction with Gate Closure #2 (bid submission at ~12-13 seconds before interval start) where a later snapshot could conflict with automated rebids; 4) Adverse interaction with Gate Closure #3 (VRE forecast at ~10 seconds before interval start) potentially reducing forecast accuracy; 5) Risk of dragging gate closures back to align with the snapshot, losing efficiency gains.

title: There are actually several different ramp trajectories that are material to dispatch in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "There are actually several different ramp trajectories that are material to dispatch in the NEM" posted at 06-04-2025 06:20:00 PM is: This technical article explains four distinct ramp profiles used in the National Electricity Market (NEM) dispatch process, using a fictitious semi-scheduled unit across three dispatch intervals to illustrate differences in target conformance, regulation FCAS cost apportionment, and the impact of semi-dispatch caps; it highlights upcoming changes to the Causer Pays method.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: Market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing – no specific discrete event; the article discusses market rules and operational practices.  
Its root_cause is: Unknown – the article is explanatory, not about a single causative event.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates is: 06-04-2025 (same as post date).  
Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators (wind/solar), scheduled generators, AEMO, AER, market participants involved in dispatch and FCAS.  
Its cause type is: Operational/technical.  
Its causes is: The existence of multiple ramp profile definitions (FinalMW-to-Target, Target-to-Target, FinalMW-to-FinalMW linear interpolation, and 4-second SCADA snapshots) used for different purposes (dispatch targets, conformance assessment, FCAS cost allocation) leading to complexity.

title: Why we have reservations about the possible solution AEMO has been considering (to date) in ICF002
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why we have reservations about the possible solution AEMO has been considering (to date) in ICF002" posted at 06-04-2025 01:45:00 PM is: This article discusses concerns about AEMO's proposed solution under ICF002 to address the delayed receipt of dispatch targets (a ~23-second 'dead zone') by shifting the dead zone earlier in the dispatch interval rather than shortening it, which may cause different problems elsewhere in the process, referencing discussions from AEMO EWCF meetings between March 2024 and February 2025 and noting an upcoming update on 8 April 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing process with potential future impact if implemented. Its root_cause is: Delayed receipt of dispatch targets due to a 'dead zone' in the dispatch process. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 06-04-2025, 01-04-2025, 19-03-2024, 21-05-2024, 11-02-2025, 08-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: NEM participants (generators, retailers, etc.). Its cause type is: Operational/process issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Delayed dispatch targets; AEMO's proposed solution to shift the dead zone instead of shortening it; Stakeholder reservations about that approach.

title: Frequency drop with trip of Callide C3 on Friday 4th April 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency drop with trip of Callide C3 on Friday 4th April 2025" posted at 04-04-2025 05:30:00 AM is: On April 4, 2025, the trip of the Callide C3 generating unit caused a sharp frequency drop on the NEM Mainland, followed by a rapid recovery, indicating a transient grid disturbance due to a sudden loss of generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: Trip of Callide C3 generating unit. Its accident scale is: Single unit trip (unknown further details). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 04-04-2025. Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (primarily Queensland). Its affected key users is: NEM market participants and grid operator (AEMO). Its cause type is: Technical failure (trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Callide C3 unit tripped, causing sudden loss of generation, leading to a frequency drop.

title: The growing NEM BESS fleet: Who is controlling all this capacity, anyway?
author: Matt Grover
classified_content: The summary of the news "The growing NEM BESS fleet: Who is controlling all this capacity, anyway?" posted at 03-04-2025 10:01:00 AM is: The article reports that the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia is experiencing a rapid expansion of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), with capacity set to triple in 2025 and more than quintuple by 2027. It focuses on the changing control of BESS capacity, showing that initially dominated by a few existing participants, the market will see a significant influx of new entrants, such that by 2027 about 40% of BESS capacity will be controlled by new players. This diversification is expected to increase competition, reduce market power, and introduce a variety of trading objectives. The article also discusses the necessary capabilities for trading BESS, including automated bid optimization and human oversight; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (2025–2027). Its root_cause is: Wave of investment in BESS and diversification of market participants. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-04-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: Market participants (gentailers, IPPs, investors, retailers) and BESS operators. Its cause type is: Market development. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Rapid increase in BESS investment and financial close of projects; [2] Entry of new market participants with no prior dispatchable capacity experience; [3] Diversification of trading strategies and contractual arrangements.

title: What is the real size of Australian corporate demand for Renewable Energy?
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "What is the real size of Australian corporate demand for Renewable Energy?" posted at 02-04-2025 10:09:00 AM is: The article analyzes the sharp decline in Australian Large-scale Generation Certificate (LGC) spot prices, which fell 43% from September to November 2024 and further after the mid-February 2025 compliance point, due to a growing oversupply of certificates from new renewable projects and slow, uncertain voluntary corporate demand, with many companies failing to honour their renewable energy pledges, highlighting that supply will expand to ~80 million LGCs annually by 2028 while mandated demand is only 33 million, and voluntary demand of 10.5 million in 2024 is insufficient, raising doubts about future certificate prices and investment. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From late 2024 (September-November) to mid-February 2025, with ongoing effects as supply continues to expand. Its root_cause is: Oversupply of renewable energy certificates (LGCs) due to massive growth in renewable generation and insufficient growth in corporate voluntary demand, compounded by regulatory changes awarding certificates to old hydro plants. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: September-November 2024 and mid-February 2025 (in addition to the main article date). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Renewable energy certificate market participants, electricity retailers, corporates with renewable energy pledges (e.g., power retailers, government agencies, water suppliers, retailers, data centres). Its cause type is: Market imbalance and behavioural (lack of follow-through on pledges). Its causes is: 1. Renewable generation supply expanding faster than mandated demand and voluntary demand; 2. Corporate voluntary demand not growing fast enough, with many companies not surrendering LGCs as pledged; 3. Government decision to award certificates to old hydro plants (REGOs) adding to supply; 4. Lack of transparency in corporate reporting of surrender.

title: What’s the AEMO been considering, as a possible solution to the delayed receipt of Target?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s the AEMO been considering, as a possible solution to the delayed receipt of Target?" posted at 01-04-2025 02:56:00 PM is: The article discusses AEMO's exploration of a solution to the problem of dispatch targets arriving ~20 seconds into a 5-minute dispatch interval, which disrupts real-time grid operations. Since mid-2024, AEMO has considered shifting the SCADA snapshot earlier (by ~20 seconds) so data gathering occurs during the preceding interval, while some participants support the change, others (including Global-Roam) have reservations about its simplicity and potential risks. The root cause is the current timing of SCADA data collection causing delayed target availability, and the issue remains unresolved as of April 2025, affecting market efficiency and system stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since at least May 2024, unresolved as of April 2025. Its root_cause is: Delayed receipt of dispatch targets due to SCADA snapshot timing that occurs at the start of the dispatch interval rather than earlier. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-04-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers), AEMO, and system operators. Its cause type is: Technical/operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Current SCADA snapshot taken at the beginning of each dispatch interval leads to dispatch targets being available ~20 seconds late; AEMO proposes shifting the snapshot ~20 seconds earlier to align data gathering with the preceding interval.

title: What’s the problem(s) with dispatch targets arriving ~20 seconds into the Dispatch Interval?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s the problem(s) with dispatch targets arriving ~20 seconds into the Dispatch Interval?" posted at 01-04-2025 10:58:00 AM is: This article highlights a systemic issue in the National Electricity Market (NEM) where dispatch targets are typically delivered to participants about 20 seconds into each 5-minute Dispatch Interval, causing delayed ramping and misalignment between reference and actual trajectories. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) notes that this delay can have negative energy revenue implications for facilities, and the problem has been discussed in EWCF meetings, with slides from March and May 2024 illustrating the ramp trajectory misalignment. The article also explores potential solutions being considered by AEMO to address the delayed receipt of targets. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (continuous operational issue). Its root_cause is: Delayed receipt of dispatch targets (~20 seconds into each interval) leads to ramp delays and trajectory misalignment. Its accident scale is: Unknown (systemic issue, not an accident). Its dates is: 01-04-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: Generation facilities (participants) in the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational/technical issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Dispatch targets are not delivered until ~20 seconds into each 5-minute Dispatch Interval; 2) This delays ramp execution, causing misalignment between the reference trajectory and actual facility response.

title: Challenges in ‘Time taken for Dispatch Targets to be made available to participants’ (ICF002)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Challenges in ‘Time taken for Dispatch Targets to be made available to participants’ (ICF002)" posted at 01-04-2025 10:04:00 AM is: This article examines the persistent issue in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) where dispatch targets are delayed by approximately 20 seconds into the next dispatch interval, stemming from computational and communication latencies. It reviews AEMO’s ongoing investigation (ICF002) presented at the EWCF meeting on 19 March 2024, detailing the dispatch process complexities, the ~35 seconds end‑to‑end calculation time, and the implications for conformance and system stability. The article also discusses AEMO’s proposed solution and reservations from market participants. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since March 2024, still under investigation. Its root_cause is: Delayed dispatch target dissemination due to inherent computational latencies in the dispatch process. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 3 March 2024, 5 March 2024, 19 March 2024. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Market participants and AEMO. Its cause type is: Technical/operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Inherent complexity of dispatch calculation; ~35 seconds computation time; ~20 seconds intrusion into next dispatch interval; delayed receipt of targets by participants.

title: Frequency excursion on Monday 31st March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency excursion on Monday 31st March 2025" posted at 31-03-2025 06:04:00 PM is: A frequency excursion occurred on the mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) just before 17:00 NEM time on 31 March 2025, with frequency progressively falling below the lower bound of the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB), as indicated by live meter readings in Brisbane; no further details or causes are provided in the brief article. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term (during the excursion). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 31-03-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland Australia (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Three themes from the Energy Storage Summit 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three themes from the Energy Storage Summit 2025" posted at 27-03-2025 11:27:00 AM is: The article reflects on three key themes from the 2025 Energy Storage Summit in Sydney: a wave of first-time battery owners entering the NEM (with fifteen new participants expected in the next two years), a focus on development and grid-connection challenges rather than underlying market drivers, and banks increasingly accepting merchant revenue models amid rising volatility. It notes that many new entrants may underestimate the operational complexity of scheduled assets, and that market changes such as the new FPP mechanism were surprisingly undiscussed. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and medium-term (next two years and beyond) as new battery participants begin operations and market structures evolve. Its root_cause is: The rapid expansion of grid-scale battery storage in the NEM, driven by renewable energy growth, leads to increased market complexity, volatility, and a need for new operational competencies and financing approaches. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 27-03-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market, NEM). Its affected key users is: Battery developers, owners, operators, investors, banks, and market participants (especially new entrants). Its cause type is: Market evolution and regulatory/policy changes. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Influx of first-time battery owners; grid connection delays due to lack of OEM-NSP communication; non-standardized GPS agreements; growing market volatility and complexity; changing investor attitudes toward merchant exposure; undiscussed policy mechanisms (FPP) that could impact storage revenues.

title: News articles (in the Australian) about losses at Zen Energy during Wind Drought
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "News articles (in the Australian) about losses at Zen Energy during Wind Drought" posted at 24-03-2024 is: The Australian newspaper published articles on 24 March 2024 (with a follow-up on 26 March) reporting that Zen Energy, a renewable energy firm developed by Ross Garnaut, incurred significant financial losses due to a prolonged "wind drought" – a period of extremely low wind speeds – which severely curtailed wind farm output and exposed the company's contracted positions to high wholesale electricity prices. Zen Energy responded publicly, acknowledging the difficulties and warning that others would face similar pain. The event highlights the vulnerability of wind-dependent generators to sustained calm weather in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: During the wind drought period (Q2 2024, particularly March 2024). Its root_cause is: A prolonged lack of wind (wind drought) over regions with high wind farm concentration. Its accident scale is: Regional weather event affecting multiple wind farms and related market participants. Its dates (if different from main date) is: 24 March 2024 (article date); wind drought period spanned several weeks in Q2 2024. Its affected region is: Australia (primarily NEM regions with high wind penetration, such as South Australia and Victoria). Its affected key users is: Zen Energy (retailer/developer), other wind farm operators, and electricity consumers indirectly via price spikes. Its cause type is: Natural/weather-related (meteorological). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Persistent high-pressure systems blocking wind flow over southern Australia; [2] Extended period of wind speeds below turbine cut-in thresholds; [3] Resulting low renewable generation coinciding with high demand periods.

title: Two diagrams to help illustrate the changes coming with FPP (Frequency Performance Payments)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two diagrams to help illustrate the changes coming with FPP (Frequency Performance Payments)" posted at 24-03-2025 09:47:00 AM is: The article explains the upcoming transition to Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) in Australia's National Electricity Market, scheduled for June 2025, which introduces a new payment mechanism for primary frequency response and replaces the Causer Pays process for Regulation FCAS cost allocation, with potential impacts on market participants and frequency control incentives. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting 8 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Transition to a new frequency performance payments mechanism to improve grid frequency control and market incentives. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 2025-06-08. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity generators, batteries, and frequency control providers. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes is: Implementation of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) replacing Causer Pays mechanism; creation of a new pot of money for primary frequency response payments; linking payments to Regulation FCAS prices.

title: Generator exposure to regulation costs under FPP
author: Jack Fox
classified_content: The summary of the news "Generator exposure to regulation costs under FPP" posted at 19-03-2025 06:00:00 PM is: The article explains that Australia's upcoming Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) mechanism, starting financial operations on 8 June 2025, will replace the Causer Pays system for recovering regulation FCAS costs in the NEM. FPP splits cost recovery into used and unused regulation components. Using Non-Financial Operation data, the analysis finds that unused regulation (about 65% of total costs) is recovered based on weekly default contribution factors, which are poorest for wind and solar generators. This exposes them to significant costs (e.g., Stockyard Hill wind farm could face over $105,000 annually from one constraint alone) and they remain liable even when not generating, such as solar at night. The article highlights that all top 10 worst default contribution factors are from variable renewable energy assets. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 8 June 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) for regulation FCAS cost recovery. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 8 June 2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market of Australia), specifically regions VIC1, QLD1, NSW1. Its affected key users is: Wind and solar generators (variable renewable energy assets) such as Stockyard Hill, Wattle Point, Bluegrass, McIntyre, Woolooga, Wellington, Stubbo, Limondale, Columboola, and New England solar farms. Its cause type is: Regulatory change (market rule change). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Introduction of FPP; split of regulation cost recovery into used and unused components; use of default contribution factors based on historic performance; assignment of poorest default contribution factors to VRE generators.

title: How did Cyclone Alfred impact electricity demand?
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "How did Cyclone Alfred impact electricity demand?" posted at 19-03-2025 10:15:00 AM is: Cyclone Alfred made landfall in Queensland on March 8, 2025, bringing extreme winds, heavy rainfall, and widespread power outages that caused atypical electricity demand patterns in south-east Queensland and northern NSW. Demand fell by up to 60% in the Gold Coast on landfall day due to network outages and reduced economic activity, with partial recovery by March 10 but lingering unplanned outages through March 14. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 5 March 2025 to 14 March 2025. Its root_cause is: Cyclone Alfred. Its accident scale is: Widespread power outages, demand reduction up to 60% in Gold Coast area (exact number of affected customers not specified). Its dates is: 5 March 2025 to 14 March 2025. Its affected region is: South-east Queensland (Gold Coast, Moreton/Brisbane) and northern NSW. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in affected areas, including public transport, shops, and remote workers. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (tropical cyclone). Its causes is: Cyclone Alfred's slow movement, extreme winds, cloud cover, heavy rainfall leading to network outages and reduced demand.

title: Today’s presentation to CEC’s Market & Grid Directorate
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Today’s presentation to CEC’s Market & Grid Directorate" posted at 17-03-2025 02:25:00 PM is: This article describes a presentation given by Dan Lee and the author to the Clean Energy Council's Market and Grid Directorate on 17 March 2025, covering five key topics regarding National Electricity Market (NEM) trends: battery arbitrage performance, battery FCAS performance, FCAS costs for VRE and batteries, the impact of the new Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) mechanism, and challenges for hybrids and existing VRE. The presentation was well-attended and builds on Dan Lee's earlier article, with further discussions expected at the Solar Storage Summit Australia in Sydney on 18–19 March 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 17-03-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery operators, VRE generators, market participants. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: The top 10 batteries under FPP
author: Jack Fox
classified_content: The summary of the news "The top 10 batteries under FPP" posted at 17-03-2025 08:00:00 AM is: This article analyzes the top 10 battery energy storage systems in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) that benefit the most from Frequency Performance Payments (FPP), based on NFO data from 9 December 2024 to 6 March 2025. It highlights that batteries outperform coal assets on a per MW net settlement basis, with Phillip Island and Bulgana BESS showing particularly high yields, while noting caveats such as seasonal effects and potential behavioral changes once financial operation begins. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 9 December 2024 (FPP implementation) with data period ending 6 March 2025; article posted 17 March 2025. Its root_cause is: Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments mechanism by the Australian Energy Market Operator. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 17-03-2025 (article date) and data period 09-12-2024 to 06-03-2025. Its affected region is: Australian NEM regions (VIC1, NSW1, SA1, QLD1). Its affected key users is: Battery storage operators. Its cause type is: Market regulation change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Implementation of Frequency Performance Payments to incentivize frequency control, leading to higher net settlements for batteries that provide regulation services.

title: Back to forecast LOR2 for NSW for this evening (Sunday 16th March 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Back to forecast LOR2 for NSW for this evening (Sunday 16th March 2025)" posted at 16-03-2025 11:20:00 AM is: A market snapshot from ez2view at the 11:15 dispatch interval on Sunday 16th March 2025 indicates a forecast for Market Demand in New South Wales climbing above 12,500 MW, leading to the reappearance of forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) conditions, which are expected to cause evening volatility in the electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: This evening (Sunday 16th March 2025). Its root_cause is: High forecast market demand exceeding available reserve thresholds. Its accident scale is: Forecast condition (not an actual accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-03-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, market participants, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Demand-driven. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Forecast market demand climbing above 12,500 MW.

title: Evening volatility in NSW on Saturday 15th March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in NSW on Saturday 15th March 2025" posted at 15-03-2025 06:24:00 PM is: On the evening of 15 March 2025, NEMwatch data showed elevated market demand in New South Wales leaving the ‘green zone’ and even more stretched conditions in South Australia, indicating evening volatility likely driven by high consumption. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (evening of 15 March 2025). Its root_cause is: High market demand exceeding normal operating ranges. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific failure reported). Its dates is: 15-03-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in those regions. Its cause type is: Demand-driven. Its causes is: Elevated demand during evening peak hours.

title: About the ‘S-HUBG’ and ‘S-HUWT’ constraint sets, and yesterday’s network outage on Yorke Peninsula
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About the ‘S-HUBG’ and ‘S-HUWT’ constraint sets, and yesterday’s network outage on Yorke Peninsula" posted at 15-03-2025 03:12:00 PM is: ElectraNet experienced multiple transmission network faults on Friday 14 March 2025, cutting power supply to the Yorke Peninsula and triggering two constraint sets: S-HUBG (now lifted) which constrained Snowtown Wind Farm to zero output, and S-HUWT (still active until 18:00 on 15 March) which is constraining other variable renewable energy (VRE) with a significant marginal value; Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 14 March 2025 to evening of 15 March 2025; Its root_cause is: Multiple faults on ElectraNet's transmission network; Its accident scale is: Multiple faults affecting power supply to Yorke Peninsula; Its dates is: 14-03-2025; Its affected region is: Yorke Peninsula; Its affected key users is: Snowtown Wind Farm and other variable renewable energy (VRE) generators; Its cause type is: Transmission network faults; Its causes is: Multiple faults on transmission network.

title: ElectraNet experienced multiple faults on the transmission network Friday 14th March 2025 impacting power supply to the Yorke Peninsula.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ElectraNet experienced multiple faults on the transmission network Friday 14th March 2025 impacting power supply to the Yorke Peninsula." posted at 15-03-2025 01:38:00 PM is: On 14 March 2025, South Australian transmission network operator ElectraNet suffered two concurrent unplanned outages of the Hummocks–Bungama and Hummocks–Waterloo 132 kV lines, triggering inter-regional transfer limit variations and invoking constraint sets (S-HUBG and S-HUWT) by AEMO at 05:05 hrs. These faults disrupted power supply to the Yorke Peninsula, reflecting a grid/system instability event that immediately impacted electricity market operations by altering transfer limits and network constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – market notices issued at 06:03 hrs on 14 March 2025, with effects ongoing until lines are restored. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outages of two transmission lines due to multiple faults. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-03-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia, specifically the Yorke Peninsula and surrounding areas served by the Hummocks–Bungama and Hummocks–Waterloo 132 kV lines. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely residential, commercial, and industrial customers in the Yorke Peninsula region). Its cause type is: Technical failure (transmission line faults). Its causes is: 1) Unplanned outage of Hummocks–Bungama 132 kV line at 0457 hrs on 14/03/2025, 2) Unplanned outage of Hummocks–Waterloo 132 kV line at 0457 hrs on 14/03/2025.

title: Sunday 16th March could be a stinker in NSW … including with sporadic forecasts of LOR2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunday 16th March could be a stinker in NSW … including with sporadic forecasts of LOR2" posted at 14-03-2025 02:48:00 PM is: AEMO issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) for the NSW region on 16 March 2025, driven by a scorching heatwave pushing market demand up to 12,300 MW, combined with reduced available generation from solar backing off in the evening and changed outage plans of firming units, resulting in a capacity shortfall where only 845 MW was available against a requirement of 1019 MW from 1730 to 1830 hrs; although the notice was later cancelled, the event highlights the risk of grid instability under extreme weather. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability (forecast LOR2 indicating system reserve shortfall) and extreme weather (heat causing high demand). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 March 2025, specifically from 1730 hrs to 1830 hrs. Its root_cause is: Extreme hot weather leading to high electricity demand and simultaneous reduction in available generation due to solar ramp-down and unexpected outages of firming units. Its accident scale is: LOR2 (Level 2 Lack of Reserve) with a forecast deficit of 174 MW (requirement 1019 MW minus available 845 MW). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-03-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales, Australia). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, market participants, and AEMO as the system operator. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand surge and generation availability issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Forecast of extreme heat on 16 March 2025 driving high market demand up to 12,300 MW; 2) Expected reduction in large solar generation as evening approaches; 3) Changed outage plans of one or more firming units reducing available capacity; 4) Overall capacity reserve falling short of the required 1019 MW.

title: Down to ~17,000 customers off supply in south-east Queensland (not forgetting northern NSW)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Down to ~17,000 customers off supply in south-east Queensland (not forgetting northern NSW)" posted at 14-03-2025 05:55:00 AM is: As of Friday morning 14th March 2025, approximately 17,000 electricity customers in south-east Queensland (primarily inland areas of the Gold Coast) remain without power due to damage caused by Tropical Cyclone Alfred, with additional customers in northern NSW also affected. The outages are attributed to fallen vegetation and damaged road access hindering restoration efforts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing as of 14-03-2025, with restoration still in progress. Its root_cause is: Severe weather and infrastructure damage from Tropical Cyclone Alfred. Its accident scale is: ~17,000 customers off supply in south-east Queensland, plus an unspecified number in northern NSW. Its dates is: 14-03-2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: South-east Queensland (especially Gold Coast inland) and northern NSW. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity customers. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (tropical cyclone). Its causes is: [Damage from Tropical Cyclone Alfred, including fallen vegetation and damaged access roads].

title: Burst of volatility in NSW and QLD (and Actual LOR1) on Thursday 13th March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Burst of volatility in NSW and QLD (and Actual LOR1) on Thursday 13th March 2025" posted at 13-03-2025 06:21:00 PM is: On 13 March 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) in the New South Wales (NSW) region from 17:30 to 18:30 (AEST), indicating a reserve capacity shortfall of 138 MW (requirement 1668 MW, available 1530 MW), accompanied by a burst of volatility in both NSW and Queensland. This event reflects temporary grid instability and immediate market reactions due to insufficient available capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13 March 2025, 17:30 to 18:30 (AEST) – short-term, within a few hours. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Level 1 (LOR1) – capacity shortfall of 138 MW (reserve requirement 1668 MW, available 1530 MW). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 13 March 2025. Its affected region is: NSW region (New South Wales, Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in NSW, but not specified). Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: An initial look at Winners and Losers from Frequency Performance Payments (FPP)
author: Jack Fox
classified_content: The summary of the news "An initial look at Winners and Losers from Frequency Performance Payments (FPP)" posted at 13-03-2025 04:30:00 PM is: The article analyzes the winners and losers under the new Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) framework in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), which replaces the Causer Pays method for allocating regulation FCAS costs and introduces incentive/penalty payments for primary frequency response. Based on Non-Financial Operation data from December 2024 to March 2025, it finds that batteries, coal, and other thermal generators are net winners (receiving incentive payments), while solar, wind, and retail customers (Residual) are net losers (paying penalties and higher costs). The transition to full financial operation is set for 8 June 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 9 December 2024 (shadow Non-Financial Operation) with full financial impact from 8 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Replacement of the Causer Pays methodology with Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) to address design flaws and incentivize primary frequency response. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (market reform). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 9 December 2024 (start of Non-Financial Operation) and 8 June 2025 (start of Financial Operation). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants including generators (coal, gas, hydro, solar, wind, battery) and retail customers (Residual). Its cause type is: Market reform / regulatory change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) to replace Causer Pays; creation of incentive payments for positive frequency response and penalty payments for negative deviations; cost recovery changes for regulation FCAS.

title: Three headline observations, about the use of proxies for System Frequency in Frequency Control mechanisms
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three headline observations, about the use of proxies for System Frequency in Frequency Control mechanisms" posted at 13-03-2025 09:02:00 AM is: This article, published on WattClarity, examines the impending transition to the new Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) process in the National Electricity Market (NEM), now just 12 weeks away. The author highlights three key observations: first, that both the old 'Causer Pays' mechanism and the new FPP process use proxies (Frequency Indicator and Frequency Measure, respectively) rather than raw frequency to apportion Regulation FCAS costs; second, that a year-long correlation analysis reveals the Frequency Indicator is a very poor proxy for actual system frequency, often suggesting the wrong direction of regulation services; and third, that the Frequency Indicator is highly predictable, which combined with self-forecasting abilities could encourage pre-emptive behaviours that worsen frequency instead of improving it. The piece underscores the potential systemic risk of designing frequency control mechanisms around flawed proxies.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from June 2025 (FPP operational start) with precursor effects visible now; Its root_cause is: Use of a poorly correlated proxy (Frequency Indicator) for frequency in regulation FCAS cost apportionment, coupled with predictability enabling gaming; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported, but systemic design flaw); Its dates (if different from main date) is: 13-03-2025; Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia); Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, loads, and self-forecasters); Its cause type is: Design flaw; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1) Use of Frequency Indicator as proxy for frequency in old Causer Pays process; 2) Adoption of Frequency Measure in new FPP, which still differs from actual frequency; 3) Correlation analysis shows Frequency Indicator is often anti-correlated with frequency; 4) Predictability of Frequency Indicator enables pre-emptive gaming behaviors that may worsen frequency stability.].

title: ‘Market Demand’ in TAS drops by 151MW on Wednesday 12th March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ in TAS drops by 151MW on Wednesday 12th March 2025" posted at 12-03-2025 12:26:00 PM is: A series of transmission line trips caused by storms in Tasmania on 12 March 2025 led to a sudden 151 MW drop in market demand (about 15% of total), with AEMO reporting 96 MW of bulk electrical load disconnection (40 MW at Nyrstar and 56 MW at Temco) due to non-credible contingency events involving multiple 220 kV and 110 kV lines. The event was later reclassified as a credible contingency, and the root cause is attributed to storm-related grid instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, occurring on 12 March 2025. Its root_cause is: Storms causing multiple transmission line trips leading to disconnection of bulk industrial loads. Its accident scale is: 151 MW demand drop (96 MW of bulk load disconnected). Its dates (if different from main date) is: Same (12-03-2025). Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS). Its affected key users is: Nyrstar (40 MW) and Temco (56 MW). Its cause type is: Extreme weather (storms) and grid instability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Sheffield George Town No.1 & No.2 220 kV lines tripped; Sheffield-Burnie No.1 220 kV line, Sheffield-Burnie 2 110 kV line, and Ulverstone-Emu Bay 110 kV line tripped; resulting in disconnection of 40 MW at Nyrstar and 56 MW at Temco.

title: AWEFS and ASEFS dispatch availability forecast outage on 11 March, 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content:
The summary of the news "AWEFS and ASEFS dispatch availability forecast outage on 11 March, 2025" posted at 12-03-2025 10:47:00 AM is: On 11 March 2025, the AWEFS and ASEFS systems that provide dispatch availability forecasts for variable renewable energy (VRE) units experienced an outage starting around the 16:00 dispatch interval, causing the system to switch to SCADA-based persistence forecasts (using each unit’s measured MW output). The outage lasted until 09:40 on 12 March, when systems returned to normal. During the outage, approximately 3,668 MW of VRE generation from units using ASEFS or AWEFS was affected, although participant self-forecasts remained unaffected and served as a fallback. The event disrupted the usual forecasting process for semi-scheduled units, potentially impacting market dispatch decisions.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 16:00 on 11 March 2025 to 09:40 on 12 March 2025 (system restoration).  
Its root_cause is: Unknown (the article does not specify why the AWEFS and ASEFS systems failed).  
Its accident scale is: Affected forecasting for approximately 3,668 MW of VRE generation capacity.  
Its dates is: 11-03-2025 (event start) and 12-03-2025 (system return).  
Its affected region is: Australia (likely the National Electricity Market, as AWEFS and ASEFS are AEMO systems).  
Its affected key users is: Market operators (AEMO), semi-scheduled VRE generators using AWEFS/ASEFS forecasts, and dispatch schedulers.  
Its cause type is: System failure (forecast software/IT outage).  
Its causes is: [Unknown, but the immediate cause was the unavailability of the AWEFS and ASEFS forecast systems, triggering a switch to SCADA substitute forecasts].

title: Gremlins in AEMO systems publish rogue APC alert for South Australia, Sunday 9th March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Gremlins in AEMO systems publish rogue APC alert for South Australia, Sunday 9th March 2025" posted at 09-03-2025 08:15:00 PM is: Due to a system glitch, AEMO erroneously published an Administered Price Cap (APC) alert for South Australia (SA1) on 9 March 2025, declaring an administered price period after the cumulative price threshold was allegedly exceeded. The notice (MN125439) was quickly corrected within 30 minutes via subsequent notices (MN125440, MN125442) confirming the error. A separate, unrelated forecast lack of reserve (LOR1) was also issued for the same region later that evening. The false alert had no actual impact on market prices or operations.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours, no lasting impact); Its root_cause is: AEMO system glitch publishing an erroneous market notice; Its accident scale is: Minor (single erroneous notice quickly corrected); Its dates is: 09-03-2025; Its affected region is: South Australia (SA1 region) within the National Electricity Market (NEM); Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, generators, and traders in SA; Its cause type is: System/software error; Its causes is: AEMO’s automated system incorrectly triggered an Administered Price Cap alert due to an internal glitch, later confirmed as an error and retracted.

title: ex-TC Alfred makes landfall overnight, many more unplanned network outages result
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ex-TC Alfred makes landfall overnight, many more unplanned network outages result" posted at 09-03-2025 10:05:00 AM is: Ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred made landfall overnight on 8-9 March 2025, causing widespread unplanned power outages across Brisbane, particularly in the northside and southside, with Energex reporting over 337,000 properties without power at peak, later dropping to around 318,000 but still over 322,000 as of Sunday morning; restoration efforts had restored power to more than 50,000 homes, but new outages continued to appear. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 8 March 2025 evening to present (9 March 2025). Its root_cause is: Ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred making landfall, bringing strong winds and rain that damaged power network infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Over 337,000 properties without power at peak, with ongoing outages around 322,000. Its dates is: 8-9 March 2025. Its affected region is: Brisbane's northside and southside, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential homes and properties. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (cyclone). Its causes is: Remnants of ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred caused strong winds and heavy rain leading to network damage; unplanned outages increased overnight despite restoration efforts.

title: What other details are visible, of those network trips in northern NSW yesterday (Fri 7th March 2025)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What other details are visible, of those network trips in northern NSW yesterday (Fri 7th March 2025)?" posted at 08-03-2025 04:07:00 PM is: The article investigates network trips in northern NSW on Friday 7 March 2025, likely caused by Tropical Cyclone Alfred, involving three 132kV AC lines to Mullumbimby, three Directlink DC cables between Terranora and Bungalora, and the Lismore SVC. It notes that no updated market notices have been issued, but constraint sets show the Directlink cables are expected out until Wednesday 12 March 17:00 (NEM time), while the Lismore SVC returned to service later that day. The trips have constrained the Terranora interconnector to flow south to support local load. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 07-03-2025 to at least 12-03-2025 (Directlink outage) with ongoing effects. Its root_cause is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred. Its accident scale is: Multiple network element trips: three 132kV lines, three Directlink cables, and one SVC. Its dates are: 07-03-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Northern New South Wales (Mullumbimby, Lismore, Terranora, Bungalora). Its affected key users are: Unknown (but likely residential and commercial customers in northern NSW and South-East Queensland experiencing power outages). Its cause type is: Natural disaster (cyclone). Its causes are: Tropical Cyclone Alfred caused the simultaneous tripping of three 132kV lines, three Directlink HVDC cables, and the Lismore Static VAR Compensator.

title: Power Outages in Northern NSW and South-East Queensland at Saturday morning 8th March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Power Outages in Northern NSW and South-East Queensland at Saturday morning 8th March 2025" posted at 08-03-2025 12:24:00 PM is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred caused widespread and severe power outages across South-East Queensland and northern New South Wales on Saturday, 8 March 2025. According to Energex, approximately 250,000 homes in SEQ lost power, with hundreds of powerlines down and several substations affected. Essential Energy reported over 42,600 homes and businesses without power in northern NSW between Tweed Heads and Grafton. The outages stem from high winds, fallen trees and debris, and damage to electrical infrastructure. Restoration efforts are hampered by ongoing adverse weather, flooding risks, and the need for detailed damage assessment. Authorities warn residents to treat all fallen powerlines as live and dangerous. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing (days to weeks). Its root_cause is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred (extreme weather event). Its accident scale is: Approximately 292,600 customers without power (250,000 in SEQ + 42,600 in northern NSW), hundreds of powerlines down, multiple substations affected. Its dates is: 08-03-2025. Its affected region is: South-East Queensland (SEQ) and northern New South Wales (Tweed Heads to Grafton). Its affected key users is: Residential homes and businesses. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (extreme weather). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High winds from Tropical Cyclone Alfred; fallen trees and vegetation debris; downed powerlines; damage to substations; risk of flooding hindering access and repairs.

title: Yass to Wagga control scheme update removes some constraints
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yass to Wagga control scheme update removes some constraints" posted at 07-03-2025 12:22:00 PM is: On February 25, 2025, AEMO removed three constraints (N>>NIL_970_051, N>>NIL_990_051, N>>NIL_991_051) from system normal sets after an update to the Yass overload trip scheme, which now manages flows on the 132 kV lines 970, 990, and 991 in both directions, making the constraints unnecessary and relieving a frequently bound corridor that had ranked among the top in duration and market impact in NSW in 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From February 25, 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Update to the Yass overload trip scheme now manages flows on the 132 kV lines, eliminating the need for the constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates is: 2025-02-25. Its affected region is: Yass to Wagga corridor, New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants in NSW (generators, retailers, and consumers impacted by the constraints). Its cause type is: Grid system update (control scheme upgrade). Its causes is: 1. AEMO updated the Yass overload trip scheme. 2. The new scheme directly manages flows on lines 970, 990, and 991 132 kV. 3. Consequently, the three constraints protecting against overload on trip of the 051 line were no longer needed in system normal sets.

title: Friday morning update from WeatherWatch, and TC Alfred
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Friday morning update from WeatherWatch, and TC Alfred" posted at 07-03-2025 10:05:00 AM is: Anthony Cornelius from WeatherWatch shared an update on Tropical Cyclone Alfred, referencing a prior analysis of a destructive supercell storm in Broken Hill; he has been posting regular updates on LinkedIn, and the brief note urges readers to stay safe. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as TC Alfred is an active tropical cyclone with potential to cause widespread power disruptions. Its root_cause is: Natural weather event (tropical cyclone and supercell storm). Its accident scale is: Unknown, but the referenced storm in Broken Hill was described as "destructive," implying significant local damage. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-03-2025. Its affected region is: Likely parts of Australia, including Broken Hill (New South Wales) and areas in the path of TC Alfred (e.g., Queensland/northern NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred; prior destructive supercell storm in Broken Hill.

title: More network trips in northern NSW, on Friday 7th March 2025 (is TC Alfred involved?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More network trips in northern NSW, on Friday 7th March 2025 (is TC Alfred involved?)" posted at 07-03-2025 09:18:00 AM is: On Friday 7 March 2025, multiple network trips occurred in northern NSW, including the unplanned outage of No 1 and 2 cables of Directlink, three 132kV lines (Lismore132–Mullumbimby 9U6, 9U7; Ewingsdale–Mullumbimby 9G5), and the Lismore SVC, leading to a non-credible contingency event declared by AEMO. Approximately 7MW of bulk load was disconnected, and Essential Energy reported that Tropical Cyclone Alfred had left over 35,000 (later ~70,000) homes and businesses without power in Northern Rivers and Far North Coast due to severe winds, heavy rain, and flooding. AEMO did not instruct load shedding; restoration may take several days.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (07/03/2025) with multi-day restoration expected. Its root_cause is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred causing severe winds, heavy rain, and flooding. Its accident scale is: Initially over 35,000 homes/businesses without power, later approximately 70,000 customers off supply. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-03-2025. Its affected region is: Northern NSW (Northern Rivers and Far North Coast). Its affected key users is: Homes and businesses (residential and commercial customers). Its cause type is: Natural disaster (cyclone). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Severe winds and heavy rain from Tropical Cyclone Alfred caused damage to transmission lines (Lismore132–Mullumbimby, Ewingsdale–Mullumbimby), Directlink cables, and Lismore SVC, along with flooding and vegetation debris leading to network trips and power outages.

title: High level uncertainty about daytime QLD (and to a lesser extent NSW) ‘Market Demand’ with TC Alfred
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "High level uncertainty about daytime QLD (and to a lesser extent NSW) ‘Market Demand’ with TC Alfred" posted at 06-03-2025 03:08:00 PM is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred is causing significant uncertainty in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) demand forecasts for Queensland and, to a lesser extent, New South Wales. The article highlights that forecast models for 'Market Demand' are highly uncertain due to the cyclone's unpredictable effects on underlying demand (temperature/humidity), widespread cloud cover suppressing rooftop solar PV output, and preemptive shutdowns of PV systems as directed by network service providers (e.g., Energex). Additionally, potential customer supply losses are not captured in the dispatch-focused forecasts, further complicating market conditions. The situation is described as "the calm before the storm" as of the afternoon of March 6, 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Extreme weather; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing over the coming days as TC Alfred approaches and passes; Its root_cause is: Tropical Cyclone Alfred disrupting weather patterns, rooftop PV generation, and grid stability; Its accident scale is: Unknown, but potentially widespread across QLD and parts of NSW due to storm impacts and preemptive measures; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-03-2025 (event and forecasts pertain to this date and subsequent days); Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) primarily, and to a lesser extent New South Wales (NSW); Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity customers, rooftop solar PV system owners, and network operators; Its cause type is: Natural disaster (tropical cyclone); Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Cyclone-induced temperature and humidity changes altering underlying demand; 2) Extensive cloud cover reducing rooftop PV output; 3) Preemptive power-down of rooftop PV systems by customers following NSP directives; 4) Potential widespread customer supply outages not accounted for in AEMO demand forecasts.

title: Overview of Regulation FCAS costs in 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Overview of Regulation FCAS costs in 2024" posted at 06-03-2025 08:47:00 AM is: The article reviews regulation FCAS costs per MW of capacity across different generation unit types in the Australian electricity market for 2024, showing that battery storage, coal, and gas units attracted low costs while wind and solar (especially non-scheduled units) incurred high costs due to frequent output deviations from expected baselines; it also notes upcoming changes to the Frequency Performance Payments system effective June 8, 2025, which will alter cost allocation patterns. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2024 data with future impact from June 8, 2025. Its root_cause is: Non-scheduled variable renewable units (especially wind and solar) experience frequent ramp-up and ramp-down events, causing deviations from the constant-output expectation used in frequency contribution assessment, leading to disproportionately high regulation FCAS cost allocation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 2024. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Scheduled, semi-scheduled, and non-scheduled generation units – particularly wind, large solar, and non-scheduled hydro units; also pumped hydro and small liquid fuel/other units. Its cause type is: Regulatory/operational (frequency control cost allocation methodology). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Non-scheduled units do not receive dispatch targets but are assessed based on constant-output expectation; 2) Output deviations from this expectation occur frequently for variable renewables; 3) Large deviations on the wrong side of the frequency indicator drive up the unit’s share of FCAS costs.

title: Regulation prices and enablement  – a historical review
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Regulation prices and enablement – a historical review" posted at 06-03-2025 08:47:00 AM is: This article provides a historical analysis of frequency regulation services (RAISEREG and LOWERREG) in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) from 2019 to 2024, examining enablement levels and prices. It notes stable minimum enablement but rising maximums and more frequent high levels, particularly in 2024, alongside a period of low regulation FCAS prices since late 2023. The piece precedes the 2025 reform that introduces frequency performance payments and changes cost recovery for primary frequency response, which will alter established cost and revenue patterns for market participants.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2025 onwards (reform implementation), with historical context covering 2019–2024. Its root_cause is: Regulatory reform to incentivise primary frequency response in the NEM, including frequency performance payments and revised cost allocation for regulation FCAS. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 06-03-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, including regions QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS. Its affected key users is: Market participants subject to FCAS cost recovery (generators, loads, and other cost recovery participants). Its cause type is: Regulatory. Its causes is: Introduction of frequency performance payments and changes to regulation FCAS cost recovery to incentivise primary frequency response; historical trends of rising enablement levels and price volatility.

title: (A first step into the complexities of) the Dispatch Interval in the Real World
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(A first step into the complexities of) the Dispatch Interval in the Real World" posted at 05-03-2025 06:42:00 PM is: This article explains the technical intricacies of the 'Magic Happens' step between 5-minute dispatch intervals in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), detailing five sequential sub-stages—Data Gathering, Pre-processing, NEMDE crunching, Post-processing, and Data Delivery—and notes that the entire process takes about 35 seconds, with dispatch targets delivered roughly 20 seconds into the next interval. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-03-2025 06:42:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO). Its cause type is: Operational/Technical. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Data gathering of SCADA, bids, and UIGF; pre-processing of constraint equations and demand forecast; NEMDE linear programming optimization; post-processing for over-constrained dispatch and price adjustments; delivery of targets via MarketNet.

title: Afternoon trip of Bayswater unit 3 delivers rapid drop in Mainland Frequency
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon trip of Bayswater unit 3 delivers rapid drop in Mainland Frequency" posted at 05-03-2025 05:18:00 PM is: A sudden trip of the Bayswater Power Station unit 3 (BW03) on 5 March 2025 caused a rapid and significant drop in mainland NEM frequency, as observed through live 100-millisecond frequency data and a market alert from the ez2view Notifications widget around the 16:10 dispatch interval (NEM time). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate frequency disruption, likely lasting minutes until grid stabilization (specific duration not given). Its root_cause is: Forced trip of Bayswater unit 3 (exact root cause not specified in the article). Its accident scale is: Single unit trip (BW03, unit 3). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-03-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely grid operators and system security controllers; consumers not directly mentioned). Its cause type is: Forced outage / generator trip. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Trip of Bayswater unit 3; specific underlying cause not reported].

title: Unplanned outage of No 1 leg of Directlink (was this related to the emergence of TC Alfred?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage of No 1 leg of Directlink (was this related to the emergence of TC Alfred?)" posted at 05-03-2025 12:31:00 PM is: On 5 March 2025, multiple AEMO market notices reported an unplanned outage of the No 1 leg of Directlink, followed by a non-credible contingency event where the Ewingsdale–Mullumbimby 132kV line and Directlink DC1 tripped simultaneously. The cause was initially unknown but later identified, though not disclosed; the article suggests strong winds from Tropical Cyclone Alfred may have contributed. Directlink DC1 was returned to service by 14:00, but the same 132kV line suffered another unplanned outage at 16:03, prompting inter-regional transfer limit variations. No load shedding occurred, and the events impacted the New South Wales region of the National Electricity Market. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Same day (5 March 2025). Its root_cause is: Unknown but suspected weather‑related (strong winds from TC Alfred). Its accident scale is: Regional – unplanned outages of an interconnector leg and a 132kV transmission line, with no load shedding. Its dates is: 05-03-2025. Its affected region is: NSW region (New South Wales, Australia). Its affected key users is: General electricity consumers in NSW and interconnected regions; AEMO confirmed no bulk electrical load disconnection. Its cause type is: Unknown / weather‑related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unplanned outage of Directlink No 1 leg; simultaneous trip of Ewingsdale–Mullumbimby 9G5 132kV line and Directlink DC1; later unplanned outage of the same 132kV line; possible contribution from strong winds associated with Tropical Cyclone Alfred.

title: Comparing Frequency Measures (Frequency, FI, FM)
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Comparing Frequency Measures (Frequency, FI, FM)" posted at 04-03-2025 08:02:00 AM is: The article details the upcoming transition from the Frequency Indicator (FI) to the Frequency Measure (FM) as part of the Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) reform effective 1 July 2025, with the FI used until 8 June 2025. The FI, a slow-moving and often positively biased signal derived from AGC, is predictable and frequently misaligned with frequency deviations, potentially allowing exploitation. The FM, an exponentially-weighted moving average of frequency deviation, is faster-moving, aligns more closely with actual frequency, and reduces predictability, thereby better incentivizing primary frequency response. The comparison shows FM crosses zero more often (4.27% vs 0.85% for FI on the mainland) and aligns with frequency deviation far more frequently, addressing the shortcomings of the FI for the new regulatory framework.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting from 1 July 2025 (payment allocation change) with indicator change on 8 June 2025. Its root_cause is: Implementation of the Incentivising primary frequency response rule, which sought to improve frequency control and cost allocation by replacing the predictable FI with a faster, more aligned FM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-06-2025 (indicator change) and 01-07-2025 (payment implementation). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (mainland and Tasmania). Its affected key users are: Market participants (generators and loads) providing regulation FCAS and frequency response. Its cause type is: Regulatory change / market design change. Its causes are: 1) Replacement of Causer Pays with Frequency Performance Payments; 2) Introduction of FM as the new frequency indicator; 3) Need to eliminate predictability and misalignment in the FI to better incentivize primary frequency response.

title: The curious case of the ‘Q^^TR_CLHA_-600’ constraint equation on Monday 3rd March 2025 (Part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The curious case of the ‘Q^^TR_CLHA_-600’ constraint equation on Monday 3rd March 2025 (Part 1)" posted at 03-03-2025 03:31:00 PM is: On March 3, 2025, the binding of the 'Q^^TR_CLHA_-600' constraint equation caused the QLD spot price to separate from NSW, with the QNI interconnector constrained to flow south, creating a counter-price flow. Many solar farms were curtailed to zero output, while three coal units continued running due to higher LHS factors, resulting in very negative CPD prices (below –$1,000/MWh) and highlighting an unfolding grid congestion event. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (ongoing during the day of March 3, 2025). Its root_cause is: binding of the 'Q^^TR_CLHA_-600' constraint equation due to transmission congestion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different) is: 03-03-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: solar farms (MOUSF1, EMERASF1, MIDDLESF1, CLERMSF1, LILYSF1) and three coal units. Its cause type is: operational constraint (grid congestion). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: constraint equation binding, interconnector QNI constrained to flow south, solar farms curtailed due to low CPD prices, coal units not curtailed due to higher LHS factors.

title: Watt’s Within a Dispatch Interval?  A *very* simplistic view, for a start…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Watt’s Within a Dispatch Interval? A *very* simplistic view, for a start…" posted at 03-03-2025 08:06:00 AM is: This article introduces a simplistic view of the 5-minute dispatch interval in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), explaining its structure (75 four-second timesteps, end-of-interval naming) and the two-step process: a brief period where market calculations occur, followed by units following targets. It is the second in a series aiming to later explore real-world complexities.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 - Less Relevant; Its category is: Energy policy; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; Its root_cause is: Unknown; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 03-03-2025 08:06:00 AM; Its affected region is: Australia (NEM); Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants; Its cause type is: Unknown; Its causes is: None.

title: Three of the common building blocks within the National Electricity Market
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three of the common building blocks within the National Electricity Market" posted at 02-03-2025 02:34:00 PM is: This article explains the various time-based building blocks (millisecond, 4-second, 5-minute, 30-minute, and quarterly timesteps) used in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), detailing their roles in metering, frequency control, dispatch, settlement, predispatch, and hedging, while noting the increasing use of 4-second data for regulation FCAS and frequency disruption analysis. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Not applicable – the article is an educational overview, not a specific event. Its root_cause is: Not applicable. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-03-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants, AEMO, energy analysts, and generators. Its cause type is: Not applicable. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Not applicable.

title: ‘Market Demand’ drops 401MW in 5 minutes on Sunday 2nd March 2025
author: Paul McArdle
