classified_content: The summary of the news "'Market Demand' drops 401MW in 5 minutes on Sunday 2nd March 2025" posted at 02-03-2025 11:39:00 AM is: On Sunday 2nd March 2025 at the 11:10 dispatch interval (NEM time), Market Demand in Victoria experienced a rapid drop of 401 MW within a 5-minute period, an event captured by market monitoring tools and attributed to the growing complexity introduced by increasing rooftop solar penetration, which reduces net demand seen by the grid; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours), as the sudden demand change may trigger frequency regulation responses and affect real-time dispatch. Its root_cause is: Rapid increase in rooftop solar output reducing market demand. Its accident scale is: 401 MW drop over 5 minutes in Victoria. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-03-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (not specified in the article). Its cause type is: Technical/operational (due to renewable generation variability). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Growing rooftop solar penetration, [2] Rapid solar output change leading to a steep decline in net market demand.

title: AER wrote letters to Semi-Scheduled participants (and others) with respect to ‘Expectations surrounding the use of self-forecasting’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER wrote letters to Semi-Scheduled participants (and others) with respect to ‘Expectations surrounding the use of self-forecasting’" posted at 28-02-2025 08:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) sent letters in late February 2025 to semi-scheduled generators and other interested parties, expressing concerns about biased self-forecasting practices that undermine system security, distort AEMO forecasts, create competitive advantages by reducing regulation FCAS cost allocations, increase costs for consumers and other participants, and adversely affect dispatch outcomes and pricing integrity. The AER expects self-forecasts to be accurate, regularly assessed, and continuously improved, warning that despite the new Frequency Performance Payments mechanism, commercial incentives for such behavior remain and will be closely monitored.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from late February 2025. Its root_cause is: Biased self-forecasts submitted by semi-scheduled generators to minimize regulation FCAS cost allocation, causing risks to system security, forecast integrity, and market efficiency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 28-02-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators, AEMO, consumers, and other market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance issue / market manipulation. Its causes is: Biased self-forecasts by semi-scheduled generators to avoid regulation FCAS costs; potential competitive advantage; impact on system security and pricing; increased costs for others.

title: Only 14 weeks to go until Frequency Performance Payments
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Only 14 weeks to go until Frequency Performance Payments" posted at 28-02-2025 02:36:00 PM is: The article announces that the full financial operation of Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) will commence on 8 June 2025, following a non-financial operation (NFO) period that started in December 2024. It outlines reforms to replace the previous Causer Pays approach for regulation FCAS cost recovery and introduce a new two-sided, zero-sum FPP system that pays units for helpful frequency contributions and charges those contributing to poor frequency performance. These changes aim to incentivize primary frequency response (PFR), which had been declining since 2019 as generators voluntarily reduced or stopped providing PFR, leading to deteriorating system frequency control. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The full financial operation starts on 8 June 2025, with the NFO period already underway since December 2024. Its root_cause is: No incentives existed for primary frequency response under prior arrangements, causing generators to reduce PFR provision and resulting in declining system frequency control. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates is: 08-06-2025 (full financial operation date, different from news date). Its affected region is: Likely Australia (operated by AEMO). Its affected key users is: Generators and other market participants providing frequency response. Its cause type is: Regulatory/market reform. Its causes is: [Lack of incentives for primary frequency response; Generators voluntarily stopped or reduced PFR provision; Resultant inadequate frequency control under regulation FCAS alone].

title: Incentivising primary frequency response
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Incentivising primary frequency response" posted at 28-02-2025 02:24:00 PM is: The article details a rule change in the National Electricity Market (NEM) that introduces Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) to incentivise primary frequency response (PFR), effective from 8 June 2025. The reform replaces the ‘Causer Pays’ procedure for allocating Regulation FCAS costs with a new system that rewards helpful frequency contributions and penalises unhelpful ones, using a 5-minute settlement based on a unit’s performance relative to system frequency. This change aims to provide financial incentives for PFR, which previously lacked direct reward, and tightens the link between performance and costs. The summary also explains the new calculation methods, including the Frequency Measure (FM), contribution factors, and the splitting of costs into used and unused Regulation FCAS portions. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 8 June 2025 onwards. Its root_cause is: The need to financially incentivise primary frequency response to ensure grid stability and replace the inadequate ‘Causer Pays’ disincentive. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 8 June 2025 (effective date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, FCAS providers, and other dispatched units with appropriate metering). Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Initiation of rule change proposal in July 2019; 2) Determination in September 2022; 3) Implementation of mandatory PFR requiring a financial incentive; 4) Replacement of the ‘Causer Pays’ procedure with FPP and changed Regulation FCAS cost allocation.

title: About System Frequency
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "About System Frequency" posted at 28-02-2025 02:09:00 PM is: This article provides an educational overview of system frequency in the National Electricity Market (NEM), explaining its definition (50 Hz target), the consequences of supply-demand imbalances, and the integrated chain of six frequency control stages—inertia, primary frequency response, regulation FCAS, contingency FCAS, 5-minute dispatch, and emergency control—along with associated markets and operational principles. It does not report a specific event but serves as a technical reference for understanding how frequency is managed to maintain grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (no discrete event; describes continuous operational framework). Its root_cause is: Not applicable (educational content). Its accident scale is: None. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 28-02-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Generators, load entities, AEMO, market participants, and all consumers connected to the NEM. Its cause type is: Educational/Informational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: No specific cause event; the article describes inherent system phenomena such as supply-demand imbalances leading to frequency deviations, and the need for frequency control mechanisms (inertia, PFR, regulation FCAS, contingency FCAS, 5-minute dispatch, emergency control) to maintain stable operation.

title: Up, up and away … Market Price Cap to increase by $2,800/MWh (or 16%)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Up, up and away … Market Price Cap to increase by $2,800/MWh (or 16%)" posted at 27-02-2025 02:37:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) announced an increase in the Market Price Cap to $20,300/MWh (a 16% rise of $2,800/MWh) effective 1 July 2025, along with a higher Cumulative Price Threshold of $1,823,600/MWh, highlighting that such annual ratchets are expected due to the energy transition's need for stronger price signals to support renewable and firming investments.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).; Its category is: market reactions.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future, from 1 July 2025 onwards.; Its root_cause is: The need to provide sufficient price signals to incentivize investment in renewable generation and firming capacity amidst the energy transition, as indicated by prior reviews.; Its accident scale is: Unknown.; Its dates is: 27-02-2025.; Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market).; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, generators, and consumers.; Its cause type is: Regulatory/administrative decision.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC determination from the 2025-26 Reliability Standard and Settings Review; prior 2022 review recommendations; ongoing energy transition requiring higher price caps to attract investment.

title: Is VRE forecastable?  (Part 1 in what might be a series)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Is VRE forecastable?  (Part 1 in what might be a series)" posted at 27-02-2025 12:54:00 PM is: The article examines the forecastability of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) in Australia's National Electricity Market, arguing that while solar and wind can be forecasted superficially, significant forecast errors persist even near dispatch time—solar becomes more predictable only about two hours out with sizable remaining error, and wind forecast error continues to grow in absolute terms despite earlier relative improvements. Based on data from the GenInsights Quarterly Updates (2024 Q2) and extrapolations to 2025, the analysis underscores the challenge for firming capacity and storage requirements, cautioning against extreme claims that VRE is either completely unforecastable or easily forecastable. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2024 Q2, with implications extending to 2025 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Inherent weather-dependent variability of VRE and limitations of current forecasting models. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27-02-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants including generators, retailers, system operators, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Forecasting and operational challenge. Its causes is: 1) Variable nature of solar and wind resources; 2) Increasing installed capacity of VRE leading to larger absolute forecast errors; 3) Stagnating relative forecast error improvement after ~2020, suggesting diminishing returns from diversity.

title: What’s happened to those Semi-Scheduled units registered to provide FCAS?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s happened to those Semi-Scheduled units registered to provide FCAS?" posted at 25-02-2025 07:22:00 PM is: The article analyzes the declining participation of semi-scheduled units (wind and solar farms) registered to provide Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) in the Australian National Electricity Market, detailing various FCAS dimensions and examining participation trends for units like Hornsdale Wind Farm 2, Kiamal Solar Farm, and Musselroe Wind Farm, noting reduced activity since ~2021 and no bids from Musselroe in 2024, with potential causes including changes in cost recovery mechanisms (transition from Causer Pays to Frequency Performance Payments) and strategic self-forecasting. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with historical trends from 2017 to 2024 affecting future FCAS market dynamics. Its root_cause is: Changes in FCAS cost recovery mechanisms and market incentives leading to reduced economic viability for semi-scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-02-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators (wind and solar farms) and FCAS market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market design changes. Its causes is: Completion of ARENA-assisted trials, reduction in economic viability, transition from Causer Pays to Frequency Performance Payments, potential gaming via self-forecasting.

title: Hot chips, algorithms and electricity markets
author: Declan Kelly
topic: Addressing the challenges of (and opportunities in) 'the rise of the machines'
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot chips, algorithms and electricity markets" posted at 25-02-2025 12:37:00 PM is: This article examines competition in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting the growing role of battery storage and algorithmic bidding software. It draws a parallel to US lawsuits alleging that frozen potato manufacturers colluded via third‑party pricing software (PotatoTrac), raising concerns that similar algorithmic collusion could emerge among battery operators in the NEM. The piece discusses how concentrated ownership of dispatchable generation and the use of common bidding platforms might reduce market competitiveness and lead to higher wholesale electricity prices, while also noting the potential benefits of lower barriers to entry and increased transparency. It concludes with recommendations to manage market concentration, keep barriers low, and review competition frameworks to mitigate risks of algorithmic collusion. Its relevance level is: 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions and competition. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing/long-term (no specific event; ongoing structural and behavioral risks). Its root_cause is: Potential use of common algorithmic bidding software by multiple battery operators, which could enable coordinated pricing or autonomous collusion, analogous to the US hot chip cartel case. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different) is: Same as main date: 25-02-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, battery operators, generators, and market regulators. Its cause type is: Regulatory/market design vulnerability (potential for algorithmic collusion). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) High concentration of dispatchable generation ownership; 2) Increasingly common use of third‑party bidding software platforms by battery operators; 3) Transparency of NEM pricing data enabling algorithmic coordination; 4) Self‑learning algorithms that may autonomously learn to collude; 5) Low barriers to entry for storage but insufficient monitoring of software market concentration.

title: Curtailment at New England Solar Farm – Sat 22nd and Sun 23rd February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment at New England Solar Farm – Sat 22nd and Sun 23rd February 2025" posted at 25-02-2025 09:36:00 AM is: The article reports significant curtailment of the New England Solar Farm unit 2 (NEWENSF2) during daylight hours on Saturday 22 February and Sunday 23 February 2025. Unit output was reduced to 0 MW for several hours each day—from 09:25 to 13:10 on Saturday and from 08:30 to 14:20 on Sunday—while the older unit 1 (NEWENSF1) was not curtailed as severely. Data from 2024 also shows low capacity factors in October due to constraint equations. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (two days). Its root_cause is: Unknown (likely transmission constraints or oversupply). Its accident scale is: No accident; unit output curtailed to zero for several hours. Its dates is: 22-23 February 2025. Its affected region is: New England, Australia. Its affected key users is: New England Solar Farm operator and grid operator. Its cause type is: Operational curtailment. Its causes is: Curtailment due to grid constraints or market conditions.

title: Pronounced ramp in ‘Market Demand’, then subsidence, in South Australia on Saturday 22nd February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Pronounced ramp in ‘Market Demand’, then subsidence, in South Australia on Saturday 22nd February 2025" posted at 22-02-2025 03:27:00 PM is: On 22 February 2025, South Australia experienced a pronounced ramp and subsequent drop in Market Demand, with a ~500MW fluctuation and a specific -229MW drop between 14:50 and 14:55 (NEM time). The likely cause was passing cloud cover affecting solar generation, leading to rapid changes in net demand. No further details were provided; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (minutes to hours); Its root_cause is: passing cloud cover affecting solar generation output; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 22-02-2025; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: meteorological; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ["passing cloud cover leading to variability in solar generation, which in turn caused rapid changes in net market demand"].

title: A closer look at NEM Mainland Frequency (at 100ms cadence) on Thursday afternoon 13th February 2025 (i.e. Part 2)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A closer look at NEM Mainland Frequency (at 100ms cadence) on Thursday afternoon 13th February 2025 (i.e. Part 2)" posted at 21-02-2025 11:36:00 AM is: This article provides a detailed analysis of frequency deviations in the National Electricity Market (NEM) Mainland on 13 February 2025, using 100ms-cadence data. It reveals that the frequency remained below 50Hz for approximately 60 minutes (13:01 to 13:59), below 49.95Hz for about 25 minutes (13:26 to 13:48), and reached a low point of 49.832Hz at 13:31:23.4, which briefly dipped below the NOFB (Normal Operating Frequency Band). The event is examined with time-stamped charts from a 24-hour view and a zoomed 4-hour window (12:00-16:00 NEM time).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13 February 2025, afternoon (specifically 13:01 to 13:59 NEM time).; Its root_cause is: Unknown (the article does not specify the cause of the frequency wandering).; Its accident scale is: Frequency deviation of up to 0.168Hz below 50Hz, lasting ~60 minutes below 50Hz and ~25 minutes below 49.95Hz.; Its dates is: 13-02-2025 (the date of the event, distinct from the article's post date).; Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (Australia).; Its affected key users is: Unknown.; Its cause type is: Grid frequency instability.; Its causes is: Unknown (no specific cause events identified in the article).

title: A curious case of UIGF for Hamilton Solar Farm on Wednesday 12th February 2025 (Part1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "A curious case of UIGF for Hamilton Solar Farm on Wednesday 12th February 2025 (Part1)" posted at 20-02-2025 01:54:00 PM is: On 12 February 2025, the Hamilton Solar Farm (unit HAMISF1) experienced an unusual UIGF behaviour where a working self-forecast switched to AEMO's ASEFS forecast, which incorrectly set the unit's availability to 0 MW for the 08:40 dispatch interval, despite no Semi-Dispatch Cap in place, allowing the unit to follow solar output but raising questions about the implications for Regulation FCAS Cost Recovery (Causer Pays) and the root cause of the forecast switch.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (12 February 2025 dispatch intervals, with ongoing cost recovery effects).  
Its root_cause is: Unknown – loss of self‑forecast and AEMO's ASEFS forecast erroneously outputting 0 MW availability.  
Its accident scale is: Single unit (small scale) but with potential financial impact on FCAS cost allocation.  
Its dates is: 2025-02-12.  
Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (National Electricity Market – NEM).  
Its affected key users is: Hamilton Solar Farm (generator), AEMO, other market participants subject to FCAS cost recovery.  
Its cause type is: Technical/operational (forecast system failure or data issue).  
Its causes is:  
1. Self‑forecast for the unit ceases, unknown reason.  
2. AEMO's ASEFS forecast then sets availability to 0 MW.  
3. No Semi‑Dispatch Cap, so unit follows solar resource normally.  
4. FCAS cost recovery implications arise due to the discrepancy.

title: Large (+405MW) jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Thursday 20th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large (+405MW) jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Thursday 20th February 2025" posted at 20-02-2025 01:09:00 PM is: On 20 February 2025, a large 405MW jump in Market Demand occurred in New South Wales between the 09:35 and 09:40 dispatch intervals, as observed via NEMwatch. This event was noted alongside previously reported 'rogue' sky-high P5 price forecasts for NSW on the same day, suggesting possible market anomalies. The demand jump, though significant, did not trigger further alerts after a subsequent drop of nearly 400MW.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).; Its category is: market reactions.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the same day, minutes to hours).; Its root_cause is: Unknown from the article; possibly a data error or sudden demand shift.; Its accident scale is: Unknown.; Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 20-02-2025.; Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW).; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, grid operators.; Its cause type is: Demand-side fluctuation or data anomaly.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Large increase in market demand of 405MW between dispatch intervals. 2. Preceded by previously reported 'rogue' sky-high P5 price forecasts (potential contributing factor).

title: A curious case of some ‘rogue’ sky-high P5 price forecasts for NSW on Thursday 20th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A curious case of some ‘rogue’ sky-high P5 price forecasts for NSW on Thursday 20th February 2025" posted at 20-02-2025 12:46:00 PM is: The article discusses anomalous P5 predispatch forecasts predicting sky-high prices in NSW for intervals 09:45–10:15 on 20 February 2025, which did not eventuate (actual prices were below $0/MWh). Possible contributing factors include a step change in NSW Market Demand and the 'N>>81_964_82' constraint equation linked to an outage of Line 81 between Liddell and Newcastle, where the forecast RHS was lower than actual. The piece is deliberately vague and highlights forecasting inaccuracies. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability (transmission line outage causing constraint binding). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (same day, potential spike did not materialise). Its root_cause is: outage of Line 81 between Liddell and Newcastle, combined with demand step changes. Its accident scale is: single transmission line outage. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-02-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales). Its affected key users is: unknown (electricity market participants generally). Its cause type is: transmission line outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [outage of Line 81 (Liddell-Newcastle), step change in NSW Market Demand].

title: AER published ‘Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin’ on Tuesday 4th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER published ‘Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin’ on Tuesday 4th February 2025" posted at 20-02-2025 10:33:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released an updated Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin and Checklist on 4 February 2025, detailing three key changes: the enablement of bid MaxAvail for semi-scheduled generators (effective from 7 August 2023), new expectations regarding self-forecasting by SSGs, and minor updates aligned with National Electricity Rule amendments. The bulletin reiterates compliance obligations for SSGs, which rely on weather-dependent fuel sources, and encourages participants to review their practices. This update follows a prior AER communication in December 2024 on self-forecasting behaviors and is based on earlier market changes implemented 18 months ago; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 4 February 2025 (bulletin publication) with underlying market change (MaxAvail) effective from 7 August 2023. Its root_cause is: Regulatory update to reflect previous market changes (MaxAvail enablement) and clarify compliance expectations on self-forecasting and NER amendments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 4 February 2025 (bulletin publication), news article posted 20 February 2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators (SSGs) and their stakeholders, including operators and compliance officers. Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance update. Its causes is: 1) Enablement of bid MaxAvail for SSGs (effective 7 August 2023); 2) AER's expectations on self-forecasting by SSGs; 3) Minor updates to align with National Electricity Rule amendments.

title: NEMDE nightmares – parallel pathways and clashing constraints
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEMDE nightmares – parallel pathways and clashing constraints" posted at 19-02-2025 04:15:00 PM is: On 11 February 2025 at 19:05 NEM time, the South Australian spot price spiked to $4,950/MWh due to clashing constraints from the energisation of Project EnergyConnect Stage 1 and a scheduled Heywood outage, forcing NEMDE to violate a constraint with a $35,000/MWh penalty, reflecting system security challenges from parallel AC pathways. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (single dispatch interval with one subsequent interval). Its root_cause is: Clashing constraints between limiting PEC-1 eastward flows (I_6F_SN_150) and limiting V-SA transfers due to Heywood outage, causing infeasibility and use of violation variable. Its accident scale is: Constraint violation degree of 75.88 MW, with PEC-1 flow exceeding secure limit by ~20 MW; no physical accident. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 11-02-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, and indirectly New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Generators (AGLHAL, HALLWF1), AEMO, market participants, and South Australian electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational/technical (constraint infeasibility). Its causes is: Energisation of Project EnergyConnect Stage 1; scheduled outage of one Heywood circuit; high demand in SA and northwest Victoria; conflicting constraints I_6F_SN_150 and #V-SA_RAMP_F; NEMDE optimization selecting cheapest violation.

title: The forgotten market and its critical role in Australia’s transition to clean energy
author: Anita Stadler
classified_content:
The summary of the news "The forgotten market and its critical role in Australia’s transition to clean energy" posted at 19-02-2025 01:30:00 PM is: This article highlights the overlooked importance of Australia's financial electricity derivatives market (ASX Energy Exchange) as coal-fired generators retire, reducing liquidity for retailers to hedge market risk. It warns that aging coal units (average retirement age ~42 years) face declining reliability (capacity factor fell to 65% in 2024 versus over 80% expected), frequent unplanned outages, and increased cycling stress, which have already caused major losses (e.g., EnergyAustralia's $1.3 billion loss in 2022). To avoid supply disruptions and extreme spot price volatility, the article stresses the urgent need to accelerate renewable buildout (6 GW/year required) and develop new hedging products suited to a weather-dependent, asset-light electricity system. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures (aging coal fleet causing unplanned outages and declining reliability) and Coal-fired policies (retirement timelines and reduced contracting). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing with critical milestones by 2028 (24% of coal capacity retiring) and 2030 (82% renewables target, 57% coal fleet at risk of early closure). Its root_cause is: Aging coal-fired generators (most over 40 years by 2030) experience thermal fatigue, mechanical stress, and increased wear and tear from cycling to accommodate renewables, leading to frequent unplanned outages, reduced operating capacity, and diminishing willingness by owners to back financial derivative contracts, thereby threatening market liquidity and supply security. Its accident scale is: Systemic – not a single accident but ongoing reliability decline of the entire NEM coal fleet (68% older than 40 years by 2030); unplanned outages are now the norm. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-02-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia (excluding Tasmania for ASX products). Its affected key users is: Electricity retailers (especially asset-light ones), large gentailers (e.g., EnergyAustralia), and ultimately residential and business consumers facing higher prices and risk of disruptions. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (thermal fatigue, mechanical stresses, cycling) compounded by market structure (reliance on coal for hedging). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- Coal generators approaching end of asset life (24% retiring by 2028, additional 33% at risk by 2030).  
- Unplanned outages becoming the norm; operating capacity declined to 65% of nameplate in 2024.  
- EnergyAustralia’s $1.3 billion loss in 2022 due to outages at Yallourn and coal supply constraints at Mount Piper.  
- Generators reduce contracted volume before closure, removing supply from ASX Energy Exchange.  
- Slow evolution of financial products (ASX only launching new Morning Peak and Evening Peak contracts in July 2025 after years of delay).  
- Inadequate buildout of renewable generation (only ~2.5 GW/year vs. required 6 GW/year).

title: The state-of-charge for 2024: The highs and lows of the big battery boom
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The state-of-charge for 2024: The highs and lows of the big battery boom" posted at 19-02-2025 06:15:00 AM is: The article provides a detailed analysis of the performance of the National Electricity Market’s (NEM) big battery fleet in 2024, highlighting a surge in market revenue driven by increased frequency of extreme price spikes, with arbitrage overtaking FCAS as the primary revenue source. It examines both macro trends (e.g., rising price-setting influence of batteries, month-to-month revenue volatility) and micro events (e.g., specific high-price days like August 5th, and AEMO directions on November 27th), while noting emerging challenges such as heightened competition in FCAS markets and operational difficulties with double price peaks. The piece underscores that while 2024 was a boom year for batteries, financial models may need to account for evolving market dynamics and regulatory interventions.; Its relevance level is: 2; Its category is: Market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout calendar year 2024; Its root_cause is: Increased frequency of extreme price spikes in the NEM due to supply tightness, islanding events, and grid conditions, which batteries captured for arbitrage revenue; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Multiple notable extreme pricing event dates in 2024 (e.g., Jan 19, Feb 13, May 7-8, Jul 28, Aug 5, Sep 23, Nov 7, Nov 27, Dec 2); Its affected region is: NEM regions (NSW, QLD, SA, VIC); Its affected key users is: Big battery operators, AEMO, electricity consumers; Its cause type is: Market volatility and grid events; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High frequency of extreme price spikes; increasing competition squeezing FCAS returns; AEMO directions to maintain state-of-charge; battery commissioning ramping up operations; diurnal price volatility patterns.

title: Large instantaneous jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Tuesday afternoon 18th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large instantaneous jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Tuesday afternoon 18th February 2025" posted at 18-02-2025 04:32:00 PM is: The article reports a significant instantaneous increase in Market Demand in New South Wales on Tuesday 18th February 2025, where demand jumped by 504 MW (7.6%) during the 16:10 dispatch interval. Similar but smaller changes occurred in South Australia and Victoria, resulting in a NEM-wide increase of 350 MW. The cause remains uncertain, with possible explanations including a data glitch, treatment of flow or losses, or the effect of increasing rooftop solar PV. The event is noted as noteworthy due to its rarity; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Unknown – possible glitch, treatment of flows/losses, or rooftop PV effect. Its accident scale is: 504 MW sudden increase in NSW market demand (moderate). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat same date) is: 18-02-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (primary), also South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants in NSW and NEM (generators, retailers, AEMO). Its cause type is: Operational anomaly / possible data glitch. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Possible data glitch in demand calculation; 2) Treatment of inter-regional flows or losses; 3) Rapid changes in rooftop solar PV output.

title: A look at curtailment through the GSD2024
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "A look at curtailment through the GSD2024" posted at 17-02-2025 02:35:00 PM is: This article analyzes Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) curtailment in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM) using data from the GSD2024 report. It reveals that total curtailment exceeded 4 TWh in 2024, representing 9.4% of potential VRE output, driven by economic curtailment (especially solar farms switching off during negative prices caused by rooftop solar) and network constraints. Solar farms face higher relative curtailment, while wind farms in regions with lower wind penetration achieve better prices. The author suggests that battery investment (9 GW) may help normalize price distributions and reduce binary pricing outcomes.; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Calendar year 2024; Its root_cause is: Excess solar generation from rooftop solar during daytime leading to negative prices, combined with network constraints that limit transmission capacity; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 2024; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia; Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled VRE generators (wind and solar farms); Its cause type is: Economic curtailment and network congestion; Its causes are: Excess solar generation from rooftop solar causing negative prices; PPAs excluding negative prices forcing utility solar to shut down; Network constraints (particularly solar); Low demand relative to supply in certain periods.

title: Reviewing LGC creation at Codrington Wind Farm over ~24 years
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing LGC creation at Codrington Wind Farm over ~24 years" posted at 17-02-2025 09:50:00 AM is: The article reviews the performance decline of Australia's 18MW Codrington Wind Farm from 2001 to 2024 using LGC data, noting a steady drop in capacity factor from ~31% (2002) to ~24% (2024), with particularly poor performance in 2022 and a recent decision to close due to high repowering costs, citing factors like ageing turbines and declining MLF; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: turbine/generator failures; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term decline from 2001 to 2024, with closure imminent in 2025; Its root_cause is: Ageing wind turbines and high repowering costs, combined with declining capacity factor and MLF; Its accident scale is: Small (18MW wind farm); Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 17-02-2025; Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Codrington Wind Farm); Its affected key users is: AEMO, renewable energy certificate market participants, wind farm owners; Its cause type is: Technical degradation and economic factors; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events): 1) Steady decline in turbine performance over ~24 years, 2) Particularly low production in 2022 from unknown reasons, 3) Declining MLF reducing deemed LGC production, 4) High cost of repowering leading to closure decision.

title: Curtailment of Large Solar reaches ~72% (NEM-wide) on Sunday 16th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment of Large Solar reaches ~72% (NEM-wide) on Sunday 16th February 2025" posted at 17-02-2025 08:52:00 AM is: On Sunday 16 February 2025, curtailment of large solar across the NEM peaked at just over 72% due to a combination of far-from-summer temperatures leading to low underlying demand (notably in NSW) and clear skies enabling high solar output (available generation ~8,000 MW out of ~9,599 MW installed capacity). This echoes a similar high curtailment event of 79.2% recorded on 1 September 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (day of event, 16 February 2025). Its root_cause is: Low underlying demand from unseasonably cool temperatures combined with clear skies causing high solar generation, leading to oversupply and necessitating curtailment to maintain grid stability. Its accident scale is: Not an accident, but curtailment severity – 72% of available large solar output curtailed. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-02-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market), with particular mention of New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Large solar generators (semi-scheduled units) and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational / technical (oversupply due to demand-supply imbalance). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Lower underlying demand across the NEM due to cool temperatures in southern regions; 2) Clear skies leading to high solar generation from both small and large installations; 3) Resulting oversupply prompting curtailment of large solar to keep the grid stable.

title: Indicators of solar farm performance with GSD2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Indicators of solar farm performance with GSD2024" posted at 17-02-2025 08:03:00 AM is: This article uses the 2024 Generator Statistical Digest (GSD2024) to assess the potential performance of five stand-alone solar CIS projects (Sandy Creek, Goulburn River, Mokoan, Campbells Forest, Hopeland) by analyzing nearby existing solar farms (Beryl, Glenrowan West, Gannawarra, Western Downs Green Power Hub). It finds that these sites face significant economic curtailment during low or negative midday prices, driving many developers to co-locate battery storage (e.g., 700 MW BESS at Sandy Creek, 280 MW/570 MWh at Goulburn River) to capture spilled generation and improve revenues. Key metrics include capacity factors (22–25% at Beryl), volume-weighted average prices (ranging from $19/MWh at Gannawarra to $64/MWh at Beryl), and heavy curtailment in spring/summer months to avoid negative prices. The analysis highlights how solar farms in the NEM are adapting to the "solar duck curve" through market-responsive operations and battery integration. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with historical data from 2024 and future impacts as projects are built. Its root_cause is: High solar penetration causing low midday prices, leading to economic curtailment and the need for battery storage to optimize revenues. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 17-02-2025 (same as post date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, specifically New South Wales, Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: Solar farm developers, operators, and investors; grid operators; and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Economic/market-driven. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Prevalence of low and negative midday electricity prices due to high solar generation; economic curtailment to avoid negative spot revenues; incorporation of battery storage to shift generation to higher-price periods; and transmission constraints (e.g., X5 constraint at Kerang) affecting some sites.

title: New lowest-ever* point for NSW ‘Market Demand’ on Sunday 16th Feb 2025 was lower than AEMO’s earlier forecasts
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New lowest-ever* point for NSW ‘Market Demand’ on Sunday 16th Feb 2025 was lower than AEMO’s earlier forecasts" posted at 16-02-2025 04:57:00 PM is: On Sunday 16 February 2025, New South Wales recorded a new all-time low for 'Market Demand', which fell significantly below AEMO’s successive forecasts. The low point, observed during the 16:35 dispatch interval, was approximately 3,000 MW lower than the earliest forecasts for the following Sunday and well below all other days in the near-term horizon. Analysis indicates that this record low was driven by a combination of very high rooftop solar photovoltaic output (with almost no cloud cover) and low underlying demand due to minimal air conditioning usage from mild weather. The article also notes two instances of 'lunar megawatts' from large solar farms, suggesting possible self-forecasting anomalies. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (day of event). Its root_cause is: high rooftop solar PV output and low air conditioning demand due to mild weather. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-02-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity market operators (AEMO), solar farm operators, and potentially generators exposed to low demand. Its cause type is: weather/seasonal and technology (rooftop solar). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] High rooftop solar PV generation from clear skies; [2] Low underlying electricity demand due to mild temperatures reducing air conditioning load.

title: ‘Lowest ever*’ point for ‘Market Demand’ in NSW crashes lower, on Sunday 16th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Lowest ever*’ point for ‘Market Demand’ in NSW crashes lower, on Sunday 16th February 2025" posted at 16-02-2025 01:08:00 PM is: On 16 February 2025, the Market Demand in New South Wales reached a record low of 2,532 MW at the 12:00 dispatch interval, 484 MW lower than the previous record from 26 October 2024, driven by mild summer temperatures reducing air-conditioning load and cloudless skies boosting rooftop solar PV output, which further ate into underlying demand. The NEM-wide market demand also dropped to 11,142 MW but remained above the all-time minimum. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 February 2025 (day of event). Its root_cause is: Mild temperatures reducing air-conditioning load and high rooftop PV output due to cloudless skies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 16-02-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and National Electricity Market (NEM)-wide. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather and solar generation. Its causes is: 1. Lower-than-typical summer temperatures leading to reduced air-conditioning demand. 2. Cloudless skies enabling high rooftop solar photovoltaic generation, further reducing net demand.
