title: An initial walk through contributions in South Australia on Wednesday 12th February 2025 (i.e. Part 1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An initial walk through contributions in South Australia on Wednesday 12th February 2025 (i.e. Part 1)" posted at 13-02-2025 02:41:00 PM is: This article provides a high-level analysis of South Australia’s electricity market on 12 February 2025, a hot day that stressed the system. It examines imports from the east (Heywood, Murraylink, and PEC stage 1) which were strong but insufficient to prevent price separation, and local supply including rooftop PV, large solar (near-96% capacity factor during daylight but rapid decline at evening), wind (low output during evening volatility and a noticeable drop at some farms), gas-fired generation (nearly all available capacity was dispatched), liquid-fuel units (higher utilisation than the previous day), battery storage (discharged strongly before the price spike, then ran dry), and negawatts (negligible). The analysis highlights significant gaps between maximum capacity, PASA availability, and actual dispatch, raising questions about commissioning, congestion, and the effectiveness of firming resources, ultimately leading to very tight spare capacity (under 100 MW) and price volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 February 2025. Its root_cause is: High demand from hot weather combined with low wind and solar output during the evening peak, limited availability of gas and liquid fuel generation, and interconnector oscillations that constrained imports at critical times. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 12-02-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Operational and market factors. Its causes is: Hot weather driving high demand; low wind generation during evening; rapid decline in solar output; limited gas and liquid fuel availability due to gaps between capacity and PASA availability; batteries exhausting early; interconnector glitch-induced oscillations.

title: Spare capacity in South Australia drops below 100MW on Wednesday evening 12th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spare capacity in South Australia drops below 100MW on Wednesday evening 12th February 2025" posted at 12-02-2025 07:13:00 PM is: On the evening of 12 February 2025, South Australia experienced a critical supply-demand situation with spare available generation dropping to just 96 MW and IRPM at 4%. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) which escalated to Level 2 (LOR2) by 19:15, requiring a reserve of 245 MW while only 107 MW was available. The tight conditions were driven by very low wind yield despite a gradual increase through the day, combined with high market demand exceeding 3,300 MW in SA and over 32,000 MW across the NEM. AEMO sought an immediate market response, warning of possible intervention; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, from 1900 hrs to 1930 hrs on 12 February 2025. Its root_cause is: Low wind generation output due to weather conditions, causing insufficient supply to meet high demand. Its accident scale is: Reserve shortfall of 245 MW required, with minimum available reserve of 107 MW. Its dates is: 12-02-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA region). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in South Australia, including residential, commercial, and industrial users. Its cause type is: Natural (weather-driven). Its causes is: 1. Low wind yield in South Australia due to calm weather conditions. 2. High market demand in SA and across the NEM. 3. Insufficient spare generation capacity and low IRPM.

title: ‘Market Demand’ soars in South Australia on Wednesday evening 12th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ soars in South Australia on Wednesday evening 12th February 2025" posted at 12-02-2025 06:51:00 PM is: On the evening of 12 February 2025, Market Demand in South Australia surged to a peak of 3,327 MW, the highest since January 2011 and fourth highest since 2008, driving electricity prices above $8,000/MWh. The spike was driven by low wind generation, constrained interconnector flow from Victoria, and oscillations caused by a rogue term in a Murraylink constraint equation, with spare capacity dropping below 100 MW; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: fuel supply issues (low wind), grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate evening of 12 February 2025; Its root_cause is: Low wind generation combined with constrained interconnector flow and a rogue constraint equation term causing oscillations; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: 12-02-2025; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, consumers; Its cause type is: Operational/technical; Its causes is: Low wind generation, constrained interconnector flow between VIC and SA, rogue term in Murraylink constraint equation.

title: Whiplash abated … market oscillations stopped on Wednesday afternoon 12th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Whiplash abated … market oscillations stopped on Wednesday afternoon 12th February 2025" posted at 12-02-2025 03:41:00 PM is: Market price oscillations that occurred on the morning of 12 February 2025 due to intermittently binding constraint equations in the 'N-BU_330_TX_ONE' set—specifically the 'N>>BU_TX_TX78_ML_LV' equation—ceased around 13:58 NEM time after an updated version of the constraint RHS was released at roughly 13:MM, which removed a rogue 3rd Murraylink term that had been identified as a contributing factor. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (morning to afternoon of 12 February 2025). Its root_cause is: Intermittent binding of a constraint equation due to an erroneous term in the RHS of 'N>>BU_TX_TX78_ML_LV'. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 12-02-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely market participants and system operators). Its cause type is: Technical/operational (constraint equation error). Its causes is: [Erroneous 3rd Murraylink term in constraint equation, Intermittent binding of the constraint, Market price oscillations].

title: A trend of daily peak SA ‘Market Demand’– from 1st January 2008 to 11th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A trend of daily peak SA ‘Market Demand’– from 1st January 2008 to 11th February 2025" posted at 12-02-2025 02:02:00 PM is: This article analyzes the long-term trend of daily peak market demand in South Australia from 2008 to February 2025, highlighting that demand on 11th February 2025 could reach as high as 3,200 MW, which would be only the sixth day above that threshold in over 17 years. It provides a chart and table of historical high-demand days, with the all-time peak of 3,402 MW recorded in January 2011. The article emphasizes the rarity of such demand levels, driven by summer heatwaves. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (day – 11th February 2025). Its root_cause is: Hot summer temperatures causing increased cooling demand. Its accident scale is: Peak demand of 3,200 MW (forecast). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 11-02-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown (all electricity consumers, especially residential and commercial). Its cause type is: Natural (weather). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: [Hot summer temperatures, Increased air conditioning load].

title: Whiplash from yo-yo-ing prices (and hence targets etc) on Wednesday morning 12th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Whiplash from yo-yo-ing prices (and hence targets etc) on Wednesday morning 12th February 2025" posted at 12-02-2025 11:10:00 AM is: On the morning of 12 February 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced severe price oscillations between –$20/MWh and $200/MWh, causing "whiplash" for unit targets. The oscillations were driven by the binding and unbinding of constraints from the newly created 'N-BU_330_TX_ONE' constraint set (invoked from the 06:05 dispatch interval on 12 Feb, established on 11 Feb). These constraints relate to an outage on one Buronga 330/220 kV transformer (#7 or #8), part of Project Energy Connect, with the primary constraint equation 'N>>BU_TX_TX78_ML_LV' showing large jumps in its RHS due to complex network flow and feedback terms, including an unusual triple counting of Murraylink flows, leading to market instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – from 06:05 dispatch interval onwards on 12 February 2025, with oscillations persisting through at least 11:00 that morning. Its root_cause is: Outage on one Buronga 330/220 kV transformer (#7 or #8) triggering a new constraint set with oscillating RHS values that cause binding/unbinding, resulting in volatile price swings. Its accident scale is: Single transformer outage (one unit). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 12-02-2025. Its affected region is: Buronga area (NSW/Vic border) within the Project Energy Connect region, affecting the broader NEM (New South Wales, Victoria, and interconnected regions). Its affected key users is: NEM generators and units with bids sensitive to the –$20/MWh to $200/MWh price range (many participants), as well as market operators and traders. Its cause type is: Equipment failure (transformer outage). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Outage of Buronga 330/220 kV transformer (#7 or #8); [2] Invocation of the new constraint set 'N-BU_330_TX_ONE' from 06:05 on 12 February; [3] Constraint equation 'N>>BU_TX_TX78_ML_LV' with complex RHS (including multiple Murraylink flow terms) causing large RHS jumps; [4] Binding and unbinding of the constraint leading to price oscillations.

title: Evening volatility in South Australia, with high demand, on Tuesday 11th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in South Australia, with high demand, on Tuesday 11th February 2025" posted at 11-02-2025 07:12:00 PM is: On the evening of 11 February 2025, South Australia experienced high market demand (peaking near 2,991 MW) and price spikes driven by interconnector constraints (Heywood and Murraylink), a decline in solar generation (especially rooftop PV), insufficient wind ramping, and reliance on gas-fired generation to fill the gap. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 11 Feb, with anticipated continuation on 12 Feb 2025). Its root_cause is: combination of high demand, constrained interconnectors, declining solar output, insufficient wind generation increase, and reliance on expensive gas. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident, only market volatility). Its dates is: 11-02-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: all electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: operational/technical. Its causes is: high demand, interconnector constraints (Heywood and Murraylink), decline in solar (especially rooftop PV), inadequate wind ramp-up, and gas generation filling the gap.

title: Wednesday 12th February 2025 could be a big day for ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wednesday 12th February 2025 could be a big day for ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia" posted at 11-02-2025 07:00:00 PM is: The article forecasts an extreme heatwave in South Australia on 12 February 2025, expected to be the hottest day in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in over five years. This heat is predicted to push market demand above 3,200 MW, potentially nearing the all-time record of 3,402 MW set in 2011. Using the Forecast Convergence widget, the author shows that demand levels above 3,000 MW are forecast for the evening, with recent adjustments downward but still indicating very high demand. If realized, this would be the highest demand since January 2014. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 February 2025. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat wave. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 12-02-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Extreme heat leading to high air conditioning usage and increased electricity demand.

title: Some remarks on Victorian demand
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some remarks on Victorian demand" posted at 10-02-2025 02:49:00 PM is: The article analyzes Victorian electricity demand patterns during summer, focusing on peak demand days on December 16, 2024 and February 2-3, 2025. It compares load shapes between 2008 and 2025, highlighting that rooftop PV has flattened grid demand while total consumption remains high, and notes that Alcoa's Portland smelter reduced load by about 300 MW around 18:00 on both February days, slightly moderating the peaks.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Extreme weather.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – during the hot weather days of February 2-3, 2025 and December 16, 2024.; Its root_cause is: High temperatures (Melbourne max ~38°C) driving increased electricity consumption, combined with population growth and structural changes in demand.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; the load reduction was a controlled operational response).; Its dates (if different from the main date) is: Event dates: 16-12-2024, 02-02-2025, 03-02-2025.; Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia.; Its affected key users is: General residential and commercial consumers, plus Alcoa's Portland aluminium smelter (as a large industrial user).; Its cause type is: Natural (extreme heat) and operational (contractual load reduction by Alcoa).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Hot weather (temperatures around 38°C), population growth since 2008, rooftop PV penetration (4.8 GW in Victoria), and Alcoa's smelter load reduction triggered by contractual arrangements.

title: New FCAS resources dominate enabled levels in 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "New FCAS resources dominate enabled levels in 2024" posted at 10-02-2025 11:41:00 AM is: The article analyzes GSD2024 data, showing that by end of 2024, new inverter-based resources (mainly grid-scale batteries, plus wind, solar, and VPPs) have increasingly dominated enabled levels in contingency FCAS services, widening the gap over traditional thermal and hydro units. Traditional resources still lead in Regulation FCAS, but their enablement declined in late 2023, driven by reduced coal fleet contributions, partly offset by steady hydro levels. The trend raises the question of whether this is driving prices down. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing through 2024, with effects persisting into 2025 given the structural shift. Its root_cause is: Rapid growth of inverter-based resources (batteries, VPPs) and declining enablement from the coal fleet in regulation services. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2025 (same as post date, reflecting analysis up to end of 2024). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Generators, FCAS market participants, and ancillary service providers. Its cause type is: Market structure and technology shift. Its causes are: Increased participation of new inverter-based resources, reduced coal-fired generation enablement, steady hydro enablement, and growth in Very fast FCAS participation.

title: Large jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Friday afternoon 7th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large jump in ‘Market Demand’ in NSW on Friday afternoon 7th February 2025" posted at 07-02-2025 08:52:00 PM is: On Friday 7 February 2025 at the 17:15 dispatch interval (NEM time), a large increase (greater than 400 MW) in 'Market Demand' was observed in the NSW region, triggering a predefined alert in the ez2view monitoring tool. The author notes this is the same alert that previously triggered on 31 January 2025 due to a very large drop in Market Demand suspected to be a data glitch, and while they do not have time to investigate further now, they record the event for future reference. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate at the dispatch interval, though considered momentary if caused by data glitch. Its root_cause is: Suspected data glitch (similar to a prior event). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-02-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Data glitch (suspected). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Large jump in Market Demand (>400 MW) observed at 17:15 dispatch interval, possibly due to erroneous data readings.

title: Berrybank 2 Wind Farm recommences operations, (a little later) on Friday 7th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Berrybank 2 Wind Farm recommences operations, (a little later) on Friday 7th February 2025" posted at 07-02-2025 11:56:00 AM is: The Berrybank 2 Wind Farm restarted operations in the 5-minute period to 11:00 NEM time on Friday 7th February 2025, following a turbine failure that occurred around 4th February 2025. The article briefly notes this recommencement alongside a similar restart of the neighbouring Berrybank 1 Wind Farm. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 4 February 2025 to 7 February 2025 (short-term, a few days). Its root_cause is: turbine failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: Failure on 04-02-2025; recommencement on 07-02-2025. Its affected region is: Berrybank, Victoria, Australia (inferred from NEM time zone). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: equipment failure (turbine). Its causes is: turbine failure at Berrybank 2 Wind Farm (specific component not disclosed).

title: Berrybank 1 Wind Farm recommences operations, on Friday morning 7th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Berrybank 1 Wind Farm recommences operations, on Friday morning 7th February 2025" posted at 07-02-2025 11:37:00 AM is: Berrybank 1 Wind Farm resumed production at 09:30 NEM time on 7 February 2025 after being offline since 4 February due to a turbine collapse at the adjacent Berrybank 2 Wind Farm, which remains shut. The failure is suspected to involve a lightning strike or weld defect, with Vestas investigating. Both farms were taken offline for safety and inspection; Berrybank 1 was cleared to restart while Berrybank 2 stays out for cause analysis. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 3 days (4–7 February 2025) for Berrybank 1; ongoing for Berrybank 2. Its root_cause is: under investigation – possible lightning strike or weld failure triggered by high winds. Its accident scale is: single turbine collapse at Berrybank 2. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: event start 04-02-2025, restart 07-02-2025. Its affected region is: Berrybank Wind Farm, Victoria, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AEMO, grid operators, GPG, Naturgy, Vestas, and potentially electricity consumers. Its cause type is: mechanical failure / extreme weather (unknown). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) turbine collapse at Berrybank 2 on 4 February 2025; 2) both farms taken offline for safety; 3) possible lightning strike or structural defect; 4) Berrybank 1 restarted after inspection; 5) Berrybank 2 remains offline for further investigation.

title: Keeping up with the curtailment 2024: A little? too much? or much ado about nothing?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keeping up with the curtailment 2024: A little? too much? or much ado about nothing?" posted at 07-02-2025 07:06:00 AM is: This article analyzes 2024 curtailment data for variable renewable energy (VRE) in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), where a total of 4.5 TWh was curtailed. Using the Generator Statistical Digest, it breaks down network curtailment (e.g., transmission constraints in Central NSW and North-West Victoria) and economic curtailment (bidding below spot prices) for large solar and wind farms, highlighting seasonal patterns such as a wind drought from April to June that reduced wind capacity factor by 11.02% between June and July, and increased curtailment in spring. The analysis notes that curtailment is a natural outcome in high-renewable grids and may signal opportunities for storage or flexible demand, though it cautions against simplistic fixes due to the interplay between network and economic effects. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2024 calendar year. Its root_cause is: Network constraints (transmission line limits) and economic bidding effects, compounded by a wind drought in Q2 2024. Its accident scale is: 4.5 TWh of total curtailed energy in the NEM in 2024. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 2024 calendar year. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Large solar and wind farms (semi-scheduled VRE units). Its cause type is: Technical and economic. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Network curtailment due to transmission constraints in Central NSW and North-West Victoria; 2) Economic curtailment due to bidding below spot prices; 3) Reduced wind production during wind drought in Q2 2024.

title: Storms on February 4, 2025, and links to wind farm outages
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Storms on February 4, 2025, and links to wind farm outages" posted at 06-02-2025 12:28:00 PM is: Severe storms on February 3-4, 2025 caused a wind turbine collapse at Berrybank Wind Farm in Victoria and a precautionary shutdown at Willogoleche Wind Farm in South Australia on February 4, reducing wind generation capacity in both regions due to damaging winds and safety measures. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term (hours to days). Its root_cause is: Storm-caused damaging winds leading to turbine collapse and safety shutdowns. Its accident scale is: One turbine collapse at Berrybank; multiple turbines unavailable (25 of 32 available at Willogoleche, with zero generation). Its dates is: 04-02-2025 (event date), distinct from the news post date of 06-02-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria (Berrybank) and South Australia (Willogoleche). Its affected key users is: Wind farm operators, electricity grid operators. Its cause type is: Natural disaster/weather. Its causes is: Severe storms on February 3-4 with damaging wind gusts; turbine structural failure at Berrybank; precautionary station outage at Willogoleche due to high winds.

title: Stubborn forecast LOR2 for NSW on Friday afternoon/evening 7th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stubborn forecast LOR2 for NSW on Friday afternoon/evening 7th February 2025" posted at 06-02-2025 11:27:00 AM is: AEMO issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) for NSW on 7 February 2025 from 16:00 to 19:30, with a capacity reserve requirement of 1092 MW but only 574 MW available, due to unplanned outages of Eraring units 1 and 3, Bayswater unit 2, and a transmission constraint on the Avon-Macarthur 330kV line, prompting AEMO to seek market response to avoid intervention. Its relevance level is: 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 07/02/2025 16:00 to 19:30. Its root_cause is: Combined generator outages and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional (NSW). Its dates are: 06-02-2025 (notice), 07-02-2025 (event). Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational/technical failures. Its causes is: Unplanned outage of Eraring unit 1, possible outage of Eraring unit 3, unplanned outage of Bayswater unit 2, transmission constraint on Avon-Macarthur 330kV line.

title: The GSD2024 (Generator Statistical Digest 2024) is released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The GSD2024 (Generator Statistical Digest 2024) is released" posted at 05-02-2025 01:26:00 PM is: The article announces the release of the Generator Statistical Digest 2024 (GSD2024), a comprehensive statistical report covering the performance of generators in Australia's National Electricity Market during calendar year 2024. The report, a collaboration between Greenview Strategic Consulting and Global-Roam Pty Ltd, contains approximately 1,350 pages across three volumes, including new content on semi-scheduled assets, FCAS markets, and bidirectional units under the IESS change. It provides historical data from 2015 to 2024, with analyst insights on topics such as FCAS resources, solar farm performance, curtailment, and battery state-of-charge. The GSD2024 is available for purchase with an optional data extract, aimed at market participants and analysts. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (the report is a retrospective analysis, not a market event). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 05-02-2025. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, analysts, regulators, investors. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: AER announces $9M fine for Callide Power Trading (with respect to 25th May 2021 explosion/incident)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content:
The summary of the news "AER announces $9M fine for Callide Power Trading (with respect to 25th May 2021 explosion/incident)" posted at 05-02-2025 09:56:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) imposed a $9 million penalty on Callide Power Trading—the highest ever for non-compliance with generator performance standards under the National Electricity Rules—stemming from the catastrophic explosion and fire at the Callide C4 unit on 25 May 2021, which caused a prolonged outage that delayed the unit's return to service until late 2024. The fine highlights the critical need for generators to maintain robust systems and protocols to ensure power system security and reliability.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant)  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 25 May 2021 (explosion/outage) to late 2024 (return to service)  
Its root_cause is: Explosion and fire at the Callide C4 unit  
Its accident scale is: Major explosion and fire leading to a multi-year outage of a large coal-fired unit  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Incident: 25 May 2021; Fine announcement: 05 February 2025  
Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide Power Station) and the National Electricity Market (NEM)  
Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the NEM, particularly in Queensland, along with system operators and market participants  
Its cause type is: Equipment failure/explosion  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Explosion within the Callide C4 turbine/generator unit leading to fire and extended operational shutdown

title: Berrybank 1 and Berrybank 2 Wind Farms offline since Tuesday morning 4th February following wind turbine collapse
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Berrybank 1 and Berrybank 2 Wind Farms offline since Tuesday morning 4th February following wind turbine collapse" posted at 05-02-2025 09:38:00 AM is: The news reports that Berrybank 1 and Berrybank 2 wind farms were taken offline on Tuesday morning, 4th February 2025, after a wind turbine collapsed due to thunderstorms and damaging winds. The operator stopped all generation as a safety measure, and the exact duration of the outage is unknown due to limited visibility of semi-scheduled assets.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 4th February 2025, with unknown end date. Its root_cause is: Wind turbine collapse caused by thunderstorms and damaging winds. Its accident scale is: One turbine collapsed, leading to full shutdown of both Berrybank 1 and Berrybank 2 wind farms. Its dates is: Event date 04-02-2025; news date 05-02-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Berrybank area). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related (extreme winds). Its causes is: Thunderstorms and damaging winds; possible structural failure of turbine.

title: Four units at Gladstone still running … was it (is it) partly a game of bluff?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four units at Gladstone still running … was it (is it) partly a game of bluff?" posted at 05-02-2025 08:59:00 AM is: Despite earlier concerns that all six coal units at Gladstone Power Station would be offline due to industrial action, four units are still running as of 5 February 2025; outage data shows reduced output in late January but availability has since increased, with all units expected back online from 9 February, suggesting the situation may be a bluff or chicken tactic in enterprise bargaining negotiations between CS Energy and unions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: late January to early February 2025. Its root_cause is: Enterprise bargaining negotiations and threatened industrial action at Gladstone Power Station, including potential bluff tactics. Its accident scale is: Up to 6 units (852 MW) potentially affected, but currently 4 units running. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-02-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, with potential impact on NEM regions (QLD, VIC, SA). Its affected key users is: AEMO, CS Energy, market participants. Its cause type is: Industrial action / negotiation bluff. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Enterprise bargaining dispute, 2) Protected industrial action threat, 3) Potential bluff by parties.

title: Last Friday’s large and (mysterious) apparent drop in NSW ‘Market Demand’ (part 3) … what helped restrain frequency?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Last Friday’s large and (mysterious) apparent drop in NSW ‘Market Demand’ (part 3) … what helped restrain frequency?" posted at 05-02-2025 08:00:00 AM is: On 31 January 2025, a data glitch in AEMO's system caused a phantom demand increase of about 1000 MW in NSW from ~16:30 to 17:15, leading to over-dispatch of supply and a frequency rise. The frequency was restrained by Primary Frequency Response and Regulation FCAS, with coal units providing ~500 MW and BESS ~200 MW of beneficial response, while wind and solar showed net detrimental deviations. The glitch was later corrected, and the article analyzes the stabilizing contributions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, approximately 45 minutes on 31 January 2025 (16:30 to 17:15). Its root_cause is: Data glitch in AEMO's system causing a phantom demand reading. Its accident scale is: Approx 1000 MW phantom demand. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 31-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Data glitch / scheduling error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. AEMO experienced a data glitch that showed a phantom demand increase from around 16:30. 2. This led to dispatching approximately 1000 MW of extra supply. 3. The glitch was corrected at around 17:15, removing the phantom demand and ramping back supply.

title: Afternoon volatility in NSW on Tuesday 4th February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon volatility in NSW on Tuesday 4th February 2025" posted at 04-02-2025 is: On the afternoon of 4th February 2025, the NSW electricity market experienced notable volatility with a sharp jump in spot prices (while southern regions saw negative prices), driven by a rapid ramp-up in NSW market demand, a spike in hydro output (likely from Tumut 3), and a constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector flow due to the N::N_CTMN_2 constraint equation, leading to a familiar pattern of price separation; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, on 4th February 2025 afternoon. Its root_cause is: Combination of sharp increase in NSW market demand, spike in hydro output, and network constraint (N::N_CTMN_2) limiting south-to-north flow. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-02-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), with minor impact on Queensland (slightly elevated prices) and southern regions (negative prices). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/network constraint. Its causes is: 1) Sharp ramp-up in NSW market demand; 2) Spike in output from NSW hydro facilities; 3) Constrained VIC1-NSW1 flow due to constraint equation N::N_CTMN_2 within N-CTMN_4_WG_CLOSE constraint set.

title: AEMO notes ‘Update to system strength requirements in North Queensland’ to cater for 0 x Gladstone units online
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes ‘Update to system strength requirements in North Queensland’ to cater for 0 x Gladstone units online" posted at 04-02-2025 09:48:00 AM is: AEMO revised system strength constraints in North Queensland, removing the requirement that at least one Gladstone unit be online, because all six Gladstone units are expected to be offline due to industrial action, thereby addressing potential grid instability; Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate and ongoing from 4 February 2025; Its root_cause is: industrial action causing all six Gladstone units to go offline; Its accident scale is: 6 generating units offline; Its dates is: 04-02-2025; Its affected region is: North Queensland, Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely electricity consumers and market participants in the region); Its cause type is: human-induced (industrial action); Its causes is: industrial action by workers at Gladstone power station, leading to all units being taken offline.

title: Remarkable electricity demand outcomes on Monday evening 3rd February 2025 (NEM-wide, in Victoria and South Australia)!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Remarkable electricity demand outcomes on Monday evening 3rd February 2025 (NEM-wide, in Victoria and South Australia)!" posted at 03-02-2025 07:30:00 PM is: On 3 February 2025, the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced historically high evening demand, with NEM-wide peak at 32,925 MW (top 0.56% of days since 2008), Victorian peak at 9,491 MW (top 0.27%), and South Australian peak at 3,150 MW (top 0.13%, the highest since 2014). Although demand fell short of earlier forecasts, these levels were remarkable for their rarity, especially in South Australia where no comparable day had occurred in over a decade. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same-day evening peak). Its root_cause is: Unknown (not specified in the article). Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-02-2025. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, Victoria, South Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers (grid-connected). Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: No cause events identified.

title: Demand, and Price, climbing in ‘NEM south’ … particularly Victoria
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Demand, and Price, climbing in ‘NEM south’ … particularly Victoria" posted at 03-02-2025 03:02:00 PM is: A mid-afternoon snapshot from NEMwatch on 3 February 2025 shows Victorian market demand climbing to 8,311 MW and rising, leading to upward pressure on electricity prices in the NEM south, despite earlier AEMO forecasts suggesting softening demand.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2.  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (mid-afternoon).  
Its root_cause is: increased market demand.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-02-2025.  
Its affected region is: Victoria (and broader NEM south).  
Its affected key users is: wholesale electricity consumers, including households and businesses.  
Its cause type is: demand-driven.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: higher than expected demand during mid-afternoon.

title: North Queensland braces for more heavy rainfall, as 9,000+ customers experiencing power outages overnight due to flooding
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "North Queensland braces for more heavy rainfall, as 9,000+ customers experiencing power outages overnight due to flooding" posted at 03-02-2025 02:46:00 PM is: Extreme rainfall from a persistent low-pressure trough has caused severe flooding in North Queensland, leading to power outages for over 9,000 customers. The Ingham substation was de-energized due to rising floodwaters, cutting supply to approximately 6,700 customers, while other outages affect Townsville and surrounding areas. Demand in the Ross zone dropped sharply, and solar generation is reduced due to heavy cloud cover, with the Sun Metals Solar Farm offline since last Friday. Heavy rainfall is expected to continue for another 36–48 hours, prolonging the disruption to the electricity network until floodwaters recede. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, ongoing from February 2, 2025, with expected duration of several days as flooding persists and water levels recede. Its root_cause is: Extreme rainfall and flooding caused by a trough colliding with humid southeasterly and moist northerly winds, leading to substation flooding and widespread power outages. Its accident scale is: 9,000+ customers without power, one substation de-energized, one solar farm offline, and reduced output from other solar farms. Its dates are: 2025-02-02 to 2025-02-03 (ongoing). Its affected region is: North Queensland, particularly the Townsville and Ingham areas. Its affected key users is: Residential customers (over 9,000) and the Sun Metals Solar Farm (generator). Its cause type is: Natural disaster / extreme weather (flooding). Its causes is: 1. Heavy rainfall from a trough and low-pressure system; 2. Flooding of the Ingham substation; 3. De-energisation of the substation to protect equipment; 4. Reduced solar irradiance due to persistent cloud cover; 5. Pre-existing unavailability of Sun Metals Solar Farm since Friday.

title: Last Friday’s large and (mysterious) apparent drop in NSW ‘Market Demand’ appears some data glitch?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Last Friday’s large and (mysterious) apparent drop in NSW ‘Market Demand’ appears some data glitch?" posted at 03-02-2025 12:21:00 PM is: The article reports a large apparent drop in NSW market demand on 31 January 2025, which was initially mysterious but later identified as a data glitch or forecasting error by AEMO. Analysis of frequency data showed over-frequency due to over-procurement, followed by a correction, indicating no actual demand change. The event was a temporary data anomaly with no physical impact on the grid.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within hours on 31 Jan 2025). Its root_cause is: AEMO data/forecasting error causing phantom demand. Its accident scale is: Negligible (data glitch, no physical impact). Its dates are: 31-01-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: AEMO, NEM market participants. Its cause type is: Data/forecasting error. Its causes are: 1) AEMO data error showing phantom demand increase; 2) Over-procurement of generation; 3) Frequency rise; 4) Correction and normalization.

title: AEMO’s demand forecasts show chance of a bigger demand day declining – for Monday 3rd February 2025
author: Paul McArdle

title: All 6 coal units at Gladstone coincidentally out on unplanned outage this coming week?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "All 6 coal units at Gladstone coincidentally out on unplanned outage this coming week?" posted at 02-02-2025 06:20:00 PM is: All six coal-fired generating units at Gladstone Power Station are scheduled for unplanned outages during the period from February 4 to February 7, 2025, as observed in the MT PASA DUID Availability data. The exact cause is unknown, but it is speculated to involve a common plant component such as coal supply or cooling water (seawater). title: A brief reminder about the complexity of measuring ‘Demand’ …
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A brief reminder about the complexity of measuring ‘Demand’ …" posted at 01-02-2025 05:45:00 PM is: This article clarifies that electricity demand is measured in three distinct ways in the NEM: 'Underlying Demand' (real consumption excluding grid-charged storage but including behind-the-meter rooftop solar), 'Operational Demand' (what AEMO sees on the grid, published since late 2014), and 'Market Demand' (used for dispatch and pricing since the NEM's start). It emphasizes that these measures are not interchangeable, with 'Market Demand' being the most relevant for wholesale price outcomes, while 'Operational Demand' guides system reliability. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Inherent complexity of defining and measuring demand in a grid with growing distributed generation and storage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-02-2025. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: None (explanatory article, not an event).

title: Forecasts ‘hotting up’, for NEMwide demand on Monday 3rd February 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecasts ‘hotting up’, for NEMwide demand on Monday 3rd February 2025" posted at 31-01-2025 01:00:00 PM is: The article discusses rising National Electricity Market (NEM) demand forecasts for Monday 3rd and Tuesday 4th February 2025, driven by a heatwave expected to bring Melbourne's hottest three-day spell in 11 years. AEMO issued local temperature alerts for South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria from 31 January to 4 February, warning of high temperatures up to 39°C, and requested market participants to review generating unit capacity accordingly. Forecast NEM-wide demand peaks at 32,633 MW for the half-hour ending 18:30 on Monday, slightly below the 2024 record but still among the highest levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 3–4 February 2025 (peak demand days). Its root_cause is: Sustained high temperatures increasing cooling load, driving up electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; a forecast event). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 31-01-2025 (article post date) and 3–4 February 2025 (event dates). Its affected region is: South Australia, Tasmania, Victoria (NEM regions with temperature alerts). Its affected key users are: Market participants (generating units and MNSP converter stations) urged to update capacity offers. Its cause type is: Natural (heatwave). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Forecast temperatures reaching or exceeding NEM Local Temperature Alert Levels at multiple weather stations in SA, TAS, VIC from 1–4 February 2025, leading to increased cooling demand.

However, coal-fired generator outages and supply constraints caused wholesale electricity prices to spike, particularly in coal-dependent states, with coal’s share of generation falling below 50% for the first time. title: Interconnector intricacies: double and triple auto-clamping of pesky negative residues
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Interconnector intricacies: double and triple auto-clamping of pesky negative residues" posted at 30-01-2025 03:58:00 PM is: This article examines the increasing occurrence of double and triple cycles of interconnector auto-clamping in the National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically to manage negative Inter-Regional Settlement Residues (IRSRs). It explains how the NEM's zonal pricing design and least-cost dispatch can create counter-price flows, leading to negative residues. An automated Negative Residue Management (NRM) procedure clamps interconnector flows when residues become too negative, but recent events—such as a triple cycle on the Heywood interconnector on December 16, 2024—show premature unclamping that re-accumulates negative residues, costing at least $100,000 per cycle and requiring ramping of 300–500 MW of generation. The article notes a sharp rise in NRM constraints since 2021, with Victoria alone incurring -$34.3 million in negative IRSRs in Q4 2024. It warns that the upcoming Project Energy Connect (PEC) Stage 2 (2027) will create transmission loops, likely exacerbating the problem, and calls for refinement of the NRM process. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 16, 2024 (with ongoing effects since 2021). Its root_cause is: Poor design of the automated NRM procedure causing premature unclamping, combined with high renewable generation and transmission congestion that increase counter-price flows. Its accident scale is: Multiple cycles per day, each affecting 300–500 MW of generation ramping and costing at least $100,000 in negative residues per cycle; cumulative quarterly cost of -$34.3 million in Victoria. Its dates is: 2024-12-16. Its affected region is: Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales (NEM regions). Its affected key users is: Consumers in importing regions (SA) who pay via TUOS charges, and generators in VIC and SA that must ramp up/down. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (automated procedure logic) and market design (zonal pricing). Its causes is: 1) NEM's zonal pricing leading to negative IRSRs, 2) Influx of solar and wind generation causing transmission congestion, 3) Automated NRM procedure with premature unclamping logic, 4) Increasing frequency of counter-price flows.

title: ~13 months later, a GSD2024-inspired review of operations at Cohuna Solar Farm through 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "~13 months later, a GSD2024-inspired review of operations at Cohuna Solar Farm through 2024" posted at 29-01-2025 03:44:00 PM is: The article reviews the operational history of Cohuna Solar Farm through 2024, highlighting a prolonged outage that began in early April 2024 and lasted until at least late January 2025, with only a tiny non-zero output observed on 6 January 2025. The outage appears linked to a rebid on 13 February 2024 citing "inverters stopped in compliance with energy safe victoria's requirements", and subsequent daily bids kept the unit's availability at zero. The article also references earlier 2023 incidents involving Energy Safe Victoria and notes the farm's eventual rebranding to Potentia Energy; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 29 March 2024 (last full output) to at least 29 January 2025 (article date), with possible gradual return-to-service in early January 2025. Its root_cause is: Compliance with Energy Safe Victoria’s safety requirements leading to inverter shutdown and subsequent zero availability. Its accident scale is: Small – a single 20 MW solar farm. Its dates are: 13 February 2024 (rebid citing ESV), 29 March 2024 (last output in 2024), 6 January 2025 (first non-zero output), and 29 January 2025 (article date). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Cohuna Solar Farm). Its affected key users are: Potentia Energy (operator, formerly Enel Green Power), AEMO (market operator), and Energy Safe Victoria (regulator). Its cause type is: Technical/Regulatory (safety compliance). Its causes are: 1) Energy Safe Victoria issued requirements causing inverters to stop on 13 February 2024; 2) Subsequent rebids and daily bids set availability to zero from late March 2024 onward; 3) Possible ongoing technical issues or SCADA signals (UIGF) kept the unit offline for ~10 months.

title: PEC Stage 1 in operation – be careful what you wish for
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "PEC Stage 1 in operation – be careful what you wish for" posted at 28-01-2025 03:24:00 PM is: The article discusses the first regular operation of Project EnergyConnect Stage 1 (PEC1), a new transmission link from South Australia to New South Wales, which is technically treated as an increase in capacity on the existing Victoria-South Australia AC interconnector. It explains that PEC1 will relieve network constraints in north-west Victoria and south-west NSW, leading to counterprice flows (westward during daytime) similar to Murraylink, and likely causing larger negative settlement residues and market confusion. The author highlights updated constraint equations (e.g., V^^V_NIL_KGTS) that include the Victoria-South Australia interconnector as a relieving term, altering dispatch outcomes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from late January 2025 until the completion of PEC Stage 2 in 2026, with ongoing effects on interconnector flows and market dispatch. Its root_cause is: Commissioning of Project EnergyConnect Stage 1 (PEC1) transmission line, which alters network constraint equations and power flows in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates are: 28-01-2025 (same as news date). Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales (NEM regions), particularly the north-west Victoria and south-west NSW constrained areas. Its affected key users are: Generators and market participants in the constrained regions, especially those offering low-priced generation, and NEM watchers/analysts. Its cause type is: Infrastructure/transmission line commissioning. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) PEC1 becomes operational, adding a new 330kV line from Bundey (SA) to Buronga (NSW); 2) AEMO incorporates PEC1 flows into the Victoria–South Australia AC interconnector definition; 3) Constraint equations are updated (e.g., V^^V_NIL_KGTS) to include the interconnector as a relieving term; 4) Resulting counterprice flows and altered dispatch outcomes similar to Murraylink’s behavior.

title: QLD ‘Market Demand’ reached 10,897MW on Friday 24th January 2025 (3rd highest day ever)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD ‘Market Demand’ reached 10,897MW on Friday 24th January 2025 (3rd highest day ever)" posted at 24-01-2025 06:49:00 PM is: On Friday 24 January 2025, Queensland’s electricity market demand hit 10,897 MW during the 17:45 dispatch interval, making it the third-highest day on record. The peak was driven by hot Brisbane weather increasing cooling loads, though it fell short of the records set on 22 January 2024 (11,036 MW) and two days earlier on 22 January 2025 (11,159 MW). Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day – the high demand likely elevated spot prices during the peak period). Its root_cause is: High ambient temperatures in Brisbane driving increased air conditioning usage. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: 24-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in Queensland, particularly residential and commercial end-users. Its cause type is: Weather-driven demand (heatwave). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Hot Brisbane weather conditions; Increased cooling demand from households and businesses; Potential lack of sufficient demand-side response or generation capacity to flatten the peak].

Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 24-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: All electricity consumers in QLD, AEMO, and market participants. Its cause type is: Weather.
