title: A quick review of Queensland bidding over recent days (incl Wed 22nd Jan 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick review of Queensland bidding over recent days (incl Wed 22nd Jan 2025)" posted at 24-01-2025 03:42:00 PM is: The article reviews Queensland's electricity market bidding and supply-side performance over four days, focusing on Wednesday 22nd January 2025 when extreme heat drove high demand and price volatility. It details that coal-fired units had ~1,700MW of unavailable capacity due to unplanned outages (Tarong North 480MW, GSTONE6 280MW) and high-temperature limitations, while variable renewable generation (solar, wind) performed poorly during the evening peak, forcing heavy reliance on gas, batteries, and hydro. The event underscores the need for more storage and firming capacity to manage future VRE-dominated grids. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Several hours on the evening of 22 January 2025, with spillover effects observed over the preceding and following days. Its root_cause is: Heatwave conditions causing high demand and reduced thermal generation efficiency, combined with unplanned coal unit outages and low renewable output during the peak period. Its accident scale is: Unavailable coal capacity of ~1,700MW (including 480MW Tarong North, 280MW GSTONE6, and partial unit deratings), 220MW battery unavailability (chiefly GREENB battery in commissioning), and suboptimal wind/solar output (e.g., wind capacity factor ~20%, solar dropped from 1,600MW to zero in two hours). Its dates is: 22 January 2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, consumers exposed to high prices, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational and weather-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Extreme high temperatures reducing coal unit efficiency; unplanned outages of Tarong North (480MW) and GSTONE6 (280MW); tripping of GSTONE2 unit; low wind farm output during evening (Coopers Gap, Dulacca, Kaban etc.); rapid decline of solar output at sunset; limited battery discharge capacity; high demand due to heatwave.

title: Reintroduction of ‘forecast LOR2’ for QLD for Friday 24th January 2025, with strengthening forecast for ‘Market Demand’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reintroduction of ‘forecast LOR2’ for QLD for Friday 24th January 2025, with strengthening forecast for ‘Market Demand’" posted at 23-01-2025 02:07:00 PM is: AEMO issued a Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for the Queensland region on 24th January 2025 from 18:00 to 19:30 due to strengthening market demand forecasts, following a previous all-time demand record. The forecast capacity reserve requirement of 816 MW exceeds the minimum available reserve of 572 MW, prompting AEMO to seek a market response. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 24th January 2025, 18:00 to 19:30 (NEM time). Its root_cause is: High market demand exceeding available reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: Forecast LOR2 condition with a reserve shortfall of 244 MW (requirement 816 MW, available 572 MW). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 24-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational (high demand). Its causes is: Strengthening forecast for market demand, possibly due to extreme temperatures (implied by record demand).

title: The two rare events that drove NEM wind power in 2024
author: Ashleigh Madden
classified_content: The summary of the news "The two rare events that drove NEM wind power in 2024" posted at 23-01-2025 01:34:00 PM is: Two rare weather events in 2024 significantly affected wind power generation in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). The first was the lingering effect of the January 2022 Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai volcanic eruption, which released massive water vapor into the stratosphere, causing a positive Southern Annular Mode (SAM) and persistent high pressure over southern Australia from March to July 2024, leading to low wind power (lowest week 223 GWh in May). The second was two rare Sudden Stratospheric Warming events in mid-July and early August 2024, which triggered a negative SAM, bringing strong winds, heavy rainfall, and flooding to southern Australia, boosting wind power to a peak of 976 GWh in late August. These events underscore the volatility of wind energy due to atmospheric drivers. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2024 (March–August 2024). Its root_cause is: Combined effect of the January 2022 Hunga Tonga volcanic eruption (water vapor in stratosphere leading to positive SAM) and two rare Sudden Stratospheric Warming events (leading to negative SAM). Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident; rare meteorological events). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 2024 (March–July for positive SAM, July–August for SSW). Its affected region is: Southern Australia (NEM states: Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales, southern Queensland, Tasmania). Its affected key users is: Wind power generators, electricity market operators, and consumers. Its cause type is: Natural (volcanic and atmospheric). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai volcanic eruption on 15 January 2022 released massive water vapor, creating an ozone hole and positive SAM in 2024; 2. Two Sudden Stratospheric Warming events in July and August 2024 disrupted the polar vortex, causing negative SAM.

title: A three-day trend of the supply-side ‘Fuel Mix’ in Queensland–Mon 20th, Tue 21st and Wed 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A three-day trend of the supply-side ‘Fuel Mix’ in Queensland–Mon 20th, Tue 21st and Wed 22nd January 2025" posted at 23-01-2025 08:12:00 AM is: This article analyzes the half-hourly fuel mix in Queensland over three days (January 20-22, 2025), noting a new all-time maximum for Market Demand on Wednesday January 22, with underlying demand likely exceeding 12,000 MW and rooftop PV still contributing about 500 MW during peak demand. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (days). Its root_cause is: record high market demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-01-2025 to 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: high demand. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Not explicitly stated, but implied by high ambient temperatures driving increased electricity consumption.

title: Another quick look at demand forecasts in QLD for this Friday, 24th Jan 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Another quick look at demand forecasts in QLD for this Friday, 24th Jan 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 09:09:00 PM is: On 22 January 2025, market demand in Queensland broke the all-time record set exactly one year earlier, landing almost 500 MW above prior P30 forecasts, and this unexpected surge occurred despite earlier predictions that Friday 24 January would approach the record. The article examines forecast convergence data from ez2view, showing that actual demand matched the higher end of prior forecasts for Friday, while the latest STPASA run projects Friday demand at 10,851 MW for 17:30, suggesting a moderation. The event highlights the volatility and unpredictability of demand forecasts in the NEM.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: Market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (22 Jan 2025 record) and short-term (forecasts for 24 Jan 2025).  
Its root_cause is: Unknown (the article notes the surge came "out of the blue" and does not identify a specific cause).  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident).  
Its dates are: 22-01-2025 (article date and record event date).  
Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia.  
Its affected key users are: All electricity consumers in Queensland (not specified further).  
Its cause type is: Unknown.  
Its causes are: Unexpected demand surge exceeding prior forecasts, possibly driven by weather or other unstated factors.

title: AEMO cancels ‘Actual LOR2’ for QLD on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO cancels ‘Actual LOR2’ for QLD on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 07:50:00 PM is: AEMO cancelled the Actual LOR2 (Lack of Reserve Level 2) condition for the Queensland region at 19:45 on 22 January 2025, lifting a serious grid alert that had been issued earlier due to a new all-time maximum demand, likely driven by extreme heat. The cancellation indicates the reserve margin improved, possibly from additional generation or demand reduction, averting potential supply shortfalls. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 January 2025 evening (from the initial LOR2 notice to cancellation at 19:45). Its root_cause is: Extreme heatwave causing record-breaking electricity demand that strained reserve margins, triggering the LOR2 condition, which was later resolved as the system stabilized. Its accident scale is: Regional grid stress (affecting entire Queensland region, but no actual load shedding reported). Its dates is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in the QLD region, including residential, commercial, and industrial users. Its cause type is: Natural (extreme weather). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Record high temperatures led to all-time maximum electricity demand in QLD; 2) Insufficient available generation or reserve capacity initially created a LOR2 shortfall; 3) Subsequent generation ramp-up or demand reduction allowed the condition to be cancelled.

title: Gladstone unit 2 switches offline, on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Gladstone unit 2 switches offline, on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 07:42:00 PM is: The news reports that Gladstone Unit 2, a coal-fired power plant in Queensland, switched offline at 19:31 NEM time on Wednesday 22 January 2025, during a period of record-high demand and elevated electricity prices. The unit's outage occurred shortly after an Actual LOR2 (Lack of Reserve Level 2) forecast had ended, raising concerns about supply adequacy. The specific reason for the unit's trip is not provided in the article. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (from 19:31 on 22 Jan 2025, ongoing until unit returns). Its root_cause is: Unknown from article. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage. Its causes is: Gladstone Unit 2 tripped offline.

title: How’s Mainland Frequency trending, on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How’s Mainland Frequency trending, on Wednesday evening 22nd January 2025?" posted at 22-01-2025 06:59:00 PM is: On Wednesday evening 22 January 2025, Queensland experienced a very tight supply-demand balance with low IRPM and an actual LOR2 condition, coinciding with a new all-time maximum demand, causing the NEM Mainland frequency to trend downward; the event highlights grid strain under record demand, with interconnectors from NSW and beyond expected to provide frequency response. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, ongoing during the evening of 22 January 2025, with potential immediate effects on frequency stability and reserve margins. Its root_cause is: New all-time maximum demand in Queensland leading to a very tight supply-demand balance and low IRPM. Its accident scale is: Moderate – LOR2 indicates a credible risk of load shedding but no confirmed supply interruptions. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and the broader NEM mainland. Its affected key users is: Wholesale electricity market participants, AEMO system operators, and potentially residential/commercial consumers if load shedding is required. Its cause type is: Demand-driven (record consumption). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Record high electricity demand in Queensland; insufficient generation reserves (low IRPM); actual LOR2 (Lack of Reserve Level 2) condition due to tight supply-demand balance.

title: AEMO declares ‘Actual LOR2’ in QLD on Wednesday 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO declares ‘Actual LOR2’ in QLD on Wednesday 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 06:51:00 PM is: AEMO declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for the Queensland region on 22 January 2025 due to a new all-time maximum demand, resulting in a capacity reserve shortfall (minimum reserve available 395 MW versus requirement of 515 MW) from 1830 hrs until 1900 hrs, prompting AEMO to seek market response and warning of possible intervention. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22-01-2025 18:30 to 19:00 hrs. Its root_cause is: record high demand exceeding available reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: LOR2 (actual lack of reserve) – reserve shortfall of 120 MW. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: QLD (Queensland region). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in QLD). Its cause type is: demand-driven. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: new all-time maximum demand in QLD leading to insufficient reserve capacity.

title: Only 400MW spare (a 4% IRPM) in the QLD-only ‘Economic Island’ on Wednesday 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Only 400MW spare (a 4% IRPM) in the QLD-only ‘Economic Island’ on Wednesday 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 06:45:00 PM is: On 22 January 2025, Queensland faced an extremely tight supply-demand balance with only 400 MW spare capacity (4% IRPM) recorded at 18:40, prompting AEMO to issue a Forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for 18:30–19:00. The condition arose from a new all-time maximum demand of 10,695 MW and declining solar PV output near sunset, with available generation at 11,095 MW. AEMO sought market responses to avert intervention, highlighting acute grid instability risk. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (18:30 to 19:00 on 22 Jan 2025). Its root_cause is: Record high demand combined with sunset-driven reduction in solar PV generation. Its accident scale is: 400 MW spare capacity (4% IRPM) and forecast LOR2 with 343 MW available vs 456 MW required. Its dates is: 22-01-2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in QLD, AEMO, and market participants. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes is: [1] New all-time maximum demand in QLD; [2] Declining solar PV output as sun sets.

title: QLD’s new all-time-maximum ‘Market Demand’ on Wednesday 22nd January 2025 was ~500MW higher than P30 predispatch forecasts
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD’s new all-time-maximum ‘Market Demand’ on Wednesday 22nd January 2025 was ~500MW higher than P30 predispatch forecasts" posted at 22-01-2025 06:37:00 PM is: On Wednesday 22 January 2025, Queensland (QLD) set a new all-time maximum Market Demand, which was about 500 MW higher than AEMO's P30 predispatch forecasts. The article compares the actual demand with successive forecasts using the ez2view 'Forecast Convergence' widget, showing that AEMO underestimated both the timing and peak of QLD's demand growth, while NSW forecasts were accurate. Technical nuances related to the 5-minute settlement framework are also noted. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 January 2025 (short-term). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: New all-time-maximum ‘Market Demand’ seen in QLD on Wednesday 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "New all-time-maximum ‘Market Demand’ seen in QLD on Wednesday 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 06:07:00 PM is: On 22 January 2025, Queensland set a new all-time peak in market demand at 11,159 MW during the 17:45 dispatch interval, surpassing the previous record from exactly one year earlier. This extreme demand, combined with constrained flows on the QNI and Directlink interconnectors, created a tight supply-demand balance that triggered an Actual LOR1 (Lack of Reserve Level 1) notice from AEMO from 17:30 to 19:30, with a reserve shortfall of about 311 MW. The QLD spot price exceeded $4,164/MWh as the region became an economic island, reflecting acute grid instability due to high consumption and limited import capacity.
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).
Its category is: Grid/system instability.
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 January 2025, afternoon/evening (17:30–19:30).
Its root_cause is: Extreme high demand (record-breaking) coinciding with constrained interconnector inflows, causing insufficient reserve capacity.
Its accident scale is: Actual LOR1 condition declared; capacity reserve requirement 854 MW, minimum available 543 MW (shortfall of 311 MW).
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-01-2025.
Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD region).
Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in QLD (wholesale market participants and end-users).
Its cause type is: Operational – demand-driven supply tightness.
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Record high market demand of 11,159 MW; 2) Constrained flow on QNI and Directlink interconnectors limiting net imports; 3) Low instantaneous reserve margin (9% IRPM); 4) Resulting AEMO declaration of Actual LOR1.

title: Daytime outage of Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) in QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Daytime outage of Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) in QLD" posted at 22-01-2025 02:54:00 PM is: This article reports a network outage on the Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) transmission line in Queensland on 22 January 2025, occurring during a hot day with record high electricity demand. The outage activates constraint set Q-BCCP_812, causing significant negative Local Price Adjustments for many generators in northern Queensland, driving their CPD prices to the market floor, while units in the south-east are less affected. The constraint is active until 16:00 today and again tomorrow, but not expected to impact evening peaks. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, within the daytime hours of 22 January 2025 and recurring the following day, but not affecting the evening peak. Its root_cause is: Outage of the Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) transmission line. Its accident scale is: Unknown (planned or forced outage, no accident scale reported). Its dates is: 22-01-2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: Queensland, particularly the north (negative LPA) and south-east (less affected). Its affected key users is: Generators (DUIDs) in northern Queensland with negative Local Price Adjustments; also constrained-up units with positive LPA. Its cause type is: Network outage (transmission line). Its causes is: A single-event network outage of the Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) line.

title: Afternoon volatility in NSW, on Wednesday 22nd January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon volatility in NSW, on Wednesday 22nd January 2025" posted at 22-01-2025 01:41:00 PM is: On a hot day in Queensland and inland Australia, NSW experienced afternoon price volatility with a spike to $14,028.17/MWh due to constrained flow from Victoria to New South Wales, while Queensland set a new all-time maximum demand.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 January 2025, short-term (afternoon peak). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat causing high demand and constrained interconnector capacity (VIC1-NSW1). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), with Queensland (QLD) experiencing high demand. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather and grid constraint. Its causes is: 1. High temperatures in Queensland and inland Australia. 2. Record high demand in QLD. 3. Constrained flow from VIC to NSW (VIC1-NSW1 constrained to flow south). 4. Resulting price spike in NSW.

title: AEMO notes ‘Pre dispatch demand forecast error’ on Wed 22nd Jan 2025 … second day in a row
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes ‘Pre dispatch demand forecast error’ on Wed 22nd Jan 2025 … second day in a row" posted at 22-01-2025 08:36:00 AM is: On January 21 and 22, 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple market notices regarding an error in the pre-dispatch demand forecast affecting all NEM regions. The error was first reported on January 21 (notice MN123513), resolved later that day (MN123516), but recurred on January 22 (MN123548). AEMO stated that dispatch results were not impacted and that they were working to resolve the issue, though no specific cause or details were disclosed. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (January 21-22, 2025, with each instance resolved within hours; no lasting market disruption). Its root_cause is: Unknown (not disclosed by AEMO). Its accident scale is: Unknown (minor, as dispatch operations unaffected and notices indicate a software or data processing error). Its dates is: January 21-22, 2025. Its affected region is: NEM regions (National Electricity Market of Australia). Its affected key users is: AEMO, NEM market participants, and entities relying on pre-dispatch forecasts for scheduling. Its cause type is: System/software error (inferred; no specific cause given). Its causes is: [1] Unspecified error in pre-dispatch demand forecast calculation; [2] Recurrence of the same issue on consecutive days.

title: Ramping constraints in South Australia, with outage on Robertstown – Tungkillo 275 kV line 1 or 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Ramping constraints in South Australia, with outage on Robertstown – Tungkillo 275 kV line 1 or 2" posted at 22-01-2025 07:56:00 AM is: On Wednesday 22 January 2025, ramping constraints were invoked in South Australia and Queensland due to outages on the Robertstown–Tungkillo 275 kV line and the Bouldercombe–Calliope River 275 kV line, respectively, coinciding with a new all-time maximum demand record in Queensland; the constraints, active for about an hour from the 07:45 dispatch interval, also triggered numerous other bound constraint sets across mainland regions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 January 2025, 07:45 dispatch interval for approximately one hour. Its root_cause is: Outages on two transmission lines causing ramping constraints that limit generator output changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown – only the specific line outages and invoked constraints are noted; no details on extent of disruption. Its dates is: 22-01-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (Robertstown–Tungkillo line) and Queensland (Bouldercombe–Calliope River line). Its affected key users is: Generators (DUIDs) subject to the bound constraints; indirectly, electricity consumers in those regions may face price impacts. Its cause type is: Transmission line outage (planned or unplanned) leading to operational security constraints. Its causes is: Outage on Robertstown – Tungkillo 275 kV line 1 or 2 in South Australia; Outage on Bouldercombe to Calliope River (812) 275 kV line in Queensland.

title: NEM batteries transitioned to bidirectional units in 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM batteries transitioned to bidirectional units in 2024" posted at 21-01-2025 02:57:00 PM is: This article details the 2024 transition of scheduled batteries in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) from separate generation and load units to bidirectional units (BDUs) as required by the IESS Rule Change. It provides a table of cutover dates for 24 battery systems, explaining the overlap period where both old and new units coexisted before the old units were deregistered. It also lists new batteries registered directly as BDUs and warns of potential double-counting of metrics like max capacity during the transition. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2024, with effects on market dispatch and registration. Its root_cause is: Implementation of the Integrating Energy Storage Systems (IESS) Rule Change. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident). Its dates (if different) are: The transition events occurred between July and December 2024, with specific cutover dates listed in the table. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery system operators, AEMO, and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: The IESS Rule Change requiring batteries to be registered as bidirectional units.

title: Operational snags at Bayswater unit 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Operational snags at Bayswater unit 2" posted at 21-01-2025 01:14:00 PM is: Bayswater unit 2 (BW02) experienced an unplanned outage on 21 January 2025 due to a boiler issue, just three days after returning from a previous unplanned outage (2 Jan to 18 Jan). The outage is expected to last until 5 February 2025. This follows a pattern of operational challenges, including an unplanned outage in July 2024, a planned outage from September to December 2024, and another unplanned outage from 12 to 22 December 2024. Additionally, AGL's Loy Yang A4 unit has been running at part-load with a flagged unplanned outage. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate; outage started 21 Jan 2025, expected to last 2 weeks until 5 Feb 2025. Its root_cause is: Boiler issue (as per rebid reason on 20 Jan 2025). Its accident scale is: Unknown (specific capacity or MW loss not given). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-01-2025 (main event), but also includes previous unplanned outages on 12-22 Dec 2024 and 2-18 Jan 2025. Its affected region is: Likely the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, specifically Bayswater Power Station (New South Wales). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (forced). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Boiler issue on 20 Jan 2025 leading to outage on 21 Jan; previous unplanned outages on 12-22 Dec 2024, 2-18 Jan 2025, and July 2024; planned outage Sep-Dec 2024; also Loy Yang A4 part-load and upcoming unplanned outage.

title: Bundey to Buronga (6F) line to be energised 0700 21st January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bundey to Buronga (6F) line to be energised 0700 21st January 2025" posted at 21-01-2025 09:14:00 AM is: AEMO issued a market notice announcing that the Bundey to Buronga 330kV (6F) transmission line will be energised at 07:00 on 21 January 2025 to enable further testing of Project Energy Connect (PEC-1) Stage 1 and HIC capacity release; future outages of the line will be notified via NOS, and no additional testing opportunities are currently forecast. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 07:00 on 21 January 2025 onwards during the testing period; exact end date unknown. Its root_cause is: Commissioning and testing of Project Energy Connect (PEC-1) infrastructure. Its accident scale is: None (planned energisation, no accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-01-2025. Its affected region is: Bundey to Buronga corridor, affecting the South Australia–New South Wales interconnector. Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, and stakeholders involved in PEC-1 testing. Its cause type is: Planned infrastructure energisation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: - AEMO scheduled energisation of the 6F line at 07:00 on 21 Jan 2025; - This action supports further testing of PEC-1 capacity release as part of the final test program.

title: Forecast ‘Market Demand’ for QLD on Friday 24th Jan 2025 approaching all-time maximum
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast ‘Market Demand’ for QLD on Friday 24th Jan 2025 approaching all-time maximum" posted at 21-01-2025 09:11:00 AM is: The article reports that on Tuesday 21 January 2025, the forecast for Queensland's Market Demand on Friday 24 January 2025 is 10,897 MW, just 139 MW below the all-time record of 11,036 MW set on 22 January 2024, with an earlier forecast having predicted a potential new record of 11,211 MW for Thursday; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 24-01-2025; Its root_cause is: Unknown; Its accident scale is: Not applicable; Its dates is: 24-01-2025; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia; Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in QLD; Its cause type is: Demand increase; Its causes is: Unknown (likely weather-related).

title: AEMC grants ‘urgent’ Rule Change Request for Port Lincoln and Snuggery as out-of-market reserve
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC grants ‘urgent’ Rule Change Request for Port Lincoln and Snuggery as out-of-market reserve" posted at 19-01-2025 10:55:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has granted an urgent rule change request from South Australia's Minister for Energy, allowing the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to negotiate with Engie to use the Port Lincoln and Snuggery generators as an out-of-market reserve for up to two years, effective from 23 January 2025, to ensure power supply reliability during the summer. This follows the earlier closure of these dispatchable generators, which threatened grid stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 23 January 2025, lasting up to two years; Its root_cause is: Closure of Port Lincoln and Snuggery generators by Engie, creating a potential supply shortfall in South Australia; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date) are: Decision made 15 January 2025, effective 23 January 2025; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users are: AEMO, Engie, and South Australian electricity consumers; Its cause type is: Regulatory/policy change; Its causes are: Early closure of Port Lincoln and Snuggery generators, urgent rule change request from SA Minister for Energy Tom Koutsantonis, need to maintain summer reliability.

title: Further on the N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV constraint around the 14:00 dispatch interval on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 8
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Further on the N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV constraint around the 14:00 dispatch interval on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 8" posted at 16-01-2025 12:35:00 PM is: This article examines the N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV constraint that bound at 14:00 on 15 January 2025, limiting the combined inverter count from New England Solar Farms to 100. Despite having more than 100 inverters available initially, the constraint equation forced the RHS to 0 MW when inverters exceeded 100, resulting in zero energy targets. From 14:40, the available inverters were reduced to exactly 100, allowing non-zero targets after a brief lag, and the constraint ceased by 15:00, with inverter numbers gradually recovering from 15:30. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 14:00 to 15:30 on 15 January 2025. Its root_cause is: The constraint equation N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV sets RHS to 0 MW when the sum of available inverters from both solar farms is greater than or equal to 101, requiring a reduction in inverter count to 100 to allow non-zero output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: New England region, Australia. Its affected key users are: Operators of New England Solar Farm 1 and New England Solar Farm 2. Its cause type is: Operational constraint. Its causes are: 1) Constraint bound at the 14:00 dispatch interval, triggering a 0 MW RHS. 2) Inverters available remained above 100 until 14:40, keeping targets at zero. 3) At 14:40, inverters were reduced to exactly 100, enabling non-zero dispatch targets. 4) Constraint ceased at 15:00, and inverters gradually increased from 15:30 onwards.

title: Ausgrid report that 140,000 customers in NSW were impacted or lost power last night Jan 15th 2025 .. Part 7
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Ausgrid report that 140,000 customers in NSW were impacted or lost power last night Jan 15th 2025 .. Part 7" posted at 16-01-2025 09:59:00 AM is: Severe thunderstorms in New South Wales on the evening of January 15, 2025, caused widespread power outages affecting 140,000 customers across Sydney, Newcastle, and the Central Coast, with 50,000 restored by morning. Ausgrid crews responded to over 560 electrical hazards, while AEMO issued a market notice reporting two 132 kV lines north of Newcastle tripped at 2019 hrs, disconnecting 80 MW of bulk electrical load without requiring load shedding. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather (primary) and grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate disruption; restoration ongoing over multiple days. Its root_cause is: Severe thunderstorms causing fallen trees and branches on powerlines, leading to line trips and customer outages. Its accident scale is: 140,000 customers impacted, 80 MW bulk load disconnected, 560 electrical hazards reported. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (Sydney to Newcastle corridor, Central Coast, Hunter region). Its affected key users is: Residential and business customers, including life support customers. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (storm). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Thunderstorms; fallen trees and branches on powerlines; two 132 kV lines (Tomago – Taree and Tomago – Brandy Hill) tripped; 80 MW bulk load disconnected.

title: Further detail for the 14:00 dispatch interval on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 6
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Further detail for the 14:00 dispatch interval on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 6" posted at 15-01-2025 08:14:00 PM is: On the 14:00 dispatch interval of 15 January 2025, the NSW spot price reached the Market Price Cap, QLD prices were elevated, and VIC prices dropped, while contingency lower FCAS prices in QLD spiked due to binding constraint equations. Four significant constraints bound, causing a significant change in Target Flow on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Generation changes included two Snowy Hydro units ramping up by 781 MW aggregate in NSW, while many solar farms in NSW ramped down, notably the New England Solar Farm. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the 14:00 dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Binding of four significant constraints combined with rapid solar farm ramp-down and hydro ramp-up causing price spikes to Market Price Cap. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NSW, QLD, VIC). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/market constraints. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Binding of four significant constraints; 2) Significant change in Target Flow on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector; 3) Ramp-down of multiple solar farms in NSW; 4) Rapid ramp-up of Snowy Hydro units.

title: Drop in NEM Mainland Frequency (around 14:00) on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 5
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Drop in NEM Mainland Frequency (around 14:00) on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 5" posted at 15-01-2025 04:41:00 PM is: This article (Part 5 of a series) reports a significant drop in NEM mainland frequency measured in Brisbane around 14:00 NEM time on 15 January 2025, which visually approaches but does not clearly exceed the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). The event coincides with previously explored volatility, and the article indicates further investigation is needed to determine precise details. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (short-term duration). Its root_cause is: Unknown (not specified in the news). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland (Australia). Its affected key users is: All electricity market participants (generators, consumers, and grid operators). Its cause type is: Grid disturbance (frequency deviation). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unspecified; under investigation as part of a broader analysis of the day's grid events.

title: About the ‘N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV’ constraint equation that violates at 14:00 on Wednesday 15th January 2025 – Part 4
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About the ‘N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV’ constraint equation that violates at 14:00 on Wednesday 15th January 2025 – Part 4" posted at 15-01-2025 04:10:00 PM is: On 15 January 2025 at 14:00, the ‘N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV’ constraint equation was invoked as part of the N-MUTW_88 constraint set, due to a brief outage of the Muswellbrook to Tamworth (88) line. The constraint equation set the combined target of New England Solar Farm 1 and 2 to 0 MW, causing a sharp reduction in solar output from 370 MW at 13:55 to 94 MW at 14:00. The violation occurred because the number of available inverters across the two solar farms exceeded 100, triggering the constraint. This contributed to a significant aggregate reduction in Large Solar supply in NSW, as noted in earlier parts of the series.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact from 14:00 to 14:35 on 15 January 2025 (duration of constraint invocation). Its root_cause is: Inverter availability count at New England Solar Farm 1 and 2 exceeded 100, causing the constraint equation to violate and force combined output to 0 MW. Its accident scale is: Reduction in combined output from ~370 MW to 94 MW (actual) and target of 0 MW; significant curtailment of solar generation. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: New England region of New South Wales, Australia (specifically the Muswellbrook-Tamworth line and New England Solar Farm site). Its affected key users is: New England Solar Farm 1 and New England Solar Farm 2 (operators/owners). Its cause type is: Technical/operational (constraint equation violation due to inverter availability exceeding threshold). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Scheduled brief outage of the Muswellbrook to Tamworth (88) line at 14:00; 2) This invoked the N-MUTW_88 constraint set, which included the ‘N_NEWENSF1+2_100-INV’ equation; 3) The equation’s logic (based on number of available inverters) triggered because the combined inverter availability exceeded 100, forcing the target to 0 MW; 4) Resulting in drastic curtailment of solar output from both units.

title: Weather a factor on Wednesday 15th January 2025? … Part 3
author: Adam Kent
classified_content: The summary of the news "Weather a factor on Wednesday 15th January 2025? … Part 3" posted at 15-01-2025 03:50:00 PM is: The news discusses a storm moving across Victoria and New South Wales on 15th January 2025, which may have caused unplanned outages of two transmission lines (Lower Tumut-Yass 330 kV and Murray-Lower Tumut 330 kV), leading to constraint violations and pricing volatility in the electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day, 15th January 2025). Its root_cause is: A large storm causing transmission line outages. Its accident scale is: Two unplanned transmission line outages (Lower Tumut-Yass 330 kV Line and Murray-Lower Tumut 330 kV Line). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria and New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (electricity market participants and consumers likely affected). Its cause type is: Weather-related (storm). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Storm moving across Victoria and NSW led to unplanned outages of "Lower Tumut – Yass 330 kV 03 Line" and "Murray – Lower Tumut 330 kV 66 line".

title: About the unplanned outage of Lower Tumut – Yass 330 kV 03 Line on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About the unplanned outage of Lower Tumut – Yass 330 kV 03 Line on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 2" posted at 15-01-2025 03:38:00 PM is: The news reports an unplanned outage of the Lower Tumut – Yass 330 kV 03 Line in NSW on 15 January 2025 at 1358 hrs, triggering AEMO market notice MN123273 and invoking constraint set N-X_LTYS_MNYS, which affected inter-regional transfer limits and contributed to early afternoon price volatility in the NSW electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate to short-term (hours to days). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of a 330 kV transmission line. Its accident scale is: Single transmission line outage. Its dates is: 15-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region, specifically Lower Tumut – Yass corridor. Its affected key users is: NEM participants, interconnectors (VIC1-NSW1, N-Q-MNSP1, NSW1-QLD1, V-S-MNSP1). Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (technical fault). Its causes is: Unplanned trip of Lower Tumut – Yass 330 kV 03 Line.

title: Early afternoon volatility in NSW, on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 1 in a multi-part exploration
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early afternoon volatility in NSW, on Wednesday 15th January 2025 … Part 1 in a multi-part exploration" posted at 15-01-2025 02:37:00 PM is: On 15 January 2025, early afternoon volatility hit the NSW electricity market due to a planned outage of the Marulan-Yass 330kV transmission line (Transgrid Line 5), which triggered binding constraint equations (e.g., N::N_MNYS_2, N^^N_BDBU_X5_BESH, V>>BDBU_ELML_BAML2) and a rapid reversal of the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector flow—from 792 MW export to 592 MW import in five minutes (a 1,300 MW swing). This, combined with a sharp drop in solar yield in NSW, drove prices high despite a 690 MW increase in hydro dispatch. The event was foreshadowed by AEMO forecasts but occurred earlier than expected, highlighting grid vulnerability due to the transmission outage and resulting constraint binding. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate short-term volatility on the afternoon of 15 January 2025, with potential residual effects until the line is restored. Its root_cause is: planned outage of the Marulan-Yass 330kV line (Transgrid Line 5). Its accident scale is: Unknown (planned outage, not an accident). Its dates (if different) is: same as main date (15-01-2025). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity market participants, including generators, retailers, and consumers in NSW facing high wholesale prices. Its cause type is: network outage (planned transmission line maintenance). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: planned outage of Marulan-Yass 330kV line; binding of constraint equations N::N_MNYS_2, N^^N_BDBU_X5_BESH, and V>>BDBU_ELML_BAML2; rapid reversal of VIC1-NSW1 interconnector flow; drop in solar yield; increased demand on supply in NSW and QLD.

title: About flexibility at Mt Piper coal-fired power station … another ‘sign o the times’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "About flexibility at Mt Piper coal-fired power station … another ‘sign o the times’" posted at 15-01-2025 01:18:00 PM is: The article details how EnergyAustralia has enhanced the flexibility of the Mount Piper coal-fired power station in NSW by lowering its minimum operating level from 320MW to 150MW over several steps and conducting a two-shifting trial on 12 October 2024, where a turbine was taken offline for up to 12 hours while the boiler remained hot, as a response to the market transformation driven by cheap renewables; this operational adaptation is a sign of the changing role of coal in electricity markets.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from October 2024 onward.  
Its root_cause is: Increasing renewable generation and market need for flexible coal plant operation.  
Its accident scale is: Not applicable (trial, not an accident).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main date: 15-01-2025; Event date: 12-10-2024.  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (National Electricity Market).  
Its affected key users is: EnergyAustralia, AEMO, electricity market participants.  
Its cause type is: Operational trial and technical modification.  
Its causes is: Increased renewable penetration necessitating coal plant flexibility; successful two-shifting test using steam bypass.

title: Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 3)
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 3)" posted at 15-01-2025 12:30:00 PM is: This article, the final instalment of a series on wholesale market modelling, explores four key 'Dark Arts' of modelling that can obscure the true cost of electricity: crude chronologies (oversimplifying time slices), carbon budgets and renewable targets (hiding costs in shadow prices), market-based new entry (a flawed iterative approach often used instead of least-cost optimisation), and post-processing of results (adding noise or simulating rare events beyond model capability). The author stresses that modellers must be transparent about these assumptions to avoid misleading conclusions about electricity prices; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant); Its category is: Energy policy; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; Its root_cause is: Unknown; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 15-01-2025; Its affected region is: Unknown; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Unknown; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 2)
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 2)" posted at 15-01-2025 11:30:00 AM is: This article explains operational dispatch modelling, the second step in wholesale market modelling, which builds on capacity expansion by simulating real-time dispatch with detailed factors such as participant bids, plant limitations (ramp rates, minimum stable levels, outages), and variations in renewable energy and demand traces. It explores various bidding assumptions (contract, game-theoretic, historical), the inclusion of operational constraints and outages, and the use of multiple weather/demand traces to understand price distributions. The piece emphasizes the difficulty of forecasting super-high price volatility due to transient, unpredictable market disturbances and notes the limitations of models in capturing such events. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Not applicable. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 15-01-2025 11:30:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 1)
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Opening the black box: A beginner’s guide to wholesale market modelling (Part 1)" posted at 15-01-2025 10:30:00 AM is: This educational article introduces capacity expansion modelling in wholesale electricity markets, explaining how linear programming and solvers (e.g., Gurobi) are used to find the least-cost combination of generation investments and dispatch, given inputs such as fuel costs, demand traces, transmission constraints, and policy targets. It emphasizes the importance of opening the "black box" of models to build intuition for energy planners, covering key assumptions, constraints, and the interpretation of shadow prices as marginal costs. title: About the short-notice ‘Actual LOR2’ condition for NSW on Monday 13th January 2025 … Part 3 of a Case Study.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About the short-notice ‘Actual LOR2’ condition for NSW on Monday 13th January 2025 … Part 3 of a Case Study." posted at 14-01-2025 10:46:00 AM is: The article details an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition that emerged unexpectedly for the New South Wales (NSW) region on the evening of 13 January 2025, with the alert being issued only about two hours prior. Analysis reveals that the forecast LOR2 only appeared in the 16:00 NEM time run (less than two hours before the actual notice) and that a sudden drop of approximately 490 MW in available generation from NSW coal units (from 6,580 MW to 6,090 MW) combined with modest wind and relatively low demand (~10,500 MW) contributed to the reserve shortfall. The article compares this event to historical patterns, noting that such short-notice LOR2 occurrences are rare (only about 20% of actual LOR2 events are unforecast within four hours). This incident is examined as part of a broader case study on forecast convergence and system reliability. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (the actual LOR2 condition occurred on the evening of 13 January 2025, with the market notice published at 17:49:35 NEM time). Its root_cause is: Sudden reduction in available generation from NSW coal-fired units (approximately 490 MW drop) coupled with low wind generation, leading to a reserve shortfall that was not forecast until very shortly before the event. Its accident scale is: Unknown (the article does not quantify the exact MW shortfall or duration; however, LOR2 indicates less than the required reserve margin). Its dates is: 13-01-2025 (the event date). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region of the Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and market participants in NSW, though no specific key users are named. Its cause type is: Technical/operational (generator unavailability or forced outages). Its causes is: 1. A drop in available generation from NSW coal units of about 490 MW between the 13:30 and 14:00 predispatch runs. 2. Modest wind generation reducing overall supply. 3. The reserve shortfall emerged with very short forecast lead time (less than 2 hours), indicating unexpected generator changes.

title: The ‘N::N_MNYS_2’ constraint equation and the ‘Actual LOR2’ in NSW on Monday 13th January 2024 … Part 2 of a Case Study.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘N::N_MNYS_2’ constraint equation and the ‘Actual LOR2’ in NSW on Monday 13th January 2024 … Part 2 of a Case Study." posted at 14-01-2025 09:33:00 AM is: The article analyzes an Actual LOR2 condition in NSW on 13th January 2025, caused by the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector being constrained in both directions—the ‘N::N_MNYS_2’ constraint equation limited exports to 0 MW, while the ‘NRM_NSW1_VIC1’ constraint limited imports to 0 MW. This reduced headroom for southern NSW generators, leading to a tight supply-demand balance and a spike in the marginal value of the constraint to –$1,315.21/MWh, highlighting a short-lived grid instability event.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13th January 2025, short-term (within that day). Its root_cause is: The ‘N::N_MNYS_2’ constraint equation bound the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector's export limit to 0 MW, combined with the ‘NRM_NSW1_VIC1’ constraint limiting imports, reducing available generation from southern NSW and triggering the Actual LOR2. Its accident scale is: Actual LOR2 condition (reserve shortfall). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 13-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Generators in southern NSW and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Technical/operational grid constraint. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ‘N::N_MNYS_2’ constraint equation bound limiting VIC1-NSW1 exports to 0 MW; ‘NRM_NSW1_VIC1’ constraint equation bound limiting imports to 0 MW; negative residues on the 330 kV lines connecting southern NSW to Sydney.

title: (Almost completely) out of the blue, an Actual LOR2 … in NSW on Monday 13th January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(Almost completely) out of the blue, an Actual LOR2 … in NSW on Monday 13th January 2025" posted at 13-01-2025 06:25:00 PM is: AEMO declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in the NSW region from 17:30 to 18:00 on 13 January 2025, with a required reserve of 714 MW but only 560 MW available, while the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector was constrained and unable to flow in either direction; the alert was later downgraded to Actual LOR1. The situation appeared to occur without a clear immediate cause, as other NEM regions and generators seemed normal. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 13-01-2025 17:30 to 18:00. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: 714 MW reserve deficit. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 13-01-2025. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users is: electricity consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: Data centres, clean energy and net zero ambitions
author: Warwick Forster
classified_content: The summary of the news "Data centres, clean energy and net zero ambitions" posted at 09-01-2025 03:10:00 PM is: This article explores the growing role of data centres in Australia’s National Electricity Market, focusing on their flat load profile, potential to become market participants, and the complexities of offering renewable energy and net-zero products to customers. It details current and future renewable energy certificate schemes (including time-stamped REGO post-2030), carbon emission scopes, network charges, and on-site solar considerations, highlighting that data centres will face increasing complexity in managing energy and emissions goals as client demand for sustainability rises. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing to 2030 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Growing data centre demand and increasing client sustainability/net-zero requirements driving need for sophisticated energy management and bespoke renewable products. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-01-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (specifically the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Data centre operators, their customers, energy retailers, and renewable energy certificate scheme participants. Its cause type is: Policy and market development. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Rapid growth in internet traffic, crypto-mining, and AI driving data centre electricity demand; 2) Changes in renewable energy certificate schemes (RET, REGO) from 2001 to 2030+; 3) Introduction of time-stamping for renewable generation matching; 4) Client net-zero ambitions requiring scope 1-3 emissions management; 5) Need to pass through energy and network costs to customers in a transparent manner.

title: Out of cycle MTPASA update identifies low reserves in VIC
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Out of cycle MTPASA update identifies low reserves in VIC" posted at 09-01-2025 12:36:00 PM is: On 8 January 2025, AEMO published an out-of-cycle MTPASA run identifying low reserve conditions (LRC) in Victoria for January to March 2025 due to additional generator outages—Loy Yang A Unit 4 forced deratings and Yallourn West Unit 4 extended outage—that reduced scheduled coal fleet availability by 6-8% (260-360 MW). The annual unserved energy forecast exceeded the reliability standard from January 2025 to January 2026 for Victoria; New South Wales faced similar risks but no LRC was declared.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: January 2025 to March 2025 (low reserve condition) and annual unserved energy exceedance from January 2025 to January 2026; Its root_cause is: Additional outages in Victoria’s coal fleet, specifically forced deratings at Loy Yang A Unit 4 and extended outage at Yallourn West Unit 4; Its accident scale is: Reduction of 260-360 MW (6-8% of Victorian coal fleet availability); Its dates is: 8 January 2025 (MTPASA run and LRC declaration), 9 January 2025 (news publication); Its affected region is: Victoria (primary), with secondary mention of New South Wales; Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in Victoria (and potentially New South Wales); Its cause type is: Unplanned generator outages and forced deratings; Its causes is: [1] Loy Yang A Unit 4 forced deratings, [2] Yallourn West Unit 4 extended outage.

title: Embankment rebuild completed at Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station, twelve months after Cyclone Jasper
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Embankment rebuild completed at Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station, twelve months after Cyclone Jasper" posted at 09-01-2025 11:34:00 AM is: CleanCo CEO Tom Metcalfe announced that the rebuild of the earthen embankment at Kuranda Weir, damaged by record flooding during Cyclone Jasper in December 2023, is nearly complete. The flooding destroyed parts of the weir and damaged the water intake for the 66MW Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station, forcing both units offline for approximately six months while temporary repairs were made. A permanent concrete dam wall is still under construction to enhance flood resilience. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Six months (December 2023 to June 2024) of reduced generation, with ongoing infrastructure upgrades extending into 2025. Its root_cause is: Cyclone Jasper in December 2023 caused extreme flooding that exceeded the weir's historical water level record, damaging the power station's water intake and embankment. Its accident scale is: Damage to Kuranda Weir's embankment and water intake; both generating units (66MW total) offline for six months. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: December 2023 (cyclone event), December 2023 to June 2024 (outage period), and January 2025 (embankment rebuild completion). Its affected region is: Barron Gorge Hydro Power Station and Kuranda Weir, Far North Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: CleanCo (operator of the power station) and the local electricity grid dependent on hydro output. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (cyclone-induced flooding). Its causes is: Cyclone Jasper in December 2023 produced record-high water levels above 12 metres at Kuranda Weir, destroying parts of the earthen embankment and damaging the power station's water intake, leading to a prolonged outage.

title: A couple of wind turbines returned to service at Murra Warra 1 Wind Farm in Victoria
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A couple of wind turbines returned to service at Murra Warra 1 Wind Farm in Victoria" posted at 07-01-2025 04:24:00 PM is: The news reports that at Murra Warra 1 Wind Farm in Victoria, a couple of wind turbines have returned to service as part of ongoing staged maintenance to replace failed bearings. The wind farm, with 61 Senvion turbines, has seen persistent availability issues since at least late 2022, with typically 21 turbines unavailable. In November 2024, 15 turbines were shut down for maintenance, but by early December 2024, the number available increased to about 45, indicating five turbines returned. The article highlights improved transparency from AEMO's new ELAV data series, which reveals the extent of technical availability problems at ageing wind farms. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from late 2022 to January 2025, with recent improvement in December 2024/January 2025. Its root_cause is: Bearing failures in Senvion turbines. Its accident scale is: Partial wind farm shutdown; 32 out of 61 turbines affected, with 15 offline for maintenance at peak. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-01-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (Murra Warra 1 Wind Farm in north-western Victoria). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, AEMO, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Technical failure (bearing failures). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Bearing failures in Senvion turbines; [2] Staged maintenance program; [3] Reduced turbine availability over two years.

title: Forecasts moderate, for NSW on Monday 6th January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecasts moderate, for NSW on Monday 6th January 2025" posted at 06-01-2025 03:35:00 PM is: AEMO cancelled a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) for the NSW region on 6 January 2025 after earlier forecasts of tightness moderated; the cancellation was based on a ~1,000 MW reduction in forecast market demand and improved available generation, despite modest wind and past-peak solar conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but cancelled on the same day (6 January 2025). Its root_cause is: Moderation of forecasts – lower demand and higher available generation than previously expected. Its accident scale is: None (no actual incident; forecast cancellation). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-01-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Forecast market demand for NSW on the afternoon/evening reduced by approximately 1,000 MW compared to earlier forecasts; [2] Forecast available generation for the evening improved beyond earlier projections.

title: Further analysis (and more questions) about four VIC Wind Farms on Monday 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Further analysis (and more questions) about four VIC Wind Farms on Monday 16th December 2024" posted at 06-01-2025 10:06:00 AM is: On 16 December 2024, during extreme heat (up to 47°C) and high wind conditions in Victoria, four wind farms in the GPG portfolio (Berrybank 1 & 2, Hawkesdale, Ryan Corner) experienced a steady decline in output and significant forecasting challenges. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had previously forecast a severe supply-demand balance (LOR3) for NSW, but load shedding was avoided despite record NEM-wide demand. Analysis shows that during semi-dispatch cap (SDC) periods, the AWEFS power curve model produced inaccurate forecasts, leading to large raw off-target measures. The decline in output appears linked to high temperatures, possible high wind speed cut-out (gusts >25 m/s), and manual adjustments to local limits (LOCL) and elements available (ELAV). The article highlights the need for better forecasting and transparency in wind farm operations during extreme weather events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 December 2024 (single-day event). Its root_cause is: Combination of extreme heat, high wind speeds, and AWEFS forecasting limitations during SDC periods. Its accident scale is: No accident reported (operational performance issues only). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16 December 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (primarily Western Plains and Moyne Shire). Its affected key users are: Wind farm operators (GPG portfolio), AEMO, electricity consumers in NEM (especially NSW/VIC due to LOR3 forecast). Its cause type is: Operational and environmental. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Extreme heat (47°C), high wind speeds causing cut-out, AWEFS forecasting inaccuracies during SDC, AEMO-imposed constraints, and auto-bidder behavior leading to output yo-yo-ing.

title: The two Frontier Economics reports pertaining to nuclear
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The two Frontier Economics reports pertaining to nuclear" posted at 06-01-2025 09:26:00 AM is: The article briefly notes two Frontier Economics reports published on 15 November 2024 and 13 December 2024, which provide independent economic assessments of including nuclear power in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), amid political uncertainty regarding the Albanese government's stability. The reports are funded by Frontier Economics and involve consultations with government and private sectors, but no further analysis is provided at this point; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (potential long-term impact if policy is adopted). Its root_cause is: Policy analysis on nuclear power integration. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 15 November 2024, 13 December 2024. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Policymakers, electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Policy/analysis report publication. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Publication of two Frontier Economics reports on nuclear power in the NEM.

title: AEMO notes hot weather forecast for SA and VIC from 3rd January to 5th January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes hot weather forecast for SA and VIC from 3rd January to 5th January 2025" posted at 03-01-2025 11:51:00 AM is: AEMO issued a market notice warning of forecast temperatures exceeding local alert levels in South Australia and Victoria from 3 to 7 January 2025, requesting market participants to review weather forecasts and update generator capacity offers accordingly to maintain grid reliability during the heatwave. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 3 January 2025 to 7 January 2025. Its root_cause is: Forecast high temperatures reaching or exceeding NEM Local Temperature Alert Levels (e.g., 39°C for most stations, 33°C for Launceston). Its accident scale is: Unknown (no actual failures reported). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 03-01-2025 (notice issue date). Its affected region is: South Australia (Port Augusta) and Victoria (Latrobe Valley, Melbourne, Mildura). Its affected key users is: All NEM market participants, especially generators and MNSP converter station operators. Its cause type is: Natural (weather). Its causes is: Forecast heatwave conditions with temperatures ≥39°C in key weather stations, triggering AEMO’s local temperature alerts.

title: AEMO publishes forecast LOR2 for NSW for Monday afternoon/evening 6th January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "AEMO publishes forecast LOR2 for NSW for Monday afternoon/evening 6th January 2025" posted at 02-01-2025 08:27:00 AM is: AEMO issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for the NSW region on 6 January 2025, initially reporting a minimum capacity reserve of 509 MW (Notice 122695) and then updating it to just 103 MW (Notice 122697) after about 140 minutes, as the forecast supply-demand balance tightened by ~400 MW. The tighter conditions are driven by forecast market demand exceeding 12,000 MW, a drop in available generation (possibly due to an extended or new outage), and low solar yield (modest potential from large solar farms) and wind yield (~700 MW) on that day. AEMO is seeking a market response and has not yet estimated the latest intervention time.

Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 6 January 2025 from 15:00 to 18:30 (NEM time).  
Its root_cause is: High market demand coinciding with reduced available generation from a suspected outage extension or new outage, plus below-average solar and wind generation forecasts.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident but a forecast reserve shortfall; the minimum reserve dropped from 509 MW to 103 MW, indicating a significant tightening).  
Its dates (if different from the main date) are: News date: 02-01-2025; Event date: 06-01-2025.  
Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.  
Its affected key users are: All electricity consumers and market participants in the NSW region, with AEMO as system operator.  
Its cause type is: Combined demand-side and supply-side factors (high demand, generation outages, low renewable output).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are:  
- Forecast market demand exceeding 12,000 MW on 6 January 2025 (higher than surrounding days).  
- A drop of ~400 MW in available generation compared to earlier forecasts, likely from an extended or new outage.  
- Low solar yield potential from large solar farms on 6 January (after strong yields on preceding days).  
- Low wind yield potential (~700 MW) from wind farms across NSW on that day.

title: Victoria sees new ‘lowest ever*’ point for ‘Market Demand’ (and Actual MSL1) on Wednesday 1st January 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria sees new ‘lowest ever*’ point for ‘Market Demand’ (and Actual MSL1) on Wednesday 1st January 2025" posted at 01-01-2025 01:39:00 PM is: On 1 January 2025, Victoria's Market Demand hit a new record low of 1,400 MW, triggering an actual MSL1 (Minimum System Load) condition from AEMO, warning of an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state. The low demand was driven by high rooftop solar PV output combined with 8 of 10 brown coal units running solely for grid stability services, pushing other generation (including large-scale renewables) out and causing deeply negative spot prices. The MSL1 condition was forecast to persist until 14:00 hrs on the same day. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (same day, 11:56–14:00 hrs on 1 Jan 2025). Its root_cause is: operational imbalance between high rooftop solar generation and minimum brown coal output required for secure system operations, leading to extremely low residual demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown (record low demand, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-01-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (VIC region). Its affected key users is: AEMO, grid operators, and market participants (especially generators impacted by negative prices). Its cause type is: operational/technical. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Record-high rooftop solar PV output on a public holiday; (2) Necessity to keep 8 out of 10 brown coal units online for "Keeping the Lights On Services"; (3) Resultant crowding out of other generation, causing demand to fall below the MSL1 threshold.
